title: Creating an Emergency Board to Investigate a Dispute Between the Long Island Rail Road Company and Certain of Its Employees

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Authority to appoint the board and receive its findings rests with the President under section 10 of the Railway Labor Act, but the board only investigates and reports; no decision authority over the parties is pooled, and the statutory scheme provides the external frame.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The board has a defined obligation to report within thirty days and the statutory status-quo period is self-executing; responsibility for the mechanism is clear.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single-purpose order addressing one labor dispute; nothing distinct is packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The only compliance rule (no change in conditions except by agreement for the statutory period) is precisely defined by section 10 of the Railway Labor Act with a clear trigger and duration.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The investigate-and-report design creates no incentive for parties or the board to undermine the dispute-resolution purpose; authority rests on an active, unexpired statute, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The board's authority is inherently time-limited: it reports within thirty days and the status-quo obligation runs thirty days after the report, so the grant self-terminates.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed; the order operates within the Railway Labor Act's ordinary procedures.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The status-quo obligation is enforceable through the statutory scheme and the affected parties (carrier and unions) each have standing and incentive to surface breaches by the other.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: This is a routine, statutorily-templated emergency board with no precedential authority claim beyond the single dispute.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the National Mediation Board's judgment is referenced as the trigger; no agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The status-quo rule contains only the narrow 'except by agreement' carve-out inherent to the statute, which does not swallow the rule.

note: Standard statutory emergency board under Railway Labor Act sec. 10; clean architecture across all flags with a self-executing time limit.

title: Restoring Certain Land Reserved for Military Purposes of the United States to the Jurisdiction of the Territory of Hawaii

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing land-status restoration; no ongoing decision authority is exercised or pooled in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The action is complete on signing; there is no implementation chain that could fail and no responsibility to assign.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single self-contained land transfer; nothing distinct is packaged with it.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time land-status restoration creates no ongoing compliance relationship for anyone, so there is no enforcement mechanism to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral rule is created, so there are no incentives on any actor class to evaluate.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing transfer grants no continuing authority that could be subject to sunset or reauthorization.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed; the order simply restores jurisdiction under section 91 of the 1900 Organic Act.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no ongoing enforcement relationship for any third party to have standing over in a completed land transfer.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Narrow administrative land action with no systemic or precedential implications beyond the described tract.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Jurisdiction moves from federal military reservation to the Territory of Hawaii under explicit statutory authority; no agency's statutory mandate is countermanded or set in conflict.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population or exemption structure exists in a one-time land restoration.

note: One-time self-executing land-status restoration; compliance-regime flags (4,5,8,11) and 6,9 are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE per the land-transfer test in the Application Notes.

title: Terminating the War Food Administration and Transferring Its Functions to the Secretary of Agriculture

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: All functions, powers, and broad delegation authority of the War Food Administrator (including redelegation power under EO 9280 para. 9) are pooled in the Secretary of Agriculture, who may exercise and subdelegate them 'to such extent and subject to such conditions as he shall determine,' concentrating substantial war-food authority in one officer.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of Agriculture is a single, named, Senate-confirmed officer clearly responsible for the transferred functions; responsibility is consolidated rather than diffused.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order does one coherent thing (terminate the WFA and move its functions to a single existing department); the transferred functions travel as a unit rather than being packaged to force acceptance of unrelated matter.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Secretary may reorganize the agencies 'in such manner as [he] may deem desirable' and continue, modify, or revoke all existing orders and regulations at will, so two officials could in good faith administer the food-control regime very differently with no defined standard.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The transfer creates no reward for undermining the food-supply purpose; authority rests on the then-active First War Powers Act, which had not lapsed, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The transferred authority derives from wartime powers (First War Powers Act) whose subject matter has a natural end-state tied to the war's resolution, yet the order permanently vests the functions in the Secretary with no reauthorization or expiration trigger.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No external oversight mechanism (judicial, congressional reporting, IG) is removed; the order reassigns internal executive functions.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: This is an intra-executive transfer of program administration, not a compliance regime resting on regulated-party self-reporting; ordinary channels for surfacing failures continue.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Amending 'all prior Executive orders in conflict... to the extent of such conflict' (Sec. 6) and continuing all WFA orders in force establishes a broad supersession precedent that entrenches consolidated wartime food authority in a peacetime cabinet department well past the WFA's dissolution.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Functions are consolidated into the Secretary of Agriculture, the department that housed the WFA; no other agency's statutory mandate is countermanded or set into jurisdictional conflict.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order contains no exemption or waiver structure carving actors out of the food-control regime.

note: Diff-aware: EO 9334 (predecessor establishing the WFA) reviewed; this order abolishes that structure and pools its broad war-powers functions and redelegation authority permanently in the Secretary of Agriculture with open-ended reorganization discretion. Firing flags 1,4,6,9.

title: Amending Executive Order No. 11248, Placing Certain Positions in Levels IV and V of the Federal Executive Salary Schedule

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order merely substitutes a position title in a salary schedule; no decision authority is created or pooled in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: A self-executing title/pay-level substitution with no implementation chain that could fail.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single-provision amendment; nothing distinct is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Placing a position in a pay level creates no ongoing compliance relationship or enforcement mechanism.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A pay-schedule listing creates no behavioral rule and thus no incentives to assess.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: No ongoing grant of authority exists; a fixed pay-level substitution has nothing to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight is removed; diff against EO 11248 shows only a wording substitution reflecting an agency reorganization.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance or enforcement relationship exists for a third party to police.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Narrow administrative pay-schedule housekeeping with no systemic or precedential implications.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only reflects the EPA's absorption of a former Interior position; no agency mandate is redirected by this listing itself.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population or exemption structure in a salary-schedule amendment.

note: Partial amendment of EO 11248 (reviewed): substitutes one position title in Level V. Narrow administrative action; compliance-regime and systemic flags NOT_APPLICABLE.

title: Amendment of Executive Order No. 10501 of November 5, 1953, Relating to Safeguarding Official Information in the Interests of the Defense of the United States

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The new historical-research exception vests in each agency head sole, self-judged discretion to grant outside persons access to classified information based on his own determinations of 'consistent with national defense' and 'trustworthy,' with no external check on the grant.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The agency head who originates the material is the named, identifiable actor responsible for access decisions and for the safeguarding steps; responsibility is clear even if discretionary.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The three amendments (declassification coverage, historical-research access, transmission of confidential material) all concern the same classification framework and could each be justified by the order's purpose; no forced-package problem.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The historical-research access standard turns on the agency head's undefined findings that access is 'clearly consistent with the interests of national defense' and that the person 'is trustworthy,' criteria two officials could apply very differently in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The amendments do not reward undermining information-security; they add a bounded research exception and tighten transmission rules. Authority rests on active constitutional/statutory classification power, not a lapsed statute.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Classification safeguarding is a standing procedural framework maintained through ordinary supersession (this very order amends numbered predecessor EO 10501, which itself supersessed EO 10290), so the permanence carve-out applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds a discretionary access channel and transmission options; it does not remove any pre-existing oversight mechanism over the classification system.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Whether classified material is improperly disclosed to a research grantee depends on the granting agency head's own safeguarding steps; no external actor has standing and access to surface an improper grant, since the determinations are internal and self-certified.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The amendments are confined refinements to an existing classification regime with no identifiable precedent usable in unrelated domains.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Each agency head acts only over material his own agency originated; no other agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The historical-research provision is an access exception concentrated in a single actor (the agency head) with discretion bounded only by his own undefined 'national defense' and 'trustworthy' findings, allowing case-by-case carve-outs from the standard access rule of section 7.

note: Partial amendment of EO 10501 (reviewed). Assessed the specific added provisions; the historical-research access exception is a discretionary, self-certified agency-head carve-out driving flags 1, 4, 8, and 11.

title: Placing an Additional Position in Level V of the Federal Executive Salary Schedule

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Adds one position to a pay level; no decision authority is created or pooled.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Self-executing pay-schedule listing with no implementation chain to fail.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single-provision addition; nothing distinct is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A pay-level listing creates no ongoing compliance relationship or enforcement mechanism.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No behavioral rule is created, so no incentives to assess.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: No ongoing grant of authority to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Diff against EO 11248 shows a pure addition of item (12); no pre-existing oversight is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance relationship exists for a third party to police.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Narrow administrative pay-schedule housekeeping with no systemic or precedential implications.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No agency mandate is redirected by adding one GSA position to the schedule.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population or exemption structure in a salary-schedule amendment.

note: Partial amendment of EO 11248 (reviewed): adds one GSA position to Level V. Narrow administrative action; compliance-regime and systemic flags NOT_APPLICABLE.

title: Blocking Government of Haiti Property and Prohibiting Transactions With Haiti

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of the Treasury is authorized to identify sanctioned Haitian nationals, promulgate rules, employ all IEEPA powers, and redelegate freely, concentrating expansive sanctions-administration and designation authority in one officer subject only to consultation with State.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of the Treasury (in consultation with State) is the named, identifiable actor responsible for administration; responsibility for implementation is clear.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: All provisions (asset blocking, trade prohibitions, evasion bar) serve the single stated purpose of pressuring the de facto Haitian regime; no unrelated matter is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Sec. 2 blocks property of any Haitian national providing 'substantial financial or material contributions' or 'doing substantial business' with the regime 'as identified by the Secretary'; 'substantial' is undefined, so two officials could designate very differently in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The prohibitions align actor incentives with the coercive purpose. Authority rests on an active national emergency declared in EO 12775 and unexpired IEEPA/NEA, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is emergency economic-sanctions authority tied to the Haitian crisis, exactly the kind of exceptional power that should track the emergency's resolution, yet the order contains no sunset or reauthorization trigger of its own (relying on the standing NEA renewal cycle only indirectly).

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order does not remove pre-existing oversight; the non-justiciability clause in Sec. 8 is a standard no-private-right provision, not a removal of otherwise-available review, and IEEPA/NEA reporting to Congress is preserved (Sec. 9(b)).

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement depends heavily on U.S. persons and financial institutions self-identifying blocked property and on Treasury designations; no external third party is given standing and information access to surface undisclosed blocked interests held offshore or through controlled entities.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The broad blocking of property of entities 'wherever located or organized, owned or controlled by' the regime, plus discretionary vessel/aircraft entry bans, establishes a sweeping precedent for extraterritorial reach usable against future targets under the same IEEPA framework.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order directs all agencies to assist and delegates to Treasury/State within their existing sanctions roles; it supplements rather than countermands any agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Broad licensing/exception authority ('except to the extent provided in regulations, orders, directives, or licenses which may hereafter be issued') is concentrated in Treasury with no criteria, allowing the agency to define the practical scope of coverage case by case.

note: IEEPA/NEA sanctions order supplementing EO 12779 and building on the emergency in EO 12775; discretionary Treasury designation, licensing, and extraterritorial blocking drive multiple flags. Predecessors 12775/12779 available in folder and consistent with this reading.

title: Exemption of Edward B. Russ From Compulsory Retirement for Age

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A one-time individual retirement exemption exercised directly by the President under statute; no ongoing decision authority is pooled in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Self-executing individual exemption for a fixed one-year term; no implementation chain that could fail.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single-subject individual action; nothing distinct is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time personnel exemption creates no ongoing compliance relationship or enforcement mechanism.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No behavioral rule is created for any actor class, so there are no incentives to assess.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The exemption is expressly limited to one year ending February 29, 1940, so it self-terminates.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed; the President exercises a specific statutory exemption power.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing enforcement relationship exists over which a third party could have standing.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Narrow individual administrative action with no systemic or precedential implications.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the State Department is affected as employer; no agency's statutory mandate is redirected.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: A single-person exemption is not a coverage-carve-out architecture applied against a regulated population.

note: One-time individual retirement exemption (extending EO 7791) with a fixed one-year term; compliance-regime flags 4,5,8,11 and flag 9 NOT_APPLICABLE.

title: Amending Executive Order No. 11174, Establishing the Presidential Service Certificate and the Presidential Service Badge

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Authority to award the certificate/badge is distributed among the several service secretaries; no single official pools discretionary power over a contested outcome.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Named service secretaries are responsible for awards to their respective personnel; responsibility is clear.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The amendment coherently renames and reestablishes one certificate/badge scheme; nothing unrelated is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: An honorific award scheme with eligibility (one year of White House assignment) creates no enforcement or compliance obligation on any regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A ceremonial badge/certificate creates no ongoing behavioral compliance rule to generate perverse incentives.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a standing procedural/ceremonial framework maintained through ordinary supersession (it amends numbered predecessor EO 11174), so the procedural-framework carve-out applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Diff against EO 11174 shows a rename and re-designation of the award; no pre-existing oversight is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance regime exists over which a third party would need standing to surface violations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Narrow ceremonial administrative action with no systemic or precedential implications.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Award authority is allocated cleanly among the service secretaries; no agency's statutory mandate is countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: An eligibility-based honor with no exemption structure applied against a regulated population.

note: Partial amendment of EO 11174 (reviewed): renames the White House certificate/badge to Presidential and adjusts award authority. Ceremonial administrative action; compliance-regime and systemic flags NOT_APPLICABLE.

title: Administration of Foreign Assistance and Related Functions and Arms Export Controls

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order redistributes numerous discrete statutory functions among the Secretaries of State and Defense and the AID Administrator, largely with consultation requirements; it disperses rather than pools authority in one hand.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each delegated function is assigned to a named officer, often with an explicit consultation partner, so responsibility for each is traceable.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Though lengthy, every amendment concerns the single coherent framework of foreign-assistance delegation under EO 12163; it modifies one framework rather than forcing acceptance of unrelated matter.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The amendments delegate specifically enumerated statutory sections with defined consultation conditions; the standards flow from the underlying appropriations and Foreign Assistance Act provisions, not from vague discretionary triggers.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The reallocation of delegated functions creates no reward for undermining foreign-assistance administration; it rests on active statutes, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a standing delegation framework maintained by ordinary supersession (it repeatedly amends numbered EO 12163 and EO 11958), so the procedural-framework carve-out applies; foreign-assistance administration is an evergreen executive function.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The amendments reassign delegated functions and preserve reservations to the President; no pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: This is an internal delegation-of-functions order, not a compliance regime relying on regulated-party self-reporting; the applicable question is answerable and clean.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order is technical reallocation of enumerated delegations with no identifiable precedent extending authority into unrelated domains.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Functions are redistributed among State, Defense, and AID with explicit consultation clauses that manage rather than create jurisdictional conflict; no agency's statutory mandate is countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No exemption or waiver architecture is created; the order allocates delegated authority rather than carving actors out of coverage.

note: Long but technical partial amendment of EO 12163 (reviewed) and EO 11958; assessed the specific delegation changes, which distribute enumerated statutory functions with consultation requirements and score clean across all flags.

title: Exemption of J. Edgar Hoover from Compulsory Retirement for Age

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A single individual retirement exemption exercised directly by the President under statute; no ongoing decision authority is pooled in a subordinate official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Self-executing individual exemption; the order preserves the Civil Service Commission's residual authority (via EO 10530/10682), so responsibility is clear.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single-subject individual action; nothing distinct is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time personnel exemption creates no ongoing compliance relationship or enforcement mechanism.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No behavioral compliance rule is created, so there are no actor incentives to assess.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Unlike a fixed-term exemption, this one runs 'for an indefinite period of time,' granting permanent relief from a recurring statutory retirement trigger with no review or reauthorization point despite the subject matter (fitness/age) being one that would ordinarily recur periodically.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order expressly preserves the Civil Service Commission's delegated authority; no pre-existing oversight is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing enforcement relationship exists over which a third party could have standing.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A single-officer exemption, even for a prominent official, is a narrow personnel action with no precedential authority-expansion identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order coordinates with rather than countermands the Civil Service Commission's delegated authority; no mandate conflict is created.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: A single-person exemption is not a coverage carve-out architecture applied against a regulated population.

note: Individual retirement exemption; the 'indefinite period' grant (contrast the fixed one-year term in order-008) triggers Flag 6. EO 10530/10682 preservation clause reviewed.

title: Amending the Foreign Service Regulations of the United States

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order sets fixed fee amounts in a schedule; no discretionary decision authority is pooled in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: A self-executing fee-schedule amendment with no implementation chain that could fail.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The several fee amendments all concern the single Foreign Service fee tariff; nothing unrelated is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The fee amounts and their exceptions are stated with precision (specific dollar figures and no-fee exceptions), leaving no room for divergent good-faith application.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A fixed consular fee schedule creates no reward for undermining its purpose; it rests on an active revenue statute, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a standing procedural framework (consular fee tariff) maintained through ordinary amendment of the Foreign Service Regulations, so the procedural-framework carve-out applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed; the order revises fee items within an existing regulatory schedule.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Fee collection is a direct, transactional consular function; the fees are collected at point of service, so there is no self-reporting compliance gap requiring an external watchdog.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Narrow administrative fee-schedule change with no systemic or precedential implications.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the State Department's own fee schedule is involved; no second agency is implicated.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The 'no fee' exceptions are narrow, specific, and defined (e.g., documents authorized to be furnished free); they do not swallow the fee rule.

note: Consular fee-schedule amendment; there is an ongoing (though transactional) fee-collection relationship so flags 4,5,8,11 are answerable and score ABSENT, while purely systemic flags 9,10 are NOT_APPLICABLE.

title: Delegation of Functions Relating to the Implementation of the Inter-American Convention on Letters Rogatory and Additional Protocol

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Designating the Department of Justice as the treaty Central Authority implements a treaty obligation; the DOJ acts within the Convention's defined framework, not as an unchecked discretionary decider.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Department of Justice is the single named, responsible actor for transmitting and processing letters rogatory; responsibility is clear.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Two closely related provisions (designation plus power to make additional designations) serving one treaty-implementation purpose; nothing unrelated is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: DOJ must proceed 'in conformity with applicable provisions of the Convention and Additional Protocol,' which supply the operative standard, so the processing function is not undefined.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A treaty central-authority designation creates no ongoing behavioral compliance rule for regulated parties, so there are no perverse incentives to assess.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The designation implements a permanent treaty function that is evergreen by nature (a standing procedural channel for judicial assistance); the permanence carve-out applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed; the order creates a new treaty-implementation channel.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no regulated-party compliance regime here; the order designates a government channel for processing international letters rogatory.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Narrow treaty-implementation designation with no systemic or precedential implications beyond the Convention.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: DOJ is assigned the central-authority role without countermanding any other agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population or exemption structure exists in a treaty central-authority designation.

note: Narrow treaty-implementation delegation designating DOJ as Central Authority; decision-structure flags answerable and clean, compliance-regime/systemic flags NOT_APPLICABLE.

title: Ethics Commitments by Executive Branch Appointees

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The waiver power in Sec. 3 vests in the President alone the ability to release any appointee from any pledge restriction whenever he 'certifies in writing that it is in the public interest,' concentrating unilateral, self-judged discretion to override the ethics rule.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Agency heads, the OGE Director, White House Counsel, and the Attorney General have clearly assigned administration and enforcement duties (Secs. 4-5), so responsibility for implementation failure is traceable.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The pledge provisions all serve the single stated purpose of post-employment ethics commitments; the trade-negotiator pledge is a subset of the same frame, not an unrelated rider.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Enforcement is precisely specified: defined pledge terms, a Statement of Covered Activities, debarment procedures conformed to 5 CFR 2637.212, and defined AG civil-action triggers, so the standard and consequences are clear.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The pledge design aligns appointee incentives with the anti-revolving-door purpose; it rests on active constitutional/statutory authority, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Government ethics is an evergreen policy mandate with no natural end-state; the permanence carve-out for standing policy frameworks applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds ethics obligations and enforcement machinery on top of existing statutory restrictions (expressly 'in addition to' them) and preserves judicial review of debarment; it removes no pre-existing oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Detection of pledge violations depends on the affected agency and the Attorney General acting on information that comes to them; no external private party is given standing or an information channel to surface breaches of the post-employment lobbying ban, and Sec. 6(c) forecloses private enforcement.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The publicly-certified presidential waiver mechanism (Sec. 3) establishes a precedent that a self-executing ethics rule can be individually suspended by the same authority that imposed it, which can normalize discretionary exemption of favored appointees over time.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order allocates roles among OGE, agency ethics officers, White House Counsel, and DOJ consistently with their existing functions; no agency's statutory mandate is countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The pledge's 'lobby' definition contains numerous carve-outs (state/local government, non-profits, higher-education and hospital representation, scientific information, testimony) and the President holds unbounded public-interest waiver discretion, allowing significant portions of otherwise-covered activity to fall outside the restriction.

note: Detailed ethics-pledge order; largely clean enforcement architecture, but unbounded presidential waiver (Sec. 3), the definitional carve-outs, and reliance on internal detection drive flags 1, 8, 9, and 11.

title: Directing the Civil Service Commission to Resume Operations Under the Civil Service Rules, and Authorizing the Adoption of Special Regulations During the Transitional Period

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Broad discretionary authority over the transition is pooled in the Civil Service Commission, including discretion to extend war-service appointments, confer competitive status, except positions to Schedule A, and set the order and timing of separations, largely on its own determination.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Commission is the single named body responsible for the transition, with a defined obligation to submit revision recommendations to the President by July 1, 1946; responsibility is clear.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: All provisions concern the single coherent task of transitioning from wartime to normal civil-service recruitment; nothing unrelated is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Key transitional standards are left to Commission discretion with soft triggers ('as rapidly as its resources will permit,' extensions 'in the discretion of the Commission,' status conferred where a rating 'does not exceed his rating by more than five points'), which different administrators could apply divergently.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order's design pushes toward converting temporary appointments to permanent competitive status, aligning with its stated merit purpose; authority rests on active civil-service statutes, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The transitional authority carries its own dated end-points (revision recommendations due July 1, 1946; Schedule A exceptions void after December 31, 1946), creating built-in reauthorization/correction moments, so the flag does not fire.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order restores rather than removes the ordinary Civil Service Rules and revokes the wartime EO 9063; it re-establishes normal oversight rather than preempting it.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Compliance with the veterans'-preference selection order depends on the appointing departments and the Commission's own recordkeeping and reports; the remedy for a wronged applicant requires the applicant to 'establish' that the order of selection was not followed, with no external actor routinely positioned to surface violations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order winds down a wartime regime and restores the standing merit system; no precedent extending authority into unrelated domains is identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Commission directs departments to cooperate within its established statutory civil-service coordination role; no agency's statutory mandate is countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Section 7 gives the Commission discretion to except professional/scientific/technical positions from the competitive service to Schedule A upon agency request, a discretionary carve-out from merit-competition coverage controlled by the Commission with only a general 'public interest' standard.

note: Post-war civil-service transition (revokes EO 9063). Broad Commission discretion over conversions, extensions, and Schedule A exceptions drives flags 1, 4, 8, and 11; built-in dated deadlines keep Flag 6 ABSENT.

title: Administration of the Agricultural Trade Development and Assistance Act of 1954

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Functions under the Act are delegated across multiple officers (Agriculture, State, Foreign Operations Administration, Budget Director, Treasury, Defense, ODM); authority is distributed rather than pooled in one hand.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each delegated function is assigned to a specific named officer, and reports to Congress are reserved to the President, so responsibility is traceable.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: All delegations implement the single Agricultural Trade Development and Assistance Act; nothing unrelated is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The delegations tie each officer to specific enumerated sections of the Act with defined purposes; the operative standards flow from the statute rather than from vague discretion.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The allocation of program-administration functions creates no reward for undermining the Act's purpose; authority rests on an active statute, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a standing delegation framework implementing permanent statutory functions and it extends Part III of EO 10476 (an existing framework maintained by supersession); the procedural-framework carve-out applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order preserves State Department foreign-policy responsibility over all functions and reserves congressional reporting to the President; no pre-existing oversight is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: This is an internal delegation-of-functions order, not a regulated-party compliance regime dependent on self-reporting; the applicable question is answerable and clean.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Technical allocation of statutory delegations; no precedent extending authority into unrelated domains is identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Functions are distributed with express subordination to State's foreign-policy responsibility and coordinated purposes; no agency's statutory mandate is countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No exemption or waiver architecture is created; the order allocates delegated authority rather than carving actors out of coverage.

note: Delegation order for PL 480 administration; extends Part III of EO 10476 (reviewed) to these functions. Distributed, statute-anchored delegations score clean across all flags.

title: Amending the Selective Service Regulations

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The amendments adjust classification, deferment, and examination procedures administered by local boards, appeal boards, and the Director within the existing Selective Service structure; no new discretionary authority is pooled in a single official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Local boards, appeal boards, and the State/national Directors have defined roles in the amended process, so responsibility for each step is traceable.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: All amendments concern the single Selective Service Regulations framework; nothing unrelated is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The amendments prescribe detailed, specific procedures (mailing questionnaires in date-of-birth order, medical-interview triggers, defined dependency-deferment criteria), leaving little room for divergent good-faith application.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The procedural refinements create no reward for undermining conscription administration; authority rests on the active Universal Military Training and Service Act, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a standing procedural framework (Selective Service Regulations) maintained through ordinary supersession, as evidenced by the long list of numbered predecessor amending orders; the procedural-framework carve-out applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The amendments retain the appeal-board and reopening machinery (indeed one amendment concerns reopening on request of the State/national Director); no pre-existing oversight is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The classification system provides registrants, dependents, and employers with defined rights to appeal and request reopening, giving external parties standing and information to contest classifications, so no third-party gap arises from these amendments.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Technical procedural amendments to an existing regulatory scheme with no precedent extending authority into unrelated domains.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The amendments operate within Selective Service; the Army Surgeon General's role in prescribing the disqualifying-conditions list is a coordination the system already contemplates, not a countermanding of any agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The amendments revise the Class III-A hardship/paternity deferment (including a child 'from the date of its conception') and other deferment categories; these are coverage carve-outs from induction, and the amendment broadens/defines who can claim exemption from service, an exemptions-scope change rather than an enforcement-mechanics one.

note: Partial amendment of the Selective Service Regulations (EO 10001 et al., reviewed); precise procedural changes are clean, but the deferment-class revisions are a coverage/exemption change triggering Flag 11.

title: Authorizing the Secretary of War to Take Possession of and Operate the Properties of the Cleveland Electric Illuminating Company

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Sweeping authority to seize and operate a private utility 'in such manner as he deems necessary' is pooled in the Secretary of War, with termination also resting on his own determination that possession is no longer necessary, and no external check on either.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of War is the single named officer responsible for the seizure and operation; responsibility is clear even though concentrated.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single-purpose seizure order for one company; nothing distinct is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The operative standard is entirely open-ended: the Secretary may operate the properties 'in such manner as he deems necessary for the successful prosecution of the War,' a standard with no defined content constraining how the takeover is administered.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The seizure aligns the operator's incentive with wartime production; authority rests on the then-active Selective Training and Service Act sec. 9 (unexpired), so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order includes an explicit termination trigger: possession 'shall be terminated within sixty days after the Secretary of War determines that possession is no longer necessary,' tying the exceptional power to the resolution of the emergency.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order does not by its text remove any pre-existing external oversight mechanism; it exercises a statutory seizure power.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: This is a direct government takeover and operation of property, not a compliance regime resting on regulated-party self-reporting, so there is no third-party enforcement chain to assess.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A presidential seizure of a private utility under a training/service statute establishes precedent for using such wartime authority to nationalize operation of private enterprises, a claim usable in future labor/production disputes beyond this single plant.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the Secretary of War is empowered; no other agency's statutory operational mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: A single-property seizure has no coverage population or exemption structure to map.

note: Wartime plant-seizure order; concentrated, standardless operating discretion in the Secretary of War (flags 1, 4, 9), but an explicit 60-day termination trigger keeps Flag 6 ABSENT. Compliance flags 8, 11 NOT_APPLICABLE for a direct takeover.

title: Inspection of Income, Excess-Profits, Estate, and Gift Tax Returns by the Committee on Internal Security, House of Representatives

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order opens tax returns to inspection by a congressional committee under statutory authority and Treasury rules; it does not pool discretionary decision authority in a single executive official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Inspection is governed by identifiable Treasury Decisions (6132, 6133) and confined to a named committee for a defined purpose, so responsibility for the access framework is clear.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single-purpose authorization of committee inspection; nothing unrelated is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The inspection is bounded by specified tax years (1950-1969), a named committee, a defined statutory investigative purpose, and existing Treasury regulations, leaving little room for divergent application.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Authorizing committee inspection of returns creates no ongoing behavioral compliance rule for taxpayers to game, so there are no perverse incentives to assess.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The authorization is inherently time-bounded: inspection is permitted only 'during the Ninety-first Congress,' so it self-expires.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order grants access under existing Treasury rules; it removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism over tax-return confidentiality.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: This is a return-inspection authorization, not a regulated-party compliance regime; there is no enforcement chain requiring a third-party watchdog.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Opening private tax returns to a congressional internal-security committee for its investigations establishes a precedent for using confidential taxpayer data as an investigative tool against individuals, a second-order effect on privacy usable beyond the immediate returns named.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The IRS retains control of the returns and the process runs through its regulations; no agency's statutory mandate is countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population or exemption structure exists in a return-inspection authorization.

note: Tax-return inspection authorization for a House committee; largely clean and self-limited to the 91st Congress, but the confidential-data-for-investigation precedent triggers Flag 9.

title: Waiver of the Time Limitations in Civil Service Rule IX To Permit the Reinstatement of Mrs. Martha Overstreet

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A one-time individual reinstatement waiver exercised directly by the President under statute; no ongoing discretionary authority is pooled in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Self-executing individual waiver; no implementation chain that could fail.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single-subject individual action; nothing distinct is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time reinstatement waiver creates no ongoing compliance relationship or enforcement mechanism.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No behavioral compliance rule is created, so there are no incentives to assess.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing waiver grants no continuing authority that could be subject to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed; the President exercises a specific statutory waiver power.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing enforcement relationship exists over which a third party could have standing.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Narrow individual administrative action with no systemic or precedential implications.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the Post Office is affected as employer; no agency's statutory mandate is redirected.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: A single-person waiver is not a coverage-carve-out architecture applied against a regulated population.

note: One-time individual civil-service reinstatement waiver; compliance-regime flags 4,5,8,11 and flags 6,9 NOT_APPLICABLE per the individual/one-time-action test.

title: Prohibiting New Investment in Burma

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of the Treasury is authorized to promulgate rules and 'employ all powers granted to me' under IEEPA and the Act, with free redelegation, concentrating broad sanctions-administration authority in one officer subject only to consultation with State.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of the Treasury (in consultation with State) and the Secretary of State (Sec. 5) are named responsible actors; responsibility for implementation is clear.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: All provisions serve the single stated purpose of prohibiting new U.S. investment in Burma; nothing unrelated is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The prohibition turns on the defined but elastic concept 'economic development of resources located in Burma' and its exclusions; combined with the facilitation ban on 'transactions that would constitute new investment,' officials could in good faith reach different conclusions about borderline contracts.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The prohibition aligns actor incentives with the coercive foreign-policy purpose; it rests on a freshly declared, active national emergency and unexpired IEEPA, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is emergency sanctions authority tied to the Burma crisis, exactly the kind of exceptional power that should track the emergency's resolution, yet the order provides no sunset or reauthorization trigger of its own.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order preserves congressional transmittal (Sec. 8(b)); the Sec. 7 no-private-right clause is a standard provision, not a removal of otherwise-available review.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement depends on U.S. persons self-restricting from prohibited new investment and facilitation; no external third party is given standing and information access to surface concealed investment structured through foreign persons or contracts.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The prohibition on U.S.-person 'facilitation' of a foreign person's transaction (Sec. 2(a)) extends the order's reach extraterritorially and establishes precedent for regulating U.S. persons' indirect involvement in third-country conduct under IEEPA.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order directs all agencies to assist and delegates to Treasury/State within their existing sanctions roles; no agency's statutory mandate is countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Broad license/regulation exceptions ('except to the extent provided in regulations, orders, directives, or licenses') are concentrated in Treasury without criteria, plus the humanitarian and pre-existing-contract carve-outs, letting the agency define the practical scope of coverage.

note: IEEPA/NEA sanctions order declaring a new Burma emergency; discretionary Treasury administration, facilitation reach, and open licensing authority drive flags 1, 4, 6, 8, 9, and 11.

title: Amendment of the Regulations Relating to the Safeguarding of Vessels, Harbors, Ports, and Waterfront Facilities of the United States

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The amended section vests the Commandant with sole discretion over whether a person's 'character and habits of life' would be 'inimical to the security of the United States' for merchant-vessel employment, and additional unilateral power to designate categories of vessels the rule does not apply to, with no external check.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Commandant is the single named decision-maker for the screening and category designations, so responsibility is clear even though concentrated.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single-provision amendment to one regulatory section; nothing distinct is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The standard for barring employment is that the Commandant be 'satisfied that the character and habits of life of such person are such as to authorize the belief that the presence of the individual... would not be inimical to the security of the United States' - an open-ended, subjective test two officials could apply very differently.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The screening rule creates no reward for undermining port security; authority rests on the active 1917/1950 waterfront-security statute, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a standing procedural framework (waterfront/vessel security regulations) maintained through ordinary supersession, as shown by the chain of numbered predecessor orders it amends (EO 10173, 10277); the procedural-framework carve-out applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The amendment revises a screening standard; it does not by its text remove any pre-existing external oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The security-screening determination is made unilaterally by the Commandant based on his own satisfaction; there is no external actor with standing and information to challenge an erroneous or arbitrary exclusion, and the affected individual has no defined channel to surface error.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A confined amendment to an existing vessel-security screening rule; no precedent extending authority into unrelated domains is identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the Coast Guard Commandant's authority is involved; no other agency's statutory mandate is countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The proviso lets the Commandant 'designate categories of merchant vessels to which the foregoing shall not apply,' an unbounded, single-actor discretion to carve whole vessel classes out of the security-screening requirement with no defined criteria.

note: Amends vessel-security screening (EO 10173/10277). The subjective 'inimical to security' standard and the Commandant's unbounded vessel-category exemption power drive flags 1, 4, 8, and 11.

title: Designating an Additional Agency Pursuant to Section 103(a) of the Renegotiation Act of 1951

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order designates the Bonneville Power Administration as a covered 'Department' for renegotiation purposes; it applies a statutory scheme rather than pooling discretionary authority in an official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The designation ties BPA into the existing Renegotiation Act framework with defined responsibilities; responsibility is clear.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single-purpose designation; nothing distinct is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The consequence of designation is precisely defined by statute: Title I applies to BPA contracts and subcontracts for amounts received or accrued on or after a stated date, leaving no ambiguity in trigger or effect.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Extending profit-renegotiation coverage to BPA contracts aligns with the Act's anti-profiteering purpose; authority rests on the active Renegotiation Act, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The designation applies a standing statutory framework to an additional agency and grants no free-standing discretionary authority that should carry its own sunset; it is administered through the Act's own terms.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The designation extends renegotiation coverage; it removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Renegotiation of contract profits is administered by the statutory renegotiation authorities against contractors with defined processes; the designation does not create a self-reporting-only regime lacking any external check.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Narrow administrative designation of one agency under a statute, with no systemic or precedential implications beyond applying the existing Act.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Designating BPA as a covered department integrates it into the renegotiation scheme; no agency's statutory mandate is countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order expands coverage to an additional agency rather than creating exemptions; no carve-out structure is introduced.

note: Statutory designation of BPA under the Renegotiation Act; there is an ongoing contract-renegotiation relationship, so compliance-regime flags are answerable and clean, while flag 9 is NOT_APPLICABLE for this narrow designation.

title: Leadership and Coordination of Nondiscrimination Laws

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order concentrates government-wide leadership and coordination of civil-rights enforcement in the Attorney General, including power to approve or require pre-approval of other agencies' nondiscrimination rules and to set enforcement standards, pooling substantial cross-agency authority in one official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Attorney General is the single named coordinator with a duty to report annually to the President; each agency retains its own implementation duty, so responsibility is traceable.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: All provisions serve the single stated purpose of consistent coordination of federal nondiscrimination laws; nothing unrelated is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Attorney General is to identify agency rules that are 'inadequate, unclear or unnecessarily inconsistent' and set 'reasonable time limits' for voluntary compliance, standards general enough that different officials could apply them to very different effect.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Centralized coordination aligns with the anti-discrimination purpose; it rests on active civil-rights statutes, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Nondiscrimination enforcement is an evergreen rights-mandate with no natural end-state; the permanence carve-out for standing rights/policy mandates applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds a coordinating check (AG approval of agency rules) rather than removing pre-existing oversight; per the direction rule, adding a new check does not fire this flag, and prior regulations continue until revoked.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The underlying nondiscrimination laws provide beneficiaries and complainants standing to surface violations through the agencies and DOJ referral; this coordination order does not create a self-reporting-only regime, so the applicable question is clean.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Vesting one department with approval authority over all other agencies' nondiscrimination rulemaking establishes a durable centralization precedent that shifts control of civil-rights policy uniformity to the AG, an institutional change persisting beyond any single rule.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order subjects each Executive agency's own statutory nondiscrimination rulemaking to Attorney General approval and standard-setting, redirecting authority those agencies otherwise exercise independently and creating a hierarchy over their existing mandates.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order expands coordinated coverage; the only carve-out (Sec. 1-503, equal-employment coordination excluded) is a defined jurisdictional limit rather than a discretionary exemption that swallows the rule.

note: Centralizes civil-rights enforcement coordination in the AG (revokes EO 11764/11914). Cross-agency approval authority drives flags 1, 9, and 10; general review standards drive flag 4.

title: Creating a Board of Inquiry to Report on a Labor Dispute Affecting the Bituminous Coal Industry of the United States

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Board only inquires and reports under Taft-Hartley Title II; the President appoints it, but no decision authority over the parties is pooled, and the statutory scheme frames the process.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Board has a defined duty to report by a date certain and its powers are those set by statute; responsibility is clear.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single-purpose order addressing one labor dispute; nothing distinct is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Board's powers and duties are those 'set forth in Title II' of the Labor Management Relations Act, a defined statutory standard, so the fact-finding function is not undefined.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: An investigate-and-report board creates no incentive to undermine dispute resolution; authority rests on the active Taft-Hartley Act, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: While the Board must report by a date certain, the order provides that it 'shall continue in existence... until the Board is terminated by the President,' leaving the continuing existence of the body dependent solely on future presidential revocation rather than an automatic end-point.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed; the order operates within the Taft-Hartley national-emergency-dispute procedure.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The dispute involves opposing parties (operators and the union) each with standing and incentive to present facts to the Board, so there is no gap in surfacing the relevant information.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A statutorily-templated board of inquiry with no precedential authority claim beyond the single dispute.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No other agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: A fact-finding board has no coverage population or exemption structure to map.

note: Taft-Hartley board of inquiry; clean fact-finding architecture, but the 'continue in existence until terminated by the President' clause leaves the body's duration open-ended, firing Flag 6.

title: Designating the Chairman of the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A one-time designation of a named individual as Chairman for a fixed statutory term; no ongoing discretionary authority is pooled by the order itself.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Self-executing personnel designation under statute; no implementation chain that could fail.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single-subject appointment designation; nothing distinct is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A chairmanship designation creates no ongoing compliance relationship or enforcement mechanism.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No behavioral compliance rule is created, so there are no incentives to assess.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The designation is expressly for 'a term of four years,' so the appointment self-limits.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed; a statutory designation is made.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing enforcement relationship exists over which a third party could have standing.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Narrow individual appointment designation with no systemic or precedential implications.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the Federal Reserve Board's own chairmanship is designated under its enabling statute; no agency's mandate is countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: A single appointment has no coverage population or exemption structure to map.

note: One-time statutory designation of a Federal Reserve Chairman for a fixed four-year term; compliance-regime flags 4,5,8,11 and flag 9 NOT_APPLICABLE.

title: Providing for the Restoration of Law and Order in the Washington Metropolitan Area

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order concentrates in the Secretary of Defense sweeping authority to deploy the Armed Forces, call National Guard units into federal service 'for an indefinite period,' and exercise the President's own powers as commander-in-chief of the D.C. militia, with only his own judgment ('as he may deem necessary/appropriate') governing use of force.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of Defense is the single named officer directed to act and to whom authority is delegated; responsibility is clear even though the authority is broad.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single-purpose civil-disturbance order; all provisions serve the restoration of order, so nothing unrelated is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The operative authorizations are defined only by the Secretary's discretion - 'all appropriate steps,' 'such of the Armed Forces... as he may deem necessary,' units called 'as he may deem appropriate' - with no defined limits on the means employed.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order aligns the military response with restoring order; authority rests on active statutes (10 U.S.C. ch. 15) and a same-day proclamation, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is emergency civil-disturbance authority - the paradigm of power that should end when the emergency resolves - yet it grants federalized Guard service 'for an indefinite period and until relieved by appropriate orders' with no automatic termination tied to the restoration of order.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order deploys forces under existing statutory authority; it does not by its text remove any pre-existing external oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: This is a direct military-deployment directive, not a regulated-party compliance regime resting on self-reporting, so there is no third-party enforcement chain to assess.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Delegating to the Secretary of Defense the President's own commander-in-chief powers over the D.C. militia and open-ended domestic troop deployment establishes precedent for broad executive delegation of domestic military authority usable in future civil disturbances.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order channels authority to the Secretary of Defense and permits subdelegation to service secretaries; no other agency's statutory operational mandate is countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: A military-deployment order has no coverage population or exemption structure to map.

note: Domestic civil-disturbance deployment order; concentrated discretionary military authority (flags 1, 4), indefinite federalization with no emergency-tied end (flag 6), and delegation precedent (flag 9). Compliance flags 8, 11 NOT_APPLICABLE for a direct deployment.

title: Regulations Relating to the Right of Members of the Uniformed Services to Incentive Pay for the Performance of Hazardous Duty Required by Competent Orders

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The regulations distribute rule-making detail to 'the Secretary concerned' for each service and set uniform standards; no single official pools discretionary authority over a contested outcome.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The service Secretaries and certifying commanding officers have defined roles in administering the flight/duty requirements; responsibility is traceable.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: All provisions concern the single subject of hazardous-duty incentive pay across defined hazard categories; nothing unrelated is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Eligibility is defined with precise, quantified requirements (specific flight hours per calendar-month periods, defined hazard categories, medical-attestation rules), leaving little room for divergent good-faith application.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The design (incentive pay for performing hazardous duty, with commanding-officer certification for excused shortfalls) aligns rewards with the intended service; authority rests on the active Career Compensation Act, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a standing procedural/compensation framework maintained through ordinary amendment; military hazardous-duty pay administration is an evergreen function, so the procedural-framework carve-out applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed; the order prescribes pay regulations under statute.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Eligibility rests on objective, records-based flight/duty logs certified through the chain of command, not solely on the member's self-report, so there is a defined internal verification path rather than an unchecked self-certification gap.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A detailed military pay-eligibility regulation with no precedent extending authority into unrelated domains.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Uniform standards apply across services with the Secretaries concerned administering; no agency's statutory mandate is countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The 'excused shortfall' provisions (certification of inability due to operations or equipment) are narrow, specific relief tied to defined conditions, not broad discretionary carve-outs that swallow the eligibility rule.

note: Detailed hazardous-duty incentive-pay regulation; precise quantified eligibility criteria and chain-of-command certification score clean across all flags.

title: Termination of the Wage and Price Regulatory Program

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order revokes EO 12092 and authorizes each agency head to wind down actions taken under it; it disperses termination authority rather than pooling new power.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each Executive agency and department head is the named actor responsible for terminating its own responsive actions; responsibility is clear.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single-purpose termination order; nothing distinct is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order terminates a regulatory program rather than creating an ongoing compliance obligation, so there is no enforcement mechanism to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Terminating the wage-price program creates no ongoing behavioral rule for regulated parties, so there are no perverse incentives to assess.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order ends an authority rather than granting a continuing one, so there is nothing to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Winding down a voluntary wage-price program removes a regulatory burden, not a pre-existing external oversight mechanism over other conduct; Flag 7 concerns removal of checks, not repeal of a program.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: With the program terminated there is no ongoing compliance regime over which a third party would need standing.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order simply dismantles an existing program under the same statutory authority that created it; no precedent extending authority into unrelated domains is identifiable.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Agencies are directed to unwind their own responsive actions; no agency's statutory mandate is countermanded or set into conflict.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Terminating a program creates no coverage population or exemption structure.

note: Revokes EO 12092 and winds down the wage-price program; decision-structure flags answerable and clean, compliance-regime and sunset flags NOT_APPLICABLE because the order removes rather than creates ongoing authority.

title: Delegating Authority of the President With Respect to Regulations Relating to Certain Allowances and Benefits to Government Personnel on Overseas Duty

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Regulation-making authority for overseas allowances is delegated principally to the Secretary of State with roles for the Budget Director and Civil Service Commission; authority is distributed across multiple officers, not pooled in one.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each delegated function is tied to a specific officer and statutory provision, so responsibility is traceable.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: All provisions concern the single coherent subject of overseas-duty allowances and benefits; nothing unrelated is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
