flags.justification: The delegations tie the Secretary to enumerated statutory sections (Overseas Differentials and Allowances Act, Foreign Service Act, etc.) that supply the operative standards; the regulation-making authority is bounded by those statutes.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Allocating allowance-regulation authority creates no reward for undermining its purpose; it rests on active statutes, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a standing delegation framework maintained by ordinary supersession, evidenced by its revocation and consolidation of numerous numbered predecessor orders (EO 10000, 10011, 10085, etc.); the procedural-framework carve-out applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order reallocates delegated regulation-making and preserves existing regulations until superseded; no pre-existing external oversight mechanism is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: This is an internal delegation-of-functions order concerning federal-employee allowances, not a regulated-party compliance regime dependent on self-reporting; the applicable question is answerable and clean.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Technical consolidation of allowance-regulation delegations; no precedent extending authority into unrelated domains is identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Functions are allocated among State, Budget, and the Civil Service Commission consistently with their roles; no agency's statutory mandate is countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No exemption or waiver architecture is created; the order allocates delegated authority rather than carving actors out of coverage.

note: Consolidating delegation order for overseas-allowance regulations (revokes/amends EO 10000, 10530, 10624, 10853 among others); distributed, statute-anchored delegations score clean across all flags.

title: Amendments to the Manual for Courts-Martial, United States, 1969 (Revised Edition)

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Several amendments enlarge unreviewable convening-authority discretion, e.g., detailing associate counsel 'in his sole discretion' and rulings on individual-counsel requests being 'a matter within the sole discretion of that authority,' with express bars on the military judge dismissing charges over the issue.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The amendments operate within a defined military-justice hierarchy (convening authority, military judge, Judge Advocate General, Courts of Military Review/Appeals) with clear roles; responsibility is traceable.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: All amendments modify the single Manual for Courts-Martial framework; nothing unrelated is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The procedural amendments are drafted with specificity (defined 'reasonably available' counsel criteria, enumerated unavailable persons, precise notice and time-limit rules), leaving little room for divergent good-faith application.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The procedural refinements do not reward undermining military justice; authority rests on the active UCMJ, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The Manual for Courts-Martial is a standing procedural framework maintained through ordinary supersession, evidenced by the chain of numbered amending orders (EO 11476 as amended by 11835, 12018, 12198, 12233, 12306, 12315); the procedural-framework carve-out applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Multiple amendments curtail judicial review within the process - 'The investigating officer will not review any denial of a request for individual military counsel' and repeated provisions that 'the military judge may not dismiss the charges or otherwise effectively prevent further proceedings based on this issue' - reducing the checking role military judges previously could exercise over counsel-denial disputes.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The military-justice system provides the accused, detailed and individual counsel, and appellate courts with standing to surface errors; the amendments do not create a self-reporting-only regime lacking external checks.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Technical amendments confined to court-martial procedure; no precedent extending authority into unrelated domains is identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The amendments operate within the military-justice structure across services; no agency's statutory operational mandate is countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The enumerated categories of persons 'not reasonably available' as individual military counsel are specific, defined restrictions, not broad discretionary exemptions from the accused's rights that swallow the rule.

note: Partial amendment of the Manual for Courts-Martial (EO 11476 et al., reviewed); precise procedure is clean, but 'sole discretion' convening-authority provisions and explicit bars on military-judge review drive flags 1 and 7.

title: United States-Iran Agreement on Release of the American Hostages (Direction to Transfer Iranian Government Assets Held by Domestic Banks)

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order directs banks to transfer Iranian assets as directed by the Secretary of the Treasury, delegates to Treasury 'all functions vested in the President' by IEEPA, and directs the Attorney General to defend the order's legality, concentrating sweeping asset-transfer and litigation-control authority in the executive.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of the Treasury and Attorney General are named responsible actors; the Federal Reserve Bank of New York is the designated custodian, so responsibility is traceable.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: All provisions implement the single hostage-release agreement (transfer of assets, nullification of attachments, litigation defense); nothing unrelated is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The directives are precise: specifically described funds/securities/deposits are 'licensed, authorized, directed and compelled' to be transferred to a named custodian, with defined interest terms and clearly identified nullified rights.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The transfer implements a negotiated agreement; it rests on the active national emergencies declared in EO 12170 and EO 12211 and unexpired IEEPA, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order effects a one-time, self-executing transfer and nullification of assets/attachments ('effective immediately'); it grants no continuing discretionary authority that should carry a sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order revokes and nullifies all rights, powers, and privileges derived from any 'attachment, injunction, other like proceedings or process, or other action in any litigation' against the assets and bars all claimants from exercising them, directly stripping courts of the ability to adjudicate creditor claims over the transferred property - a core removal of pre-existing judicial oversight that defeats the affected parties' access to review.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: This is a direct, compelled asset transfer, not an ongoing regulated-party compliance regime dependent on self-reporting, so there is no third-party enforcement chain to assess.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Nullifying private creditors' attachments and immunizing compliant banks from liability establishes a powerful precedent that the executive may extinguish vested litigation rights over blocked foreign assets by fiat to serve a foreign-policy settlement, usable in future asset disputes.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order directs Treasury, the Attorney General, and the FRBNY within their existing roles; no agency's statutory operational mandate is countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: A compelled one-time asset transfer has no coverage population or exemption structure to map.

note: Algiers Accords asset-transfer order (builds on emergencies in EO 12170/12211). The wholesale nullification of judicial attachments and bar on claimants is a core removal of judicial oversight (Flag 7 CRITICAL), with concentration (1) and precedent (9).

title: Grazing Fees

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order directs the Secretaries of Agriculture and Interior to set fees by a fixed statutory formula; the fee level is determined by the formula, not by discretionary authority pooled in an official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The two named Secretaries are responsible for applying the formula; responsibility is clear.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single-purpose order establishing a grazing-fee formula; nothing unrelated is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The fee is set by a precisely specified formula (defined base, indices, a 25 percent annual change cap, and a $1.35 floor), with all component indices defined in Sec. 2, leaving essentially no room for divergent good-faith computation.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A formula-based fee creates no notable reward for undermining rangeland management; it rests on active statutes (PRIA 1978, FPASA), so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a standing administrative fee-setting mechanism (grazing-fee formula) that is evergreen by nature and administered under continuing statutory authority; the procedural-framework carve-out applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Sec. 3 expressly preserves all existing rules, practices, and regulations relating to the statutory fee formula; no pre-existing oversight is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The fee is a mechanically computed charge applied to grazing permittees; there is no violation-detection regime dependent on self-reporting that would require an external watchdog.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A formula for a specific fee on public rangelands; no precedent extending authority into unrelated domains is identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The two Secretaries act 'to the extent permitted by law under the various statutes they administer'; the order coordinates rather than countermanding either's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order sets a fee for all covered public-rangeland grazing with no exemption or waiver carve-out structure.

note: Formula-based grazing-fee order; the fully specified computation and preservation of existing rules yield a clean architecture across all flags.

title: Amendments to the Manual for Courts-Martial, United States, 1984

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: These amendments make largely technical procedural and evidentiary adjustments within the existing military-justice hierarchy; no new discretionary authority is pooled in a single official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The amendments operate within the defined roles of convening authority, staff judge advocate, military judge, and reviewing authorities; responsibility is traceable.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: All amendments modify the single Manual for Courts-Martial framework; nothing unrelated is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The amendments are drafted with rule-by-rule specificity (defined apprehension authority, waiver rules, record-of-trial contents, service-of-recommendation procedures), leaving little room for divergent good-faith application.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The procedural refinements do not reward undermining military justice; authority rests on the active UCMJ, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The Manual is a standing procedural framework maintained through ordinary supersession, evidenced by the chain of numbered amending orders (EO 12473 as amended by 12484, 12550, 12586); the procedural-framework carve-out applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The amendments adjust procedure and one privilege rule (Mil. R. Evid. 506 on government-information privilege, including Inspector General records) without removing pre-existing external oversight of the courts-martial process.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The military-justice system provides the accused, counsel, and appellate courts standing to surface errors; the amendments do not create a self-reporting-only regime lacking external checks.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Technical amendments confined to court-martial procedure and evidence; no precedent extending authority into unrelated domains is identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The amendments operate within the military-justice structure across services; no agency's statutory operational mandate is countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The amendments contain no broad or discretionary exemption structure carving actors out of coverage; the privilege rule is a defined evidentiary protection, not a coverage carve-out.

note: Partial amendment of the 1984 Manual for Courts-Martial (EO 12473 et al., reviewed); these are more purely technical than order-031 and score clean across all flags.

title: Establishing a Special Emergency Board To Report on the Adjustment of Wages for Employees of the Pacific Electric Railway Company

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order vests in the Economic Stabilization Director the power to make the Special Board's recommended wage adjustments effective or to override them ('unless and except to the extent the Economic Stabilization Director otherwise directs'), concentrating final control over the wage outcome in a single official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Special Board and the Economic Stabilization Director have defined reporting duties and dates; responsibility for each step is traceable.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single-purpose order addressing one wage dispute; nothing distinct is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Board's recommendations are bounded by 'the limitations of the Act of October 2, 1942 and the Executive Orders and Directives issued thereunder,' and the effectiveness mechanism is defined by dates and the Director's action, so the process is not undefined.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The wage-adjustment mechanism does not reward undermining the stabilization program; authority rests on the then-active First War Powers Act and stabilization statutes, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The Board is inherently time-limited with a fixed report deadline (October 15, 1943) and a defined 10-day follow-on for the Director's report; the mechanism self-terminates rather than continuing indefinitely.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing external oversight mechanism is removed; the order operates within the wartime wage-stabilization framework and requires filing copies with the War Labor Board and others.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The dispute involves opposing parties (company and employees) with standing to present evidence, and multiple agencies receive the report, so relevant information is surfaced through defined channels.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A single-dispute wage board operating within the existing stabilization program; no precedent extending authority into unrelated domains is identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order coordinates the Special Board, Economic Stabilization Director, War Labor Board, and IRS within the stabilization framework; the Director's role is contemplated by that framework, so no agency's mandate is countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: A wage-recommendation board for one company has no coverage population or exemption structure to map.

note: Wartime special emergency wage board; largely clean with a fixed report deadline, but final control over making the wage adjustment effective rests with the Economic Stabilization Director (Flag 1).

title: Transfer of the Functions of the Smaller War Plants Corporation to the Reconstruction Finance Corporation and the Department of Commerce

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order splits the Smaller War Plants Corporation's functions between the RFC and the Department of Commerce along defined statutory lines; it distributes rather than pools authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The RFC board of directors and the Secretary of Commerce are named responsible actors, with reporting duties preserved (Sec. 3); responsibility is traceable.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order does one coherent thing (wind down the SWPC by reassigning its functions to two existing bodies); nothing unrelated is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Functions are transferred by reference to specific statutory sections with defined exceptions; the Budget Director resolves allocation details, so the transfer is not left to open-ended discretion over conduct standards.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Reassigning wind-down functions creates no reward for undermining the transferred programs; authority rests on the then-active First War Powers Act, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order winds down a wartime corporation by moving its functions into permanent bodies (RFC, Commerce) that administer them under their own continuing statutory authority; it is a reorganization terminating a temporary entity rather than granting fresh open-ended emergency power that should carry a sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Reporting requirements under the June 11, 1942 Act are expressly preserved (Sec. 3); no pre-existing external oversight is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: This is an intra-executive transfer of program administration, not a compliance regime resting on regulated-party self-reporting; the applicable question is answerable and clean.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A defined reorganization dissolving a war corporation into existing agencies; no precedent extending authority into unrelated domains is identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Functions are divided between the RFC and Commerce along the statutory lines already governing them, with the Budget Director allocating personnel and funds; this is orderly reassignment, not a countermanding of an existing statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No exemption or waiver architecture is created; the order transfers functions rather than carving actors out of coverage.

note: Reorganization dissolving the Smaller War Plants Corporation into the RFC and Commerce along statutory lines with preserved reporting; clean architecture across all flags.

title: Educational Technology: Ensuring Opportunity for All Children in the Next Century

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order directs all agencies to prefer schools/non-profits in transferring surplus computer equipment; implementation is distributed across agencies, GSA, and OSTP with no discretionary authority pooled in a single official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Agencies, GSA, and OSTP have defined roles (identifying equipment, reporting, receiving implementation plans); responsibility is traceable, though the duties are largely hortatory.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: All provisions serve the single stated purpose of getting educationally useful federal equipment to classrooms and encouraging employee volunteering; nothing unrelated is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The core duties are softened by open-ended qualifiers - agencies act 'to the extent permitted by law and where appropriate,' give 'highest preference' and 'attempt to give particular preference,' and 'encourage' employees to help - so whether and how much any agency does is left to undefined discretion.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The equipment-donation design does not reward undermining its educational purpose; it rests on active statutes (Stevenson-Wydler, FPASA), so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order establishes a standing administrative practice for routine surplus-equipment transfer, an evergreen administrative function, and it supersedes numbered predecessor EO 12821; the procedural-framework carve-out applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order routes transfers through existing GSA surplus-property processes and adds reporting to OSTP; it removes no pre-existing external oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: This is a preference/donation program for surplus equipment, not a compliance regime whose violations need external detection; recipient schools have direct interest in surfacing available equipment, so no third-party gap arises.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A surplus-equipment donation preference with defined recipients; no precedent extending authority into unrelated domains is identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: GSA's surplus-property role and agency donation authorities are used as they exist; no agency's statutory mandate is countermanded, and plans are coordinated through OSTP.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order grants a preference rather than imposing an obligation with exemptions; there is no carve-out structure that swallows a coverage rule.

note: Surplus-computer-to-schools program (supersedes EO 12821). Largely clean and hortatory; the pervasive 'to the extent permitted / where appropriate / encourage' qualifiers make the duties discretionary, firing Flag 4 only.

title: Inspection by Department of Agriculture of Income Tax Returns Made Under the Internal Revenue Code of 1954 of Persons Having Farm Operations

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order opens certain returns to inspection by the Department of Agriculture for statistical use under Treasury rules; it does not pool discretionary decision authority in a single official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Inspection is governed by a specified Treasury Decision and confined to Agriculture for a defined statistical purpose, so responsibility for the access framework is clear.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single-purpose authorization of return inspection; nothing distinct is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The authorization is bounded (returns for taxable years beginning on or after January 1, 1967, 'for statistical purposes only,' inspected in an IRS office under Treasury regulations), leaving little room for divergent application.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Authorizing statistical inspection of returns creates no ongoing behavioral compliance rule for taxpayers to game, so there are no perverse incentives to assess.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The inspection authorization is confined to a defined class of returns for statistical use under continuing Treasury regulations; it grants no free-standing discretionary authority that should carry a sunset, and access is limited to what is 'readily available.'

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order grants access under existing Treasury rules; it removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism over tax-return confidentiality.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: This is a return-inspection authorization for statistics, not a regulated-party compliance regime; there is no enforcement chain requiring a third-party watchdog.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Opening taxpayers' returns to another department, even for statistics, incrementally normalizes cross-agency access to confidential tax data, a second-order privacy effect that broadens the precedent for inter-agency use of return information.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The IRS retains control of the returns and the process runs through its regulations; Agriculture only inspects, so no agency's statutory mandate is countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population or exemption structure exists in a return-inspection authorization.

note: Tax-return inspection authorization for Agriculture (statistical use); tightly bounded and clean, but the cross-agency confidential-data-access precedent triggers Flag 9.

title: Exclusion of the Defense Mapping Agency Reston Center and Elements Under the Joint Special Operations Command From the Federal Labor-Management Relations Program

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order exercises the President's specific statutory power (5 U.S.C. 7103(b)(1)) to exclude named national-security components; it applies a defined statutory authority rather than pooling new discretionary power in a subordinate.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: A self-executing amendment adding two named components to the excluded list; no implementation chain that could fail.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single-purpose amendment adding specific national-security exclusions; nothing unrelated is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Excluding agencies from labor-management coverage creates no ongoing compliance obligation on a regulated party with an enforcement mechanism to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A coverage exclusion for named components creates no ongoing behavioral compliance rule generating perverse incentives.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This amends a standing framework (the exclusion list of EO 12171) maintained through ordinary supersession; the President's exclusion determination for national-security components is evergreen by nature, so the procedural-framework carve-out applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: By excluding these components from Chapter 71, the order removes the collective-bargaining and associated grievance/oversight framework that Federal Labor-Management Relations Program coverage otherwise provided to those employees - a removal of a pre-existing statutory check for the affected units.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: A coverage exclusion is not a compliance regime whose violations require third-party detection.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A confined addition of two national-security components to an existing exclusion list; no precedent extending authority into unrelated domains beyond the routine use of the same statutory power is identifiable.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The exclusion concerns DoD components' own labor status; the FLRA's role is defined by the statute that authorizes such exclusions, so no agency's operational mandate is countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order is itself an exclusion (exemption) from the labor-management program, and the underlying authority lets the President carve out components on his own national-security determination; this amendment adds 'all elements under [JSOC's] operational control,' an open-ended expansion of who is exempt from coverage.

note: Partial amendment of EO 12171 (reviewed) excluding DoD national-security components from the labor program; removal of collective-bargaining coverage fires Flag 7 and the open-ended 'operational control' exclusion fires Flag 11. Compliance-conduct flags 4,5,8 NOT_APPLICABLE.

title: Redistribution of Maritime Functions

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order splits the Bureau of Marine Inspection and Navigation's functions between the Bureau of Customs (under Treasury) and the Coast Guard (under Navy) along defined operational lines; it distributes rather than pools authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Transferred functions are placed under named officers (Commissioner of Customs under the Secretary of the Treasury; Commandant of the Coast Guard under the Secretary of the Navy); responsibility is traceable.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order does one coherent wartime thing (redistribute maritime inspection/navigation functions); the two-way split follows function type rather than packaging unrelated matter.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The functions transferred to each recipient are enumerated in detail (registry, tonnage, inspection, licensing, casualty investigation, etc.), so administering officials have defined mandates rather than open-ended discretion over conduct standards.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Reassigning maritime regulatory functions creates no reward for undermining them; authority rests on the then-active First War Powers Act, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order includes an explicit termination clause: it remains in force only 'until the termination of Title I of the First War Powers Act, 1941,' tying the wartime reorganization to the statute's lapse.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order relocates operational functions between agencies; it does not by its text remove any pre-existing external oversight mechanism over those functions.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: This is an intra-executive reorganization of maritime regulatory functions, not a compliance regime resting on regulated-party self-reporting; the applicable question is answerable and clean.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A wartime functional reorganization with an express war-tied termination; no precedent extending authority into unrelated domains is identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order dissolves the Bureau of Marine Inspection and Navigation and redistributes its and the Secretary of Commerce's maritime functions (and certain Maritime Commission training functions) to Customs and the Coast Guard, redirecting operational mandates those bodies held; the transfer of funds is restricted to appropriated purposes, but the reassignment moves statutory operational authority laterally between agencies.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No exemption or waiver architecture is created; the order reassigns functions rather than carving actors out of coverage (the vessel-inspection waiver authority in Sec. 6 is a pre-existing statutory power merely reassigned, not a new carve-out).

note: Wartime maritime reorganization (references EO 8976, reviewed) with an explicit war-tied termination keeping Flag 6 ABSENT; the lateral redistribution of a dissolved bureau's operational functions to Customs and the Coast Guard fires Flag 10.

title: Authorizing the Appointment of Mrs. Ruth Edna McC. Somers to a Position in the Government Service Without Regard to the Civil Service Rules

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A one-time individual appointment authorization exercised directly by the President under statute; no ongoing discretionary authority is pooled in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Self-executing individual authorization; no implementation chain that could fail.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single-subject individual action; nothing distinct is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time appointment authorization creates no ongoing compliance relationship or enforcement mechanism.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No behavioral compliance rule is created, so there are no incentives to assess.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing authorization grants no continuing authority that could be subject to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed; the President exercises a specific statutory appointment-authorization power for one person.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing enforcement relationship exists over which a third party could have standing.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Narrow individual administrative action with no systemic or precedential implications.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No agency's statutory mandate is redirected by authorizing one appointment.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: A single-person appointment exception is not a coverage carve-out architecture applied against a regulated population.

note: One-time individual civil-service appointment authorization; compliance-regime flags 4,5,8,11 and flags 6,9 NOT_APPLICABLE per the individual/one-time-action test.

title: Waiver Under the Trade Act of 1974 With Respect to the Soviet Union

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The President exercises a specific statutory waiver power (Trade Act sec. 402(c)(2)) after making the required congressional report; the action applies a defined statutory authority rather than pooling new discretionary power.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: A self-executing presidential waiver following the statutorily-required report to Congress; responsibility rests with the President and the mechanism is complete on issuance.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single-purpose waiver as to one country; nothing distinct is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A Jackson-Vanik freedom-of-emigration waiver lifts a trade restriction; it creates no ongoing compliance obligation on a regulated party with an enforcement mechanism to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The waiver removes a statutory bar rather than creating a behavioral compliance rule, so there are no perverse incentives on a regulated actor class to assess.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: A Trade Act sec. 402 waiver is, by the statute's own terms, subject to annual renewal and congressional review; the reauthorization pressure the flag looks for is supplied by the underlying statutory framework, so the flag does not fire.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The waiver is issued only after 'the report to the Congress required by section 402(c)(2)'; it uses the statutory oversight process rather than removing any pre-existing check.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Lifting a trade restriction is not a compliance regime whose violations require third-party detection.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A single-country statutory trade waiver with no systemic or precedential authority-expansion identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No agency's statutory operational mandate is redirected or countermanded by the waiver.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The waiver itself is a statutorily-defined country-specific exception, not a discretionary exemptions architecture built into a coverage rule the order creates.

note: Statutory Jackson-Vanik trade waiver for the USSR issued after the required congressional report; decision-structure flags answerable and clean, compliance-regime and systemic flags NOT_APPLICABLE for this one-shot statutory waiver.

title: Continuance of Certain Federal Advisory Committees

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order continues listed advisory committees and assigns the President's FACA functions for each to the designated department/agency head; authority is distributed among many agency heads rather than pooled in one.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each committee's FACA functions are assigned to a specifically named department/agency head, with annual congressional reporting reserved; responsibility is traceable.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Continuing a list of advisory committees together is administrative housekeeping under FACA; the committees remain independently identified rather than being fused to force acceptance of unrelated matter.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Continuing advisory committees creates no ongoing compliance obligation on a regulated party with an enforcement mechanism to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Advisory committees give non-binding advice and create no behavioral compliance rule generating perverse incentives.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order builds in a sunset: each listed committee 'is continued until September 30, 1989,' and it revokes several orders for committees whose work is completed, so continuation requires affirmative future renewal rather than defaulting to permanence.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order expressly preserves the annual reporting-to-Congress function ('except that of reporting annually to the Congress') while assigning other FACA functions to agency heads; no pre-existing external oversight is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Advisory-committee continuance is not a compliance regime whose violations require third-party detection.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Routine administrative continuance/revocation of advisory committees with no systemic or precedential implications.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: FACA functions are assigned to each committee's designated agency head consistent with existing arrangements; no agency's statutory mandate is countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Continuing advisory committees creates no coverage population or exemption structure.

note: Routine FACA advisory-committee continuance (supersedes EO 12534; revokes several completed-committee orders). Built-in 1989 sunset keeps Flag 6 ABSENT; compliance-regime and systemic flags NOT_APPLICABLE.

title: Authorizing and Directing the Secretary of War to Take Possession of and Operate the Inglewood Plant of North American Aviation, Inc.

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order pools in the Secretary of War sweeping authority to seize and operate the private aircraft plant 'to do all things necessary or incidental thereto,' make compensation adjustments by his own further orders, and employ personnel as he deems necessary, with termination resting solely on the President's own determination.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of War is the single named officer directed to seize and operate the plant; responsibility is clear even though the authority is broad.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single-purpose seizure order for one plant; nothing distinct is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The operative standard is open-ended - the Secretary is to operate the plant and 'do all things necessary or incidental thereto' and make 'such necessary or appropriate adjustments... as further orders hereafter issued by the Secretary of War shall provide' - with no defined limits on the means employed or the terms of compensation.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The seizure aligns operation with defense production; it rests on the active unlimited-national-emergency proclamation and constitutional/commander-in-chief authority (not a lapsed statute), so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is emergency seizure authority that should end when the crisis resolves, and while the order says possession terminates when the President 'determines that the plant will be privately operated in a manner consistent with the needs of the national defense,' that end-point is tied entirely to the President's own discretionary determination rather than a defined external condition or automatic expiration - reasonable-permanence carve-out does not apply to an exceptional-powers seizure.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order does not by its text remove any pre-existing external oversight mechanism; it exercises asserted emergency/commander-in-chief seizure power.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: This is a direct government takeover and operation of a plant, not a compliance regime resting on regulated-party self-reporting, so there is no third-party enforcement chain to assess.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A presidential seizure of a private plant to break a strike, resting largely on the Constitution and an emergency proclamation rather than a specific seizure statute, establishes strong precedent for executive nationalization of private enterprise during labor disputes, usable well beyond this plant.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the Secretary of War is empowered to operate the plant; no other agency's statutory operational mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: A single-plant seizure has no coverage population or exemption structure to map.

note: Wartime plant-seizure order to end a strike; concentrated standardless operating discretion in the Secretary of War (flags 1, 4), a termination tied only to presidential discretion (flag 6), and a strong nationalization precedent (flag 9). Compliance flags 8, 11 NOT_APPLICABLE for a direct takeover.

title: Reduction of 100,000 Federal Positions

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order sets a government-wide percentage reduction and leaves the method (attrition or early-out) to each department and agency head's discretion, with OMB issuing implementing instructions; authority to execute is distributed across agency heads, not pooled in one.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The order mandates a 4 percent FTE reduction with staged target dates but names no actor responsible for verifying achievement or correcting shortfalls; OMB only 'issue[s] detailed instructions,' and for independent agencies compliance is merely 'requested,' so if the targets are missed no one in particular is accountable.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single-purpose workforce-reduction order; nothing distinct is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The reduction targets are quantified, but the mechanism for compliance is undefined: exemptions are whatever OMB deems 'necessary for the delivery of essential services and compliance with applicable law,' and there is no stated consequence for an agency that fails to hit its numbers, so implementation could vary widely.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Requiring reductions 'through attrition or early out' and counting positions on an FTE basis creates an incentive to cut wherever easiest (e.g., padding the required 10 percent SES/GS-14/15 share via early-outs, or shedding filled service positions) rather than eliminating genuinely unneeded positions, potentially undermining the efficiency purpose; authority rests on active statutes, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The reduction program carries its own defined end-point - staged targets culminating in '100 percent by the end of fiscal year 1995' - so it self-terminates rather than granting open-ended authority.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order directs a personnel reduction; it does not by its text remove any pre-existing external oversight mechanism over the affected agencies.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Whether an agency actually meets its FTE-reduction targets depends on the agency's own reporting to OMB with no external actor given standing and information to surface an agency that overstates compliance or evades the cuts through reclassification, so verification rests on self-report.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A flat across-the-board FTE cut applied uniformly regardless of workload can degrade delivery of statutorily-mandated services and drive contracting-out to circumvent the ceiling, second-order effects that shift rather than reduce the actual work and can make future staffing harder to right-size.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Each agency reduces its own positions within its own workforce; no agency's statutory mandate is redirected to or countermanded by another agency, and independent agencies are only 'requested' to comply.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: OMB is authorized to issue 'exemptions necessary for the delivery of essential services and compliance with applicable law' with no defined criteria bounding them, a single-actor discretion to carve agencies or positions out of the reduction that could be expanded to swallow the target.

note: Across-the-board FTE-reduction mandate; quantified targets but undefined compliance/consequence machinery, self-reported achievement, and unbounded OMB exemption authority drive flags 2, 4, 5, 8, 9, and 11. Built-in FY1995 end-point keeps Flag 6 ABSENT.

title: Extension of the Provisions of Executive Order No. 9001 of December 27, 1941, to Contracts of the Department of Commerce

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: By full incorporation of EO 9001, the order confers on the Secretary of Commerce (and officers he designates) 'all of the functions and powers' that EO 9001 vested in the War, Navy, and Maritime contracting authorities - broad war-contracting powers - now pooled in an additional department head; the predecessor EO 9001 text is not in the referenced-predecessors folder, so the specific scope is inferred from this order's own incorporating language and cannot be fully verified.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of Commerce is the single named officer to whom the incorporated powers are extended; responsibility is clear.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single-purpose extension of one framework to one department; nothing distinct is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The extended war-contracting powers of EO 9001 are inherited 'subject to the limitations and regulations contained in such Executive Order,' but because EO 9001's text is unavailable the operative standards cannot be confirmed as bounded; on the face of this order the Secretary receives broad, generally-described contracting functions whose application standard is not defined here.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Extending war-contracting authority to Commerce creates no evident reward for undermining the war effort; it rests on the active First War Powers Act, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is war-contracting authority under the First War Powers Act - exceptional power tied to the war's resolution - yet the extension to Commerce contains no sunset or reauthorization trigger of its own (and any such limit would depend on EO 9001, whose text is unavailable to confirm).

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order extends contracting authority; nothing in its text removes a pre-existing external oversight mechanism, though EO 9001's own provisions (unavailable) could bear on this.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: This extends internal contracting powers to a department rather than creating a regulated-party compliance regime dependent on self-reporting; the applicable question, on the available text, is answerable and clean.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Extending the special war-contracting regime to yet another department broadens the class of agencies exercising expedited, limitation-relaxed contracting authority, a second-order institutional spread of emergency contracting power across the executive branch.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order adds Commerce to the set of departments exercising EO 9001 contracting powers for its own contracts; it does not redirect or countermand another agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No exemption or waiver architecture is created by this extending order on its available text; it extends powers rather than carving actors out of coverage.

note: Full-incorporation extension of EO 9001's war-contracting powers to Commerce. EO 9001 is NOT in the referenced-predecessors folder, so the comparison to its specific limitations could not be made; flags 1, 4, 6, and 9 are scored on the incorporating language and the nature of war-contracting authority, with that limitation noted.

title: Amending Executive Order No. 7908 of June 9, 1938, and Executive Order No. 10046 of March 24, 1949

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The amendments substitute an agency name ('Bureau of Land Management' for 'General Land Office'), add a proviso about exchanged lands, and revise a land-transfer paragraph between Agriculture and Interior; no discretionary decision authority is pooled in a single official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The amendments assign administration of the described lands to the Secretary of Agriculture and the Department of the Interior under the Bankhead-Jones and Taylor Grazing Acts; responsibility is clear.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The two amendments both concern the administration and transfer of the same land holdings under the Bankhead-Jones Act framework; nothing unrelated is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The amendments tie administration of the lands to specific statutes (Bankhead-Jones Titles III/IV, Taylor Grazing Act) with defined provisions and conditions; the operative standards flow from those statutes rather than from vague discretion.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Reassigning land administration between departments creates no reward for undermining the land-management purpose; it rests on active statutes, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This amends a standing land-administration framework maintained through ordinary supersession (it amends numbered predecessors EO 7908, 8531, and 10046), and land administration is an evergreen function; the procedural-framework carve-out applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The amendments update an agency name and transfer land jurisdiction subject to 'valid existing rights'; no pre-existing external oversight mechanism is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: This is an internal land-administration/transfer amendment, not a regulated-party compliance regime dependent on self-reporting; the applicable question is answerable and clean.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Technical amendments to land-transfer and administration provisions; no precedent extending authority into unrelated domains is identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The amendments transfer jurisdiction over described lands between Agriculture and Interior under explicit statutory authority and preserve valid existing rights; this is an authorized reassignment, not a countermanding of a statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No exemption or waiver architecture is created; the amendments concern land jurisdiction, not carve-outs from a coverage rule.

note: Partial amendment of EO 7908 (as amended by EO 8531) and EO 10046 (reviewed): agency-name substitution, an exchanged-lands proviso, and a land-jurisdiction transfer under the Bankhead-Jones/Taylor Grazing Acts; clean across all flags.

title: Amending Executive Order No. 11649, Regulations Governing the Seals of the President and the Vice President of the United States

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The amendment broadens a permitted-use exception for the Presidential/Vice-Presidential seals; no discretionary decision authority is pooled in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: A self-executing definitional amendment to a permitted-use rule; no implementation chain that could fail.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single-provision amendment of one subsection; nothing distinct is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The amendment specifies exactly which uses of the seals are permitted (encyclopedias, dictionaries, books, journals, etc., 'incident to a description or history of seals, coats of arms, heraldry, or the Presidency'), a precise enumerated standard leaving little room for divergent application.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Broadening a permitted-use exception for seals creates no ongoing behavioral compliance rule generating perverse incentives for a regulated actor class.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This amends a standing regulatory framework (seal-use regulations) maintained through ordinary supersession by amending numbered predecessor EO 11649; the procedural-framework carve-out applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Diff against EO 11649 shows the amendment enlarges the permitted-use exception; no pre-existing external oversight mechanism is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: A permitted-use exception for seals is not a compliance regime whose violations require third-party detection.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Narrow administrative amendment of seal-use rules with no systemic or precedential implications.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency is implicated by an amendment to the seal-use regulations.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The amendment defines a narrow permitted-use exception with enumerated categories; it does not create a broad or discretionary exemption structure that swallows the rule restricting seal use.

note: Partial amendment of EO 11649 (reviewed): broadens the permitted-use exception for the Presidential/Vice-Presidential seals. Narrow, precise administrative action; compliance-conduct and systemic flags (5,8,9,10) NOT_APPLICABLE.

title: Delegating to the Director of Selective Service Authority to Issue Rules and Regulations Under the Military Selective Service Act

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order delegates to the Director of Selective Service the President's own authority to 'prescribe the necessary rules and regulations to carry out the provisions of the Act,' pooling broad rulemaking power over conscription in a single official, with prior presidential control converted into a delegated function.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Director is the single named actor responsible for the rules, with a defined process and the President retaining ultimate authority; responsibility is traceable.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single-purpose delegation of rulemaking authority; nothing distinct is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The rulemaking is bounded by the Act and by a specified procedure (Federal Register publication per sec. 13(b), a defined 10-day/30-day interagency consultation-and-objection process), so the delegation's exercise is procedurally constrained rather than standardless.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Delegating rulemaking creates no reward for undermining the conscription statute; authority rests on the active Military Selective Service Act, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This delegates rulemaking within a standing statutory framework (Selective Service administration) maintained through ordinary supersession, with existing regulations continuing until amended; the procedural-framework carve-out applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds an interagency consultation-and-referral check (heads of named departments may request that a proposed rule be referred to the President) and preserves the President's own authority; it adds rather than removes oversight, so per the direction rule Flag 7 does not fire.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The Selective Service system provides registrants defined appeal and reopening rights over classifications, and the delegated rules are published for notice; this delegation does not create a self-reporting-only regime lacking external checks.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Converting the President's personal conscription-rulemaking power into a standing delegation to the Director establishes precedent for durable devolution of a sensitive presidential regulatory function to an agency head, an institutional change persisting beyond any single regulation.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The consultation requirement coordinates with Defense, Justice, Labor, HEW, Transportation, and others rather than countermanding any of their statutory mandates; it manages interagency input rather than creating mandate conflict.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order delegates rulemaking authority; it creates no exemption or waiver structure carving actors out of coverage (and expressly withholds from the Director the power to waive sec. 13(b)).

note: Delegates presidential Selective Service rulemaking to the Director with a defined interagency consultation/referral safeguard. Concentration of rulemaking (flag 1) and the delegation precedent (flag 9) fire; the added consultation check keeps Flag 7 ABSENT.

title: Providing for the Coordination and Control of the Rubber Program

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order directs the Chairman of the War Production Board to 'assume full responsibility for and control over the Nation's rubber program in all of its phases' and creates a Rubber Director who may direct numerous other agencies with 'full compliance' required; this pools total, unchecked authority over an entire wartime commodity sector in a single official, the core of the order's design.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Chairman of the WPB and the Rubber Director are named as the responsible actors, with the Director 'responsible to the Chairman'; responsibility is clear even though the authority is sweeping.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: All provisions serve the single stated purpose of coordinating and controlling the rubber program; nothing unrelated is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Rubber Director may direct other agencies 'to execute such aspects of the rubber program in such manner and for such period of time as he deems advisable,' an open-ended standard with no defined limits on the directives he may issue or the compliance he may compel.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Consolidating rubber control aligns with the stated wartime-supply purpose; authority rests on the then-active First War Powers Act, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is sweeping wartime commodity-control authority whose natural end-state is the war's resolution, yet the order grants the WPB Chairman and Rubber Director open-ended control with no sunset, reauthorization trigger, or termination condition of its own.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: By empowering the Rubber Director to direct the OPA, Department of Agriculture, Office of Defense Transportation, Board of Economic Warfare, and other agencies with mandatory 'full compliance,' the order subordinates and effectively overrides those agencies' independent decision-making over rubber matters, disabling the checks their separate authority would otherwise provide (subject only to the sec. 5 savings for the Price Administrator's Emergency Price Control Act powers).

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: This is a direct intra-government command-and-control reorganization of the rubber program, not a regulated-party compliance regime resting on self-reporting, so there is no third-party enforcement chain to assess.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Vesting one official with total command over an entire commodity sector and the power to direct multiple statutory agencies establishes a strong precedent for centralized executive czar-style control usable across other sectors under the same war-powers framework.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order authorizes the Rubber Director to direct and countermand the operational functions of numerous agencies with existing mandates (Rubber Reserve Company, Petroleum Coordinator, Office of Defense Transportation, OPA, Department of Agriculture, Board of Economic Warfare) for rubber-program purposes with 'full compliance' compelled, laterally overriding those agencies' statutory authority in a way that directly defines the order's control structure.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: A command-and-control coordination order over the rubber program has no coverage population or exemption structure to map; the sec. 5 savings clause preserves a pre-existing statutory power rather than creating a discretionary exemption from a coverage rule.

note: Wartime rubber-program 'czar' order concentrating total sectoral control in the WPB Chairman/Rubber Director; drives CRITICAL on Power Concentration and Inter-Agency Cannibalization, plus PRESENT on flags 4, 6, 7, and 9. Compliance-regime flags 8, 11 NOT_APPLICABLE for a direct command reorganization.

title: Waiver of the Time Limitations in Civil Service Rule IX To Permit the Reinstatement of Van C. Brodrick

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A one-time waiver reinstating a single named individual; no discretionary authority is pooled in any official going forward.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Self-executing individual reinstatement recommended by the Postmaster General and concurred in by the Civil Service Commission; no failure chain to trace.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single-provision order with only one action (one reinstatement); there is nothing to bundle against.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time reinstatement creates no ongoing compliance rule for anyone to enforce.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral relationship exists; a single corrective reinstatement creates no incentive structure.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing reinstatement grants no ongoing authority and has nothing to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No existing oversight mechanism is removed; the order is concurred in by the Civil Service Commission.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing enforcement regime exists for a one-time reinstatement.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow individual reinstatement correcting a clerical error has no systemic or precedential implications.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency's mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order is itself an individual waiver, not a coverage scheme with exemptions.

note: Narrow individual personnel action; per Application Notes the always-answerable decision-structure flags (1,2,7) score ABSENT while compliance-regime flags are NA for lack of an ongoing relationship.

title: Chemical Warfare Review Commission

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Creates a purely advisory bipartisan commission that reports to the President; it makes no binding decisions and holds no implementation authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Commission reports to the President with clear reporting deadlines and the Secretary of Defense provides support; responsibility is defined.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose (review chemical warfare posture); no distinct remedies or problem frames are bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: An advisory commission enforces no standard against any party; there is no compliance relationship.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A time-limited advisory body reporting recommendations creates no ongoing incentive for regulated behavior.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Sec. 4(b) sets an explicit termination: 30 days after report submission or September 30, 1985, whichever is earlier.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Reserves the President's FACA reporting-to-Congress function and adds no removal of existing oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No enforcement regime exists; the body only advises.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A short-lived study commission with defined deliverables establishes no precedent usable in unrelated contexts.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Secretary of Defense provides support and agencies supply information to the extent permitted by law; no existing mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population or exemption structure exists for an advisory commission.

note: Clean, time-limited advisory FACA commission with an explicit sunset; compliance-regime flags NA for lack of an ongoing regulatory relationship.

title: Interagency Task Force on the Roles and Missions of the United States Coast Guard

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: An interagency advisory task force reporting to the President through the Secretary of Transportation; it recommends but decides nothing binding.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Chair, Staff Director, and reporting lines through the Secretary of Transportation are defined; responsibility is clear.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose (review Coast Guard roles and missions); no unrelated remedies packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Advisory task force enforces nothing against any regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A time-limited advisory review creates no ongoing compliance incentive structure.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Sec. 5(a) sets a 6-month existence from first meeting unless extended by the Secretary of Transportation.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Adds a review body; removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No enforcement chain exists for an advisory body.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Recommendations feed budget decisions but the task force itself establishes no cross-domain precedent or authority claim.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Draws representatives from many agencies to advise; does not redirect or countermand any agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage or exemption structure exists for an advisory task force.

note: Clean interagency advisory task force with explicit time limit and defined reporting; compliance-regime flags NA.

title: Waiver under the Trade Act of 1974 with respect to the Hungarian People's Republic

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A one-time statutory waiver exercised after the required report to Congress; no ongoing discretionary authority is pooled.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The President acts under an express statutory procedure (Section 402(c)(2)) that required a report to Congress; responsibility is clear.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single-action waiver; nothing distinct is packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A waiver of statutory restrictions creates no new enforcement standard against any party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing compliance relationship is established by a single trade-restriction waiver.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The Trade Act's own scheme contemplates annual renewal of such waivers, but the order itself states no expiration or reauthorization trigger for the waiver it grants, leaving continued effect until affirmatively changed.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The waiver was made only after the statutorily required report to Congress; no existing oversight is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance enforcement regime exists for a trade-restriction waiver.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A single-country statutory waiver made under an express congressional framework has no identifiable cross-domain precedential effect from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No other agency's statutory mandate is implicated or redirected.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order is itself a single waiver, not a coverage regime with an exemption structure.

note: Very short statutory waiver; Flag 6 PRESENT because the waiver's continued effect is not tied to an expiration in the order's own text despite the emergency/trade subject having a natural annual-renewal cadence.

title: Nuclear Safety Oversight Committee

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A five-member advisory committee reporting to the President and cabinet secretaries; it advises and monitors but holds no binding decision authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Coherent single purpose (advise on nuclear safety progress and TMI recommendations); no unrelated remedies packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: An advisory monitoring committee enforces no standard against any regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A short-lived advisory body creates no ongoing regulated-conduct incentive.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Sec. 1-402 sets termination on September 30, 1980.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Adds an oversight/monitoring layer; removes no pre-existing check (and expressly relies on the NRC's continuing role).

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No enforcement chain exists for an advisory monitoring body.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population or exemption scheme exists for an advisory committee.
