title: Authorizing the Secretary of the Navy to Take Possession of and Operate Certain Petroleum Plants and Facilities

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of the Navy is authorized to seize and operate listed petroleum plants 'in any manner that he deems necessary,' hire agents, exercise owners' contractual rights, and take 'any other steps' he deems necessary, with all discretion pooled in one official and no external check.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary determines the necessity, scope, and duration of seizure and when productive efficiency is 'restored,' with no independent actor able to correct his implementation judgments.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single purpose (seize/operate petroleum facilities to sustain the war effort); no unrelated remedies packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Secretary may take 'such appropriate disciplinary action, not inconsistent with law, as may be necessary,' an undefined standard two officials could apply very differently.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Authority rests on the active, unexpired Selective Service Act/War Labor Disputes Act; the seizure is tied to restoring production, so no structural incentive to reward non-compliance is created. Zombie trap does not fire: no acknowledged statutory lapse.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Sec. 7 ties termination to restoration of prior productive efficiency (max 60 days after that determination), giving a defined end tied to the emergency's resolution.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Directs the FBI to investigate on the Secretary's request and commands all federal agencies to cooperate 'to the fullest extent of their authority,' subordinating their independent functions to the seizure.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Oversight of the seizure's conduct depends on the Secretary and executive agencies he directs; no external actor with standing and information is positioned to surface abuses of the possession power.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order operationalizes a broad precedent for federal seizure and operation of private industry by executive fiat, expandable to other industries under the same statutory framing.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Sec. 6 directs Justice, NLRB, Labor, Interior, Selective Service, and OPA to cooperate to the fullest extent of their authority, subordinating their statutory operations to the Navy Secretary's seizure objectives.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Coverage is a fixed enumerated list of named plants; there is no discretionary exemption structure that swallows the rule.

note: Wartime industrial-seizure order concentrating sweeping operating discretion in one Secretary; the ongoing operation/discipline relationship makes all 11 flags applicable. Flag 6 ABSENT because termination is tied to restored production. Zombie trap not fired (active unexpired statute).

title: Amending Executive Order No. 11246, Relating to Equal Employment Opportunity

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The amendment adds 'sex' to the prohibited grounds in EO 11246 and preserves the existing Civil Service Commission and Secretary of Labor compliance roles; it distributes rather than pools authority. Predecessor EO 11246 not in referenced-predecessors, but this partial amendment touches only the coverage-ground definition, assessable from its own text.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Sec. 104 assigns the Civil Service Commission responsibility for prompt, fair complaint consideration with a defined review-and-appeal path; responsibility is named.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The amendment does one thing (extend the nondiscrimination policy to sex across the same provisions); no unrelated remedies are bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Enforcement mechanics (contract compliance clauses, compliance reports, Secretary of Labor authority) are carried forward from EO 11246 with defined triggers and consequences; the amendment adds a well-specified ground.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Extending the prohibited grounds to sex does not reward non-compliance or create gaming incentives beyond those inherent in the underlying program; no perverse design feature is added.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: An anti-discrimination mandate is evergreen by nature (per the permanence carve-out); its permanence is appropriate, not a design gap.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The amendment preserves the impartial-review and Civil Service Commission appeal mechanisms; it removes no existing oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement continues to rest largely on contractor compliance reports and agency review; applicants and employees have limited defined standing and information to surface violations, a gap the sex amendment inherits from the framework.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The amendment cleanly extends an existing category; no cross-domain precedent or new authority claim is created from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Allocates roles between contracting agencies, the Civil Service Commission, and the Secretary of Labor consistently with the existing framework; no statutory mandate is countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The amendment broadens coverage; it introduces no exemption or waiver structure.

note: Diff-aware partial amendment adding 'sex' to EO 11246's prohibited grounds; predecessor text not in the folder but the amended provision is assessable alone. Flag 6 ABSENT under the evergreen anti-discrimination carve-out.

title: Establishing a Committee and a Council Relating to the Status of Women

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Creates an interdepartmental committee of ex officio cabinet members plus a citizens' advisory council; authority is diffuse and advisory, not pooled in one official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The bodies review, evaluate, stimulate cooperation, and report annually but no actor is assigned obligation or capacity to correct failure to advance women's status; accountability rests in a committee with no enforcement authority.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Coherent single purpose (advance the status of women through a committee and council); no unrelated remedies packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Purely advisory/coordinating bodies enforce no standard against any regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: An advisory clearinghouse creates no ongoing regulated-conduct incentive structure.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: A standing structure to advance an evergreen rights/status objective falls within the permanence carve-out; continued existence is appropriate to the evergreen subject.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Adds coordinating and reporting machinery; removes no pre-existing oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No enforcement regime exists for advisory bodies.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A coordinating committee and advisory council create no authority claim usable in unrelated domains from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Cabinet secretaries serve ex officio and agencies cooperate to the extent practical; no statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage or exemption structure exists for advisory bodies.

note: Advisory committee/council on the status of women; Flag 2 PRESENT because no actor can correct failure to advance the stated goal. Flag 6 ABSENT under the evergreen carve-out.

title: Authorizing the Secretary of the Interior to Take Possession of and Operate Certain Coal Mines (Ford Collieries; Rochester and Pittsburgh Coal)

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of the Interior is authorized to seize and operate the named mines 'in such manner as he deems necessary,' do all things incidental to production and distribution, and take disciplinary action, pooling sweeping operating discretion in one official with no external check.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary alone determines scope, necessity, and when productive efficiency is restored (the termination trigger), with no independent actor able to correct that judgment.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single purpose (seize and operate two named coal companies' mines); no unrelated remedies packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Authorizes 'such appropriate disciplinary action, not inconsistent with law, as may be necessary,' an undefined standard subject to inconsistent application.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Authority rests on the active, unexpired War Labor Disputes/Selective Training and Service Acts and is tied to restoring production; no design feature rewards undermining the purpose. Zombie trap not fired: no acknowledged statutory lapse.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Sec. 5 ties termination to restoration of prior productive efficiency (within 60 days of that determination), a defined end tied to the emergency.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Directs all federal agencies including the War Manpower Commission, Selective Service, War Department, and Justice to cooperate 'to the fullest extent possible,' subordinating their functions to the seizure.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Oversight of seizure conduct depends on the Secretary and agencies he directs; no external actor with standing and information is positioned to surface abuses of the possession power.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Establishes and applies a broad precedent for executive seizure and operation of private mines, readily extendable to other named companies or industries under the same framing.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Commands the War Manpower Commission, Selective Service, War Department, and Justice to cooperate to the fullest extent, subordinating their statutory operations to the Interior Secretary's seizure objectives.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Coverage is two named companies' mines with no discretionary exemption structure.

note: Wartime coal-mine seizure paralleling order-057; sweeping operating discretion in one Secretary and an ongoing operation/discipline relationship make all 11 flags applicable. Flag 6 ABSENT (termination tied to restored production); zombie trap not fired (active statute).

title: Declassification of Selected Records Within the National Archives of the United States

flags.name: Power Concentration

flags.name: Accountability Gaps

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single action (declassify listed records); nothing distinct is packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time declassification creates no ongoing compliance standard to enforce against any party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing regulated-conduct relationship is created.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing declassification grants no ongoing authority to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Preserves FOIA exemptions other than national-security; removes no pre-existing oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No enforcement regime exists for a one-time declassification.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Affected agency heads retain a role in protecting exempt information alongside the Archivist; no statutory mandate is countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture

title: Establishing the President's Foreign Intelligence Advisory Board

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Establishes a board of outside members to advise the President and review intelligence activities; it holds no operational authority over any agency.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Board conducts continuing review and reports to the President at least every six months; its role and reporting cadence are defined.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Coherent single purpose (advise the President on foreign-intelligence activities); no unrelated remedies packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: An advisory review board enforces no standard against any regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: An advisory board creates no ongoing regulated-conduct incentive.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The Board is created with no termination or reauthorization trigger and continues until revoked; a standing discretionary review body with no built-in review-and-reauthorization moment fires the flag.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Revokes EO 10656 (predecessor not in folder) but the operative provisions add an oversight/review board rather than removing any existing external check; direction is additive.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps

title: Authorizing the Secretary of War to Take Possession of and Operate the Plants of Cudahy Brothers Company

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of War is authorized to seize and operate the named meat-processing plants 'in any manner that he deems necessary,' exercise the company's contractual rights, and take 'any other steps' he deems necessary, pooling sweeping operating discretion in one official with no external check.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary alone determines scope, necessity, and when productive efficiency is restored (the termination trigger), with no independent actor able to correct that judgment.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single purpose (seize and operate one named company's plants); no unrelated remedies packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: This order binds operation to the War Labor Disputes Act and a specific dated NWLB directive order; unlike the coal/petroleum seizures it does not add an open-ended 'disciplinary action as necessary' clause, so the operating standard is comparatively defined.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Authority rests on the active, unexpired War Labor Disputes/Selective Training and Service Acts and is tied to restoring production; no design feature rewards undermining the purpose. Zombie trap not fired.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Sec. 4 ties termination to restoration of prior productive efficiency (within 60 days of that determination), a defined end tied to the emergency.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Unlike the broader seizure orders, this one does not command all federal agencies to subordinate their functions; it directs operation under the War Labor Disputes Act and an existing NWLB directive, so no pre-existing oversight is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Oversight of the seizure's conduct depends on the Secretary; no external actor with standing and information is positioned to surface abuses of the possession power.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture

note: Narrower single-company wartime seizure; the ongoing operation relationship makes all 11 flags applicable, but it scores lower than order-057/060 because it binds operation to a specific NWLB directive and omits the open-ended discipline and all-agency-cooperation clauses (F4, F7, F10 ABSENT). Zombie trap not fired (active statute).

title: Amending the Selective Service Regulations

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Amended Sec. 1622.22(a) makes deferment turn on activities 'identified as essential by the Director of Selective Service upon the advice of the National Security Council,' and the new Sec. 1628.11(e) lets the Director suspend a registrant right at will; discretionary classification authority is concentrated in the Director. Predecessor Selective Service EOs (10001 etc.) are largely not in the folder; this partial amendment is assessed on the provisions it revises.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Classification decisions run through local boards with defined criteria and appeal structures inherent in the Selective Service system; a responsible actor exists at each step.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The amendments coherently revise related deferment classes (II-A, II-C, III-A) and examination procedure under one regulatory purpose; no unrelated matter is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: 'Necessary to the maintenance of the national health, safety, or interest in an activity identified as essential' is an open-ended standard two officials could apply to substantially different deferment outcomes in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The amendments tighten deferment criteria and add a voluntary early-examination option; no design feature rewards behavior undermining the draft's purpose. Authority rests on the active Military Selective Service Act; zombie trap not fired.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: These are standing procedural draft-classification rules revised through ordinary supersession of numbered predecessor orders, fitting the standing-procedural-framework carve-out.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The amendments preserve local-board processing and appeal; no pre-existing oversight is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Registrants themselves initiate classification and examination requests and can appeal; the individuals affected have standing and information to contest determinations, so no third-party gap exists.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Tying essential-activity deferment to Director/NSC designation expands executive discretion to shape who is deferred, a lever usable to steer manpower allocation beyond the stated classification-housekeeping purpose.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The NSC advises on essential activities but no agency's statutory operational mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The deferment classes are effectively exemptions from induction, and the essential-activity determination is concentrated in the Director on NSC advice with broad, non-specific criteria, giving discretion to expand who is carved out of the draft.

note: Diff-aware partial amendment of the Selective Service Regulations; most numbered predecessor EOs not in the folder, so assessed on the amended provisions. Deferment discretion concentrated in the Director on undefined 'essential activity' criteria drives Flags 1, 4, 9, and 11.

title: Revoking Rhodesian Sanctions

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Revokes three sanctions EOs and distributes enforcement/implementation across the Secretaries of State, Treasury, Commerce, and Transportation; no authority is pooled in one official. Revoked predecessors (11322, 11419, 11978) not in referenced-predecessors, but the revoking mechanics are assessable from the text.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Named Secretaries retain enforcement authority for pre-revocation transactions and are directed to implement, with consultation through the Secretary of State; responsibility is defined.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose (terminate Rhodesian/Zimbabwe sanctions); the three revoked orders all concern the same sanctions regime, so no unrelated matter is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Enforcement of the winding-down regime is assigned to named Secretaries with a clear savings clause for prior violations; the transitional standard is defined.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Terminating sanctions with a preserved-liability savings clause creates no incentive rewarding evasion; authority rests on the active UN Participation Act. Zombie trap not fired.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order is itself a self-executing revocation terminating authority rather than granting ongoing authority, so there is nothing further to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Removes a sanctions program but preserves enforcement and liability for prior conduct; no external oversight mechanism over remaining authority is disabled.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement for prior violations remains with named Secretaries under existing sanctions-enforcement machinery; the winding down does not create a new self-reporting-only regime.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A revocation with an explicit savings clause for prior acts creates no cross-domain precedent identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Allocates transitional roles among State, Treasury, Commerce, and Transportation with State as consultative coordinator; no statutory mandate is countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order lifts a coverage regime; it introduces no waiver structure that swallows a rule.

note: Clean sanctions-revocation with preserved-liability savings clauses; revoked predecessor EOs not in the folder but the revoking/transition mechanics are fully assessable. Flag 6 NA because the order terminates rather than grants ongoing authority.

title: Reserving Certain Public Lands as Administrative Sites for the Cabeza Prieta and Kofa Game Ranges

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Self-executing withdrawal of two described 80-acre parcels for Interior Department administrative use; no discretionary authority is pooled. Supersedes EO 6910 (predecessor reviewed) only as to the described lands.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: A specific, self-executing land reservation; no implementation failure chain exists.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single land-reservation action; nothing distinct to bundle.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A land withdrawal creates no ongoing compliance standard for any party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing regulated-conduct relationship is created.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing land reservation grants no ongoing authority to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No enforcement regime exists for a land reservation.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Reserving two small parcels has no systemic or precedential implication.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the Interior Department is implicated; no second agency's mandate is redirected.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage or exemption structure exists.

note: Self-executing land reservation superseding EO 6910 (reviewed) for two described parcels; decision-structure flags ABSENT, compliance/systemic flags NA.

title: Prescribing Rates of Charges for Certain Hospitalization and Dispensary Services and Delegating Authority to Prescribe Such Rates

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Sec. 2 delegates to a designee (via EO 10530) the President's authority to prescribe hospitalization/dispensary rates 'from time to time,' and makes Sec. 1's rates subject to amendment, supersedure, or revocation at any time under that delegated authority, pooling ongoing rate-setting discretion in the delegate.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The rate-prescribing authority is delegated to a defined official under EO 10530's framework; a responsible actor is identifiable.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Sets rates and delegates the rate-setting authority for the same subject and revokes a superseded rate order; all provisions serve the single hospitalization-charges purpose.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The rates are specified as precise dollar amounts per service category with a defined effective date; application is clear.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Fixed per-diem/outpatient charges under an active statute create no incentive rewarding conduct that undermines the purpose. Zombie trap not fired.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a standing administrative rate framework maintained through ordinary supersession (it revokes EO 9411 and amends EO 10530), fitting the standing-procedural-framework carve-out.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing external oversight of these charges is removed; the order sets rates and a delegation, adding administrative machinery.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Charges are applied by the facility against defined payors at fixed rates; no self-reporting-dependent enforcement regime with a missing third party is created.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A hospitalization-charge rate order with delegated adjustment authority has no identifiable cross-domain precedent from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Amends the President's own delegation order (EO 10530); no other agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Rates differ by defined payor category (US employees vs. others) but this is a rate schedule, not a discretionary exemption structure that swallows coverage.

note: Rate-setting plus delegation order; revokes EO 9411 and amends EO 10530 (neither predecessor in the folder, but the operative rate/delegation mechanics are self-contained). Flag 1 PRESENT for the ongoing delegated rate-adjustment discretion.

title: Authorizing the Secretary of the Interior to Take Possession of and Operate Certain Coal Mines

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of the Interior is authorized to take possession of 'any and all such mines' producing bituminous coal and operate them 'in such manner as he may deem necessary,' an open-ended, industry-wide seizure discretion pooled in one official with no external check.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary alone determines which mines to seize, how to operate, and when productive efficiency is restored (the termination trigger); no independent actor can correct that judgment.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single purpose (seize and operate bituminous coal mines to sustain the economy in the war-to-peace transition); no unrelated remedies packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Secretary may take disciplinary action and apply to the Wage Stabilization Board for 'appropriate changes' following 'such negotiations as he may deem necessary,' undefined standards subject to inconsistent application.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order invokes wartime seizure authority to preserve 'the national economic structure' during the war-to-peace transition, extending emergency operating authority into peacetime economic management, which incentivizes continued use of the emergency framework rather than reverting to ordinary labor law. Zombie trap not fired: the statute is not textually acknowledged as expired; it invokes an active statute for a transition rationale.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Although Sec. 8 ties possession of any given mine to restored efficiency, the order is grounded in a wartime emergency yet justifies itself by peacetime economic-transition needs with no end tied to the war's actual conclusion; an emergency-powers grant used for post-emergency purposes lacks the natural end-state the carve-out requires, so the flag fires.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Directs all federal agencies to cooperate 'to the fullest extent possible,' subordinating their independent functions to the seizure.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Oversight of the seizure's conduct depends on the Secretary; no external actor with standing and information is positioned to surface abuses of the industry-wide possession power.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Using wartime seizure authority to manage a peacetime economic transition establishes a precedent for extending emergency industrial-control powers past the emergency that spawned them, expandable to other industries.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Subordinates all federal agencies' cooperation to the Secretary's objectives and channels wage decisions through the Wage Stabilization Board and Economic Stabilization Director, redirecting those bodies' functions to serve the seizure.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Coverage is all bituminous coal mines with interruptions; no discretionary exemption structure carves actors out.

note: Post-VJ-Day industry-wide coal seizure (May 1946) invoking wartime authority for peacetime economic-transition purposes; that mismatch drives Flags 5, 6, and 9. Zombie trap NOT fired because the order does not acknowledge the statute as expired (narrowed v1.2.2 test).

title: Establishment of the Retraining and Reemployment Administration

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Creates a Retraining and Reemployment Administrator within the Office of War Mobilization with 'general supervision and direction of the activities of all Government agencies' relating to retraining/reemployment and power to issue regulations and directions, concentrating cross-agency direction in one appointed official under the Director of War Mobilization.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Administrator directs all relevant agency activities and a Policy Board only assists; if the reemployment mission fails, responsibility is diffused across 'existing Government agencies and officials' the Administrator works through, with no single actor obligated to correct failure.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Coherent single purpose (coordinate retraining and reemployment of discharged servicemembers and war workers); related sub-functions serve that one aim.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A coordinating administration developing programs enforces no compliance standard against regulated parties.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A program-development and coordination body creates no ongoing regulated-conduct incentive structure.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Created under the First War Powers Act to address a demobilization problem with a natural end-state (completion of postwar reabsorption), yet the order sets no termination or reauthorization trigger and continues until revoked; the temporary-program subject fires the flag.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Adds a coordinating layer and directs consultation with congressional committees; removes no pre-existing external oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No enforcement regime exists for a coordinating administration.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Placing 'general supervision and direction' over all agencies' retraining/reemployment activities in one War Mobilization office establishes a precedent for centralized executive coordination overriding normal agency lines, usable beyond the demobilization context.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Administration is given supervision and direction over the retraining/reemployment activities of many agencies (Labor, FSA, WMC, Selective Service, VA, CSC, War, Navy, WPB) and 'all prior Executive Orders in conflict are amended accordingly,' redirecting those agencies' functions to a central coordinator.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage or exemption structure exists for a coordinating body.

note: War-powers demobilization coordination administration with cross-agency directive authority; Flag 6 PRESENT because the demobilization problem has a natural end-state but no sunset. Flag 10 fires on the sweeping 'general supervision and direction of all Government agencies' grant.

title: Evaluation of Flood Hazard in Locating Federally Owned or Financed Buildings, Roads, and Other Facilities

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Directs each executive agency head to evaluate flood hazards in its own siting, lending, and disposal decisions; responsibility is distributed across agency heads, not pooled in one official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each agency evaluates flood hazards 'as far as practicable' and self-certifies in appropriation requests; no external actor determines whether an agency's flood-hazard evaluation was adequate, so failure to prevent uneconomic floodplain use has no clear correcting actor.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose (reduce federal floodplain exposure across construction, lending, disposal, and planning); the provisions are facets of the one aim.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The standard is to preclude 'uneconomic, hazardous, or unnecessary use of flood plains' only 'as far as practicable'; two agency officials could reach very different siting decisions in good faith under this open-ended qualifier.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The 'as far as practicable' latitude weakens the mandate but does not affirmatively reward behavior that undermines the flood-loss-reduction purpose; no gaming incentive is designed in. Zombie trap not applicable.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Establishes an ongoing agency directive with no termination or reauthorization mechanism; it continues until revoked and provides only reporting, which the flag notes is not a sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Adds evaluation and reporting requirements (including statements to the Bureau of the Budget); removes no pre-existing external oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Compliance rests on agency self-evaluation and self-certification in budget requests; no external actor with standing and information is positioned to surface an agency's failure to weigh flood hazards.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A floodplain-evaluation directive has no identifiable cross-domain authority claim or precedent beyond its stated purpose from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Assigns the Army/TVA a flood-information support role and directs other agencies to cooperate; this coordinates rather than countermanding any agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The pervasive 'as far as practicable' and 'whenever practical and economically feasible' qualifiers let each agency self-exempt from the floodplain-avoidance requirement on its own economic judgment, a discretionary carve-out broad enough to soften the rule.

note: Government-wide floodplain-evaluation directive resting on agency self-evaluation with pervasive 'as far as practicable' latitude; that latitude drives Flags 4, 8, and 11, and the absence of any sunset fires Flag 6.

title: American Heritage Rivers, Amending Executive Orders 13061 and 13080

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Amends EO 13061 and EO 13080 solely to raise a numeric cap from 'ten' to 'up to 20' designated/recommended rivers; no decision authority is added or pooled. Predecessor EO 13061 reviewed: designation authority already rested with the President on CEQ-facilitated recommendations, unchanged by this amendment.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The single numeric change leaves EO 13061's defined recommendation, designation, and revocation responsibilities intact; no accountability structure is altered.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: A single one-clause numeric amendment; nothing distinct is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Raising a designation cap creates no ongoing enforcement standard; the underlying initiative created no new regulatory authority (EO 13061 Sec. 1(d)).

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A numeric cap increase creates no regulated-conduct incentive relationship.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: A one-clause amendment to a cap grants no ongoing authority of its own to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight is removed by raising the cap.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No enforcement regime is affected by the cap change.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Doubling a designation cap for a voluntary, support-only river program has no systemic or precedential implication from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The amendment implicates no agency's statutory mandate beyond the existing CEQ-coordinated framework it leaves unchanged.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage or exemption structure is created or altered by a cap increase.

note: Diff-aware single-clause amendment (ten to up to 20) of EO 13061/13080; predecessor EO 13061 reviewed and its designation architecture is unchanged, so the amendment is structurally trivial.

title: Excusing Federal Employees From Duty on December 24, and for One-Half Day on December 31, 1954

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A one-time scheduling order excusing employees on specified dates; department heads retain narrow exclusion authority for special public reasons, but no ongoing discretionary authority is pooled.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: A self-executing holiday closure with a clear effect; no implementation failure chain exists.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single scheduling action; nothing distinct to bundle.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Excusing employees from duty on set dates creates no ongoing compliance standard to enforce.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A one-time day-off order creates no ongoing regulated-conduct incentive.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order is self-limiting to two specified 1954 dates and grants no continuing authority to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No enforcement regime exists for a scheduling order.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A holiday closure has no systemic or precedential implication.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency's statutory mandate is redirected.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The narrow 'special public reasons' exclusion applied by department heads is a minor operational carve-out, not a coverage regime with an exemption structure to assess.

note: Narrow one-time federal holiday-closure order; decision-structure flags ABSENT, all compliance-regime and systemic flags NA.

title: Authorizing the Secretary of War to Take Possession of and Operate the Plants of the American Enka Corporation

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of War is authorized to seize and operate American Enka's named plants 'in such manner as he may deem necessary for the effective prosecution of the war,' pooling sweeping operating discretion in one official with no external check.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary alone determines the necessity, scope, and duration of the seizure, including when possession is 'no longer necessary,' with no independent actor able to correct that judgment.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single purpose (seize and operate one named company's plants for the war effort); no unrelated remedies packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: This brief order ties operation to 'all applicable laws' and adds no open-ended disciplinary-action clause, so the operating standard is comparatively defined.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Authority rests on the active, unexpired War Labor Disputes/Selective Training and Service Acts and is tied to war prosecution; no design feature rewards undermining the purpose. Zombie trap not fired.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Possession terminates within 60 days after the Secretary determines it is no longer necessary for the war, a defined end tied to the emergency.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Unlike the broader seizure orders, this short order does not command all federal agencies to subordinate their functions and removes no pre-existing external oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Oversight of the seizure's conduct depends on the Secretary; no external actor with standing and information is positioned to surface abuses of the possession power.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Operationalizes a broad precedent for executive seizure and operation of private industry, extendable to other named companies under the same framing.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No other agency's statutory operations are directed or subordinated in this brief order.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Coverage is one named company's plants with no discretionary exemption structure.

note: Brief single-company wartime plant seizure; the ongoing operation relationship makes all 11 flags applicable, and concentrated discretion (Flag 1 CRITICAL) is present, but narrower drafting than order-057/060 avoids the vague-discipline and all-agency clauses (F4, F7, F10 ABSENT). Zombie trap not fired (active statute).

title: Philadelphia Transportation Company, Possession and Control by the Secretary of War

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: In one sentence the Secretary of War is authorized to take possession and control of the Philadelphia Transportation Company's systems and 'utilize such systems for such purposes connected with the war emergency as he may deem needful or desirable,' an extremely broad, unbounded discretion pooled in one official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary alone determines the purposes of use and when possession is 'no longer necessary,' with no independent actor able to check or correct that judgment.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single purpose (seize and operate one transit system for the war emergency); nothing distinct is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The seizure authorization sets no compliance standard applied to any regulated party's ongoing conduct.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A one-time seizure tied to the active war emergency creates no ongoing regulated-conduct incentive; zombie trap not fired (active statute).

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Possession terminates when the Secretary determines it is no longer necessary for war-emergency purposes, a defined end tied to the emergency.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: This brief order commands no agencies to subordinate functions and removes no pre-existing external oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing enforcement regime is created; there is no compliance relationship for a third party to check.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The exceptionally broad 'such purposes as he may deem needful or desirable' framing establishes a precedent for open-ended executive seizure and repurposing of private infrastructure under emergency authority.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency's statutory mandate is implicated or redirected.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage or exemption structure exists for a single-entity seizure.

note: Terse single-entity war seizure with unusually open-ended use authority ('as he may deem needful or desirable') driving Flags 1 and 9; several compliance-regime flags NA because no ongoing regulated relationship is created. Zombie trap not fired.

title: Authorizing the Acquisition of Land Near Petersburg, Virginia, for Emergency Conservation Work

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Authorizes acquisition of a described ~170-acre parcel and allocates a fixed $30,000 for it; expenditure runs through the Director of Emergency Conservation Work and the Interior/NPS chain, not pooled discretionary authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The funding transfer and acquisition responsibilities are assigned to named officials (Director of ECW, Secretary of the Interior, Director of NPS); responsibility is defined.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single-action land acquisition/funding authorization; nothing distinct to bundle.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A fixed-sum land acquisition creates no ongoing compliance standard for any party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A one-time acquisition and fixed allocation create no ongoing regulated-conduct incentive.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: A one-time, fixed-sum acquisition authorization grants no ongoing authority to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No enforcement regime exists for a land acquisition.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A defined-parcel conservation acquisition has no systemic or precedential implication.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Transfers funds between the ECW appropriation and Interior/NPS for the stated purpose with express authorization; this is an authorized allocation, not a countermanding of any agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage or exemption structure exists.

note: One-time land acquisition with a fixed $30,000 allocation for a defined parcel; decision-structure and Flag 10 ABSENT, compliance-regime flags NA per the fixed-sum-authorization guidance.

title: Federal Civilian and Military Pay Increases

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Adjusts statutory pay schedules pursuant to defined statutory formulas (subchapter I of Ch. 53, 37 U.S.C. 1009, the Executive Salary COLA Act); no discretionary authority is pooled, the amounts flow from statute.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: A self-executing pay adjustment tied to statutory schedules with a defined effective date; no implementation failure chain exists.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: All provisions serve the single purpose of implementing statutorily mandated pay adjustments across pay systems; no unrelated matter is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Setting pay-schedule rates creates no ongoing compliance standard to enforce against a regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Statutorily formula-driven pay adjustments create no regulated-conduct incentive to game.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a standing administrative pay-adjustment maintained through ordinary supersession (it supersedes EO 12010), fitting the standing-procedural-framework carve-out.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing external oversight is removed; the rates implement statutory schemes.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance-enforcement regime is created by a pay adjustment.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A formula-driven pay adjustment has no identifiable cross-domain precedent from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Supersedes the prior year's pay order; no agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: A government-wide pay schedule has no exemption structure to assess.

note: Routine annual statutory pay-adjustment order superseding EO 12010; standing-procedural-framework carve-out clears Flag 6, and compliance-regime flags are NA.

title: Reaffirming Policy of Full Participation in the Defense Program by All Persons Regardless of Race, Creed, Color, or National Origin

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Directs all agencies and contracting agencies to act, and creates a five-member Committee on Fair Employment Practice to receive and investigate complaints; authority is distributed, not pooled in one official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Committee may 'receive and investigate complaints' and 'recommend' measures but has no enforcement power; if discrimination persists, no named actor has both obligation and capacity to compel correction, leaving accountability in a committee that only recommends.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose (non-discrimination in defense employment); the training, contract-clause, and committee provisions all serve that one aim.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The contract clause obligates contractors 'not to discriminate' but the order specifies no trigger, consequence, or standard for enforcement of that obligation; the Committee only investigates and recommends, so two officials could apply it very differently.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The non-discrimination directive does not reward conduct undermining its purpose; the weakness is under-enforcement, not a perverse incentive. Zombie trap not applicable.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: An anti-discrimination mandate is evergreen by nature under the permanence carve-out; its permanence is appropriate, not a design gap.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Creates a new investigatory committee (added oversight machinery); removes no pre-existing external check, so Flag 7 does not fire on newly-added, imperfect oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement depends on the Committee receiving complaints; while affected workers can complain, no actor has both standing and the compliance-monitoring access to systematically surface contractor discrimination, and the Committee lacks enforcement authority to act on what it finds.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Establishing a federal fair-employment committee and mandatory non-discrimination contract clauses set an institutional precedent for federal regulation of private employment discrimination extendable well beyond the wartime defense context.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Directs contracting agencies to add clauses and places the Committee in the OPM; it coordinates rather than countermanding any agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The non-discrimination duty applies broadly to defense industries and government with no discretionary exemption structure carving actors out.

note: Landmark fair-employment order; the ongoing contractor non-discrimination duty makes all 11 flags applicable. A genuine rights mandate (Flag 6 ABSENT under the evergreen carve-out) but with a recommend-only committee and undefined enforcement driving Flags 2, 4, and 8, plus a real institutional precedent (Flag 9). No CRITICAL because it substantially advances its stated purpose.

title: Termination of Urban and Community Impact Analyses

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A one-sentence revocation of EO 12074; no authority is created or pooled. Revoked predecessor EO 12074 not in referenced-predecessors, but a full revocation is assessable from the text.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Self-executing revocation ending an analysis requirement; no ongoing implementation to fail.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single-action revocation; nothing distinct to bundle.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Revoking an internal analysis requirement creates no ongoing compliance standard against any party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Ending an internal analysis requirement creates no ongoing regulated-conduct incentive.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: A self-executing revocation grants no ongoing authority to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Revoking the Urban and Community Impact Analyses requirement removes a pre-existing internal analytic check on federal policy's urban/community effects; this is removal of an existing review mechanism, which the flag targets.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing enforcement regime is created by the revocation.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Ending one impact-analysis requirement, without more in the text, does not establish an identifiable cross-domain precedent beyond removing that internal step.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency's statutory mandate is redirected by the revocation.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage or exemption structure is created.

note: One-sentence revocation of EO 12074 (predecessor not in the folder); Flag 7 PRESENT because it removes a pre-existing internal impact-analysis review step. Compliance-regime flags NA.

flags.name: Power Concentration

flags.name: Accountability Gaps

flags.name: Vague Enforcement

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing regulated-conduct relationship is created.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing land reservation grants no ongoing authority to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps

title: Inspection of Income, Estate, and Gift Tax Returns by the Senate Committee on Government Operations

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Opens specified tax returns to inspection by a named Senate committee under existing Treasury rules; authority to inspect is granted to a congressional committee, not pooled in an executive official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Commissioner of Internal Revenue is expressly directed to ensure compliance with the Privacy Act conditions before any inspection; a responsible actor is named.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose (authorize committee inspection of returns for defined study); no unrelated matter is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Inspection conditions are precise: written purpose statement, jurisdiction nexus or taxpayer consent, compliance with Treasury Decision 6132 and the Privacy Act; two officials would apply the same clear conditions.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The bounded, condition-laden inspection authorization creates no incentive rewarding misuse; access is gated on written justification. Zombie trap not applicable.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Inspection authority is limited to returns for 1955-1975 'during the Ninety-fourth Congress,' an explicit temporal limit functioning as a sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Adds Privacy Act safeguards to the inspection process; it removes no pre-existing oversight and in fact conditions access on additional checks.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The Commissioner gates each inspection on a written justification and Privacy Act compliance, providing an external check with both standing and information over the committee's access.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Authorizing congressional committee access to individual tax returns, even conditioned, normalizes a precedent for legislative inspection of confidential taxpayer data usable in other oversight contexts and implicating taxpayer privacy beyond the stated study.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The IRS retains and applies its confidentiality gatekeeping; no agency's statutory mandate is countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Access is bounded by specific conditions rather than a discretionary exemption structure; the order narrows rather than broadens who may inspect.

note: Tightly-conditioned tax-return inspection authorization with an explicit Congress-limited sunset and Privacy Act gating; clean on nearly all flags, with Flag 9 PRESENT for the taxpayer-privacy precedent it establishes.

title: Drug Policy Functions

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Makes the Associate Director for Drug Policy 'primarily responsible' for formulating, coordinating, and overseeing all international and domestic drug-abuse functions across all executive agencies, concentrating cross-agency drug-policy coordination in a single EOP official. Referenced Reorg Plan No. 1 of 1977 and EO 12045 not in the folder, but the concentrating provision is assessable from the text.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The order names the Associate Director as directly responsible for the drug-policy staff and its activities; responsibility is clearly assigned.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single purpose (clarify drug-policy responsibility within the Domestic Policy Staff); nothing distinct is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: An intra-EOP allocation of policy-coordination duties enforces no standard against a regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A staff-responsibility clarification creates no ongoing regulated-conduct incentive.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: A permanent administrative-structure clarification of an ongoing EOP coordination function fits the standing administrative-structure carve-out; no natural end-state calls for reauthorization.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Reassigns coordination duties among EOP staff; removes no pre-existing external oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No enforcement regime is created.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Clarifying which EOP official coordinates drug policy has no identifiable cross-domain precedent from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Making one EOP official responsible for 'coordinating and overseeing... drug abuse functions by all Executive agencies' asserts a coordinating authority over other agencies' existing drug-related operations, creating potential jurisdictional friction with those agencies' mandates.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage or exemption structure exists for a staff-coordination order.

note: EOP drug-policy coordination clarification; Flag 1 and Flag 10 PRESENT because it centralizes oversight of all agencies' drug-abuse functions in one Associate Director. Referenced EO 12045/Reorg Plan not in the folder but the operative provisions are self-contained.

Revoked predecessors (EOs 4652, 4872, 4873) not in referenced-predecessors, but a described-tract revocation is assessable from the text.

title: Amending Executive Order Nos. 11803, 11837, and 11842 to Provide Authority to Increase the Number of Members of the Presidential Clemency Board

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The amendment authorizes the President to 'appoint such additional members to the board as he shall from time to time determine to be necessary,' an open-ended, unbounded self-expansion power over the Clemency Board membership vested solely in the President. Predecessor EO 11803 not in referenced-predecessors, so the baseline board size is not verifiable, but the amending clause is assessable on its own text.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The amendment only changes who may enlarge the board; the President as the appointing actor is clearly identified.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: A single one-clause membership amendment; nothing distinct to bundle.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Enlarging a clemency board's membership creates no ongoing compliance standard against any regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A board-size amendment creates no regulated-conduct incentive relationship.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This one-clause amendment adds no new standing authority of its own to sunset; the underlying board's timing is governed by the unamended predecessor.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing external oversight is removed by allowing more board members.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No enforcement regime is affected by a membership change.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Permitting a variable clemency-board size, without more in the text, establishes no identifiable cross-domain precedent.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency's statutory mandate is implicated by a board-membership amendment.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage or exemption structure is created.

note: Diff-aware one-clause amendment enlarging the Presidential Clemency Board; predecessor EO 11803 not in the folder so the prior fixed size is not verifiable, but the open-ended presidential self-expansion clause fires Flag 1.

title: Establishing the National Wage Stabilization Board and Terminating the National War Labor Board

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Creates a tripartite six-member board (public, employee, employer representatives) requiring a balanced quorum; authority is distributed across constituencies, not pooled in one official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Board is a defined organizational entity within Labor with named powers and a transfer of pending cases; responsibility for wage-stabilization functions is clearly assigned to it.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Coherent single purpose (transition wage-stabilization functions from the NWLB to a new board and wind down dispute cases); the provisions serve that one aim.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Board inherits subpoena and wage-adjustment powers and may act by 'mediation, voluntary arbitration, or arbitration under rules established by the Board' with broad discretion over which disputes to take and how, leaving the operative standard for intervention loosely defined.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Continuing wage-stabilization under the active Stabilization Act and First War Powers Act creates no design feature rewarding conduct that undermines stabilization. The order does not acknowledge any statutory lapse, so the zombie trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Wage stabilization was an emergency/wartime program with a natural end-state (return to free wage-setting), yet the new Board is created with no termination or reauthorization trigger and continues until revoked; the emergency-program subject fires the flag.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Transfers functions between two executive boards; it does not remove any external judicial, legislative, or IG oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The tripartite structure gives employer and employee representatives standing and information to surface issues within the Board's own disputes process, so no third-party enforcement gap exists.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Perpetuating wartime wage-control machinery into the postwar period through a successor board establishes a precedent for continuing emergency economic-control institutions past the emergency that authorized them.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: It terminates the National War Labor Board, transfers its personnel, records, funds, and functions, and amends EO 9617, reassigning an existing body's operational mandate to the new Board; this is a lateral reassignment of an existing agency's authority.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The Board's jurisdiction over disputes is defined by statute and prior orders; no discretionary exemption structure carves parties out of wage stabilization.

note: Reorganization terminating the NWLB and creating the Wage Stabilization Board; Flag 10 fires on the reassignment of the NWLB's mandate, and Flag 6 on continuing emergency wage machinery with no sunset. Zombie trap not fired (no acknowledged statutory lapse).

title: Authorizing the Department of Defense and the Department of Commerce to Exercise Functions and Powers Under Title II of the First War Powers Act, 1941, as Amended

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Authorizes the Defense and Commerce Secretaries to enter and modify contracts 'without regard to the provisions of law relating to the making, performance, amendment, or modification of contracts,' with power to delegate and sub-delegate that authority to any military or civilian official, concentrating extraordinary contracting discretion in the departments.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The order preserves Comptroller General access to contractor books (para. 11), public-inspection data (para. 6), and profit-limitation and warranty clauses; responsible actors and audit paths are named.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Coherent single purpose (authorize emergency defense-contracting procedures and extend them to Commerce); the numerous paragraphs all implement that one authority.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order specifies detailed conditions, warranties, fee caps, applicable labor-standards acts, and audit rights; the contracting standards are precisely bounded despite the broad authority.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Authorizing contracts without advertising or competitive bidding and permitting advance payments creates incentives for favoritism and cost-inflation by contractors and officials, though the order counters these with warranties, fee caps, and audit rights. Zombie trap not fired: authority rests on the active, amended First War Powers Act, not an acknowledged lapse.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is emergency defense-contracting authority under the First War Powers Act with a natural end-state (end of the defense emergency), yet the order sets no termination or reauthorization trigger and continues until revoked; the emergency-powers subject fires the flag.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The core grant lets contracts be made 'without regard to the provisions of law relating to the making, performance, amendment, or modification of contracts' and waives advertising, competitive bidding, and bonding, bypassing the ordinary statutory procurement checks that would otherwise apply.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Comptroller General audit access and public-inspection requirements give external actors standing and information to surface contracting abuses, so no third-party gap exists.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Broadly waiving normal procurement law and extending the waiver from Defense to Commerce establishes a precedent for suspending competitive-contracting safeguards, expandable to other departments under the same emergency framing.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Extends parallel authority to Commerce with a proviso preserving its other procurement authority and non-interference with existing law; it grants rather than countermanding any agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order exempts covered contracts wholesale from advertising, competitive bidding, and bonding requirements, a broad standing carve-out from the normal procurement regime that the departments control.

note: Emergency defense-contracting authorization waiving normal procurement law; the pervasive 'without regard to' waivers drive Flags 1, 7, and 11, but strong retained audit/Comptroller General checks keep Flags 2 and 8 ABSENT. Zombie trap not fired (active First War Powers Act).

title: Establishing the President's Economic Policy Board, and for Other Purposes

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Board 'will oversee the formulation, coordination, and implementation of all economic policy of the United States' and 'serve as the focal point for economic policy decision-making,' with the Secretary of the Treasury as chair and principal spokesman, concentrating economic-policy control in one chaired body and its Treasury chair.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Board's chair, executive committee, and executive director (Assistant to the President for Economic Affairs) are defined with clear coordination responsibilities; responsible actors are named.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Beyond establishing the Board, Sec. 6 amends the composition of the Council on Wage and Price Stability, the Council on International Economic Policy, the National Advisory Council (EO 11269), and the East-West Trade Policy Committee (EO 11789), packaging several distinct membership changes to other bodies into the board-creation order.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A coordinating policy board enforces no compliance standard against a regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: An economic-policy coordination body creates no ongoing regulated-conduct incentive.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Creates a standing economic-policy coordinating board that should be subject to periodic reauthorization pressure yet has no termination or reauthorization trigger and continues until revoked.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Adds a coordinating layer and reshuffles council memberships; it removes no external judicial, legislative, or IG oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No enforcement regime is created for a coordinating board.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Centralizing oversight of 'all economic policy' and decision-making in a Treasury-chaired board establishes a precedent for consolidating economic-policy authority in the EOP, with implications for how future economic decisions are routed beyond this order.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: By claiming to oversee the implementation of 'all economic policy' and amending the leadership of several existing councils (including via EO 11269 and EO 11789 amendments), the Board asserts coordinating primacy over bodies that already hold economic-policy mandates, creating jurisdictional friction. Referenced EOs 11269/11789 not in the folder, but the amending clauses are self-describing.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage or exemption structure exists for a coordinating board.

note: Establishes a Treasury-chaired board to oversee 'all economic policy' while amending several other councils' memberships; the sweeping coordination claim drives Flags 1, 9, and 10, and the packaged council amendments fire Flag 3. Referenced EO 11269/11789 not in the folder but the amending clauses are self-contained.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No enforcement regime exists for a land restoration.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Restores surplus military land to the Territory; no agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture

title: Amending Paragraph 1 of Title II of Executive Order No. 9001 Concerning Reports of Contracts and Purchases

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Amends the public-inspection reporting clause of EO 9001; the War/Navy Secretaries and Maritime Commission Chairman each control disclosure of their own data, distributing rather than pooling authority. Predecessor EO 9001 not in referenced-predecessors, so the exact prior text is unverifiable, but the amended clause is assessable on its face.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Named officials (Secretary of War, Secretary of the Navy, Maritime Commission Chairman) are each responsible for maintaining data and determining public inspection; responsibility is defined.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: A single-clause reporting-provision amendment; nothing distinct to bundle.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The amendment governs data-maintenance and disclosure, not enforcement of a compliance standard against a regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A reporting/disclosure amendment creates no regulated-conduct incentive relationship.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: A one-clause amendment to a reporting provision adds no new standing authority of its own to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The amendment narrows the public-inspection duty by excluding 'restricted, confidential, or secret contracts or purchases' from the data made available, reducing the pre-existing public-disclosure check on wartime contracting relative to EO 9001's baseline.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance-enforcement regime with a third-party role is created by the amendment.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Narrowing a public-disclosure clause for classified contracts, without more, establishes no identifiable cross-domain precedent from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Applies uniformly to the War Department, Navy Department, and Maritime Commission over their own data; no agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The classified-contract exclusion concerns disclosure of reporting data, not exemption of parties from a coverage regime.

note: Diff-aware amendment narrowing EO 9001's public-inspection reporting clause to exclude classified contracts; predecessor EO 9001 not in the folder, but the reduction in the pre-existing disclosure check is evident on the amended text and fires Flag 7.

title: Amendment of Executive Order No. 10841, Relating to International Cooperation Under the Atomic Energy Act

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The amendment adds a presidential-referral step: each atomic-cooperation determination under EO 10841 sec. 2(a)(2) must be referred to the President and becomes effective only if not disapproved, which disperses authority back toward the President rather than pooling it. Predecessor EO 10841 reviewed: it had delegated the determination jointly to Defense and the AEC.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The amendment inserts a clear presidential-review checkpoint into an existing, well-defined determination process; responsible actors remain identified.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: A single-clause amendment adding a referral proviso; nothing distinct to bundle.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The added standard is precise: determinations become effective 15 days after referral unless the President disapproves; the trigger and timing are defined.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Adding a presidential-review checkpoint to atomic-cooperation determinations creates no ongoing regulated-conduct incentive to game.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: A one-clause amendment adding a review step grants no new standing authority of its own to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The amendment adds a presidential-disapproval check to the delegated determination process; it strengthens rather than removes oversight, so Flag 7 does not fire on newly-added machinery.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance-enforcement regime against a regulated party is created; this is an internal executive determination process.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Inserting a presidential-review step into an existing determination process establishes no identifiable cross-domain precedent from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Adds presidential review over a Defense/AEC joint determination without reassigning either body's mandate; no statutory operation is countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage or exemption structure is created.

note: Diff-aware amendment of EO 10841 (predecessor reviewed) adding a presidential-disapproval checkpoint to atomic-cooperation determinations; it adds a check rather than removing one, so Flag 7 is ABSENT and the order scores clean.

title: Amendments Relating to the National Research Council

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Restates and updates the functions of the National Research Council (a nongovernmental Academy body) by amending EO 2859; it encourages cooperation and describes cost principles but pools no governmental decision authority in any single official.
