Predecessor EO 2859 in referenced-predecessors (per INDEX row 3); this is a wholesale restatement of that framework's functions.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Council operates under the Academy's congressional charter with OMB Circular A-122 cost oversight and OMB Director authority to set cost limits; responsible actors are identified.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Coherent single purpose (update the NRC's functions and cost/acquisition provisions); no unrelated matter is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Cost recovery is bounded by OMB Circular A-122 and specific OMB Director written requirements; the applicable standard is defined.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The charter bars the Academy from receiving compensation for government services and subjects it to cost principles, so no design feature rewards cost-inflation or undermining the mission. Zombie trap not applicable.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The Council is a standing scientific-advisory structure maintained through ordinary supersession (this order amends and restates EO 2859), fitting the standing administrative-structure carve-out.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Adds OMB cost oversight and preserves the congressional charter; removes no pre-existing external oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The Academy's cost recovery is subject to OMB audit-style cost principles, giving an external actor standing and information over its charges, so no third-party gap exists.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Updating an advisory Council's functions and the sole-source acquisition proviso establishes no identifiable cross-domain authority claim from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Agencies may voluntarily engage the Academy 'to the extent permitted by law'; no agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The sole-source acquisition proviso is bounded to cases where the Academy is uniquely qualified and otherwise complies with law; it is a narrow, criteria-bound carve-out, not one that swallows competition broadly.

note: Wholesale restatement/update of the National Research Council's functions amending EO 2859 (predecessor in the folder); a nongovernmental advisory-science framework with bounded OMB cost oversight scores clean across all applicable flags.

title: Amendment of Executive Order No. 1888 Relating to Conditions of Employment in the Panama Canal Service

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The amended paragraphs repeatedly vest discretion in 'the Governor' to authorize pay increases for up to 200 aliens, set pass/reduced-rate privileges, prescribe medical-charge rates and regulations, and defer/commute leave 'in his discretion,' concentrating multiple employment-terms decisions in a single official. Predecessor EO 1888 in referenced-predecessors (INDEX row 2); this partial amendment revises specific paragraphs whose discretionary structure is assessable.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Governor is the named responsible actor for each discretionary determination; the chain of responsibility is clear even though the discretion is broad.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The amendments coherently revise related paragraphs of one employment-conditions order (pay, passes, medical care, leave); all serve the single Panama Canal service-conditions subject.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Several standards are open-ended: medical charges are set 'under regulations to be prescribed by the Governor,' passes are granted 'as may be authorized by the Governor in his discretion,' and leave commutation turns on whether the employee 'cannot, in the judgment of the Governor, be spared,' allowing very different applications by different officials.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The employment-terms amendments do not reward conduct that undermines their purpose; the war-related leave-commutation proviso is time-limited to the war and six months after. Zombie trap not fired: it self-limits rather than continuing past an acknowledged lapse.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: These are standing procedural employment-conditions rules maintained through ordinary supersession of numbered prior orders (amending EO 1888 and revoking EOs 8986, 9164), fitting the standing-procedural-framework carve-out; the one war-contingent clause carries its own express time limit.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Revises employment terms and revokes two superseded orders; it removes no external judicial, legislative, or IG oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: These are internal federal employment-condition rules applied by the Governor to employees who can seek review through ordinary personnel channels; no self-reporting-only enforcement regime with a missing third party is created.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Revising Panama Canal pay and leave paragraphs establishes no identifiable cross-domain authority claim from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Concerns one governance structure (the Panama Canal service under the Governor); no other agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The pay rules default to a $100/50-cents cap for non-citizens but let the Governor exempt up to 200 named aliens and grant emergency exceptions in his discretion, a discretionary carve-out from the citizen-preference coverage rule concentrated in one official.

note: Diff-aware amendment of EO 1888 (predecessor in the folder) revising Panama Canal employment conditions; the repeated Governor discretion drives Flags 1, 4, and 11, while the war-contingent leave clause is self-limiting so Flag 6 stays ABSENT.

title: President's Economic Policy Advisory Board

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Establishes a private-member FACA advisory board that advises the President and works with the Cabinet Council on Economic Affairs; it conducts review and reports but holds no binding decision authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Board has a designated Chair and the Assistant to the President for Policy Development as Secretary, reporting to the President; responsible actors are named.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Coherent single purpose (advise on domestic and international economic policy); nothing distinct is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: An advisory board enforces no compliance standard against a regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: An advisory economic board creates no ongoing regulated-conduct incentive.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Sec. 4(b) sets termination on December 31, 1982, unless sooner extended.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Adds an advisory body; removes no pre-existing external oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No enforcement regime exists for an advisory board.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A time-limited advisory board establishes no authority claim usable in unrelated domains from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Board works with the existing Cabinet Council and agencies furnish information to the extent permitted by law; no statutory mandate is redirected.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage or exemption structure exists for an advisory board.

note: Clean private-sector economic advisory board with an explicit sunset; unlike order-089's Economic Policy Board, this one only advises and coordinates with an existing council, so it fires no power/cannibalization flags. Compliance-regime flags NA.

title: Establishing the National War Labor Board

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Board 'shall finally determine' certified labor disputes and may take jurisdiction 'on its own motion,' with binding authority to impose mediation, voluntary arbitration, or Board-rule arbitration; final dispute-resolution authority is pooled in a single wartime board whose determinations are not subject to external review.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Board is a defined twelve-member tripartite body with a Chairman and quorum rules; a responsible actor for dispute resolution is clearly identified.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Coherent single purpose (peaceful wartime adjustment of labor disputes); the procedural steps all serve that one aim.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Board may take jurisdiction 'in its discretion' and 'finally determine the dispute... by mediation, voluntary arbitration, or arbitration under rules established by the Board,' leaving broad, loosely-defined discretion over which disputes it resolves and by what standard.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The no-strike/no-lockout dispute machinery does not reward conduct undermining its purpose; parties must exhaust other procedures first. Authority rests on the active wartime state of war; zombie trap not fired (no acknowledged statutory lapse).

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The Board is expressly a wartime measure ('for the duration of the war') addressing an emergency with a natural end-state, yet the order sets no termination or reauthorization trigger and continues until revoked; the emergency subject fires the flag.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Sec. 7 expressly preserves the Railway Labor Act, NLRA, FLSA, and other statutes; the Board adds dispute machinery without displacing existing statutory review regimes.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The tripartite structure and the Secretary of Labor's certification role give employer, employee, and public representatives standing and information within the disputes process, so no third-party enforcement gap exists.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Creating a board that 'finally determines' labor disputes on its own motion establishes a precedent for compulsory federal adjudication of private labor disputes usable to expand executive control over labor relations beyond the wartime context.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Board absorbs the National Defense Mediation Board (EO 8716, in referenced-predecessors), which 'shall cease to exist,' transferring its employees, records, funds, and pending cases; this is a lateral reassignment of an existing body's operational mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Sec. 2 excludes disputes with their own adjustment procedures until exhausted, a defined jurisdictional sequencing rule rather than a discretionary exemption that swallows coverage.

note: Wartime NWLB creation with binding dispute-determination authority; Flag 10 fires on absorbing the National Defense Mediation Board (EO 8716, in the folder) and Flag 6 on emergency machinery with no sunset. Zombie trap not fired (active war state, no acknowledged lapse).

title: President's Private Sector Survey on Cost Control in the Federal Government

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Establishes a large private-member advisory committee that studies cost control and advises the President, Secretary of Commerce, and agency heads; it holds no binding authority and only recommends.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Committee has a designated Chairman, a management office, and Commerce Department support, reporting its recommendations; responsible actors are named.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Coherent single purpose (private-sector survey on federal cost control); the review areas all serve that one aim.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A study/advisory committee enforces no compliance standard against a regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A cost-control study committee creates no ongoing regulated-conduct incentive relationship.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Sec. 4(b) sets termination on December 31, 1982, unless sooner extended.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Adds a review committee with agency-information access; removes no pre-existing external oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No enforcement regime exists for a study committee.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Granting a privately-funded, private-member committee in-depth access to review the structure, personnel, and operations of executive agencies establishes a precedent for private-sector actors examining internal agency operations, with implications (private funding of a federal review body, information access) beyond the stated cost-control aim.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Agencies furnish information to the Committee via the Secretary of Commerce to the extent permitted by law; no agency's statutory operational mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage or exemption structure exists for a study committee.

note: Privately-funded cost-control survey committee (the 'Grace Commission') with an explicit sunset; largely clean, but Flag 9 PRESENT for the precedent of private-sector, privately-funded in-depth review of internal agency operations. Compliance-regime flags NA.

title: President's Commission on Executive Exchange

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Order abolishes a commission and directs the OPM Director to terminate its functions by a fixed date; no discretionary decision authority is pooled in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The OPM Director is the named actor responsible for completing termination by September 30, 1991, so responsibility is clear.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order does only one thing (abolish the Commission and revoke EO 12493); nothing distinct is packaged with it.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time abolition/termination creates no ongoing behavioral rule for anyone to comply with, so there is no enforcement standard to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing regulatory relationship or incentive structure is created by a wind-down directive.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order itself terminates the program with a hard completion deadline (September 30, 1991); it grants no continuing authority that could lack a sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Nothing removes or bypasses any existing oversight mechanism; it simply revokes EO 12493 and abolishes the body it created.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: A one-time abolition creates no compliance regime requiring third-party monitoring.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Abolishing an executive-exchange commission has no identifiable precedential or downstream effect beyond ending that program.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only OPM is implicated, in a housekeeping wind-down role; no agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order defines no coverage population and grants no exemptions, so there is no exemption structure to assess.

note: Revokes EO 12493 (text reviewed); revocation abolishes a voluntary executive-exchange commission with a clean fixed-date wind-down, so nothing structurally weighty remains.

title: Amending Executive Order No. 10923 to Extend the Time Within Which Emergency Board No. 136 Shall Report

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order only extends a statutory reporting deadline for an existing Railway Labor Act emergency board; no decision authority is concentrated.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The board reports to the President under Section 10 of the Railway Labor Act; responsibility for the report is clear and statutorily defined.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single-purpose deadline extension; nothing is packaged with it.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A deadline extension for a fact-finding board imposes no compliance standard on any regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral incentive is created by moving a report date; the statutory RLA process governs the parties.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The board's authority is inherently time-bound by the RLA process and the fixed report date; the extension itself sets a date certain (before May 24, 1961).

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Nothing removes existing oversight; the statutory 30-day cooling-off period is preserved and folded into the extension.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance regime is established; the RLA dispute-resolution structure supplies the enforcement chain.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A one-time report-date extension for a single airline dispute has no precedential or systemic downstream effect.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No agency mandate is implicated or redirected; the board is an ad hoc statutory body.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population or exemption structure exists in a deadline extension.

note: Partial amendment of EO 10923 touching only the report-date mechanism (predecessor text not in folder, but the amended provision is fully described in this order's own text); preserves the statutory 30-day period, so the mechanism is neither weakened nor structurally significant.

title: Amending Executive Order 8588 and Prescribing Regulations Governing Transportation of the Immediate Family of Civilian Officers and Employees

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Authority to authorize shipments and grant extensions rests with each department/agency head under detailed statutory travel regulations; discretion is bounded and distributed, not pooled.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The head of each department or establishment is the named responsible actor for authorizations and extensions.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The two parts (amending goods-transport regs and adding family-transport regs) both serve the single coherent subject of relocation-expense reimbursement.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Standards are precise (weight caps, cost ceilings, six-month/one-year/two-year time limits, 'most economical route'); two officials would apply them consistently.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Reimbursement is capped at the most economical route and requires actual costs, giving employees no gaming advantage that undermines the cost-control purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a standing procedural framework for routine relocation reimbursement, maintained through ordinary supersession (it itself amends EO 8588 as previously amended by EO 9122); evergreen administrative rules of this kind do not require reauthorization pressure.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: It subjects family transport to the Standardized Government Travel Regulations and standard voucher/audit processes; no existing oversight is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Reimbursement runs through the standard voucher and itemization process (Sec. 15 of EO 8588), giving disbursing/audit officials access to verify claims.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A relocation-expense reimbursement schedule has no identifiable precedential reach into unrelated domains.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Each agency administers reimbursement for its own transfers; no agency's mandate is redirected or duplicated.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The one carve-out (Foreign Service officers, per EO 8588 Sec. 16) is narrow and defined; extensions are bounded and do not swallow the rule.

note: Diff-aware review of EO 8588 (as amended by EO 9122) confirms the amended Sections 5 and 12 tighten/clarify shipment means and time limits rather than weaken any mechanism; clean administrative reimbursement architecture.

title: Establishment and Administration of the Peace Corps in the Department of State

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of State establishes the Peace Corps and delegates to a Director 'such of the functions... as the Secretary shall deem necessary,' an open-ended delegation of Mutual Security Act authority to a single new official with no external check specified in the order.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The order names the Secretary and Director but specifies no reporting, review, or correction mechanism if the Peace Corps fails its stated training/service mission; accountability runs internally to the same officials who administer it.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose (establishing and financing the Peace Corps); no distinct policy questions are packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: This is an establishment/delegation order creating an assistance agency, not a compliance regime; there is a clear administrative relationship but no vague enforcement standard against a regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order invokes an active statute (Mutual Security Act of 1954, as amended); no lapsed authority is continued and no incentive rewards undermining the mission.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The Peace Corps is created as a permanent agency financed from existing appropriations with no review, renewal, or expiration trigger; a new program authority of this kind should carry reauthorization pressure it lacks.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight is removed; Sec. 4 expressly preserves EO 10893 and treats delegations as additive.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: As a program-establishment order rather than a regulatory-compliance instrument, its ongoing relationship runs through appropriations oversight; no self-reporting enforcement gap is created.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Creating a permanent overseas-assistance corps by open-ended delegation of Mutual Security Act functions establishes institutional and delegation precedent extending beyond the immediate program.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Sec. 4 specifies the order shall not supersede or derogate from EO 10893 and that delegations are additive, avoiding conflict with existing State/foreign-assistance mandates.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage-and-exemption structure is created; the order establishes and funds an agency rather than regulating a population.

note: References EO 10893 only as additive (Sec. 4), so no diff-aware weakening; scored on its own terms as an establishment order with an open-ended delegation to a new Director and no sunset.

title: United States Circuit Judge Nominating Commission

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Authority is distributed across thirteen panels that only recommend nominees; final selection remains the President's constitutional prerogative, and panels act solely on presidential request.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Panels report their recommendations to the President, who retains and exercises the appointment decision; responsibility is clear.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single coherent subject (judicial-nominee recommendation panels); nothing unrelated is packaged in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: This is an advisory nominating body, not an enforcement regime; the selection standards (Sec. 4) are detailed and consistently applicable.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Panel members are barred from consideration as nominees during service and for one year after (Sec. 5), directly foreclosing the obvious self-dealing incentive.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The Commission terminates December 31, 1978 unless extended (Sec. 8) and panel appointments terminate 30 days after report (Sec. 7); explicit expiration mechanisms are present.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight is removed; the order adds a public-notice and screening process ahead of nomination.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Public notice inviting suggestions (Sec. 3) gives outside parties access and standing to surface candidate information; not a self-reporting compliance regime.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A time-limited advisory nominating commission with defined standards has no identifiable precedent-setting effect beyond judicial selection.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No agency's statutory mandate is redirected; the panels are freestanding advisory bodies.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order regulates no covered population and grants no exemptions; it establishes advisory panels.

note: Revokes and re-establishes EO 11972/11993 (both reviewed); the new order carries forward the same clean structure (advisory panels, self-dealing bar, explicit termination) with only clarifying changes, so no diff-aware weakening.

title: Setting Aside Submerged Lands of the Continental Shelf as a Naval Petroleum Reserve

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Administration of the reserve and the transferred Interior functions are pooled in the Secretary of the Navy, including sole discretion over disposition of suspense-account remittances 'as the Secretary of the Navy may deem to be proper' with no external check.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of the Navy is the single named administrator; responsibility for the reserve's management is clear even if broad.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: All provisions serve the single act of reserving continental-shelf oil/gas lands and transferring their administration to the Navy.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A land-reservation/administration transfer creates no ongoing compliance rule for a regulated party to be enforced against.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral regime is created; the order sets aside land and transfers administrative functions.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The reservation and the transferred operating rules 'shall continue in effect, until changed by the Secretary of the Navy' with no review or expiration trigger; the discretionary suspense-account/administration authority granted should carry a reauthorization mechanism it lacks.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No external oversight mechanism is removed; the order preserves an existing litigation stipulation (Sec. 2) and transfers administrative functions.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance regime requiring third-party monitoring is established by a land reservation.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Asserting 'paramount rights, full dominion, and power' over the entire continental shelf and placing revenue in a Navy-controlled suspense account establishes a resource-control precedent reaching well beyond a single reserve.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order strips Interior of its functions under the December 1950 Gulf oil-and-gas notice and substitutes 'Secretary of the Navy' for 'Secretary of the Interior,' redirecting an operational mandate one agency already held to another (Sec. 3).

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No covered population or exemption structure exists in a land-reservation order.

note: Revokes EO 9633 (Interior control of shelf resources; text not in folder, comparison limited to this order's text) and diff-aware transfers Interior's operating functions to the Navy, which drives the Flag 10 and Flag 1 findings.

title: Committee on Selection of the Director of the Federal Bureau of Investigation

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order only extends by 30 days the reporting deadline of an existing selection committee; no decision authority is concentrated.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Committee remains the named actor reporting to the President and Attorney General; the amendment leaves that responsibility intact.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single-purpose deadline amendment; nothing is packaged with it.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A committee-deadline extension imposes no compliance standard on any regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral incentive arises from moving a report date.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The amended provision sets a date certain (report no later than June 11, 1977); the committee's task is inherently self-terminating.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No existing oversight is removed; the reporting obligation to both the President and Attorney General is preserved.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance regime is created by a deadline extension.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A 30-day deadline extension for an FBI-director selection committee has no precedential or systemic downstream effect.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No agency mandate is redirected; the committee is an ad hoc advisory body.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population or exemptions exist in a deadline amendment.

note: Partial amendment of EO 11971 Sec. 3(a) touching only the report deadline (predecessor not in folder, but the amended clause is quoted in full here); the mechanism is preserved and merely extended, so structurally clean.

title: Computer Software Piracy

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Responsibility is distributed across every executive agency head, with OMB and the CIO Council in coordinating/advisory roles; no single official holds concentrated discretionary authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each agency head both adopts anti-piracy procedures and self-assesses compliance via CIO Council performance measures (Sec. 2(b)); the implementing agency effectively determines whether it has failed, with no independent correction actor named.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: All provisions serve the single stated purpose of ensuring government software-copyright compliance.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Procedures 'may include' inventories and recordkeeping and contractor measures are taken 'as the agency head deems appropriate,' so two agency heads could adopt substantially different compliance regimes in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Agencies gain nothing from non-compliance; the incentive structure aligns with the copyright-compliance purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is an evergreen standing compliance policy (ongoing copyright compliance has no natural end-state); permanence is appropriate under the carve-out, and it runs through ordinary OMB oversight and CIO Council revision.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: It adds oversight (OMB monitoring under the Clinger-Cohen Act, CIO Council review) rather than removing any; the national-security and law-enforcement carve-outs (Secs. 6-7) preserve, not reduce, existing protections.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement rests on agency self-assessment and contractor certifications/written assurances with no external actor given standing and access to surface violations; copyright holders are not brought into the enforcement chain.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: An internal-management software-compliance directive that expressly creates no rights (Sec. 9) has no identifiable precedential reach into unrelated domains.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: It uses the pre-existing CIO Council (EO 13011) and OMB's Clinger-Cohen role rather than redirecting or duplicating any agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The national-security and law-enforcement provisions (Secs. 6-7) are narrow non-disclosure protections, not coverage carve-outs that let actors escape the copyright-compliance rule.

note: Incorporates the CIO Council established by EO 13011 Sec. 3 by reference for advisory use (not amending it); scored on this order's own self-assessment-based compliance structure.

title: Establishing the Cabinet Committee on Economic Policy

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Committee is purely advisory ('advise and assist the President') and is chaired by the President himself; it holds no operative decision authority to concentrate.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Committee advises the President, who retains all decision authority; there is no operational failure chain to trace.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose (coordinating national economic policy advice); nothing distinct is packaged in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A cabinet advisory committee creates no compliance standard enforced against any regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral rule or incentive for any regulated actor is created by an internal advisory body.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The Committee is created permanently with no review, renewal, or termination mechanism; a standing advisory coordination body of this kind would ordinarily carry some reauthorization trigger and none is present.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Sec. 4 expressly disclaims subjecting any agency or its statutory functions to the authority of another; no existing oversight is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance regime requiring third-party monitoring exists for an internal advisory committee.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: An internal advisory committee that expressly preserves all existing agency functions (Sec. 4) has no identifiable downstream precedential effect.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Sec. 4's construction clause specifically prevents any redirection or subordination of agency statutory functions; coordination is advisory only.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No covered population or exemption structure exists for an advisory committee.

note: Advisory cabinet committee with a strong non-abrogation clause (Sec. 4); only the absence of any sunset/review on a standing body fires.

title: Providing for the Screening of the Ready Reserve of the Armed Forces

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of Defense (and Treasury for the Coast Guard) holds discretionary authority to define 'extreme personal or community hardship,' determine 'critical military skill,' and set screening regulations, with no external check specified in the order.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of Defense (and Treasury for the Coast Guard) is the named responsible actor, and Section 10 requires periodic reports to the President allowing evaluation of the screening.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: All provisions serve the single purpose of prescribing Ready Reserve screening criteria.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Key criteria are open-textured and delegated: 'extreme personal or community hardship as defined by the Secretary of Defense' and 'critical military skill as determined by the Secretary,' allowing substantially different transfer decisions in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The criteria track genuine readiness and hardship considerations; no actor gains by undermining the screening's stated manpower-balancing purpose, and the order rests on active statutory authority.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Continuous Ready Reserve screening is an evergreen standing military-personnel function with no natural end-state; permanence is appropriate under the carve-out and it is maintained by ongoing regulation and reporting (Sec. 10).

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing external oversight is removed; the order adds periodic presidential reporting (Sec. 10).

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Transfer determinations rest on the Service's and Secretary's own assessments (e.g., hardship 'upon request,' availability 'as determined by the Service concerned') with no external actor having standing and access to surface erroneous or manipulated classifications.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A reserve-screening classification scheme has no identifiable precedential reach beyond military manpower administration.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Secretary of Labor's List of Critical Occupations role is a defined, cooperative input rather than a redirection or countermanding of any agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The scheme is built on broad, Secretary-controlled carve-outs from Ready Reserve status (hardship, critical-occupation, critical-skill, and voluntary-agreement exceptions), several defined at the Secretary's discretion, making retention or transfer heavily dependent on discretionary exemption determinations.

note: Self-contained statutory implementation (Armed Forces Reserve Act); scored on discretionary definitional authority pooled in the Secretary of Defense and the exemption-heavy screening structure.

title: Establishing Apache Migratory Waterfowl Refuge (Arizona)

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A self-executing land reservation setting aside ~2,680 acres for Agriculture as a bird refuge; no discretionary decision authority is pooled in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Department of Agriculture is the named administering body and existing forest/game-law enforcement continues; no failure chain is left unassigned.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single act of reserving described lands as a refuge; nothing distinct is packaged with it.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time land set-aside creates no ongoing compliance rule for a regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral regime or incentive structure is created by a land reservation.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing land reservation grants no continuing operational authority that could require a sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No existing oversight is removed; the order preserves the land's forest-conservation use and applicable game-law enforcement.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance regime requiring third-party monitoring is established by a land set-aside.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow single-refuge land reservation has no systemic or precedential implications to assess.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The refuge use is expressly made consistent with, and subordinate to, the existing Apache National Forest conservation use; no mandate conflict is created.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No covered population or exemption structure exists in a land-reservation order.

note: Narrow self-executing land reservation; several compliance-regime and systemic flags are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE per the Application Notes, while decision-structure flags are cleanly ABSENT.

title: Federal Space Management

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Administrator of General Services is given broad authority to select, acquire, and manage federal space and to issue binding regulations/standards, with only presidential approval of annual plans as a check; operative siting decisions rest largely in one official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Administrator is the clearly named responsible actor, must annually submit plans for presidential approval, and must consult and give notice to affected agencies (Sec. 1-203).

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: All provisions serve the single subject of planning, acquiring, and managing federal space; the urban-development criteria are facets of that same purpose.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Siting is governed by open-ended, multi-factor 'consideration' criteria (social, economic, environmental, cultural conditions; 'first consideration' to central business areas) that two Administrators could apply to substantially different results in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order directs agencies to economize on space; no actor gains by undermining the efficient-utilization purpose, and it rests on active statutory authority.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a standing procedural framework for routine federal-space administration, maintained through ordinary supersession (it revokes and replaces EO 11512); an evergreen administrative function of this kind needs no reauthorization trigger.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight is removed; it adds coordination with OMB and consultation with state/local officials and affected agencies.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: State, regional, and local officials are given consultation rights and the affected agency receives notice before final action (Sec. 1-203(c),(e)), providing external actors with access to surface objections.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A federal space-management policy directed at urban revitalization has no identifiable precedential reach beyond property/space administration.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Administrator's authority derives from the Federal Property Act and includes required consultation with affected agencies; it does not countermand any agency's separate statutory mandate (agencies using space under other authority conform only 'to the extent they have the authority').

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The only carve-out (agencies acquiring space under other authority conform only where they have authority to, Sec. 1-302) is a defined legal-limits clause, not a discretionary exemption that swallows the rule.

note: Supersedes EO 11512 (reviewed); diff-aware, EO 12072 preserves GSA-Administrator authority and the agency-notice/president-review checks while adding urban-siting criteria, so it neither weakens the prior oversight nor adds new structural collapse beyond concentrated siting discretion and its vague criteria.

title: Employee-Management Relations in the Foreign Service of the United States

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The head of each foreign affairs agency may unilaterally and 'in his sole judgment' suspend any provision of the Order in claimed emergencies (Sec. 3(b)), and may override a final Board appeal decision (Sec. 9(a)), concentrating override authority in agency heads.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Although a Board and Commission are created, the agency head can override the Board's final appeal decision and suspend provisions in his sole judgment, so ultimate responsibility for adverse outcomes circles back to the same management being reviewed.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: All provisions serve the single coherent subject of a Foreign Service employee-management relations framework.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The suspension power turns on the agency head determining 'in emergency situations that this is necessary in the national interest' with no defined trigger, and the Commission judges organization eligibility 'in its sole discretion' (Sec. 7(a)(2)), inviting inconsistent application.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The framework's incentives (recognition, consultation, grievance resolution) broadly align actors with the stated goal of orderly labor relations; no design rewards undermining that purpose, and it rests on active statutory authority.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: A standing labor-relations framework is evergreen by nature (an ongoing rights/procedure structure) and includes its own periodic conferral and annual review mechanism (Sec. 11); permanence is appropriate under the carve-out.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Sec. 3(b) lets an agency head suspend the Order's protections for any post or activity, and Sec. 9(a) makes the Board's otherwise-final appeal decision reversible by the agency head, disabling the very review checks the Order creates when management chooses.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Recognized organizations, a public-member disputes panel, and an independent grievance panel (Secs. 6, 10) give external actors standing and access to surface unfair practices; enforcement does not depend on management self-reporting.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The 'sole judgment' emergency-suspension and management-override provisions establish a template for a labor-rights regime whose core protections are defeasible at management's discretion, a precedent applicable to other personnel systems.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Commission draws on Labor, CSC, and OMB representatives in defined roles; Sec. 18's amendments to EO 11491 and EO 11264 slot the Foreign Service into existing structures rather than countermanding any agency mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The Sec. 3(b) power to 'suspend temporarily any provision of this Order with respect to any post, bureau, office, or activity' at the agency head's sole judgment is a broad, discretionary carve-out capable of removing whole units from coverage.

note: Partial amendments in Sec. 18 to EO 11491 and EO 11264 add the Foreign Service to existing lists (not full incorporation); the order is scored on its own extensive framework, where agency-head sole-judgment suspension and override powers drive multiple flags without rising to core collapse (the framework still substantially delivers its recognition/grievance machinery).

title: Authorizing the Secretary of the Interior To Withdraw and Reserve Lands of the Public Domain and Other Lands

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order delegates to the Secretary of the Interior the President's own power to withdraw, reserve, modify, or revoke withdrawals of public and other federally controlled lands, pooling a broad land-control authority in one Secretary.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary is the single named actor, and every order must have prior approval of the Budget Director and Attorney General and be published in the Federal Register, keeping responsibility traceable.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single subject (delegating land-withdrawal authority to Interior); nothing distinct is packaged in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A delegation of land-withdrawal authority creates no ongoing compliance rule enforced against a regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral regime or incentive for a regulated actor is created by an internal delegation of authority.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The delegation of discretionary withdrawal authority is granted permanently with no review or expiration; a broad ongoing discretionary grant of this kind should carry reauthorization pressure and none is provided.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order preserves the Budget Director/Attorney General approval and Federal Register publication checks and requires concurrence of any other affected agency head; no existing oversight is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance regime requiring third-party monitoring is created by a delegation of withdrawal authority.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Broadening the delegation from EO 9146 ('public lands') to 'lands of the public domain and other lands owned or controlled by the United States' expands the delegable authority's reach, establishing precedent for Secretary-level control over a wider land base.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order requires prior concurrence of the head of any other department or agency whose lands are affected, expressly preventing redirection of another agency's jurisdiction without its consent.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No covered population or exemption structure exists in a delegation of withdrawal authority.

note: Supersedes EO 9146 (reviewed); diff-aware, it re-grants the same delegation with the same BoB/AG-approval and inter-agency-concurrence safeguards but broadens the land base to 'other lands owned or controlled,' which drives the Flag 9 finding while the preserved checks keep Flag 2/7/10 clean.

title: Relating to Voluntary Agreements

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The amendment distributes Defense Production Act Section 708 voluntary-agreement functions across the GSA Administrator and several named Secretaries rather than pooling them, and reserves the antitrust-immunity-related authority to the GSA Administrator alone under Title I; authority is spread, not concentrated.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Specific officials are named as responsible for each delegated function; responsibility is clearly assigned.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single-purpose amendment of one section (Sec. 501) of EO 10480; nothing distinct is packaged in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The delegated functions and the requirement to act only after consultation with, and per guidelines of, the OMB Director are defined; the standard for exercising the advisory-committee authority is bounded.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The amendment adds an OMB-consultation control on establishing advisory committees, reinforcing rather than undermining the statute's purpose; it rests on the active Defense Production Act.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a partial amendment to a standing mobilization-authority framework maintained through ordinary supersession; the specific provision touched grants no new open-ended authority requiring its own sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The amendment adds a check (advisory committees may be established 'only after consultation with, and in accordance with guidelines and procedures established by' OMB); it removes no existing oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Voluntary agreements under DPA Section 708 operate through a defined federal-approval and consultation process that gives OMB and delegated officials oversight access; no self-reporting-only enforcement gap is created by this amendment.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A technical redelegation of voluntary-agreement authority among named agencies has no identifiable precedential reach beyond defense-mobilization administration.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The amendment allocates DPA functions among agencies under a defined coordination structure (subject to Sec. 101 and OMB guidelines) rather than countermanding any agency's separate statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage-and-exemption structure is created; the amendment reassigns delegated functions and adds a consultation requirement.

note: Partial amendment of EO 10480 Sec. 501 (predecessor reviewed); diff-aware, it narrows/reallocates Section 708 authority and adds an OMB-consultation gate on advisory committees, strengthening rather than weakening the mechanism, so all applicable flags are clean.

title: Garnishment of Federal Employees' Pay

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order designates OPM (with the Postmaster General for USPS) to promulgate implementing regulations under a specific statutory grant (5 U.S.C. 5520a); rulemaking authority is assigned, not discretionarily pooled without check.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: OPM and the Postmaster General are the clearly named actors responsible for their respective regulations.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single purpose (designating who issues pay-garnishment regulations); nothing distinct is packaged in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order simply designates regulation-writers under a defined statute; the enforcement standard is supplied by 5 U.S.C. 5520a and the forthcoming regulations, not left ambiguous by this order.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A designation to promulgate garnishment regulations creates no incentive to undermine the statute's purpose; it rests on an active statutory authorization.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This implements a standing statutory pay-garnishment scheme; the underlying administrative function is evergreen and revised through ordinary rulemaking, so the permanence carve-out applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No existing oversight is removed; the order allocates a new statutory rulemaking task.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Garnishment enforcement is driven by creditors/courts obtaining legal process, external actors with standing and incentive to act; this designation order creates no self-reporting-only gap.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow designation of which agencies write garnishment regulations has no systemic or precedential implications to assess.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: OPM and USPS are given non-overlapping, defined rulemaking domains (executive-branch civilians vs. Postal Service employees), avoiding jurisdictional friction.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The USPS carve-out is a clean jurisdictional split assigning rulemaking to the Postmaster General, not a discretionary exemption from coverage.

note: Narrow statutory-implementation designation order; Flag 9 is NOT_APPLICABLE as genuinely non-systemic, and the compliance-regime flags are answerable and clean because the substantive standard comes from 5 U.S.C. 5520a.

title: Revocation of Executive Order No. 5791 Withdrawing Public Lands in Colorado

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A self-executing revocation of a prior land withdrawal, effective on the filing of a resurvey plat; no decision authority is concentrated in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The action is automatic upon a defined event (filing of the resurvey plat); there is no discretionary implementation to leave unaccountable.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single act revoking one prior withdrawal order; nothing is packaged with it.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time land-status revocation creates no ongoing compliance rule for a regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral regime or incentive structure is created by a revocation.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing revocation grants no continuing authority that could require a sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No existing oversight mechanism is removed; the order simply lifts a pending-resurvey withdrawal.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance regime requiring third-party monitoring is created by a revocation.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow revocation of a single township's withdrawal has no systemic or precedential implications to assess.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No agency mandate is redirected; the order restores land status without reassigning jurisdiction.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No covered population or exemption structure exists in a land-status revocation.

note: Revokes EO 5791 (a land withdrawal; predecessor not in folder, but the revoked order is fully identified as a pending-resurvey withdrawal); self-executing land-status action, so compliance-regime and systemic flags are NOT_APPLICABLE and decision-structure flags are ABSENT.

title: Modification of Executive Order No. 8507 (Anti-Aircraft Firing Range, California)

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A self-executing modification eliminating two described sections from a War Department firing-range withdrawal; no discretionary decision authority is pooled.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The land-status change is automatic and defined; there is no discretionary implementation left unaccountable.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single act modifying one withdrawal to exclude described parcels (retaining specified access roads); nothing distinct is packaged in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time land-status modification creates no ongoing compliance rule for a regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral regime or incentive structure is created by a land modification.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing land-status modification grants no continuing authority requiring a sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No existing oversight is removed; the order narrows a prior withdrawal's footprint.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance regime requiring third-party monitoring is created.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow parcel-exclusion modification has no systemic or precedential implications to assess.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the War Department's own withdrawal is narrowed; no other agency's mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No covered population or exemption structure exists in a land-status modification.

note: Partial modification of EO 8507 (predecessor not in folder, but the modified withdrawal is fully described here as an anti-aircraft firing range); self-executing land-status change with compliance-regime and systemic flags NOT_APPLICABLE.

title: Further Amendments to Executive Order No. 12757 (Enterprise for the Americas Initiative)

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The amendment adds delegated functions to the Secretary but conditions them on acting 'in accordance with recommendations of the Council and in consultation with the Secretary of State,' distributing rather than concentrating authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary is the named delegate, acting on Council recommendations and in consultation with State; responsibility is clear.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: All amendments serve the single subject of updating the Enterprise for the Americas delegation to reflect new appropriations-act authority.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: This is a delegation/statutory-citation update; the substantive standards are set by the cited statutes, and the amendment adds no ambiguous enforcement trigger.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A technical delegation update creates no incentive to undermine the program's purpose; it rests on active appropriations and assistance statutes.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: A partial amendment updating statutory references in a standing delegation grants no new open-ended authority that would itself require a sunset; the added Sec. 6 ties references to successor statutes, keeping the delegation tethered to Congress's enactments.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No existing oversight is removed; consultation with State and reliance on Council recommendations are preserved/added.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The delegated debt-reduction/environmental-fund functions operate through a defined Council-and-consultation process; this technical amendment creates no self-reporting-only enforcement gap.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: New Sec. 6's forward-incorporation of 'substantially the same' future provisions is a routine drafting device tied to Congress's own re-enactments, not a precedent extending authority into unrelated domains.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The amendment coordinates the Secretary's role with the Council and State rather than countermanding any agency's separate statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage-and-exemption structure is created; the amendment updates delegations and statutory references.

note: Partial amendment of EO 12757 (as amended by EO 12823; predecessors not in folder, but the amended clauses are fully quoted here); diff-aware, it adds appropriations-act functions under a Council-recommendation/State-consultation constraint, strengthening rather than weakening the delegation's controls.

title: Implementing the Omnibus Trade and Competitiveness Act of 1988 and Related International Trade Matters

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order distributes numerous statutory trade functions across USTR, and the Secretaries of Commerce, State, Defense, and Treasury and interagency groups, with express presidential oversight (Sec. 5-101); authority is dispersed among many named officials, not pooled in one.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each delegated function names a specific responsible official, and Sec. 5-101 subjects all actions to presidential oversight and direction; responsibility is clearly assigned throughout.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order packages many substantively distinct matters (GATT accession, wine/steel/telecom trade, Toshiba-Kongsberg sanctions, FCPA amendments, CFIUS investment review, a superconductivity commission, Buy American provisions) into a single omnibus instrument, foreclosing targeted acceptance or correction of any one delegation.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The delegated functions and the specific sanctions (defined three-year procurement/import bans on named companies, with defined exception authorities) are precise; the standards trace to identified statutory sections rather than open-ended discretion.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The delegations and sanctions align actors with the statute's trade-enforcement purposes; no design rewards undermining that purpose, and the order rests on active trade statutes.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order implements standing trade statutes through ordinary delegation, and its time-bound elements have built-in termination (sanctions run three years; the Superconductivity Commission disbands within a year, Sec. 3-401(7)); no open-ended crisis authority lacks a needed sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order preserves presidential oversight and interagency consultation and, in the CFIUS amendments, adds dissent-reporting-to-the-President mechanisms; Sec. 5-102's regulatory-review provision channels rather than removes OMB review under EO 12291.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement runs through mandatory sanctions administered by agencies (Treasury on imports, each agency on procurement) and congressional reporting (Secs. 2-201, 2-441), not voluntary self-reporting by the regulated parties.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The CFIUS amendments (Sec. 3-201) build out a standing national-security investment-review apparatus with regulation-issuing authority and confidential-information handling, an institutional precedent whose reach extends well beyond the immediate Omnibus Trade Act implementation.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Functions are allocated to agencies within their existing trade competencies with defined coordination (interagency groups, consultation requirements); the order does not redirect appropriated funds or countermand any agency's separate statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The Toshiba-Kongsberg sanctions are subject to broad exception authority delegated to the Secretary of Defense, the Secretary of the Treasury, and every agency head over their own procurement (Sec. 2-201(3)), a diffuse, discretionary carve-out capacity over the sanctions' coverage.

note: Large omnibus trade-implementation order that partially amends EO 11858 (CFIUS; predecessor not in folder, but the amendments are fully specified here) by adding investigation/reporting authority; scored on its own text, with bundling, the CFIUS institutional precedent, and diffuse sanction-exception discretion firing while delegations remain otherwise clean.

title: Designating the Secretary of the Interior to Accept Retrocession of Certain Criminal and Civil Jurisdiction Over Indian Country

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of the Interior is empowered to exercise the retrocession-acceptance authority 'without the approval, ratification, or other action of the President or of any other officer,' concentrating a jurisdiction-acceptance power in one Secretary with the presidential check expressly removed.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary is the single named actor, and acceptance is effected only by Federal Register publication specifying the jurisdiction and effective date, keeping each action traceable.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single subject (designating the Secretary to accept retrocessions of jurisdiction); nothing distinct is packaged in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A designation to accept jurisdictional retrocessions creates no ongoing compliance rule enforced against a regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral regime or incentive for a regulated actor is created by an acceptance-designation.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The Secretary's discretionary acceptance authority is granted permanently with no review or expiration trigger; an ongoing discretionary grant of this kind would ordinarily carry reauthorization pressure and none is provided.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order expressly strips the ordinary presidential review by authorizing action 'without the approval, ratification, or other action of the President or of any other officer of the United States,' removing a pre-existing check on the exercise of this authority.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance regime requiring third-party monitoring is created by a jurisdiction-acceptance designation.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Vesting a Secretary with authority to accept transfers of criminal and civil jurisdiction over Indian country by mere Federal Register notice, free of presidential ratification, establishes a jurisdiction-reallocation precedent with consequences for tribal-state-federal authority beyond any single retrocession.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order requires consultation with the Attorney General before accepting criminal-jurisdiction retrocession (Sec., final proviso), coordinating rather than countermanding DOJ's role; no agency mandate is redirected.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No covered population or exemption structure exists in a jurisdiction-acceptance designation.

note: Self-contained delegation under 25 U.S.C. 9 and 1323(a); the express removal of presidential ratification drives the Flag 1, 7, and 9 findings, while the AG-consultation proviso and Federal Register publication keep accountability and inter-agency flags clean.

title: Amendment of Section 3 of Executive Order No. 8802 (Committee on Fair Employment Practice)

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The amendment only changes the Committee's composition to a chairman and six members; it concentrates no decision authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: A membership-size change leaves the Committee as the identified body; no failure chain is altered or left unassigned.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single-purpose amendment to committee size; nothing distinct is packaged in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Changing a committee's number of members imposes no compliance standard on any regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral regime or incentive is created by resizing a committee.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: A one-provision membership amendment grants no ongoing operational authority that could itself require a sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No existing oversight is removed by changing the number of committee members.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance regime requiring third-party monitoring is created by a membership change.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow committee-resizing amendment has no systemic or precedential implications to assess.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No agency mandate is implicated or redirected by changing committee membership.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No covered population or exemption structure exists in a committee-size amendment.

note: Partial amendment of EO 8802 Sec. 3 (as amended by EO 8823; predecessors not in folder, but the amended provision, committee composition, is fully described here); the change is a trivial membership resize, so most compliance-regime and systemic flags are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE.

title: Adjustments of Certain Rates of Pay and Allowances

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order applies fixed statutory pay schedules to defined pay systems; it exercises no discretionary authority to concentrate.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Pay adjustments are set by attached schedules under cited statutes with a defined effective date; there is no discretionary implementation to leave unaccountable.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: All provisions serve the single coherent subject of adjusting federal pay rates and allowances across statutory pay systems.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A fixed-sum pay adjustment creates no ongoing behavioral compliance rule to be enforced against a regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Setting statutory pay rates creates no compliance-regime incentive for any regulated actor to game.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: A one-time pay-rate adjustment grants no continuing operational authority that could require a sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No existing oversight is removed; the order implements statutory pay-adjustment mechanisms.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance regime requiring third-party monitoring is created by a pay adjustment.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A routine pay-rate adjustment has no systemic or precedential implications to assess.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No agency's statutory mandate is redirected; the order applies government-wide statutory pay schedules.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No covered population or exemption structure exists in a pay-rate adjustment.

note: Supersedes EO 12330 (a prior pay adjustment; predecessor not in folder, but this is the canonical narrow administrative pay-adjustment type), so compliance-regime and systemic flags are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE and the decision-structure flags are cleanly ABSENT.

title: Historically Black Colleges and Universities

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of Education coordinates a government-wide initiative but sets annual goals only 'in consultation with' OMB and other agency heads, and each agency implements its own review; authority is shared, not pooled in one official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each agency reviews its own programs, designates its own implementer, and self-reports participation levels; the same agencies that may have created barriers determine and report on their own compliance, with the Secretary's annual report to the President the only aggregation and no independent correction actor named.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose (increasing HBCU participation in federally sponsored programs); nothing distinct is packaged in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Agencies are told to 'identify, reduce, and eliminate barriers' and 'establish annual goals' with no defined standard, metric, or enforcement trigger, so two agencies could implement to substantially different levels in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Agencies are directed to increase HBCU participation and report on it; no design rewards undermining that goal, though enforcement is soft (captured under Flags 2/4).

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The initiative addresses overcoming the effects of discriminatory treatment, an evergreen equity mandate with no natural end-state; permanence is appropriate under the carve-out, and annual goal-setting and reporting provide ongoing review.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight is removed; the order adds annual goal-setting, agency liaisons, and a presidential report.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Progress depends on each agency's self-review and self-reported participation levels; while HBCU presidents may comment on implementation (Sec. 1-107(b)), no external actor is given standing and access to independently surface an agency's non-compliance.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: An internal directive to increase HBCU participation in existing programs has no identifiable precedential reach into unrelated domains.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Secretary of Education coordinates via consultation and liaisons rather than redirecting or countermanding any agency's statutory mandate; each agency acts within its own existing authorities (Sec. 1-104).

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage-and-exemption structure is created; the order directs increased participation rather than defining a regulated population with carve-outs.

note: Self-contained initiative order; the soft, self-assessed goal-setting structure fires the accountability, vague-enforcement, and third-party flags without rising to core collapse, since the coordinating-and-reporting machinery does operate.

title: Providing for the Conduct of the Mobilization Effort of the Government

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order creates a Director of Defense Mobilization who shall 'direct, control, and coordinate all mobilization activities of the Executive Branch' including production, procurement, manpower, stabilization, and transport, and may act through any officer or agency as he determines; this pools sweeping cross-government operational authority in a single official with no external check specified, defeating any diffusion of that authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Director is named and reports to the President 'from time to time,' but with authority spanning all mobilization activities and self-directed methods, the only correction path runs back through the same concentrated official and the President, leaving no independent mechanism if the effort fails.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose (establishing central direction of the mobilization effort); the breadth is the grant itself, not a packaging of unrelated matters.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Director may issue whatever 'directives, consonant with law, on policy and operations' he deems necessary and act 'in such manner as he shall determine,' an open-ended standard under which the scope and content of binding direction are essentially undefined.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order rests on the then-active Defense Production Act of 1950 and a live mobilization emergency; no lapsed statute is continued, and no design rewards undermining the mobilization purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a crisis/mobilization authority grant, exactly the kind of exceptional power that should be tied to the emergency's resolution, yet it contains no review, renewal, or expiration trigger and persists until revoked.
