flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing external oversight mechanism is identified and removed; the order centralizes direction over functions delegated by other orders but does not strip a named review process.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: This is an internal executive-direction order allocating command over mobilization activities; it establishes no regulated-party compliance regime requiring third-party monitoring.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Vesting one Director with control over 'all mobilization activities' and the power to act through any agency establishes a centralization precedent for exercising broad cross-agency operational command by executive order, usable well beyond this specific effort.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: By subjecting all agencies' Defense Production Act and mobilization functions to the Director's 'direction and control' and making his directives binding on every department (para. 4), the order overrides the ordinary independent operation of those agencies' mandates and redirects them under a single controller.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order defines no covered population or exemption structure; it allocates internal executive command over mobilization.

note: Self-contained mobilization-command order under the Defense Production Act; the sweeping single-Director control over all executive-branch mobilization drives CRITICAL Flag 1 and PRESENT findings on accountability, vague direction, missing sunset on emergency power, precedent, and cross-agency redirection, while compliance-regime flags (8, 11) are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE.

title: Planning, Acquisition, and Management of Federal Space

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The GSA Administrator is given broad authority to plan, acquire, assign, and manage federal space and to issue standards, with the only check being an affected agency head's ability to request presidential review; operative space decisions rest largely in one official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Administrator is the named responsible actor, must submit annual plans for presidential approval, and must give affected agencies written notice with a 30-day review-to-the-President path (Sec. 2(b)).

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: All provisions serve the single subject of federal space planning, acquisition, and management.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Site selection turns on open-ended policy considerations ('impact... on improving social and economic conditions,' development/redevelopment needs) that two Administrators could weigh to substantially different siting outcomes in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Agencies are directed to economize on space; no design rewards undermining the efficient-management purpose, and it rests on active statutory authority.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a standing procedural framework for routine federal-space administration, maintained through ordinary supersession (it supersedes EO 11035); an evergreen administrative function of this kind needs no reauthorization trigger.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight is removed; the order adds consultation with the Bureau of the Budget, HUD, HEW, Commerce, and state/local officials and an agency review-to-the-President path.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Affected agency heads and state/local/regional officials are given consultation and review rights (Secs. 2(a)(7), 2(b)), providing external actors with access to surface objections; no self-reporting-only enforcement gap arises.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A federal space-management framework has no identifiable precedential reach beyond property/space administration.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Administrator's authority derives from the Federal Property Act with required consultation and an agency-appeal-to-the-President safeguard; it does not countermand any agency's separate statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage-and-exemption structure is created; the order sets space-acquisition policy subject to law (Sec. 4).

note: Supersedes EO 11035 (predecessor not in folder, but this is a standing space-management framework); the predecessor to EO 12072 (order-112), it shows the same clean structure, concentrated-but-checked GSA authority with vague siting criteria.

title: Suspending Certain Statutory Provisions Relating to Employment in the Canal Zone

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A self-executing suspension of specified statutory employment provisions under express statutory authority; no discretionary decision authority is pooled in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The suspension is defined by its own terms (with an express compensation-limit proviso preserved); there is no discretionary implementation left unaccountable.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single act suspending compliance with two identified appropriations-act sections; nothing distinct is packaged in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time suspension of statutory provisions creates no ongoing behavioral compliance rule enforced against a regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing compliance-regime incentive is created by suspending employment provisions; it rests on active statutory suspension authority.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: A self-executing suspension tied to the effective dates of the named acts grants no continuing discretionary authority requiring a separate sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order suspends statutory employment requirements but preserves the compensation-limit provisions of those sections; it removes no external oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance regime requiring third-party monitoring is created by a statutory suspension.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow suspension of specific Canal Zone employment provisions under express statutory authority has no identifiable precedential reach beyond that administrative context.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No agency's statutory mandate is redirected; the order acts on statutory employment restrictions, not agency jurisdiction.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No covered population with an engineered exemption structure exists; the retained compensation-limit proviso is a defined carve-back, not a discretionary exemption.

note: Self-executing statutory suspension with an express preserved compensation-limit proviso; compliance-regime and one-time-action flags are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE and the decision-structure flags are cleanly ABSENT.

title: Extension of the Provisions of Executive Order No. 9001 to Contracts of the Immigration and Naturalization Service

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order extends EO 9001's broad war-contract powers to the Attorney General and his designees for INS detainee feeding/care contracts, authorizing them to exercise the full contracting authority (made without regard to normal contracting law) that EO 9001 vested in the War/Navy Secretaries and Maritime Commission, concentrating expansive contracting discretion in one department head.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Attorney General 'and such other officers and employees as he may designate' exercise these powers, but no reporting or correction mechanism is specified in this order, and the diffuse redelegation to unnamed designees clouds who answers for a contracting failure.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single act extending an existing contract-authority framework to one new subject (INS detainee feeding/care); nothing distinct is packaged in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Because EO 9001 authorized contracting 'without regard to' otherwise-applicable statutory contracting requirements, extending it removes the ordinary bounded contracting standard, leaving the operative constraints ('subject to the limitations and regulations contained in such Executive Order') dependent on that broad framework rather than a precise standard here.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order rests on the then-active First War Powers Act, 1941 and an ongoing war; no lapsed statute is continued, and no design rewards undermining the stated wartime-facilitation purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This extends an exceptional wartime contracting power, exactly the kind of authority that should end with the emergency, yet the extension carries no review or expiration trigger of its own.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: By incorporating EO 9001's authority to contract without regard to normal contracting-law requirements, the extension carries forward the bypass of ordinary procurement oversight into the INS context.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: War-contract authority exercised free of standard procurement checks and administered internally by the Attorney General's designees leaves no external actor with standing and access to surface improper contracting for detainee feeding/care.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Extending emergency war-contracting powers to a domestic law-enforcement/detention function (feeding and care of persons in INS custody) establishes precedent for importing exceptional wartime contracting authority into detention administration.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order grants the Attorney General the same functions for INS contracts rather than countermanding another agency's mandate; it extends, not reassigns away from, an existing framework.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order defines no covered population with an exemption structure; it extends contracting authority for a government function.

note: Full incorporation of EO 9001 for a new subject (INS detainee feeding/care), so the extending order inherits EO 9001's broad war-contract flag findings; EO 9001's own text was NOT in referenced-predecessors, so the comparison relies on this order's description and the well-established First War Powers Act contracting framework it invokes, noted as a limitation.

title: United States Advisory Council on the National Information Infrastructure

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Council is purely advisory to the Secretary of Commerce; it holds no operative decision authority to concentrate, and the Secretary merely appoints members and receives advice.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of Commerce is the named responsible official for the Council's administration and appointments; the Council only advises.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose (advising on National Information Infrastructure development); the enumerated issues are facets of that one subject.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A FACA advisory council creates no compliance standard enforced against any regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral rule or incentive for a regulated actor is created by an advisory body.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The Council exists for a defined two-year period unless its charter is extended (Sec. 4(b)); an explicit expiration mechanism is present.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight is removed; the Council operates under the Federal Advisory Committee Act with GSA guidelines and congressional reporting preserved.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance regime requiring third-party monitoring exists for an advisory council.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A time-limited advisory council that only offers recommendations has no identifiable precedential reach beyond its subject.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Council draws information from agencies as available but redirects no agency's statutory mandate; the Sec. 4(a) transfer of FACA functions to the Secretary is a routine advisory-committee administration device.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No covered population or exemption structure exists for an advisory council.

note: Clean FACA advisory council with an explicit two-year sunset; compliance-regime flags are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE because the body advises rather than regulates.

title: Providing for the Termination of the Office of War Information and Disposition of Its Functions

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: During the wind-down the Secretary of State is given discretion to transfer or terminate any function of the interim information Service and to designate its head, and the Budget Director determines what personnel/records/funds relate to each transferred function, pooling reorganization discretion in those officials.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each step names a responsible actor (Secretary of State for the interim Service, Director of the OWI for winding up abolished functions, Secretary of the Treasury for final liquidation), keeping responsibility traceable.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: All provisions serve the single coherent purpose of terminating OWI and disposing of its and certain OIAA functions.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: An agency-termination and function-disposition order creates no ongoing behavioral compliance rule enforced against a regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing compliance-regime incentive is created by a reorganization/termination; it rests on active First War Powers Act authority.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order itself is self-terminating, with hard dates abolishing the interim Service and OWI (September 15 and December 31, 1945); it grants no open-ended continuing authority that could lack a sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No external oversight mechanism is removed; the order dissolves an agency and reassigns its functions with Budget Director involvement.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No regulated-party compliance regime requiring third-party monitoring is created by an agency termination.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Terminating a wartime information office and folding residual information functions into State is a defined wind-down; the transfer of a foreign-information function to State is incidental and not shown by the text to create broader precedent.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order transfers OWI and certain OIAA functions into State, the Bureau of the Budget, and the Treasury and revokes conflicting provisions of prior orders (EO 8840, EO 9182), reassigning operational functions across agencies by executive action.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No covered population or exemption structure exists in an agency-termination order.

note: Terminates OWI and partially amends/revokes provisions of EO 8840 and EO 9182 (predecessors not in folder, but the affected paragraphs are identified here); scored on this order's own reorganization text, with wind-down discretion (Flag 1) and cross-agency function transfers (Flag 10) firing while it remains self-terminating.

title: Authorizing the Appointment of Russell Sturgis Without Regard to the Civil Service Rules

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A one-person civil-service-exception appointment authorization under express statutory authority; no ongoing decision authority is concentrated.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The action is a self-contained authorization with a defined limit (no competitive classified status conferred); there is no discretionary implementation left unaccountable.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single act authorizing one appointment; nothing distinct is packaged in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A single appointment authorization creates no ongoing behavioral compliance rule enforced against a regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing compliance-regime incentive is created by a one-person appointment.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: A one-time appointment authorization grants no continuing operational authority requiring a sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order relies on a statutory exception to the Civil Service Rules and expressly withholds competitive classified status; it removes no external oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance regime requiring third-party monitoring is created by a single appointment.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A single named-individual appointment has no systemic or precedential implications to assess.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No agency's statutory mandate is redirected by authorizing one appointment in the Wage and Hour Division.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: A single individual's civil-service exception is a defined one-off, not a coverage-wide exemption structure to assess.

note: Narrow single-person administrative appointment with a statutory basis and an express status limitation; most compliance-regime and systemic flags are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE.

title: Federal Compliance With Fuel Use Prohibitions

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Responsibility rests with each executive agency head for its own facilities, with the Secretary of Energy setting guidelines and providing advice and OMB reviewing plans; authority is distributed, not pooled in one official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each agency head is expressly made responsible for compliance (Sec. 1-101) and must submit annual conversion plans through the Secretary of Energy to OMB, keeping responsibility clear.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: All provisions serve the single subject of federal compliance with statutory fuel-use prohibitions.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The standards are supplied by the Powerplant and Industrial Fuel Use Act and Energy Department guidelines, and compliance is tracked through defined surveys, annual plans, and OMB submission; the requirements are determinate.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order requires agencies to budget for and not divert conversion funds (Secs. 1-401, 1-402); no design rewards undermining compliance, and it rests on an active statute.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a standing compliance framework implementing a permanent statute, with ongoing annual planning and OMB review; the evergreen administrative-compliance function needs no reauthorization trigger.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight is removed; the order adds Energy Department oversight, OMB plan review, and coordination with EO 12088 pollution-control compliance.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Compliance is externally checked through mandatory survey results and annual plans submitted to the Secretary of Energy and OMB, not left to unverifiable agency self-reporting alone.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: An internal federal fuel-use compliance directive has no identifiable precedential reach beyond agency energy administration.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Secretary of Energy sets guidelines and advises while agencies comply for their own facilities; no agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Sec. 1-501 provides that exemptions 'may only be granted under the provisions of' the Act, tying any carve-out to defined statutory criteria rather than discretionary agency waiver.

note: Self-contained statutory-compliance order (Powerplant and Industrial Fuel Use Act); clean architecture with distributed agency responsibility, OMB/Energy oversight, and statutorily bounded exemptions references EO 12088 without amending it.

title: Exemption of Abraham Straus From Compulsory Retirement for Age

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A one-person, one-year retirement exemption under express statutory authority; no ongoing decision authority is concentrated.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The action is a self-contained, date-limited exemption; there is no discretionary implementation left unaccountable.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single act exempting one named individual; nothing distinct is packaged in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A single retirement exemption creates no ongoing behavioral compliance rule enforced against a regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing compliance-regime incentive is created by a one-person exemption.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The exemption is expressly limited to one year (ending January 31, 1943); a defined expiration is present in the text itself.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order applies a statutory exemption to one employee; it removes no external oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance regime requiring third-party monitoring is created by a single exemption.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A single named-individual retirement exemption has no systemic or precedential implications to assess.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No agency's statutory mandate is redirected by one employee's retirement exemption.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: A single individual's statutory retirement exemption is a defined one-off, not a coverage-wide exemption structure to assess.

note: Narrow single-person administrative exemption with a statutory basis and a built-in one-year term; most compliance-regime and systemic flags are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE, and Flag 6 is ABSENT because the order carries its own expiration date.

title: Modification of Executive Order No. 6957 Withdrawing Certain Public Lands (Alaska)

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order modifies a prior withdrawal only to authorize the Secretary of the Interior to issue one coal-prospecting permit to a named individual under a specific leasing act; no broad discretionary authority is pooled.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of the Interior is the named actor acting under a defined leasing statute for one described permit; responsibility is clear.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single act modifying one withdrawal to permit a single coal-prospecting permit; nothing distinct is packaged in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time land-status modification enabling a specific permit creates no ongoing behavioral compliance rule for a regulated population.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing compliance-regime incentive is created by authorizing a single permit.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing land-status modification grants no continuing authority requiring a sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No external oversight is removed; the permit issues under the named leasing act's existing framework.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance regime requiring third-party monitoring is created by a single-permit land modification.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow one-permit modification of a single withdrawal has no systemic or precedential implications to assess.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only Interior's own withdrawal is narrowed to allow a permit; no other agency's mandate is redirected.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Authorizing one named permit is not a coverage-wide exemption structure to assess.

note: Partial modification of EO 6957 (predecessor not in folder, but the modified withdrawal, Alaska classification lands, is identified here); a self-executing single-permit land action, so compliance-regime and systemic flags are NOT_APPLICABLE.

title: Establishing an Emergency Board To Investigate a Dispute Between The Long Island Rail Road and Certain of Its Employees

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A three-member emergency board is created solely to investigate and report on a rail dispute under Section 9A of the Railway Labor Act; it holds no operative decision authority to concentrate.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Board is the named actor and must report its findings to the President within 30 days; responsibility is clear and statutorily defined.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single-purpose emergency board for one identified dispute; nothing distinct is packaged in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A fact-finding emergency board imposes no compliance standard on any regulated party; the RLA supplies the status-quo obligation.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing compliance-regime incentive is created; the statutory RLA process governs the parties, and the board only investigates.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The Board terminates upon submission of its report (Sec. 4), and the status-quo maintenance period is fixed at 120 days; explicit termination mechanisms are present.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight is removed; the order invokes the statutory RLA emergency-board process, including the conflict-of-interest bar on members.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No regulated-party compliance regime requiring third-party monitoring is established by a fact-finding board.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A statutorily mandated emergency board for a single dispute (the President 'shall appoint' upon request) has no identifiable precedential reach beyond that dispute.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No agency's statutory mandate is redirected; the board is an ad hoc statutory body.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No covered population or exemption structure exists for a fact-finding emergency board.

note: Statutorily mandated Railway Labor Act emergency board with a built-in report deadline and self-termination; compliance-regime flags are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE and the decision-structure/sunset flags are cleanly ABSENT.

title: Protection and Enhancement of the Cultural Environment

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Responsibility is distributed across all federal agency heads (for properties under their control) and the Secretary of the Interior and the independent Advisory Council on Historic Preservation; no single official holds concentrated discretionary authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each agency head has defined inventory, referral, and preservation duties with deadlines, and the Secretary of the Interior has defined review and criteria-setting duties; responsibility is clearly assigned.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: All provisions serve the single coherent subject of federal stewardship of historic and cultural properties.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Key duties turn on undefined judgment terms such as 'substantially altered,' 'questionable actions,' and referral where property is 'likely to meet the criteria,' allowing two agency heads to reach substantially different preservation decisions in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Because the agency head that wishes to sell, demolish, or alter a property is the same actor who decides whether to refer 'questionable actions' to Interior/the Council, an agency inclined to dispose of a property has a built-in incentive to under-identify it as historic to avoid triggering review.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Historic-property stewardship is an evergreen policy mandate with no natural end-state; permanence is appropriate under the carve-out, and the order builds in recurring review (annual submission of procedures, Sec. 2(e)).

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds oversight, requiring Advisory Council comment opportunities and Interior review before disposal of eligible properties; it removes no pre-existing check.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Identification of at-risk historic properties depends on the controlling agency's own referral, and while the Advisory Council may comment, no external actor is given standing and access to independently surface an agency's failure to refer a property before it is altered or demolished.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A federal historic-preservation stewardship directive furthering named preservation statutes has no identifiable precedential reach into unrelated domains.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order coordinates agency heads with Interior and the Advisory Council in defined consultative roles consistent with the cited preservation statutes; it redirects no agency's separate statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage-and-exemption structure is created; the order imposes stewardship duties rather than defining a regulated population with carve-outs.

note: Self-contained preservation-policy order; the self-referral structure and undefined judgment terms fire vague-enforcement, perverse-incentive, and third-party flags, but the added Advisory Council/Interior review keeps the accountability and oversight architecture clean.

title: Creating a Board of Inquiry To Report on Certain Labor Disputes Affecting the Maritime Industry

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A three-member Board of Inquiry is created under Section 206 of the Labor-Management Relations Act solely to inquire and report; it holds only the statutory Title II powers and no operative decision authority to concentrate.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Board is the named actor and must report to the President by a date certain under Section 206; responsibility is clear and statutorily defined.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single-purpose board of inquiry into identified maritime disputes; nothing distinct is packaged in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A fact-finding board of inquiry imposes no compliance standard on any regulated party; the Taft-Hartley statutory process governs the parties.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing compliance-regime incentive is created; the board only investigates and reports under the active LMRA.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The Board's core task ends with its report, and it continues only 'to perform such other functions as may be required under such Act, until the Board is terminated by the President'; its authority is inherently tied to the statutory national-emergency-dispute process, so no missing sunset on an open-ended grant arises.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight is removed; the order invokes the statutory Section 206 inquiry process.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No regulated-party compliance regime requiring third-party monitoring is established by a fact-finding board.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A statutory board of inquiry into a specific maritime strike, a standard step toward a possible Taft-Hartley injunction, has no identifiable precedential reach beyond that dispute.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No agency's statutory mandate is redirected; the board is an ad hoc statutory body.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No covered population or exemption structure exists for a fact-finding board of inquiry.

note: Statutory Taft-Hartley board of inquiry with a fixed report date and presidential-termination provision; compliance-regime flags are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE and the decision-structure/sunset flags are cleanly ABSENT.

title: Abolishing the Government Patents Board and Providing for the Performance of its Functions

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order transfers all functions of the Government Patents Board and its Chairman to the Secretary of Commerce, who may perform them through any officer he designates, moving a formerly board-held function into a single Secretary's hands.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of Commerce is the single named actor for the transferred functions and for disposition of property/personnel/records; responsibility is clear.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: All provisions serve the single act of abolishing the Board and reassigning its functions; nothing distinct is packaged in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: An abolition/function-transfer order creates no ongoing behavioral compliance rule enforced against a regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing compliance-regime incentive is created by abolishing a board and transferring its functions.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: A one-time abolition and function-transfer grants no new open-ended operational authority that could itself require a sunset; the transferred patent functions are a standing administrative function.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order eliminates the Board and specifically strips out its conference-and-consultation function (Sec. 2 excepts only that function from transfer, meaning it disappears), removing the collegial check that the multi-member Board and Chairman consultation had provided over patent determinations.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No regulated-party compliance regime requiring third-party monitoring is established by an abolition/transfer order.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Consolidating a patents board's functions into Commerce is a defined reorganization; the text shows no broader precedential reach beyond patent administration.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Functions of a body created under EO 10096 are transferred to Commerce with continuity of existing determinations preserved (Sec. 4); this consolidates rather than pits agencies against each other, and no statutory mandate of a separate agency is countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No covered population or exemption structure exists in an abolition/transfer order.

note: Amends EO 10096 (revoking its Secs. 3(a),(c) and abolishing the Board it created; predecessor not in folder, but the affected provisions are identified here); diff-aware, moving the function from a board to a single Secretary and dropping the consultation function drives the Flag 1 and Flag 7 findings.

title: Establishment of a Fetal Tissue Bank

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order vests the Secretary of Health and Human Services with authority to establish the tissue bank, set procedures for tissue availability, select the peer-review process, and develop fetal cell lines, concentrating program design and operation in a single Secretary with no external check specified.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary is the single named responsible actor and must report progress to the President by a date certain (Sec. 4), keeping responsibility traceable.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose (establishing and operating a human fetal tissue bank for research); nothing distinct is packaged in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Tissue is to be made available for 'meritorious research projects selected through an appropriate peer review process' and cell lines developed 'in a manner consistent with current policy,' undefined standards under which the Secretary's implementation could vary substantially.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Restricting the source to ectopic pregnancies and spontaneous abortions is designed to avoid incentivizing tissue procurement; no design rewards undermining the stated research purpose, and no lapsed statute is continued.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The tissue bank is established as an ongoing program with only a one-time progress report and no review, renewal, or expiration trigger; a newly created program authority of this kind should carry reauthorization pressure it lacks.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order directs compliance with 'all other applicable legal requirements related to fetal tissue' (Sec. 3); it removes no pre-existing oversight and layers a peer-review process on top.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Distribution runs through a peer-review process and a registry of physicians/hospitals, giving external researchers and reviewers access to the program's operation; it is a distribution program rather than a self-reported regulated-party compliance regime.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Establishing a federally operated fetal tissue bank limited to specified sources creates an institutional and definitional precedent for federal handling of fetal tissue whose framing forecloses and legitimizes particular research pathways beyond the immediate program.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only HHS is implicated; no other agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The source restriction (ectopic pregnancies and spontaneous abortions only) is a defining coverage limit on the program itself, not a discretionary exemption that lets covered actors escape a rule.

note: Self-contained program-establishment order; concentrated Secretary-level program design, undefined 'meritorious'/'appropriate peer review' standards, the missing sunset on a new program, and the fetal-tissue precedent fire, while accountability and oversight remain reasonably defined.

title: Exclusions From the Federal Labor-Management Relations Program

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The President personally makes the determinations that named DOJ subdivisions have a primary intelligence/security function and excludes them from collective-bargaining coverage under 5 U.S.C. 7103(b), a self-exercised determination with no external check identified.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The determinations are expressly set forth and attributed to the President under a specific statutory grant; responsibility for the exclusions is clear.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single subject (excluding named DEA and Marshals Service subdivisions from Chapter 71 coverage); nothing distinct is packaged in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The excluded units are enumerated by name (specific DEA and Marshals Service offices/divisions), so application is determinate rather than ambiguous.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The exclusion rests on active statutory authority (5 U.S.C. 7103(b)) and a genuine national-security predicate; no design rewards undermining a stated purpose, and no lapsed statute is continued.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a standing determination under a permanent statutory scheme, maintained through ordinary supersession (it amends the standing list in EO 12171); such exclusion determinations are revised by routine amendment, so the permanence carve-out applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order removes the named DOJ subdivisions from the Federal Labor-Management Relations Program (Chapter 71), stripping those units of the collective-bargaining and Federal Labor Relations Authority oversight structure that otherwise applied.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The order removes units from a compliance regime rather than creating one; it establishes no ongoing regulated-party rule requiring third-party monitoring.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: By re-issuing the exclusions to cure the formal defect a court found in EO 12559 (expressly setting out the determinations), the order entrenches a template for using 7103(b) national-security determinations to remove additional units from labor-relations coverage in the future.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order adjusts a list of excluded DOJ subunits under presidential statutory authority; it redirects no other agency's separate statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order's entire operation is to expand a discretionary, President-controlled exclusion architecture, carving named units out of statutory labor-management coverage based on a national-security determination the President alone makes.

note: Amends EO 12171 by replacing Sec. 1-209 (predecessor not in folder, but the amended list item and its context are fully explained here, including the AFGE v. Reagan history); diff-aware, it re-establishes and slightly restructures the DOJ exclusions, so the coverage-removal and exemption-architecture flags fire.

title: Blocking Iraqi Government Property and Prohibiting Transactions With Iraq

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of the Treasury is authorized to take 'such actions, including the promulgation of rules and regulations, as may be necessary,' to define exceptions by regulation, and to redelegate, concentrating broad discretionary implementation of the sanctions in one Secretary.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of the Treasury (in consultation with State) is the named implementing actor, and the order is transmitted to Congress under the National Emergencies Act/IEEPA reporting framework, keeping responsibility traceable.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: All prohibitions serve the single coherent purpose of blocking Iraqi property and prohibiting transactions in response to the Kuwait invasion emergency.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Core prohibitions are qualified by open-ended language ('any activity that promotes or is intended to promote' importation/exportation; any transaction whose 'purpose' is evading the order), inviting substantially different enforcement judgments about intent in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order invokes an active, unexpired national emergency (declared in EO 12722) under IEEPA and the National Emergencies Act; no lapsed statute is continued, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire, and no design rewards undermining the sanctions' purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The blocking and transaction prohibitions are emergency/exceptional-powers measures, exactly the kind of authority that should end with the emergency, yet the order sets no review or expiration trigger of its own and persists until separately terminated.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order is transmitted to Congress and operates within the IEEPA/National Emergencies Act reporting-and-review structure; it removes no pre-existing external oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Compliance rests on U.S. persons refraining from prohibited transactions with enforcement by Treasury; the order creates no external actor with standing and access to surface concealed dealings, a gap inherent in this self-compliance sanctions structure.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The sweeping blocking of an entire foreign government's property and a comprehensive transaction ban, with implementation defined by later Treasury regulation, reinforces a broad IEEPA-based precedent for executive economic control usable against other targets.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order directs all federal agencies to carry out its provisions within their authority and centralizes rulemaking in Treasury under IEEPA; it does not countermand any agency's separate statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Nearly every prohibition is qualified 'except to the extent provided in regulations that may hereafter be issued,' vesting the Secretary of the Treasury with broad, discretionary authority to define exceptions to the sanctions with no external check on those exemption grants.

note: Revokes EO 12722 only 'to the extent inconsistent' while preserving its prior administrative actions (predecessor not in folder, but this order acts under the active EO 12722 emergency); scored on its own IEEPA sanctions architecture, with concentrated Treasury discretion, intent-based prohibitions, missing emergency sunset, and broad regulatory exceptions firing but not collapsing the order's blocking purpose (Zombie Trap does not fire, active emergency).

title: To Provide a Guide on Ethical Standards to Government Officials

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order sets conduct standards applicable to top officials and directs each department/agency head to implement them internally; it concentrates no decision authority in any single official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each department and agency head is told to 'review or issue internal directives' to assure high standards, but no actor is assigned responsibility or a mechanism for detecting or correcting violations by the covered senior officials, so responsibility for enforcement failure is diffuse.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single coherent subject (ethical-conduct standards for senior officials); nothing distinct is packaged in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The standards turn on open-ended concepts, prohibiting conduct that 'may result in, or create the appearance of' use of office for private gain, loss of impartiality, or adverse effect on public confidence, with no defined enforcement trigger or consequence, so application would vary widely in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The standards discourage self-dealing and outside compensation tied to office; the incentive structure aligns with the integrity purpose rather than rewarding its undermining.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Ethical-conduct standards for officials are an evergreen policy mandate with no natural end-state; permanence is appropriate under the carve-out.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Sec. 7 expressly provides that nothing supersedes, alters, or interprets any existing law or regulation; no pre-existing oversight is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Compliance depends on each covered official's self-restraint and internal agency directives, with no external actor given standing or access to surface violations of these appearance-based standards.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A hortatory ethics guide that expressly disclaims altering existing law (Sec. 7) has no identifiable precedential reach beyond internal conduct guidance.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Each agency head implements the standards internally; no agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The Paragraph 5 carve-outs (bona fide expense reimbursement, participation in charitable/civic/political activities, meritorious-service awards) are narrow, defined exceptions, not broad discretionary exemptions that swallow the ethics rule.

note: Self-contained ethics-standards guide; the appearance-based standards, reliance on official self-restraint and internal directives, and absence of an enforcement/correction mechanism fire the vague-enforcement, accountability, and third-party flags without core collapse, and the express savings clause keeps oversight clean.

title: Authorizing the Secretary of War to Take Possession of and to Operate Certain Plants and Facilities of Montgomery Ward & Co.

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of War is authorized to seize and operate the company's plants and facilities 'in any manner that he deems essential,' to exercise the company's contractual rights, employ personnel, and 'take any other steps that he deems necessary,' pooling sweeping seizure-and-operation discretion over a private company in one official with no external check specified.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of War is the named actor, but his authority is defined almost entirely by his own judgment ('deems essential,' 'deems necessary'), and the only reporting duty is a narrow one to the President if operating income is insufficient to cover accrued wage increases, leaving no independent check on how the broad seizure power is exercised.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose (seizing and operating the identified Montgomery Ward facilities to prevent war-effort disruption); nothing distinct is packaged in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The scope of operation is bounded only by what the Secretary 'deems essential' or 'necessary,' an open-ended standard under which the extent of the government's operational and contractual actions is largely undefined.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order rests on the then-active War Labor Disputes Act and Selective Training and Service Act during an ongoing war; no lapsed statute is continued, and the seizure is aimed at enforcing, not undermining, the wartime labor-relations structure.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order contains a defined termination trigger: possession must be terminated within sixty days after the Secretary determines that prior productive efficiency has been restored (para. 4), tying the emergency power to a resolution condition.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: By authorizing the Secretary to seize and operate a private company and exercise its contractual rights 'without regard to' ordinary constraints and to displace normal corporate governance, the order bypasses the ordinary legal and market checks that governed the company's operations.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: This is a one-time government seizure and operation, not an ongoing regulated-party compliance regime requiring third-party monitoring of violations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Authorizing outright federal seizure and operation of a private retailer's nationwide facilities to enforce labor-board directives establishes a far-reaching precedent for executive takeover of private enterprise, applicable well beyond this dispute.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Other federal agencies are directed to cooperate with and assist the Secretary of War (para. 3); their mandates are enlisted in support, not redirected or countermanded into conflict.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order defines no covered regulated population with an exemption structure; it authorizes seizure of specifically identified facilities.

note: Self-contained wartime plant-seizure order; the near-unbounded 'deems essential/necessary' seizure-and-operation power in a single Secretary drives CRITICAL Flag 1 plus PRESENT accountability, vague-enforcement, oversight-bypass, and precedent findings, though the sixty-day restoration trigger keeps Flag 6 ABSENT and the active wartime statutes keep the Zombie Trap from firing.

title: Presidential Advisory Committee on Gulf War Veterans' Illnesses

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Committee is purely advisory, reporting to the President through three Secretaries and expressly barred from conducting research or advising on legal liability; it holds no operative decision authority to concentrate.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Committee reports through named Secretaries to the President on a defined schedule; the advisory role and reporting lines are clear.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single coherent subject (advising on Gulf War veterans' illnesses); the enumerated review topics are facets of that one purpose.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A FACA advisory committee creates no compliance standard enforced against any regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral rule or incentive for a regulated actor is created by an advisory committee.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The Committee must submit a final report by December 31, 1996 and terminates 30 days after submitting it (Secs. 2(f), 4(b)); explicit expiration mechanisms are present.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight is removed; the Committee operates under FACA with GSA guidelines and congressional reporting preserved (Sec. 4(a)).

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No regulated-party compliance regime requiring third-party monitoring exists for an advisory committee.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A time-limited advisory committee that only reviews and recommends, expressly not conducting research or addressing liability, has no identifiable precedential reach beyond its subject.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Committee draws information and support from DoD, VA, and HHS in defined advisory/support roles; the Sec. 4(a) transfer of FACA functions is a routine administration device that redirects no agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No covered population or exemption structure exists for an advisory committee.

note: Clean FACA advisory committee with explicit final-report deadline and self-termination; compliance-regime flags are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE because the body advises rather than regulates.

title: Continuation of Export Control Regulations

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order continues the entire Export Administration Act regulatory apparatus under presidential IEEPA authority and preserves the Secretary of Commerce's broad power to administer, amend, or revoke the export-control regulations, keeping expansive licensing/control authority centered in the executive with no external check added.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of Commerce is the named administrator of the continued regulations, and the President declares the emergency subject to IEEPA/National Emergencies Act reporting; responsibility is traceable.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single coherent purpose (continuing lapsed export-control regulations and related delegations under IEEPA); nothing distinct is packaged in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order continues an existing, detailed body of export-control regulations (15 CFR Parts 768-799) and specifies that IEEPA Sections 203(b)(2) and 206 control over inconsistent regulations; the operative standards are the established EAA rules, not left ambiguous by this order.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Section 1 continues the Export Administration Act framework 'Notwithstanding the expiration of the Export Administration Act of 1979,' exercising authority under a framework whose own statute the order acknowledges has lapsed; this is the Zombie Emergency Trap, since the executive keeps administering the export-control regime without the reauthorized statute, reducing pressure to let Congress reauthorize the EAA on its own terms.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order states it 'shall remain in effect until terminated' and expresses intent to terminate upon enactment of an EAA reauthorization, tying its continuation to a defined resolution condition (statutory reauthorization); that reauthorization linkage supplies the natural end-state.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order is transmitted within the IEEPA/National Emergencies Act framework and continues existing regulatory oversight; it removes no pre-existing external check.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The continued EAA regime includes established administrative sanctions and a licensing/enforcement apparatus administered by Commerce; this continuation order creates no new self-reporting-only enforcement gap.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Using IEEPA to continue an entire lapsed statutory regulatory regime by declaring the lapse itself an emergency establishes a significant precedent for indefinitely sustaining expired statutory frameworks through emergency authority, reusable whenever other authorizing statutes lapse.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order incorporates existing delegations (EO 12002, 12214, 12131 as amended) and continues Commerce's administration; it redirects no other agency's separate statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order continues an existing licensing/control regime rather than creating a new coverage-and-exemption structure; its licensing exceptions are the established EAA regulatory framework, not a broad new discretionary carve-out.

note: Full incorporation of the Export Administration Act framework and delegations in EO 12002/12214/12131 (as amended by EO 12551) notwithstanding the EAA's expiration (predecessors not in folder, but the delegations are described here); the acknowledged statutory lapse plus continued authority fires the Zombie Emergency Trap under Flag 5, while the express reauthorization-linked termination intent keeps Flag 6 ABSENT.

title: Designation of Under Secretary and Assistant Secretaries of the Interior to Act as Secretary of the Interior

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order designates a defined succession order (Under Secretary, then named Assistant Secretaries) to act during the Secretary's absence or sickness; it distributes acting authority by a clear rule rather than concentrating it.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The acting officials are specifically identified in a defined order of succession; responsibility during the Secretary's absence is clear.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single subject (order of acting succession for the Secretary of the Interior); nothing distinct is packaged in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: An acting-succession designation creates no ongoing behavioral compliance rule enforced against a regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing compliance-regime incentive is created by an internal succession designation.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: A standing acting-succession designation grants no operational program authority that could require a sunset; it merely names who acts during absences, revised through ordinary supersession.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No external oversight is removed; the designation operates under the cited succession statute (R.S. 179).

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No regulated-party compliance regime requiring third-party monitoring is created by a succession designation.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow acting-succession designation has no systemic or precedential implications to assess.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only internal Interior officials are designated; no other agency's statutory mandate is redirected.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No covered population or exemption structure exists in a succession designation.

note: Supersedes EO 7277 (a prior Interior succession designation; predecessor not in folder, but this is a narrow acting-succession housekeeping order); compliance-regime and systemic flags are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE and the decision-structure flags are cleanly ABSENT.

title: Establishing the Necedah Migratory Waterfowl Refuge (Wisconsin)

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A self-executing land reservation (~40,500 acres) set aside for Agriculture as a wildlife refuge under statutory authority; no discretionary decision authority is pooled in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Department of Agriculture is the named administering body, with rulemaking for the refuge assigned to the Secretary of Agriculture; responsibility is clear.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single act of reserving described lands as a refuge (and correspondingly excluding them from a prior withdrawal); nothing distinct is packaged in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time land set-aside creates no ongoing behavioral compliance regime; the hunting/trespass prohibition is enforced under the Secretary's existing refuge regulations, not a new ambiguous standard.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing regulated-party incentive structure is created by establishing a refuge.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing land reservation grants no continuing operational authority that could require a sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No external oversight is removed; the order sets aside land and correspondingly amends EO 6964 only to exclude these lands from a prior classification withdrawal.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing regulated-party compliance regime requiring third-party monitoring is established by a land reservation.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A single-refuge land reservation has no systemic or precedential implications to assess.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The reservation consolidates acquired and intermingled public lands for Agriculture and merely excludes them from a prior general withdrawal (EO 6964); no agency's statutory mandate is redirected into conflict.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No covered regulated population with an exemption structure exists; the refuge's hunting rules are administered under the Secretary's standard authority.

note: Self-executing land reservation that partially amends EO 6964 only to exclude these lands (and references the EO 7908 title transfer; predecessors not in folder, but the affected provisions are identified here); compliance-regime and systemic flags are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE and decision-structure flags are ABSENT.

title: Central Intelligence Agency Retirement and Disability System

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Director of Central Intelligence maintains the retirement system and fund and is given 'sole discretion' to determine appropriate allotments/assignments of annuities and to prescribe implementing regulations, pooling administration of the CIA retirement fund in one official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Director is the single named administrator operating under the detailed statutory principles of the CIA Retirement Act of 1964; responsibility is clear.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: All provisions serve the single subject of conforming and administering the CIA retirement and disability system; nothing distinct is packaged in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order sets precise administrative rules (anti-assignment protections, application deadlines tied to the 115th birthday and 30-year limits, deposit requirements) with determinate standards; only the annuitant's own voluntary allotments are left to the Director's discretion.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The rules govern orderly administration of retirement benefits under an active statute; no design rewards undermining the system's purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: A retirement and disability system is a standing, evergreen administrative structure with its own statutory accountability; permanence is appropriate under the carve-out, and it is maintained through ordinary statutory amendment.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order conforms the CIA and Civil Service retirement systems and preserves statutory constraints (e.g., anti-assignment except as Federal law provides); it removes no pre-existing external oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Benefit administration operates through a statutory application, deduction, and Treasury-deposit framework with defined deadlines; this is a benefits-administration scheme, not a self-reported regulated-party compliance regime lacking third-party checks.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Conforming the CIA retirement system to civil-service rules is a defined administrative alignment with no identifiable precedential reach beyond that benefits system.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order aligns the CIA system with the Civil Service Retirement and Disability System rather than redirecting or countermanding another agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage-wide exemption structure is created; the discretion is limited to an individual annuitant's own allotment choices, not carve-outs from the system's coverage.

note: Self-contained statutory-conforming order under the CIA Retirement Act of 1964; only the DCI's concentrated administration of the fund fires (Flag 1), with the otherwise precise benefits-administration rules keeping every other flag clean.

title: Transfer of Jurisdiction Over Certain Lands Containing Oil and Gas Deposits From the War Department to the Department of the Interior

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A self-executing transfer of oil-and-gas jurisdiction over described parcels from the War Department to Interior, subject to War's primary flood-control/navigation jurisdiction; no broad discretionary authority is pooled in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of the Interior is directed to take necessary protective action, and royalties are directed to the Treasury as miscellaneous receipts; responsibility is clearly assigned.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Single act transferring oil-and-gas jurisdiction over the described lands; the detailed parcel descriptions are the subject, not distinct packaged matters.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time jurisdictional transfer of land creates no ongoing behavioral compliance rule enforced against a regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing compliance-regime incentive is created by a jurisdictional transfer of land.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing jurisdictional transfer grants no continuing operational program authority that could require a sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No external oversight is removed; the order preserves the War Department's primary jurisdiction and routes royalties to the Treasury.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing regulated-party compliance regime requiring third-party monitoring is established by a jurisdictional transfer.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow transfer of oil-and-gas jurisdiction over specific parcels to prevent drainage loss has no systemic or precedential implications to assess.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The transfer to Interior is expressly made 'subject to the primary jurisdiction of the War Department' for flood-control and navigation (para. 3), coordinating the two agencies' roles rather than countermanding War's mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No covered regulated population or exemption structure exists in a jurisdictional land transfer.

note: Self-executing transfer of oil-and-gas jurisdiction over described parcels with War's primary jurisdiction expressly preserved; compliance-regime and systemic flags are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE and the decision-structure/inter-agency flags are cleanly ABSENT.

title: Assigning Authority to Order Certain Persons in the Ready Reserve to Active Duty

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The President's statutory authority to order Ready Reserve members to active duty is delegated to the Secretary of Defense with power to redesignate to the service Secretaries, concentrating an individual-liberty-affecting call-up decision in a single official with no external check named in the order.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility for call-up decisions is clearly placed on the Secretary of Defense and designated service Secretaries; the failure chain is traceable to a named actor.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order addresses a single subject (activating a defined class of non-participating reservists) and bundles no distinct policy questions or problem frames.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The trigger is precisely defined by three cumulative criteria (Sec. 1: not participating, unfulfilled obligation, under 24 months served) and the consequence (service to 24 months, Sec. 2) is exact, leaving little room for divergent good-faith application.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order relies on an active, unexpired appropriation-act authority (Title I of the 1967 DoD Appropriation Act) with an internal June 30, 1968 cutoff; no lapsed statute is being continued and no design feature rewards undermining the purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The delegated authority is expressly time-limited to 'until June 30, 1968' (Sec. 1), providing exactly the reauthorization pressure the flag looks for on a discretionary call-up power.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism; it merely delegates a statutory call-up authority and adds fairness-consideration requirements (Sec. 3).

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Reservists ordered to duty are directly affected parties with obvious incentive and standing to contest an erroneous call-up, so enforcement does not depend solely on agency self-reporting.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order applies a narrow, statute-bounded call-up authority to a defined reservist class with a fixed expiration; no precedent usable in unrelated contexts or definitional import from other domains is identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Authority runs within the Department of Defense chain (SecDef to service Secretaries); no second agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Rather than carve-outs that swallow the rule, Sec. 3 directs 'appropriate consideration' of family responsibilities and essential employment, which narrows hardship-based coverage under bounded criteria rather than creating broad discretionary exemptions.

note: Clean, tightly-bounded delegation of an active statutory authority with an explicit built-in sunset date; only Flag 1 fires for pooling the call-up decision in the SecDef.

title: Continuing the Regulation of Exports

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order continues and re-vests broad export-control administration in the Secretary of Commerce under section 5(b) of the 1917 Act (Secs. 1-3), pooling discretionary export-license authority in a single cabinet official; per the incorporated EO-11533 framework, referral to the Export Administration Review Board is initiated only by the Secretary himself.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Administration is clearly assigned to the Secretary of Commerce, who reports to the President under the incorporated EO-11533 (Sec. 4); the responsible actor is named.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order bundles three distinct rationales under one continuance frame -- national-security export vigilance, foreign-policy furtherance, and protecting the domestic economy from 'excessive drain of scarce materials' and inflation (preamble) -- combining separable policy purposes that would not naturally travel together, obscuring which justifies the continued controls.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The continued regime rests on the open-ended section 5(b) emergency authority to control exports 'from the standpoint of their significance to the national security,' a standard broad enough that two administering officials could reach substantially different license decisions in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Sec. 1 expressly continues export-control administration 'Notwithstanding the expiration of the Export Administration Act of 1969' by substituting the never-expiring section 5(b) of the 1917 Act, the textbook Zombie Emergency Trap: the order acknowledges the authorizing statute lapsed yet exercises its framework without it, removing any pressure to let the EAA be reauthorized on its own terms and defeating the ordinary check that statutory expiration is meant to impose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The continued controls rest on standing national-emergency declarations (Proclamations 2914 and 4074) and section 5(b), with no expiration or reauthorization trigger -- an emergency-derived authority that should have a natural end tied to the emergency but continues indefinitely 'until amended or revoked' (Secs. 2-3).

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order continues an existing administrative structure and removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism; it caps a fine and narrows a civil penalty (Sec. 2) but does not disable judicial or congressional review that previously applied.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Export-control enforcement runs through Commerce licensing of regulated exporters with no external actor given standing or access to surface violations; compliance depends on the regulated exporters and the agency, with no third-party mechanism in the text.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: By treating section 5(b) of the Trading with the Enemy Act as a standing substitute for lapsed trade legislation, the order establishes precedent that a permanent emergency statute can indefinitely backfill any expired regulatory framework, an authority claim reusable well beyond export controls.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order preserves existing allocations of authority (Sec. 4 expressly does not affect other agencies' section 5(b) delegations) and continues the established Commerce/State/Defense review structure without redirecting any agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The regime continues export-license discretion concentrated in the Secretary of Commerce, whose validated-license power under the incorporated framework functions as an unbounded case-by-case exemption authority over who may export controlled items, with no defined external check on grants.

note: Full incorporation of the EO-11533 export-administration framework (predecessor text available; the EO-11907 amendment itself was not in the folder but is not needed to code the continuance). The 'notwithstanding the expiration' language squarely fires the Zombie Emergency Trap, elevating Flag 5 to CRITICAL.

title: American Defense Service Medal

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Authority to prescribe award regulations is distributed across three officials (Secretaries of War, Navy, and Treasury) rather than pooled in one; the decision structure is answerable and clean.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility for award regulations rests with the three named service Secretaries; the accountable actors are identifiable.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order does a single thing -- establish one service medal and delegate its award regulations -- with no distinct policy questions packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Establishing a decoration creates no ongoing behavioral rule anyone must comply with, so there is no enforcement mechanism to assess for vagueness.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A discretionary medal award creates no ongoing regulatory or compliance relationship whose incentives could be perverse; the emergency proclamations invoked are the qualifying period, not a lapsed-statute continuation.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The medal is tied to defined proclaimed-emergency service periods, and a permanent standing decoration honoring past service is evergreen by nature with no natural end-state requiring reauthorization pressure.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism; it creates a new award and delegates its regulation.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
