flags.justification: There is no compliance regime whose violations a third party would need standing to surface; the order only authorizes discretionary recognition.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Establishing a service medal is a narrow administrative honor with no systemic or precedential implications identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The three named Secretaries each prescribe regulations for their own service branches; no agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded, so coordination is clean rather than conflicting.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: There is no coverage population subject to a rule from which anyone could be exempted; the order confers eligibility rather than imposing an obligation.

note: Narrow administrative honor order; compliance-regime flags (4, 5, 8, 11) and systemic Flag 9 are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE, while the always-answerable decision-structure flags (1, 2, 7) plus Bundling and Inter-Agency (10) score cleanly ABSENT.

title: Export of Banned or Significantly Restricted Substances

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Authority is deliberately distributed: each regulating agency lists its own substances (1-102), State runs notifications (1-2), a multi-agency task force advises (1-304), Commerce grants licenses only after mandatory consultation (1-306), and unresolved State/Commerce disputes are referred to the President (1-303) -- no single actor holds unchecked authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each function has a named responsible actor -- regulating agencies for listing, State for notification and distribution, Commerce for licensing, the Regulatory Council for the annual report (1-401) -- with a defined evaluation report to the President (1-601).

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order coherently serves one purpose (responsible handling of banned/restricted substance exports) through complementary notification, control, and reporting mechanisms, each independently justified by that purpose.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Whether an extremely hazardous substance warrants export controls turns on undefined qualitative judgments -- 'substantial threat to human health,' 'clear and significant harm to the foreign policy interests,' export licenses granted 'only in exceptional cases' (1-301) -- on which two officials could in good faith reach different determinations.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order states its intent to 'resort to the imposition of export controls only in a very few instances' (1-301), creating an institutional incentive for the responsible agency to under-list candidates and keep substances off the Commodity Control List, blunting the order's own protective purpose; no lapsed statute is continued so the Zombie sub-flag does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order builds in recurring reauthorization-style pressure through mandatory annual reports (1-403), an 18-month-and-annual effectiveness evaluation submitted to the President with recommendations (1-601), and a standing revision duty on agency lists (1-102).

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds oversight (annual Federal Register reporting, interagency consultation, effectiveness evaluation) and expressly preserves existing statutory procedures including required industry consultations (1-307); it removes no pre-existing check.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement depends on agency self-reporting and inter-agency notification with no external actor given standing or access to surface non-compliant exports; the recipient foreign governments receive notice but the order gives no domestic third party a mechanism to detect unreported exports.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order imports a sprawling cross-statutory definition of 'banned or significantly restricted substance' (1-101, drawing on FDCA, TSCA, FIFRA, CPSA and more) into export-notification law, establishing a definitional framework and notification precedent extendable to other export-regulation contexts beyond the stated substances.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order layers coordination on top of each agency's existing statutory role -- State concurrence subject to Commerce (1-303), Commerce acting under its Export Administration Act mandate -- with a defined presidential tie-breaker rather than redirecting or countermanding any agency's statutory authority.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order builds in categorical carve-outs from export controls -- specified subsections excluded outright (1-301) and a broad 'medicine or medical supplies' exclusion carried from the Export Administration Act -- and the policy default is non-control, so exemption rather than coverage is the norm.

note: Unusually well-architected order with distributed authority, mandatory consultation, and layered reporting/evaluation, which clears the decision-structure and oversight flags; weaknesses cluster in vague hazard thresholds, an explicit under-enforcement intent, and a control-averse exemption default.

title: Establishing the President's Committee on Civil Rights

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order creates a fifteen-member advisory committee with only study-and-recommend authority (paras. 2, 5); no operative decision authority is pooled in any single official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Committee is a named body charged to report to the President in writing (para. 5); its limited responsibility -- to inquire and recommend -- is clearly located and traceable.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order has a single object -- creating a committee to study strengthening civil-rights protections -- and bundles no distinct policy remedies.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: An advisory study committee that only inquires and recommends imposes no behavioral rule to enforce, so there is no enforcement mechanism to assess for vagueness.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A study-and-report committee creates no ongoing compliance relationship whose incentives could be perverse; no statute is being continued past a lapse.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The Committee has an explicit self-termination trigger -- 'Upon rendition of its report to the President, the Committee shall cease to exist' (para. 6) -- which is the built-in expiration mechanism the flag rewards.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order removes no existing oversight; it creates a new advisory body and directs agencies to cooperate with it.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no compliance regime with violations for a third party to surface; the Committee merely studies and recommends.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order's effect is confined to producing a report and recommendations to the President; it creates no operative authority or definitional framework with identifiable downstream reuse in unrelated contexts.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Executive agencies are directed to cooperate with and furnish information to the Committee (paras. 3-4), a coordination duty that does not redirect or countermand any agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: There is no regulated population subject to coverage from which exemptions could be carved; the order imposes no obligation on outside parties.

note: Clean advisory-committee order with an explicit self-termination clause; compliance-regime flags (4, 5, 8) and Flag 11 are NOT_APPLICABLE because the body only studies and recommends, while all decision-structure flags score ABSENT.

title: Foreign Intelligence Electronic Surveillance

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order authorizes the Attorney General alone to approve warrantless electronic surveillance for foreign intelligence without a court order (1-101), pooling a significant surveillance-approval power in a single official, checked only by the statutory certification the AG himself makes.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The order names the Attorney General as responsible for approvals and enumerates the specific certifying officials (1-103); responsibility is clearly located, and each certifier must be Senate-confirmed.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order coherently implements one subject -- FISA authorization mechanics -- through delegation and certifier designation, with no distinct policy questions packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order channels all authority through the detailed statutory standards of FISA (Secs. 102 and 104), expressly conditioning warrantless approval on 'the certifications required by that Section' (1-101), so the operative standard is the statute's defined one rather than an open-ended one.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order implements an active, current statute (FISA 1978) and adds a requirement that surveillance comply with FISA as well as EO 12036 (1-104, 1-105); no design feature rewards undermining the purpose and no lapsed statute is continued.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The delegation of warrantless-surveillance approval authority is a standing grant with no expiration or reauthorization trigger; surveillance powers of this kind have a natural end-state (the statutory framework's own reauthorization pressure) that this permanent delegation does not track.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds oversight rather than removing it -- amending EO 12036 (1-104, 1-105) to require that any electronic surveillance be conducted in accordance with FISA and its court-order regime -- so the before/after direction is toward more, not less, external check.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: For the warrantless-surveillance track (1-101) there is no external actor with standing or access to surface an improper approval; oversight depends on the AG's own certification and executive self-policing, with surveillance targets typically unaware, so no third party can detect non-compliance.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: By operationalizing an executive channel for warrantless foreign-intelligence surveillance approved by the Attorney General, the order entrenches a surveillance-authorization precedent and definitional framework reusable to expand executive electronic-surveillance authority in adjacent national-security contexts.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order designates certifying officials across State, Defense, CIA, and FBI within their existing national-security roles and amends EO 12036 to harmonize with FISA; it redirects no agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order defines who may approve and certify surveillance rather than carving classes out of coverage; per the Flag 4/11 distinction, its warrantless track is a coverage-scope question governed by FISA's own criteria, not a discretionary exemption swallowing a rule.

note: Amends EO 12036 (Secs. 1-104, 1-105); the predecessor EO-12036 text was not in the referenced-predecessors folder, so the amendment was assessed as an added FISA-compliance requirement (new oversight, not removed) from the amending order's own text. The AG's warrantless-approval authority drives Flags 1, 8, and 9.

title: Authorizing the War Department, the Navy Department, and the United States Maritime Commission to Perform the Functions and Exercise the Powers Described in Title II of an Act Approved December 18, 1941

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order vests broad, redelegable war-contracting authority in the Secretaries of War and Navy and the Maritime Commission -- to contract 'without regard to the provisions of law relating to the making, performance, amendment, or modification of contracts' and to settle claims 'in the judgment of' each department (Title I) -- concentrating sweeping discretionary procurement power with power of successive redelegation.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The order names the responsible officials (the two Secretaries and the Maritime Commission) and imposes concrete duties and reporting on them (Title II), so responsibility for procurement decisions is traceable to identified actors.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order coherently implements one statutory purpose -- expediting war-effort procurement -- and its provisions (contracting powers plus protective regulations) are each justified by that single purpose.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Core contracting decisions turn on undefined discretionary standards -- amendments and settlements 'whenever, in the judgment of' the department 'the prosecution of the war is thereby facilitated,' contracts for anything 'necessary, appropriate, or convenient' 'without any restriction of any kind' (Title I, paras. 2-3) -- on which officials could reach widely divergent good-faith decisions.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Waiving advertising, competitive bidding, and bonds (Title I, para. 4) and permitting amendments 'with or without consideration' rewards non-arm's-length contracting and creates incentives for waste or favoritism inconsistent with efficient procurement, though anti-abuse guardrails (Title II bars on cost-plus-percentage and fee caps) partly counter it; the wartime statute is active, so the Zombie sub-flag does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order grants sweeping emergency war-procurement authority with no expiration or reauthorization trigger; this is exactly the crisis-response power that should have a natural end tied to the war's resolution, and its absence is a real design gap under the permanence carve-out (contrast the evergreen cases).

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order affirmatively strips pre-existing external checks on procurement, authorizing contracting 'without regard to the provisions of law relating to the making, performance, amendment, or modification of contracts' and dispensing with advertising and competitive bidding (Title I), disabling the ordinary competitive-procurement oversight regime.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: With competitive bidding waived, there are no losing bidders or external competitors with standing and incentive to surface improper contracting; enforcement of the Title II protective terms depends on department self-scrutiny (e.g., advance payments made 'only after careful scrutiny' by the departments themselves, Title II para. 4).

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order establishes a broad precedent that statutory procurement-law safeguards can be set aside wholesale by executive order under a war-effort rationale, an authority claim and framework readily extendable to other emergency-procurement contexts beyond this Act.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order authorizes War, Navy, and the Maritime Commission to act in coordination with OPM within their own procurement domains; it does not redirect or countermand another agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order exempts the covered war contracts wholesale from the normal body of federal contracting law (Title I, paras. 1-4), a categorical carve-out broad enough to displace the default procurement rules for the entire covered class, controlled by the contracting departments' own discretion.

note: Broad WWII war-procurement authority that waives normal contracting law wholesale; multiple flags fire but none rises to CRITICAL because Title II's protective regulations (fee caps, anti-cost-plus-percentage, Walsh-Healey and Davis-Bacon preservation, quarterly reporting, non-discrimination) meaningfully constrain the grant, so its stated purpose is not defeated by any single design collapse.

title: Revocation of Executive Order No. 9165, Providing for the Protection of Essential Facilities from Sabotage and Other Destructive Acts

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order simply revokes EO 9165 and concentrates no authority; if anything it dissolves the Office of Civilian Defense facility-security coordination role rather than pooling power in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The order preserves the War Department's continuing facility-security functions and permits necessary cooperative arrangements, so the responsible actor after revocation (the War Department) remains identifiable.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order does one thing -- revoke a prior order with a narrow savings clause -- and bundles no distinct policy questions.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A revocation that ends a coordination program imposes no new behavioral rule to enforce, so there is no enforcement mechanism to assess for vagueness.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Revoking a prior order creates no ongoing compliance relationship whose incentives could be perverse; no lapsed statute is continued.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order grants no ongoing authority that could be sunset; it is a one-time, self-executing revocation.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Per the diff against EO 9165, the revoked order was itself a coordination/inspection program, not an external oversight check on other bodies; ending it removes a facility-security coordination function rather than any pre-existing oversight mechanism, and the FBI's investigative role that EO 9165 preserved is untouched.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: With the program revoked and no new compliance regime established, there is no ongoing set of violations for a third party to surface.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The revocation expressly does not terminate the War Department's facility-security functions or preclude cooperative arrangements, so it produces no meaningful downstream gap or precedent extendable to unrelated contexts.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: By dissolving the OCD-led multi-agency facility-security program while preserving War Department functions, the order removes a coordination structure rather than redirecting or countermanding any agency's statutory mandate; the savings clause avoids leaving conflicting parallel authority.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order establishes no coverage population from which exemptions could be carved; it terminates a program.

note: Diff-aware coding against EO-9165 (predecessor text available): the revoked order was an inter-agency facility-security coordination program with an express FBI-investigation savings clause, and the revocation carries its own savings clause preserving War Department functions, so no oversight is stripped and the compliance-regime flags are NOT_APPLICABLE for a clean terminating order.

title: Excusing From Duty on Wednesday, September 23, 1936, Until 1 p.m. Employees of the Executive Departments, Establishments and Other Agencies of the Federal Government in the District of Columbia

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order is a self-executing one-time leave grant excusing DC federal employees until 1 p.m. on a specific date; it concentrates no decision authority in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The order is self-executing with no implementation on which anyone could fail; there is no failure chain to trace, so no accountability gap exists.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order does a single thing -- a one-day partial leave -- and bundles nothing.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time leave excusal imposes no ongoing behavioral rule to enforce, so there is no enforcement mechanism to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A single-day scheduling action creates no ongoing compliance relationship whose incentives could be perverse; no statute is continued past a lapse.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order grants no ongoing authority to sunset; it is a one-time, self-executing action expiring by its own terms at 1 p.m. that day.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism; it merely excuses employees for part of one day.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no compliance regime with violations for a third party to surface; it is a one-time leave grant.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A single-day partial leave excusal is a trivial administrative action with no systemic or precedential implications identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order applies uniformly to DC federal employees without redirecting any agency's statutory mandate; no second agency's authority is implicated.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order confers a benefit (time off) rather than imposing an obligation; the 'special public reasons' and 'existing law' carve-outs narrow the benefit, not coverage of a rule, so there is no exemption structure to swallow a rule.

note: Trivial one-day partial-leave order; only the always-answerable decision-structure flags (1, 2, 7) plus Bundling are scored (all ABSENT), and every compliance-regime and systemic flag is NOT_APPLICABLE. Near the administrative-only boundary but not fully so.

title: Defining Additional Functions and Duties of the War Production Board

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order pools sweeping war-production authority in the WPB Chairman -- the former powers of OPM, the Supply Priorities and Allocations Board, and the President's Sec. 120 National Defense Act authority (para. 1) -- exercisable 'through such officials or agencies and in such manner as he may determine; and his decisions shall be final' (para. 3), an explicitly unreviewable one-man authority that is the order's core design; per the diff against EO 9024 it extends the already-final Chairman authority to further absorbed functions.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: While the Chairman is a named actor, his decisions being 'final' (para. 3) with no correction mechanism means that when production direction fails there is no actor with both obligation and capacity to correct him short of the President, leaving a real accountability gap around the concentrated authority.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order coherently consolidates war-production functions under one board and abolishes the superseded OPM; its provisions serve the single stated purpose of effective war procurement and production.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Chairman's directive authority over the 'policies, plans, procedures, and methods' of all federal agencies for war procurement (via the incorporated EO 9024 para. 2) is defined by no bounded standard, and his final unreviewable discretion means two occupants could direct compliance very differently in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order consolidates active wartime production authority under an active war declaration; no design feature identifiably rewards undermining the stated purpose, and no lapsed statute is being continued so the Zombie sub-flag does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order grants sweeping emergency war-production authority with no expiration or reauthorization trigger; this crisis-response power has a natural end tied to the war's resolution, and the absence of any sunset is a real design gap under the permanence carve-out.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Making the Chairman's decisions 'final' (para. 3) forecloses ordinary administrative review of his war-production directives, and the order absorbs and abolishes OPM (para. 4), eliminating a pre-existing body whose separate direction had previously supplied a check within the production apparatus.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Agency compliance with the Chairman's directives (EO 9024 para. 3) is enforced through the war-production hierarchy itself, with no external actor given standing or access to surface non-compliance with the Chairman's final and unreviewable decisions.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Concentrating cross-agency directive authority in a single Chairman whose decisions are 'final' establishes a precedent for a wartime production czar wielding unreviewable authority over all federal agencies' procurement, a framework extendable to further executive centralization of the war economy beyond the immediate consolidation.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order abolishes the Office of Production Management and transfers its personnel, records, property, and funds to the WPB (para. 4), and empowers the Chairman to override the policies and methods of every federal department for war production (via incorporated EO 9024), redirecting existing agencies' operational authority into the WPB.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order establishes no exemption or waiver structure carving actors out of coverage; it consolidates directive authority rather than exempting anyone from a rule.

note: Diff-aware/full-incorporation coding against EO-9024 and EO-8942 (both predecessor texts available): the order extends and absorbs the already-final WPB Chairman authority and abolishes OPM. The 'decisions shall be final' one-man war-production authority is a core design collapse of external check, firing Flag 1 CRITICAL; no Zombie trap since the wartime authority is active.

title: Rules Governing the Granting and Issuing of Passports in the United States

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Para. 124 vests the Secretary of State with discretionary authority to 'refuse to issue a passport,' 'restrict,' 'withdraw or cancel a passport already issued' with no stated criteria and no external check, and numerous provisions (e.g., paras. 22, 31, 34, 114, 126) let the Secretary demand 'such further information as he may deem necessary' — decision authority over eligibility pools in a single official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The order provides no mechanism to challenge or correct a wrongful denial, restriction, or cancellation under para. 124/125; the Secretary of State both administers and adjudicates issuance with no named actor responsible for correcting an erroneous refusal.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order addresses a single coherent subject — the rules for granting and issuing passports — and does not package substantively distinct policy questions or conflate multiple problem frames under one stated purpose.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Para. 124 permits the Secretary to refuse or restrict passports 'in his discretion' and para. 125 withdraws protection for use 'in violation of the conditions or restrictions' with no defined standard, so two officials could in good faith reach different denial/withdrawal decisions on the same facts.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The fee structure and evidentiary requirements are ordinary administrative mechanics that align applicant incentives with truthful documentation; no design feature rewards conduct that undermines the order's purpose, and no lapsed statute is invoked, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a standing procedural framework for a permanent administrative function (passport issuance) that is maintained through ordinary supersession — para. 127 explicitly supersedes EO 5860 as amended by EO 6650 — evidencing revision by routine amendment rather than a discretionary emergency grant, so the permanence carve-out applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order supersedes a prior passport-rules order (EO 5860) with a comparably detailed framework and does not remove any pre-existing judicial, congressional, or administrative review mechanism that applied to passport issuance.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement of eligibility rests on applicant self-declaration plus supporting-witness affidavits, but no external actor has both standing and incentive to surface a wrongful denial or an improperly issued passport — the Secretary's discretionary determinations under para. 124 are checked by no third party.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The undefined discretionary power in para. 124 to refuse or restrict passports establishes a precedent for using passport control as a tool to limit citizens' foreign travel for reasons untethered to citizenship or documentation, a downstream capability beyond the order's stated administrative purpose.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order operates within the State Department's own statutory passport authority and does not redirect, override, or duplicate another agency's statutory mandate; incidental references to War Department verification (para. 99) are cooperative, not conflicting.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The fee exemptions (Part IX, paras. 91-99) are narrow, specifically defined categories (federal officers on duty, seamen, grave-visiting relatives) that do not swallow the coverage rule; they exempt only from the fee, not from the underlying eligibility and documentation requirements.

note: A comprehensive standing passport framework superseding EO 5860 (as amended by EO 6650), both of which were available; the diff shows a detailed predecessor replaced by an equally detailed successor, so Flags 6 and 7 clear, but the retained broad discretionary denial/restriction power (para. 124) fires the concentration/accountability/vagueness cluster.

title: Excluding Land From Sitgreaves National Forest and Reserving it for Townsite Purposes

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order is a one-time, self-executing land-status change (exclusion from the forest and reservation for townsite purposes); no ongoing decision authority is concentrated in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The action is complete on issuance with a defined metes-and-bounds description; there is no implementation that could fail and thus no unassigned responsibility.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order addresses a single tract and a single purpose (forest exclusion plus townsite reservation of the same 3.35 acres), with nothing distinct bundled against it.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A self-executing land-status revocation creates no ongoing compliance rule for any actor, so there is no enforcement mechanism to assess for vagueness.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order creates no ongoing regulatory relationship or behavioral incentive for any actor class; no statute is invoked past its lapse, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing land reservation grants no ongoing authority that could be subject to reauthorization pressure, so there is nothing to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order is issued under standing statutory land authority and removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no ongoing enforcement chain — the land status changes definitionally on issuance — so no third-party surfacing role applies.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: This is a narrow administrative land action with no systemic or precedential implications beyond the specific 3.35-acre tract.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Acting on the Secretary of Agriculture's recommendation within existing forest-management authority, the order redirects no other agency's statutory mandate; the transfer of the tract to townsite status is a routine coordinated action.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order has no coverage population or exemption structure — it is a single land-status change — so there is nothing to exempt from.

note: A narrow, self-executing land-exclusion and townsite-reservation order; the compliance-regime flags (4, 5, 8, 11) and the narrow-order flags (6, 9) are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE because no ongoing regulatory relationship or authority grant exists, while the always-answerable decision-structure flags (1, 2, 7) score ABSENT.

title: Suspension of Eight-hour Law as to Work by the Alaska Railroad, Department of the Interior

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order delegates to the Secretary of the Interior sole authority to determine, without external check, which work the eight-hour suspension applies to — 'with respect to which the Secretary of the Interior shall find such suspension to be essential to the accomplishment of the purpose of this order.'

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility is clearly named: the Secretary of the Interior makes the essentiality finding and the order's purpose and the mandatory overtime-pay proviso define what a correct implementation looks like.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order addresses a single question — suspending the eight-hour limit for Alaska Railroad rehabilitation work — with nothing distinct bundled in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The trigger — work the Secretary finds 'essential to the accomplishment of the purpose of this order' — is an undefined standard that two officials could apply differently in good faith when deciding which work qualifies for the suspension.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The mandatory time-and-a-half overtime proviso for hours over forty aligns incentives against unlimited hours; the order relies on the 1892 act's active 'extraordinary emergency' exception rather than any expired statute, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The suspension is expressly time-limited to 'a period of five months,' which is an affirmative expiration mechanism, so the absence-of-sunset flag does not fire.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order suspends a substantive statutory hour limit under the statute's own emergency exception; it removes no pre-existing judicial, congressional, or administrative oversight mechanism over the railroad's operations.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Affected employees, whose wages and overtime are protected by the proviso, have standing and incentive to surface violations of the forty-hour/overtime terms, so no third-party gap exists in the ongoing wage-compliance relationship.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order establishes a template for invoking the 1892 act's 'extraordinary emergency' exception to suspend the eight-hour law via unilateral executive finding, a precedent usable to erode statutory labor-hour protections in other public-works contexts.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the Department of the Interior (operator of the Alaska Railroad) is implicated; no second agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The suspension itself functions as a discretionary carve-out from the eight-hour-law's coverage, controlled entirely by the Secretary of the Interior's unbounded essentiality finding over 'all work' by the railroad's laborers and mechanics, with no defined criteria limiting the scope of who is removed from the statutory protection.

note: A time-limited (five-month) suspension of the eight-hour law invoking the 1892 act's active extraordinary-emergency exception; the explicit expiration clears Flag 6 and the wage proviso clears several flags, but the unbounded Secretary-of-Interior essentiality finding fires Flags 1, 4, and 11.

title: Creating an Emergency Board To Investigate a Dispute Between the Trans World Airlines, Inc., and Certain of its Employees

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Authority is distributed by statute: the National Mediation Board judges the commerce threat, the President appoints a three-member board that only investigates and reports, and the board holds no unilateral decision power over the parties.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility is clearly assigned — the board must report findings to the President within thirty days — and the statutory status-quo obligation is defined, so there is a named actor with a defined obligation at each step.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order addresses one dispute and one remedy (creating an investigative board under the Railway Labor Act); nothing distinct is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order's operative terms are precise: a three-member board, a thirty-day reporting deadline, and a statutorily-defined status-quo freeze period, leaving no ambiguity for divergent good-faith application.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The conflict-of-interest bar (no member 'pecuniarily or otherwise interested' in any carrier or employee organization) and the temporary status-quo freeze align incentives with neutral fact-finding; no lapsed statute is invoked, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The board's authority is inherently time-bounded — it reports within thirty days and the freeze runs thirty days after the report — providing a built-in natural expiration.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order operates within the Railway Labor Act's own procedures and removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism; it adds an investigative board rather than stripping any check.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Both the carrier and the employees' union have standing and incentive to enforce the statutory status-quo freeze against each other, so no third-party gap exists in the compliance relationship.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: This is a routine, statutorily-templated emergency-board creation with no authority claim or precedent extending beyond the standard Railway Labor Act dispute process.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order works cooperatively with the National Mediation Board's statutory role and redirects no agency's mandate; the ad hoc board supplements rather than displaces existing authority.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The status-quo freeze applies uniformly to the carrier and its employees with no exemption or waiver structure carving anyone out of coverage.

note: A clean, statutorily-templated Railway Labor Act emergency board with a conflict-of-interest bar, a thirty-day deadline, and a defined status-quo freeze; every flag scores ABSENT because an ongoing (if brief) compliance relationship exists but is cleanly designed.

title: Generalized System of Preferences for Developing Countries

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order makes technical tariff-schedule modifications under the Trade Act of 1974's own delegated authority; it concentrates no new discretionary decision power in any single official beyond the statutory GSP framework it amends.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The amendments are self-executing schedule changes with defined effective dates and annexes; the President is the named responsible actor and the Trade Act framework supplies the accountability structure.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order coherently addresses a single subject — adjusting GSP eligible-article designations and limitations — with all sections serving that unified purpose; nothing unrelated is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Coverage is defined precisely by enumerated tariff-schedule annexes (Annexes I-IV) with specific article listings and effective dates, leaving no ambiguity about which articles receive preferential treatment.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order adjusts eligible-article lists per the Trade Act's competitive-need and eligibility provisions; no design feature rewards conduct undermining the GSP's purpose, and no lapsed statute is invoked, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a standing procedural framework maintained through ordinary supersession — it amends and re-amends prior annexes of EO 11888 that are themselves periodically revised — evidencing routine amendment rather than a frozen discretionary grant, so the permanence carve-out applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order amends tariff schedules under the Trade Act's own procedures (including the Section 503(a)/131-134 advice process it invokes) and removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement of tariff classifications runs through Customs entry and consumption processes where competing domestic producers and importers have standing and incentive to contest misclassification, so no third-party gap exists.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order is a technical adjustment of preference eligibility within an existing statutory scheme; no identifiable authority claim or definitional framework extends its effect into unrelated domains.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order modifies the Tariff Schedules administered under the Trade Act and redirects no other agency's statutory mandate; implementation runs through the ordinary Customs/trade machinery.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The article-eligibility exclusions (e.g., General Headnote 3(c)(iii) and per-country carve-outs) are the substantive design of the GSP itself, defined by specific enumerated lists rather than broad discretionary waivers that swallow coverage.

note: A partial amendment to the annexes of EO 11888 (not present in referenced-predecessors/, so the diff against the predecessor's exact prior annex text could not be made); the amending order's own text shows only precise, enumerated tariff-schedule adjustments within the Trade Act framework, all scoring ABSENT.

title: Creating an Emergency Board to Investigate a Dispute Between Trans World Airlines, Inc., and Certain of its Employees

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Authority is distributed by statute: the National Mediation Board judges the commerce threat, the President appoints a three-member board that only investigates and reports, and the board holds no unilateral power over the parties.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility is clearly assigned — the board reports findings to the President within thirty days — and the statutory status-quo obligation defines correct implementation.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order addresses one dispute and one remedy (an investigative board under the Railway Labor Act); nothing distinct is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The operative terms are precise: a three-member board, a thirty-day reporting deadline, and a statutorily-defined status-quo freeze, leaving no room for divergent good-faith application.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The conflict-of-interest bar and the temporary status-quo freeze align incentives with neutral fact-finding; no lapsed statute is invoked, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The board's authority is inherently time-bounded — it reports within thirty days and the freeze runs thirty days after the report — a built-in natural expiration.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order operates within the Railway Labor Act's procedures and removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism; it adds an investigative board rather than stripping a check.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Both the carrier and the employees' union have standing and incentive to enforce the statutory status-quo freeze against each other, so no third-party gap exists.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: This is a routine, statutorily-templated emergency-board creation with no authority claim or precedent extending beyond the standard Railway Labor Act dispute process.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order works cooperatively with the National Mediation Board's statutory role and redirects no agency's mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The status-quo freeze applies uniformly to the carrier and its employees with no exemption or waiver structure carving anyone out.

note: A clean, statutorily-templated Railway Labor Act emergency board (nearly identical in structure to order-164) with a conflict-of-interest bar, a thirty-day deadline, and a defined status-quo freeze; all flags score ABSENT on their own merits.

title: Termination of Emergency Authority for Export Controls

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order revokes a prior emergency-based delegation and returns export-control authority to the reauthorized Export Administration Act's statutory framework; it concentrates no new authority in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility is clear — existing rules continue under the Act's proper authorities and named prior delegation orders (EO 12002, EO 12214) remain in effect — so the administering actors and their obligations are identifiable.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order coherently effects a single action — terminating the IEEPA emergency and transitioning export controls to the reauthorized statute — with the saving clauses serving that same purpose.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order specifies precisely which regulations continue in force (15 CFR Ch. III, Subch. C, Parts 368-399) and under what authority, leaving no ambiguity about the ongoing export-control compliance standard.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order does the opposite of the Zombie Emergency Trap: it rescinds the economic emergency once Congress reauthorized the Act (Public Law 99-64), removing rather than perpetuating emergency authority, so no perverse incentive and no Trap fires.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order is itself a termination action rescinding an emergency declaration; it grants no continuing authority that would require a sunset mechanism.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: By moving export controls back onto the reauthorized statutory Act, the order restores the ordinary legislative framework rather than removing any pre-existing oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The continuing export-control regime operates under the Act's established enforcement structure (licensing, penalties for violations preserved by Sec. 2) with the same third-party and governmental enforcement standing as before; the order creates no new self-reporting-only gap.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order narrows executive claims by ending an emergency and returning to statutory authority; it establishes no precedent or definitional framework that expands authority into unrelated contexts.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order preserves existing delegations (EO 12002, EO 12214) and redirects no agency's statutory mandate; it effects an orderly transition of authority basis without inter-agency conflict.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order carves no actor out of coverage; the saving clauses preserve existing rules and pending violations uniformly, with no discretionary waiver structure.

note: A termination order revoking EO 12470 and rescinding an economic emergency after Congress reauthorized the Export Administration Act; EO 12470 was not in referenced-predecessors/ so its exact text could not be compared, but the amending order's self-contained saving clauses show a clean wind-down that restores statutory authority — all flags ABSENT.

title: Termination of the Air Coordinating Committee

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order terminates a committee and assigns only routine wind-up duties to the FAA Administrator; it concentrates no ongoing decision authority in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility for winding up outstanding affairs is clearly assigned to the Administrator of the Federal Aviation Agency (para. 3), leaving a named actor with a defined obligation.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order does one coherent thing — terminate the Air Coordinating Committee and revoke the orders that created and amended it; nothing distinct is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Terminating a committee creates no ongoing behavioral compliance rule that any actor must follow, so there is no enforcement mechanism to assess for vagueness.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order abolishes a body rather than creating any ongoing regulatory relationship or incentive for any actor class; no lapsed statute is invoked, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order is itself a termination and grants no continuing authority; the wind-up duty is inherently self-extinguishing, so there is nothing to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Abolishing a coordinating committee removes an inter-agency coordination body, not a pre-existing external oversight or review mechanism over any authority, so Flag 7 (removal of oversight) does not fire.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: With no ongoing regulated conduct created, there is no enforcement chain in which a third party would need standing or incentive to surface violations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Terminating an aviation coordinating committee and directing routine wind-up establishes no authority claim or precedent usable in unrelated contexts; the effect is contained to dissolving one body.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order dissolves an inter-agency committee and assigns wind-up to the FAA Administrator without redirecting or countermanding any agency's statutory operational mandate; the coordination functions simply cease.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order has no coverage population subject to an ongoing rule, so there is no exemption structure to assess.

note: A termination order abolishing the Air Coordinating Committee and revoking EO 10655 and EO 10796 (neither in referenced-predecessors/, but the order fully extinguishes rather than modifies them, so the diff is immaterial); the compliance-regime flags (4, 5, 8, 11) and Flag 6 are NOT_APPLICABLE because no ongoing authority or regulatory relationship survives, while decision-structure flags score ABSENT.

title: Delegation of Functions of the President Respecting the Transfer of Wheat to Pakistan

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Section 1 empowers the Director for Mutual Security to perform the President's statutory functions 'without the approval, ratification, or other action of the President,' pooling operational authority over the wheat transfer in a single official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Accountability is preserved by structure: the delegated functions are made 'subject to the responsibilities of the Secretary of State with respect to foreign policy' (Sec. 4), and the termination-of-assistance function is expressly retained (Sec. 2), leaving named actors responsible.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order coherently allocates functions under a single statute (the June 25, 1953 wheat-transfer Act) among the Director and the Secretary of State; nothing distinct is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: This is an intra-executive delegation of the President's functions for a one-time wheat transfer to Pakistan; it creates no ongoing behavioral compliance rule for any regulated party to enforce, so vague-enforcement analysis does not apply.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A fixed-purpose delegation to negotiate and effect a single price-support wheat transfer creates no ongoing regulatory relationship or behavioral incentive for any actor class; no lapsed statute is invoked, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The delegated authority is inherently self-limiting to a specific statutory task (the Pakistan wheat transfer under a specific Act) that resolves on completion, so no separate sunset mechanism is needed and its absence is not a design gap.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order allocates presidential functions among executive officials and removes no pre-existing judicial, congressional, or administrative oversight mechanism; the State Department foreign-policy responsibility (Sec. 4) is expressly preserved.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The order effects a one-time intergovernmental wheat transfer with no ongoing regulated-party conduct, so there is no enforcement chain requiring third-party standing or incentive.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow delegation to execute a single congressionally-authorized wheat transfer establishes no authority claim or precedent usable in unrelated contexts beyond ordinary Section 301 delegation practice.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order coordinates the Director for Mutual Security and the Secretary of State with a clear hierarchy (Sec. 4 subordinates the delegation to State's foreign-policy responsibilities), redirecting no agency's statutory mandate into conflict.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order has no coverage population subject to an ongoing rule and thus no exemption structure to assess.

note: A narrow, fixed-purpose intra-executive delegation of presidential functions for a single wheat transfer to Pakistan; the compliance-regime flags (4, 5, 8, 11) are NOT_APPLICABLE for lack of any ongoing regulatory relationship, while the 'without approval of the President' clause (Sec. 1) fires Flag 1 for authority pooling, mitigated by the retained State Department check that clears Flag 2.

title: Prescribing Regulations Governing the Exportation of Articles and Materials Designated in the President's Proclamation of February 25, 1941 (and Amending Regulations of January 15, 1941)

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order incorporates and extends EO 8640's framework, under which the Secretary of State 'may issue general licenses authorizing the exportation to all or certain areas' of the listed materials in his discretion, pooling export-licensing authority in a single official under the July 2, 1940 defense act.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility is named: the Secretary of State issues licenses under rules and directives communicated through the Administrator of Export Control (per incorporated EO 8640), giving identifiable actors with defined roles in the export-control chain.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order does one coherent thing — add specified drugs and leather goods (belladonna, atropine, sole/belting leather) to the existing export-control list and amend EO 8640 accordingly; nothing substantively distinct is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The extended framework (EO 8640, para. 1) lets the Secretary of State issue general licenses 'in accordance with rules and regulations prescribed by the President or such specific directives as may from time to time be communicated,' an open-ended standard under which two officials could reach different licensing decisions in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The export-licensing regime aligns exporter incentives with obtaining licenses for controlled materials; the order invokes the active July 2, 1940 defense act, not any expired statute, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire and no other perverse incentive is evident.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The export-control authority is discretionary, defense-emergency-linked power (materials controlled 'to expedite the strengthening of the national defense') that should carry a natural end tied to the defense emergency, yet the order supplies no expiration or reauthorization mechanism, continuing until revoked.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds materials to an existing export-control list and amends EO 8640; it removes no pre-existing judicial, congressional, or administrative oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Export-control compliance depends on exporters obtaining licenses and on the licensing agency, with no external actor holding both standing and incentive to surface unlicensed or evasive exports of the listed materials, leaving enforcement reliant on the regulated parties and the agency itself.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: By construing the President's proclamation to sweep additional broad commodity categories (medicinal alkaloids and leather) into the discretionary defense-export-control regime, the order extends a precedent for continually expanding executive control over ordinary trade goods via proclamation without further legislative action.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order operates within the State Department/Administrator of Export Control licensing structure and Commerce's statistical schedules referentially; it redirects no agency's statutory operational mandate into conflict.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The incorporated EO 8640 authorizes the Secretary of State to issue broad 'general licenses authorizing the exportation to all or certain areas or destinations' at his discretion, a general-license waiver power concentrated in one official that can effectively remove destinations or materials from the export-control default without bounded criteria.

note: This order amends EO 8640 to add belladonna/atropine drugs and leather to the pre-defense export-control list; because it extends and operates through EO 8640's framework (which was available in referenced-predecessors/), the discretionary general-license and directive-based licensing authority of the parent order is assessed as inherited, firing the concentration/vague-enforcement/exemptions/sunset cluster on this wartime-precursor export-control regime.

title: Declaring a Public Policy Against Discrimination on the Basis of Age

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Authority is distributed to each department and agency head to enunciate the policy and amend procurement regulations; no single official holds discretionary eligibility or enforcement power over the anti-discrimination policy.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The order declares a policy and directs procurement regulations be amended to give notice, but names no actor responsible for correcting or remedying age-discrimination violations if the policy fails; 'take appropriate action to enunciate this policy' assigns notice-giving, not accountability for outcomes.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order addresses a single problem frame (age discrimination by federal contractors) with a coherent set of provisions; no distinct policy questions are packaged together to foreclose targeted correction.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order declares a non-discrimination policy but specifies no enforcement trigger, no consequence for non-compliance, and no defined standard for what constitutes a 'bona fide occupational qualification'; two officials could in good faith reach substantially different enforcement decisions.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No design feature rewards contractors for non-compliance or implementing agencies for failure; the bona-fide-qualification carve-outs track legitimate exceptions rather than gaming opportunities. No lapsed statute is invoked, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is an evergreen anti-discrimination policy mandate whose subject matter has no natural end-state that reauthorization pressure would track, matching the EO 9981 permanence carve-out; the absence of a sunset is not a design gap here.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds a policy declaration and continuous-notice requirement to procurement regulations; it removes, limits, or bypasses no pre-existing oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement depends on the contracting agencies and regulated contractors themselves; the order creates no external actor with standing and incentive to surface age-discrimination violations, and affected older workers are given no enforcement mechanism.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order explicitly aims to 'encourage and hasten the acceptance' of the non-discrimination principle 'by all sectors of the economy, private and public,' using federal procurement as leverage to project anti-age-discrimination norms beyond direct federal employment, a deliberate precedent-setting effect on the broader labor market.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Each agency head acts within its own procurement authority to amend regulations; no agency's existing statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded, and the Federal Procurement and Armed Services Procurement Regulations are amended in parallel without jurisdictional conflict.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The policy carves out discrimination 'upon the basis of a bona fide occupational qualification, retirement plan, or statutory requirement' with no defined criteria bounding these exceptions; 'retirement plan' in particular is broad enough that a sophisticated contractor could structure age limits to qualify, potentially swallowing coverage.

note: A hortatory policy-declaration order with an ongoing compliance relationship (federal contractors) but no enforcement machinery; the undefined 'bona fide occupational qualification / retirement plan' carve-outs drive both the Flag 4 and Flag 11 findings.

title: Authorizing the Secretary of the Interior to Take Possession of and Operate the Mines, Collieries, and Preparation Facilities of Certain Coal Companies

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of the Interior is 'authorized and directed to take possession' and to 'operate ... in such manner as he deems necessary' and 'do all things necessary,' pooling seizure, operation, and disciplinary authority over private property in a single official with no external check; this concentration is the order's core operative design.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary determines both how to operate the facilities and, under Sec. 5, when 'productive efficiency ... has been restored,' the very condition that triggers termination of his own possession; the actor responsible for correcting failure is the same actor who judges whether failure occurred.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order addresses a single problem (labor-disruption interruption of wartime coal production at named companies) with an integrated seizure-and-operate remedy; no distinct policy questions are packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Secretary is directed to 'take such appropriate disciplinary action, not inconsistent with law, as may be necessary' against employees to maintain production, with no defined standard, trigger, or bounded consequence; two officials could apply this discretion very differently.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Government operation of the seized mines with existing terms of employment guaranteed removes the strike pressure that would ordinarily discipline both labor and management, creating an incentive to prolong dependence on federal operation rather than resolve the dispute. The authority derives from the active War Labor Disputes Act, not a lapsed statute, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Sec. 5 provides an explicit termination trigger, requiring the Secretary to relinquish possession within sixty days after productive efficiency is restored to pre-interruption levels; a natural end-state tied to the emergency is built in, so this absence flag does not fire.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order directs other federal agencies to cooperate and preserves existing employment terms subject to Sec. 5 of the War Labor Disputes Act; it removes, limits, or bypasses no pre-existing external oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Whether operation continues or terminates depends entirely on the Secretary's own assessment of restored efficiency, and management is confined to functions 'consistent with the aims of this order'; no external party with standing has access to surface a premature or improper continuation of the seizure.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order establishes and normalizes the precedent of executive seizure and operation of named private companies' property under a wartime labor-disputes statute, an authority claim readily transferable to other industries and labor conflicts beyond the four listed coal companies.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order directs the War Manpower Commission, National Selective Service System, War Department, and Department of Justice 'to cooperate ... to the fullest extent possible' and act through instrumentalities the Secretary designates, subordinating these agencies' operations to the Interior Secretary's seizure objective without defined hierarchy for mandate conflicts.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order applies to a fixed, enumerated list of four named coal companies with no waiver or carve-out mechanism that any actor could invoke to escape coverage; there is no exemption structure to undermine the rule.

note: Wartime seizure order concentrating sweeping operate-and-discipline authority in one Secretary who also judges his own termination trigger; scored CRITICAL on Flag 1 for core design collapse of external check, but Flag 6 clears because Sec. 5 supplies an explicit emergency-tied termination condition.

title: Establishing the National Space Council

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Chairman (Vice President) 'shall serve as the President's principal advisor on national space policy,' sets the agenda, establishes procedures, and unilaterally 'authorize[s] the establishment of such committees ... as he deems necessary,' concentrating agenda and structural control of the Council in one official, though the Council is advisory rather than operative.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Council is directed to 'monitor and coordinate implementation' and 'facilitate resolution of differences,' but Sec. 2(c) states its creation 'shall not interfere with existing lines of authority,' so if coordination fails no actor holds both the obligation and the capacity to correct it, the Council reports and advises without enforcement.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order coherently establishes one coordination body for national space policy across civil, security, and commercial sectors; the multi-sector scope is intrinsic to the single stated purpose, not distinct problems bundled to foreclose correction.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Sec. 4(c) directs each agency head to 'ensure that its space-related activities conform to national space policy and strategy' with no defined conformance standard, trigger, or consequence, and the Council merely 'facilitate[s] resolution of differences'; conformance obligations are undefined enough to produce inconsistent application.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: As an advisory-and-coordination body with no budgetary or authority expansion tied to failure, the design creates no material incentive for any actor to undermine its stated purpose. No lapsed statute is invoked, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The Council itself is created with no termination date or reauthorization requirement, continuing until revoked; a standing interagency policy-coordination body is discretionary authority that should face periodic reauthorization pressure, unlike the private-citizen advisory Board in Sec. 5 which is subject to the Federal Advisory Committee Act's renewal regime.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds a new coordination layer and expressly preserves existing lines of authority (Sec. 2(c)) and FACA reporting for the advisory Board; it removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism, and any imperfection in the new machinery is assessed under other flags per the direction rule.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Agency conformance to space policy is monitored only by the Council itself using information the agencies supply on request (Sec. 4(b)); no external third party has standing and access to surface an agency's non-conformance, and the private-citizen Board is purely advisory to the Vice President.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Vesting the Vice President as chair of a standing council that 'monitor[s] and coordinate[s] implementation' across national-security, intelligence, and commercial space activities establishes an institutional precedent that shifts space-policy gravity into the Executive Office of the President, with reach into DCI and NSC domains beyond the stated coordination purpose.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Council is directed to 'monitor and coordinate implementation' of space policy by executive departments and to require conformance (Sec. 4(c)), overlaying a coordination authority atop NASA, DoD, DCI, and Commerce space mandates; although Sec. 2(c) disclaims interference, the parallel monitoring-and-conformance structure creates jurisdictional friction. Sec. 6 also amends EO 12660 to substitute the National Space Council for the Economic Policy Council in the Microgravity Research Board's reporting chain, a clean lateral reassignment confirmed against the EO 12660 text.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order applies uniformly to all executive departments and agencies with space-related activities; it contains no waiver, carve-out, or exemption mechanism that any actor could invoke to escape the coordination framework.

note: Establishes a standing interagency space-policy council chaired by the VP with conformance and monitoring authority; Sec. 6's one-line amendment to EO 12660 was verified against the predecessor text and is a clean reporting-chain substitution. No CRITICAL because the body is advisory/coordinative and expressly preserves existing lines of authority.

title: Preservation and Display of Enemy Flags Captured by the Navy and the Coast Guard

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order directs the Secretary of the Navy to deposit captured flags at the Naval Academy (and Coast Guard captures at the Coast Guard Academy) for preservation; it is a ministerial custodial directive that concentrates no discretionary eligibility, enforcement, or interpretive authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility is clearly and singularly assigned to the Superintendents of the respective academies 'under the care of' whom the items are deposited; the custodial chain is unambiguous.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order addresses a single custodial matter (where captured enemy flags are preserved and displayed); nothing distinct is packaged with it.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order creates a one-time-and-recurring custodial deposit directive with no ongoing behavioral rule for any regulated party to comply with, so the vague-enforcement question does not map onto it.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: There is no regulated actor whose incentives could be perverted by a directive to deposit captured flags at a named academy; no ongoing compliance relationship exists. Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire (no lapsed statute).

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a standing custodial procedure that supersedes the 1849 Polk order through ordinary supersession, evidencing that this category of rule is maintained by amendment rather than left to require formal reauthorization; the procedural-framework evergreen carve-out applies, so the absence of a sunset does not fire.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order supersedes an 1849 custodial order (a predecessor not available in the referenced-predecessors folder) but that superseded order was itself a custodial directive; nothing in the text removes judicial, IG, congressional, or other external oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no enforcement chain or ongoing compliance behavior for a third party to surface; the order simply directs deposit of captured items for preservation and display.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: This is a narrow administrative custodial order with no systemic, precedential, or downstream policy implications beyond the physical location of captured flags.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Navy and Coast Guard academies are the custodians; the order assigns custody within the naval establishment's own domain and creates no conflict with any agency's existing statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order establishes no coverage population with exemptions; it is a custodial directive with no waivers or carve-outs, so the exemptions question does not apply.

note: Narrow custodial/administrative order directing deposit of captured enemy flags; compliance-regime and systemic flags (4,5,8,9,11) are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE, decision-structure flags score cleanly ABSENT. The superseded 1849 Polk order was not in the referenced-predecessors folder, but it was itself a custodial directive so no adverse diff arises.

title: Extension of Trust Periods on Indian Lands Expiring During the Calendar Year 1949

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order is a self-executing extension of trust periods by twenty-five years for patents expiring in 1949; it vests no discretionary decision authority in any official, so no power is concentrated.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The extension operates automatically by the order's own terms, so there is no implementation actor whose failure to perform could create an accountability gap; the mechanism executes itself.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order does one thing, extend expiring trust periods for a defined year, and expressly excludes cases Congress reserved to itself; nothing distinct is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order automatically extends trust restrictions and imposes no ongoing behavioral rule requiring enforcement against any regulated party; there is nothing for an enforcing official to apply.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A self-executing trust-period extension creates no ongoing compliance relationship whose incentives could be perverted. The authorizing statutes (1887, 1906, 1917 Acts) are cited as active, not lapsed, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order is inherently time-bounded, extending trusts by a defined twenty-five-year term for patents expiring in a single named year, so it contains its own natural expiration rather than open-ended authority; the absence flag does not fire.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order expressly preserves congressional authority ('not intended to apply to any case in which Congress has specifically reserved to itself authority'); it removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The extension is automatic with no enforcement chain or compliance behavior for a third party to surface; there is no ongoing regulatory relationship.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: This is a routine annual trust-period extension of the kind issued repeatedly under settled statutory authority; it establishes no new precedent or downstream consequence beyond preserving the existing status quo of the affected lands' trust protection.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the trust status of Indian lands is affected, operating under existing Interior/BIA statutory authority; no second agency's mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The single carve-out (cases Congress reserved to itself) is a jurisdictional boundary defined by Congress, not a discretionary exemption structure; with no ongoing compliance population, the exemptions-architecture question does not apply.
