note: Self-executing, time-bounded routine extension of Indian-land trust periods; compliance-regime flags (4,5,8,11) are NOT_APPLICABLE because no ongoing regulatory relationship is created, and the decision-structure and effect flags all score cleanly ABSENT.

title: Inspection by the Office of Price Administration of Corporation Statistical Transcript Cards Prepared from Income and Declared Value Excess-Profits Tax Returns

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order simply opens a defined set of corporate statistical transcript cards to inspection by the Office of Price Administration under regulations the Secretary of the Treasury already prescribed; it vests no new discretionary authority in any single official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Inspection is governed by named Treasury regulations (Treasury Decision 5173) whose administration rests with the Secretary of the Treasury; responsibility for the inspection regime is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order authorizes a single, defined data-inspection access for one agency over one tax year's cards; nothing distinct is packaged with it.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Inspection is expressly bound to 'the rules and regulations prescribed by the Secretary of the Treasury in Treasury Decision 5173,' incorporating a defined compliance standard by reference; the terms of access are not left ambiguous.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Granting one agency inspection access to another's statistical cards under fixed rules creates no incentive for any actor to undermine the order's purpose. The authorizing Internal Revenue Code sections are active, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The inspection authority is inherently bounded to cards for a single defined taxable-year window (ending after June 30, 1942, and before July 1, 1943), so it carries its own natural limit rather than open-ended authority; the absence flag does not fire.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order expressly conditions inspection on 'compliance with the rules and regulations prescribed by the Secretary of the Treasury,' operating within existing Treasury safeguards rather than bypassing any oversight; no pre-existing check is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The order creates an ongoing access relationship governed by Treasury's own regulations, which retain administrative control over inspection compliance; the Secretary of the Treasury's rule-based oversight supplies the checking function, so no third-party gap fires.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: By extending inspection of confidential corporate tax-return-derived data to another agency (OPA) on the model of the prior year's cards, the order incrementally normalizes cross-agency access to taxpayer information, a precedent for expanding disclosure of otherwise confidential return data to additional agencies and years.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order lets OPA inspect Bureau of Internal Revenue cards under Treasury's own regulations; it neither redirects Treasury's mandate nor creates conflicting parallel authority, since Treasury retains control of the rules governing access.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order defines a precise coverage scope (corporation cards for one taxable-year window) with no waiver or exemption mechanism that any actor could invoke to escape it; there is no exemption structure to undermine the rule.

note: Narrow data-access order granting OPA inspection of a defined year's corporate tax statistical cards under existing Treasury regulations; scores cleanly except for a modest Flag 9 finding on the precedent of expanding cross-agency access to confidential return-derived data.

title: Establishing the Operations Coordinating Board

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Board, chaired by the Under Secretary of State, is given cross-agency authority to coordinate 'detailed operational planning,' 'timely and coordinated execution,' and to 'initiate new proposals for action' implementing national security policy, concentrating operational-coordination authority over multiple agencies in one interdepartmental body reporting only to the NSC.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Board advises, coordinates, and initiates but its outputs run to the NSC via periodic reports (Sec. 2), and Sec. 6 disclaims abrogating any agency's statutory functions, so if coordinated execution of a security policy fails, no single actor holds both the obligation and capacity to correct it, responsibility diffuses across the Board and the several agencies.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order coherently establishes one coordinating board for integrated implementation of national security policy; the multiple coordination functions all serve that single stated purpose rather than packaging distinct policy questions.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Board's function to ensure each security action 'make its full contribution to ... the particular climate of opinion the United States is seeking to achieve in the world' supplies no defined standard, trigger, or consequence; two officials could apply this coordination mandate in substantially different ways in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: As a coordination-and-advisory board whose authority does not expand through failure and which is barred from abrogating agency functions, the design creates no material incentive to undermine its stated purpose. No lapsed statute is invoked, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The Operations Coordinating Board is established with no termination date or reauthorization requirement, continuing until revoked; a standing interagency operational-coordination body created to implement policy is discretionary authority that should face periodic reauthorization pressure and lacks any such mechanism.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds a new coordination layer and Sec. 6 expressly preserves functions vested by law in any agency; it removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism, and abolition of the Psychological Strategy Board (Sec. 4) transfers that body's affairs to the OCB rather than eliminating a check.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The Board coordinates and reports internally to the NSC using assistance the represented agencies furnish; no external third party has standing and access to surface a failure of coordinated implementation of national security policy, which by nature operates outside public oversight.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Creating a standing board to coordinate 'the timely and coordinated execution' of national security policy across State, Defense, CIA, and FOA and to shape 'the particular climate of opinion' abroad institutionalizes a covert-and-overt operations coordination apparatus whose precedent and reach extend well beyond any single policy, absorbing the abolished Psychological Strategy Board's functions.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Board overlays operational-coordination authority atop State, Defense, CIA, and FOA mandates and abolishes the Psychological Strategy Board (Sec. 4), winding up its affairs through the OCB; although Sec. 6 disclaims abrogating statutory functions, the parallel coordination-and-execution structure creates jurisdictional friction among agencies that already hold these mandates.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order applies to all agencies assigned national-security-policy implementation responsibilities with no waiver or carve-out mechanism any actor could invoke to escape the coordination framework; Sec. 6's savings clause preserves statutory functions rather than exempting actors from coverage.

note: Establishes a standing national-security operations-coordination board with cross-agency execution-coordination authority; multiple flags fire on the undefined 'climate of opinion' coordination mandate and the standing-body permanence, but none rises to CRITICAL because Sec. 6 preserves agency statutory functions and the body is coordinative rather than directive.

title: Partial Revocation of Executive Order No. 5214 of October 30, 1929, Withdrawing Public Lands for Naval Purposes

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order self-executingly revokes a prior land withdrawal as to a precisely metes-and-bounds-described 18-acre tract; it concentrates no discretionary decision authority, and the only ongoing task (providing ingress/egress) is a ministerial function of the Secretary of the Navy.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The revocation operates automatically by the order's terms and the sole residual duty (ingress and egress) is clearly assigned to the Secretary of the Navy; the accountability chain is unambiguous.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order does one thing, partially revoke a naval land withdrawal for a described tract; nothing distinct is packaged with it. EO 5214, the predecessor being partially revoked, was not present in the referenced-predecessors folder, but the diff is a straightforward release of a described tract from withdrawal.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A self-executing partial revocation of a land withdrawal imposes no ongoing behavioral rule requiring enforcement against any regulated party; there is nothing for an enforcing official to apply.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Releasing a described tract from naval withdrawal creates no ongoing compliance relationship whose incentives could be perverted. The authorizing 1910 Act is cited as active, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a one-time, self-executing land-status change with no ongoing grant of authority that could be subject to sunset or reauthorization.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order revokes a prior land withdrawal as to a tract; it removes no judicial, legislative, or administrative oversight mechanism, and the Secretary retains regulatory authority over ingress and egress.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no enforcement chain or ongoing compliance behavior for a third party to surface; the order simply releases a described tract from naval withdrawal.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: This is a narrow administrative land-status revocation of a specific 18-acre tract with no systemic, precedential, or downstream policy implications.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The tract's status changes within the Navy's own domain and the Secretary of the Navy retains control over access; no second agency's statutory mandate is implicated or redirected.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order establishes no coverage population with exemptions; it is a one-time land-status revocation with no waivers or carve-outs, so the exemptions question does not apply.

note: Narrow, self-executing partial revocation of a naval land withdrawal for a described 18-acre tract; compliance-regime and systemic flags (4,5,6,8,9,11) are NOT_APPLICABLE because it is a one-time land-status action, decision-structure flags score cleanly ABSENT. Predecessor EO 5214 was not in the referenced folder but the diff is a simple tract release.

title: Maintaining Unofficial Relations with the People on Taiwan

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Nearly all functions conferred on the President by the Taiwan Relations Act are delegated to the Secretary of State (Sec. 1-101), and Sec. 1-203(b) grants the Secretary unilateral power to amend or modify contracts 'with or without consideration' whenever he 'determines in each case' it protects U.S. foreign-policy interests, concentrating broad discretionary authority in one Secretary.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of State exercises the delegated functions and himself makes the case-by-case 'necessary to protect the foreign policy interests' determinations that trigger extraordinary contract modifications; the actor exercising the discretion also judges whether its conditions are met, with no defined external correction mechanism.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order coherently implements a single statutory scheme (the Taiwan Relations Act) through delegations, law specifications, and contract-authority provisions all serving the unofficial-relations purpose; it does not package distinct policy questions to foreclose targeted correction.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Sec. 1-203(b) permits contract amendments 'irrespective of ... rights which may have accrued' whenever the Secretary 'determines in each case that such action is necessary to protect the foreign policy interests of the United States,' an open-ended standard under which two officials could reach substantially different decisions in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The delegation implements the Taiwan Relations Act's framework for unofficial relations and creates no design feature rewarding an actor for undermining that purpose. The authorizing Act is active and unexpired, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a standing delegation implementing an ongoing statutory framework for maintaining unofficial relations, an arrangement that is evergreen by nature (it persists as long as the recognition situation and the Act do) rather than an emergency or temporary grant needing reauthorization pressure; the permanence carve-out applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Sec. 1-202 specifies numerous procurement and contracting statutes (including competitive-procurement and fee-limitation provisions) that the procurement of services 'may be effected without regard to,' affirmatively bypassing a suite of pre-existing statutory contracting safeguards and controls for Institute contracts.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The contract-modification and law-waiver authority is exercised by the Secretary of State on his own determination, and ordinary procurement-oversight actors are removed by Sec. 1-202's 'without regard to' waivers; no external third party retains standing and access to surface improper exercise of the contracting discretion.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order institutionalizes a mechanism for conducting substantive relations with a de-recognized government through an 'unofficial instrumentality' (the American Institute in Taiwan / Coordination Council) exempt from normal procurement law, establishing a durable template for maintaining functional relations with non-recognized entities that extends well beyond the Taiwan case.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Functions are delegated to the Secretary of State (with narrow personnel functions to OPM in consultation with State) pursuant to the Taiwan Relations Act; the allocation is a coherent delegation, not a redirection or countermanding of another agency's existing statutory mandate producing jurisdictional conflict.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Sec. 1-202 lists eleven procurement, competition, and fee-limitation statutes that Institute-contract procurement may proceed 'without regard to,' a broad structural set of exemptions from generally applicable contracting law concentrated in the Secretary's implementation, systematically removing these transactions from the coverage those statutes would otherwise impose.

note: Implements the Taiwan Relations Act by delegating broad discretion to the Secretary of State and waiving numerous procurement statutes for Institute contracts (Sec. 1-202), driving the Flag 7/11 findings; EO 10789 is cited only as a reference standard for hazardous-risk indemnification, not amended. No CRITICAL because the design still substantially achieves its unofficial-relations purpose.

title: Statistical Policy Functions

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order transfers statistical-policy functions from the Secretary of Commerce to the Director of OMB and requires redelegation to the Administrator of OIRA, consolidating government-wide statistical-policy authority (including the EO 10253 power to issue binding regulations all executive agencies must adhere to) into OMB/OIRA; measured against the immediate baseline (EO 12013), which had placed these functions in Commerce, this re-pools the authority into the President's budget office.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The order clearly locates the transferred functions in the Director of OMB and the OIRA Administrator and directs the Director to take all steps to effectuate the transfers (Sec. 6); responsibility for the reassigned functions is unambiguously placed.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Though the order amends several predecessor orders (EO 11541, 10253, 10033, 12013, 11961), each amendment serves the single coherent purpose of relocating statistical-policy functions from Commerce to OMB/OIRA; these are not distinct unrelated policy questions packaged to foreclose targeted correction.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The transferred functions operate through the pre-existing EO 10253/10033 framework, under which the responsible official issues binding regulations and orders that 'shall be adhered to by all agencies'; the enforcement standard is inherited and defined, and this order merely substitutes which official exercises it.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: An intra-executive transfer of statistical-policy functions to OMB pursuant to the Paperwork Reduction Act creates no design feature rewarding any actor for undermining its purpose. The authorizing Paperwork Reduction Act of 1980 and Reorganization Plan No. 2 of 1970 are active, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a standing procedural/administrative reorganization of statistical-policy functions that, as its own text shows, is maintained through ordinary supersession and amendment of numbered predecessor orders (reversing EO 12013, amending EO 10253/10033); the procedural-framework evergreen carve-out applies, so the absence of a sunset does not fire.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order reassigns operational functions among executive officials and preserves congressional reporting (Sec. 4 keeps the Commerce periodic-reports-to-Congress function); it removes no pre-existing judicial, IG, or congressional oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The underlying statistical-policy framework retains its inherited compliance structure (binding regulations all agencies must adhere to, plus preserved congressional reporting), and this order only changes which official administers it; it introduces no new self-reporting reliance that would create a third-party gap.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Relocating government-wide statistical-policy authority into OMB and specifically OIRA folds statistical policy into the centralized regulatory-review apparatus, a structural shift that makes federal statistical activities subject to OIRA's broader information-and-regulatory-affairs agenda beyond the stated transfer of functions.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order redirects functions, records, property, personnel, and unexpended appropriations from the Secretary of Commerce to the Director of OMB (Sec. 5), reassigning authority Commerce held under the amended EO 10253/10033/11961 framework; verified against the predecessor texts, this is a lateral reallocation of an operative mandate between agencies, reversing the Commerce-ward transfer EO 12013 had made.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order contains no waiver or exemption mechanism carving any actor out of the statistical-policy coverage; it reassigns which official administers the framework without creating exemptions from it.

note: Diff-aware intra-executive reorganization reversing EO 12013 and moving statistical-policy functions from Commerce to OMB/OIRA (revoking EO 12013, amending EO 10253/10033/11961), all predecessors verified in the referenced folder; scores modestly on Flag 1 (re-pooling into OMB/OIRA), Flag 9 (folding statistics into the OIRA regulatory-review apparatus), and Flag 10 (lateral resource/function reallocation from Commerce).

title: Revocation of Executive Order No. 6014 of February 6, 1933, Withdrawing Public Lands in Oregon

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A one-sentence, self-executing revocation of a prior land withdrawal; no discretionary decision authority is created or pooled in any official, the effect flows automatically upon filing of the resurvey plat.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The action is self-executing and complete on filing of the plat; there is no implementation chain that could fail and no ongoing responsibility to trace.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order contains a single provision (revoking one land withdrawal) with nothing to bundle against.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time land-status revocation creates no ongoing compliance or enforcement relationship for any party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing regulatory relationship or behavioral rule exists that could create an incentive for any actor; the statutes invoked (1910/1912 land acts) are active, not lapsed.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a one-time, self-executing revocation with no ongoing grant of authority that could sunset; it terminates a prior withdrawal rather than creating standing authority.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order removes a prior withdrawal restriction (restoring lands to appropriation) and touches no oversight mechanism; nothing pre-existing is excluded or bypassed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no ongoing enforcement regime for which third-party standing to surface violations could be relevant.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow, township-specific land-status revocation carries no systemic or precedential implications identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency's statutory mandate is implicated, redirected, or countermanded by revoking a land withdrawal.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order establishes no coverage population and thus no exemption structure.

note: Predecessor EO 6014 was available and reviewed; it was a permanent land withdrawal pending resurvey, and order-181 cleanly revokes it upon filing of the resurvey plat. Nearly administrative; only Flags 1, 2, 7 answerable, all ABSENT.

title: Possession and Operation of the Transportation System, Plants, and Facilities of the Scranton Transit Company Located in and About the City of Scranton, Pennsylvania

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Sec. 1 authorizes and directs the Director of the Office of Defense Transportation to seize and operate the transit system and 'do anything that he may deem necessary,' with the sole check on duration being the Director's own determination (Sec. 4); discretionary operational authority is pooled in a single official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The same Director who seizes and operates the system also unilaterally determines when possession is 'no longer necessary' (Sec. 4), so the actor responsible for implementation also judges whether it has succeeded, leaving no independent correction mechanism.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order addresses a single coherent action (seizure and operation of one company's facilities); no distinct policy questions or problem frames are packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Standards for operation are open-ended ('in such a manner as he may deem necessary,' 'do anything that he may deem necessary'), so two officials could in good faith reach substantially different decisions about what the seizure authorizes.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Sec. 4 ties termination to the objective disappearance of war-effort necessity, and Sec. 2 preserves existing employment terms, so the design does not reward undermining the stated purpose; the invoked statutes (War Labor Disputes Act, First War Powers Act) were active and unexpired in June 1945, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is an emergency/exceptional-powers seizure whose subject matter has a natural end-state (the war effort), yet termination rests solely on the Director's discretionary determination rather than any fixed reauthorization pressure; per the Flag 6 emergency-powers logic (EO 9066 anchor) this fires.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Sec. 3 bars any attachment, garnishment, or execution against the seized property without the Director's prior written consent, disabling ordinary judicial process that would otherwise reach the property.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The seized operation runs directly under the Director's control with the war effort as the object; there is no self-reporting compliance regime whose violations would require an external actor to surface them.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: By transferring to the Director the President's Sec. 1 seizure powers under the Act of August 29, 1916 and cutting off creditor process (Sec. 3), the order extends a reusable template for wartime seizure of private transportation and exclusion of ordinary legal remedies applicable well beyond this one company.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Sec. 5 expressly transfers to the Director functions, powers, and duties vested by statute in the Secretary of War under the 1916 Act, redirecting authority another official already held by statute.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order targets one named company's facilities and creates no exemption or waiver structure that could swallow its coverage.

note: Wartime plant-seizure order; predecessor EO 8989 (creating the Office of Defense Transportation) was reviewed and confirms the Director's role, but order-182 does not amend it, only preserves ODT-issued operating terms (Sec. 2). Zombie Emergency Trap checked and does not fire: the invoked wartime statutes were active, not lapsed, in June 1945.

title: President's Foreign Intelligence Advisory Board

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Board's members, Chairman, Vice Chairman, and Executive Director all serve at the pleasure of and are appointed by the President, and the Board reports directly to and is staffed as authorized by the President (Secs. 1, 3), pooling control of this oversight body entirely in the President with no external check.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Board only advises and recommends (Secs. 3-4); it has no authority to compel correction, and if the intelligence community fails to act on its findings no named actor bears both the obligation and capacity to remedy the failure.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order does one thing—establish a single advisory board with defined review functions—without packaging distinct policy questions or conflating separate problem frames.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Board is a purely advisory review body with no enforcement authority over any regulated party, so there is no enforcement mechanism whose clarity could be assessed.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: An advisory board assessing intelligence quality and reporting to the President creates no design feature rewarding actors for undermining that purpose; no lapsed statute is invoked so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The Board is established with no termination date or reauthorization requirement, continuing indefinitely until revoked; a standing presidential advisory body of this kind (contrast the self-terminating committee in EO 9981 Sec. 6) has no built-in reauthorization pressure.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds a new advisory review layer over the intelligence community and removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism; per the direction rule, a newly-added (even non-binding) check does not fire this flag.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The Board is itself the reviewing body reporting to the President; there is no regulated-party compliance regime for which external third-party standing to surface violations would apply.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Sec. 5 grants the Board access 'to the full extent permitted by applicable law to all information necessary,' establishing a standing, presidentially-controlled channel into all intelligence-community information whose precedent and reach extend beyond mere quality assessment.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Board reviews and advises intelligence agencies (including the DCI and CIA) but does not redirect or countermand any agency's statutory operational mandate; its role is layered above, not in conflict with, existing mandates.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order creates an advisory board, not a coverage regime with a population that could claim exemptions.

note: Establishes a presidential advisory board; no predecessor is amended by number. Compliance-regime flags 4, 8, 11 are NA because the Board is an advisory reviewer, not a regulator of any ongoing compliance relationship.

title: Authorizing the Secretary of the Navy to Take Possession of and Operate the Plant of the Howarth Pivoted Bearings Company at Philadelphia, Pennsylvania

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of the Navy is directed to seize and operate the plant and may 'exercise any existing contractual or other rights of the said company, and take such other steps as may be necessary or desirable,' concentrating open-ended operational authority in one official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Termination rests solely on the President's determination that the plant will be operated privately 'in a manner consistent with the war effort,' with no independent standard or actor to judge or correct the seizure's success.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order addresses a single action—seizure and operation of one named plant—with no distinct policy questions packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Secretary may 'take such other steps as may be necessary or desirable' and 'do all things necessary or incidental,' an open-ended standard under which different officials could in good faith reach substantially different operating decisions.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The seizure's purpose (effective wartime production) is directly advanced by operating the plant, with no design feature rewarding actors for undermining it; no lapsed statute is invoked, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This exceptional wartime seizure has a natural end-state (the war effort) but its termination depends solely on a discretionary presidential determination rather than any fixed reauthorization mechanism, matching the EO 9066 emergency-powers pattern that fires this flag.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Unlike order-182, this order contains no clause barring judicial process or attachment; it removes no pre-existing external oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The Navy operates the plant directly for wartime production; there is no self-reporting compliance regime whose violations an external party would need standing to surface.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Authorizing the Secretary to exercise 'any existing contractual or other rights of the said company' establishes a reusable precedent for the executive to assume a private firm's contractual position through wartime seizure, extendable to other plants.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order directs a single department (the Navy) to act and does not redirect or countermand another agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order targets one named plant and creates no exemption or waiver structure.

note: Wartime single-plant seizure with no predecessor amended by number. Scores lower than order-182 chiefly because it lacks the creditor-process bar (Flag 7) and the statutory power-transfer from another official (Flag 10).

title: Performance of Commercial Activities

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Director of OMB controls implementation guidance, cost-comparison methodology, savings-estimate exceptions, and the tracking system (Sec. 2), concentrating authority over how the privatization studies are conducted and judged in a single office.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Agencies self-conduct A-76 studies of their own activities and report their own progress 'through the Director of OMB' (Sec. 1(h)), so the implementing agencies also assess whether they are meeting the order's goals, with no independent actor able to correct failure.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order pursues one coherent objective—systematic A-76 review of commercial activities for privatization—without packaging unrelated policy questions under a single frame.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Key triggers turn on undefined terms—private performance is required except where costs are 'unreasonable' (Sec. 1(a)) and savings estimates may be excepted at OMB discretion (Sec. 1(e))—leaving officials to reach substantially different determinations in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Sec. 1(e) lets agencies retain expected first-year savings as 'incentive compensation to reward employees,' creating a design incentive to project or book conversions to contract for budgetary reward independent of whether privatization actually serves economy and efficiency.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order imposes an open-ended, recurring obligation to study 'not less than 3 percent' annually 'until all identified potential commercial activities have been studied' (Sec. 1(d)) with no termination or reauthorization mechanism, continuing indefinitely until revoked.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds OMB tracking and public-review publication requirements (Sec. 2(b),(c)) and removes no pre-existing oversight; Sec. 5 preserves existing procurement procedures, so nothing is excluded or bypassed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement rests on agency self-study and self-reporting; while Sec. 2(b) requires public publication of inventories and lists, no external actor is given standing or incentive to challenge an agency's own cost comparisons, so surfacing gamed studies depends on the regulated agencies themselves.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Institutionalizing recurring 3-percent-per-year privatization studies with retained-savings incentives creates a standing structural pressure toward contracting-out across the federal workforce that persists and compounds well beyond any single year's studies.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: OMB and OPM are assigned coordinating and personnel-policy roles consistent with their existing functions; the order creates a hierarchy under OMB rather than countermanding another agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Sec. 1(a) exempts activities where 'statute or national security requires government performance or where private industry costs are unreasonable,' and Sec. 1(e) allows OMB-authorized exceptions—discretionary carve-outs, the 'unreasonable cost' trigger being broad enough for agencies to engineer qualification.

note: Government-wide privatization-study framework; no predecessor amended by number (references OMB Circular A-76 by reference, not an EO). Broad ongoing compliance regime, so all four compliance-regime flags apply.

title: Chemical and Biological Weapons Proliferation

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of State unilaterally determines which foreign persons and countries have violated the standards and which sanctions to impose or waive (Secs. 4-5), and controls duration (Sec. 6), concentrating sweeping determinative authority in one cabinet officer.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The same Secretaries who determine violations also determine whether sanctions should be lifted or not applied 'due to significant foreign policy or national security reasons' (Sec. 5(c)), so the implementing officials also judge their own enforcement, and Sec. 8 forecloses any external party from enforcing the order.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order coherently addresses a single declared threat (chemical and biological weapons proliferation) through negotiation, export controls, and sanctions; these are integrated components of one frame, not distinct policy questions forced together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Sanctions turn on discretionary determinations that a foreign person 'knowingly and materially contributed' (Sec. 4(b)(1)) or that lifting a sanction serves 'foreign policy or national security interests' (Sec. 6), standards open enough that different officials could reach substantially different enforcement decisions in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The sanctions design aligns actor incentives with the stated purpose (Sec. 4(b)(4) lifts sanctions only on cessation of proliferation activity); the order invokes an active, newly-declared national emergency under IEEPA/NEA, not a lapsed statute, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order declares a national emergency and continues sanctions in force until the Secretary discretionarily determines lifting is warranted (Sec. 6); emergency-based authority of this kind has a natural end-state but is given no fixed reauthorization mechanism, matching the EO 9066 pattern.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Sec. 8 provides the order creates no right or benefit enforceable at law against the United States, insulating the sweeping sanction determinations from judicial review that might otherwise reach them.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement is a direct government function—the Secretaries impose sanctions on foreign persons and countries—rather than a self-reporting regime requiring an external actor with standing and incentive to surface violations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order builds a reusable, expandable sanctions architecture keyed to a single Secretary's proliferation determinations, establishing precedent for executive sanctioning of foreign persons and countries (including import bans and landing-rights termination) applicable to adjacent national-security domains.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order allocates roles among State, Commerce, Treasury, and Defense with consultation requirements (Sec. 7) that coordinate rather than countermand their existing statutory authorities.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Sec. 4(b)(5) permits the Secretaries to grant 'appropriate exemptions' for military requirements, sole-source suppliers, spare parts, servicing, humanitarian items, and pre-existing contracts, and Sec. 5(c) lets any individual country sanction be waived for foreign-policy reasons—broad discretionary carve-outs concentrated in the Secretaries.

note: IEEPA/NEA national-emergency sanctions order; references EO 12730 by number but only as an authority the Secretary uses, not amending it, so no diff comparison was called for. Zombie Emergency Trap checked: the emergency is freshly declared and active, not lapsed.

title: Tribal Colleges and Universities

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Authority is distributed across the Secretary of Education, the White House Initiative Office, participating agencies, and a Board of Advisors with defined roles (Secs. 2-5); no single official holds unchecked determinative power over the program's core functions.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each agency sets its own Five-Year Plan goals and self-reports annual performance against its own objectives (Sec. 5(a),(c)), and the Board and Initiative only advise and combine reports with no enforcement authority, so an agency that fails its self-set goals faces no actor with power to correct it.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order's multiple purposes all coherently serve the single stated aim of strengthening tribal colleges' access to federal resources; they are complementary facets of one frame, not distinct policy questions forced into a package.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Agency obligations are hedged throughout with 'to the extent permitted by law,' 'strive to include,' and self-set 'annual goals' (Secs. 4, 5), leaving the actual standard of required conduct undefined enough that agencies could in good faith reach very different levels of effort.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Because each agency sets its own participation goals and measures itself against them (Sec. 5(a)(3), 5(c)), a rational agency is incentivized to set undemanding goals it can easily report meeting, undermining the order's aim of meaningfully increased resource commitment; no lapsed statute is invoked so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Sec. 7 provides the Board terminates two years after the date of the order unless affirmatively renewed by the President, and Sec. 5 frames the substantive work as a defined Five-Year Plan—an explicit reauthorization mechanism, so this absence flag does not fire.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds new reporting, planning, and advisory-board oversight layers and removes no pre-existing external check; per the direction rule, added (even imperfect) machinery does not fire this flag.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The program's progress depends on agency self-assessment reported up through the Initiative Office; while the Board reviews reports, no external actor with independent standing and access is empowered to surface an agency's shortfall against its plan beyond that internal review.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order builds durable institutional machinery (a standing White House Initiative Office, agency liaisons, integrated Five-Year Plans, cross-references to EOs 12928 and 12999) that establishes a replicable template for targeted set-aside coordination across the executive branch beyond tribal colleges specifically.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Participating agencies retain their own program authority and are asked to coordinate and detail personnel 'with their consent' and 'to the extent permitted by law' (Secs. 3-4); the order layers coordination on top of existing mandates rather than countermanding them.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order sets participation goals but creates no exemption or waiver structure carving actors out of its coverage; 'to the extent permitted by law' is a legal-limits savings clause, not a discretionary exemption gutting the rule.

note: References EO 12928 (reviewed; SDB/HBCU/MI procurement goals) and EO 12999 only as cross-references incorporating their goal frameworks, not amending them. PRESENT on Flags 2,4,5,8,9 (raw 5); notable that Sec. 7's explicit two-year termination clears Flag 6.

title: Exemption of Edward M. Weeks From Compulsory Retirement for Age

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A one-time, individually-directed exemption exercising a specific statutory power (sec. 204 of the Act of June 30, 1932) for one named person; no ongoing discretionary authority is pooled in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The action is self-executing and fixed in duration (six months ending February 29, 1940); there is no implementation chain that could fail and no ongoing responsibility to trace.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order contains a single provision exempting one named individual, with nothing to bundle against.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time individual retirement exemption creates no ongoing compliance relationship or enforcement standard for anyone.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral rule is created for any actor class; the statute invoked (Act of June 30, 1932) is active, not lapsed, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The exemption is expressly time-limited to a six-month period ending February 29, 1940—a built-in expiration—so this absence flag does not fire (and the underlying authority is a one-time exercise, not standing authority).

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order exercises a statutory exemption power and removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no ongoing enforcement regime for which third-party standing to surface violations could be relevant.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A single-person, six-month retirement exemption carries no systemic or precedential implications identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency's statutory mandate is implicated or redirected by exempting one Treasury employee from age retirement.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order is itself a single individual exemption, not a coverage regime with an exemption structure that could swallow a rule; there is no population of coverage to map exemptions against.

note: Narrow individual personnel action extending prior exemptions (EO 7413, reviewed; identical single-person structure). Only the always-answerable decision-structure flags plus time-limited Flag 6 apply, all clean. Compliance-regime and systemic flags are NA per the NA-vs-ABSENT test.

title: Modifying Executive Order of June 8, 1926, Creating Reservoir Site Reserve No. 17, Pacific Slope Basins, California

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A self-executing land modification enabling the Federal Power Commission to license transmission-line projects on described lands; no discretionary authority is newly pooled in any single official, and the modification operates on identified parcels by its own terms.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The action is a defined land-status modification with a built-in condition (licensees must remove works at their own expense when lands are needed for reservoir purposes); no open implementation chain lacks a responsible actor.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order contains a single provision modifying one reservoir-site reserve for a defined purpose, with nothing to bundle against.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: This is a one-time modification of land-reserve status; it establishes no ongoing enforcement standard applied to a regulated population (the removal condition is a specific licensing term set by the FPC's own licenses, not an enforcement regime created here).

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The modification creates no ongoing behavioral rule generating incentives for any actor class; the land-act statutes invoked are active, not lapsed, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a one-time, self-executing modification of a land reserve with no standing grant of authority that could require reauthorization or sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order modifies a land reserve to enable FPC licensing and removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism; nothing is excluded or bypassed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing self-reporting enforcement regime is created for which third-party standing to surface violations would apply.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A parcel-specific reservoir-reserve modification enabling transmission-line licensing carries no systemic or precedential implications identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order enables the Federal Power Commission to license within the reserve, coordinated with the Secretary of the Interior's reservoir needs; it accommodates rather than countermands either body's mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order modifies land-reserve coverage for a specific purpose but establishes no exemption or waiver structure against a population of coverage.

note: Narrow land-reserve modification; predecessor is an unnumbered Executive Order of June 8, 1926 (not citeable by number in referenced-predecessors), so a full diff could not be made, but the modification is a bounded, conditioned enabling of FPC licensing. Flag 10 is ABSENT (two bodies coordinated cleanly) rather than NA.

title: Revoking Executive Order No. 8458 of June 27, 1940, as Amended by Executive Order No. 8532 of September 4, 1940, and Authorizing Persons Appointed from the Emergency Replacement List To Acquire a Classified Civil Service Status

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order revokes a prior replacement-list authority and lets qualifying appointees acquire classified status under fixed statutory and pre-existing criteria (Act of Nov. 26, 1940; EO 8743); status accrual follows defined conditions rather than pooling discretionary authority in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Eligibility runs on objective, verifiable conditions (appointment from the list after June 30, 1941, entry on duty by Dec. 31, 1942, six months' continuous service); there is no open-ended implementation whose failure lacks a responsible actor.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Revoking the Emergency Replacement List and granting a status pathway to persons already appointed from it are two facets of a single coherent civil-service transition, not distinct policy questions forced together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The eligibility conditions for acquiring classified status are precise and date-bounded (specific service dates and durations, cross-referenced to the Act of Nov. 26, 1940 and EO 8743), leaving little room for divergent good-faith application.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order regularizes the status of already-appointed employees on fixed criteria, creating no incentive to undermine the civil-service system; the invoked statutes are active, not lapsed, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order's operative effects are date-bounded (revocation effective Dec. 31, 1941; eligibility windows closing Dec. 31, 1942), and it operates within the standing civil-service framework maintained through routine supersession (it revokes EO 8458 as amended by EO 8532), so no open-ended authority lacks reauthorization pressure.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Status is acquired 'in accordance with the applicable provisions' of the governing Act and EO 8743, preserving the Civil Service Commission's ordinary examination and rule framework rather than removing any pre-existing oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Classified-status determinations run through the Civil Service Commission's existing procedures under EO 8743 and the governing Act, an external administrative check rather than pure self-certification by the affected employees.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order winds down a temporary wartime replacement list and channels a defined cohort into classified status on existing terms; it establishes no reusable precedent or definitional expansion beyond this transition identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Civil Service Commission acts within its own statutory mandate over classified-service status; no second agency's authority is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The one carve-out (temporary census-force employees appointed through civil service examinations, in para. 1) is narrow and specifically defined, not a broad or discretionary exemption capable of swallowing the rule.

note: Diff-aware: predecessors EO 8458 (Emergency Replacement List) and EO 8743 (classified-service extension) were reviewed; order-190 cleanly revokes the temporary list and regularizes appointees under EO 8743's existing, procedurally-checked framework, strengthening rather than weakening the status mechanism. All eleven flags applicable (an ongoing status-determination relationship exists) and all ABSENT.

title: Amending the Selective Service Regulations Concerning the Ordering of Registrants for Induction

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The amendment operates through local boards acting under a defined regulatory framework (Selective Service Regulations, 32 CFR Ch. XVI); it adds a narrow procedural rule and does not pool eligibility, enforcement, or interpretive authority in a single unaccountable official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility rests with the local board acting under Section 1631.7 within the existing Selective Service structure; the failure chain is traceable to a named, statutorily-supervised body.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order contains a single, discrete procedural amendment (allowing induction ordering of a registrant who refused the physical exam) and bundles no distinct policy questions or problem frames.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The trigger is precise — a Class I-A or I-A-O registrant who 'refused or otherwise failed to comply with an order... to report for and submit to an armed forces physical examination' — and the consequence (may be selected and ordered to report, with acceptability determined after reporting but before induction) is clearly specified.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The amendment closes a gap by which a registrant could avoid induction by refusing the physical exam, aligning the incentive with compliance rather than rewarding evasion; the underlying Military Selective Service Act of 1967 is active and unexpired, so no Zombie Emergency Trap arises.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a standing procedural amendment to an ongoing administrative framework (Selective Service Regulations) that is maintained through routine supersession — this order itself amends a chain of numbered predecessor orders (10001, 10202, 10659, etc.) — placing it in the standing-procedural-framework evergreen category rather than firing for absent sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The amendment removes no existing oversight mechanism; it operates 'notwithstanding Part 1628 or any other provision of these regulations' only to sequence the physical exam after reporting, and preserves the acceptability determination as a precondition to actual induction.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement does not depend on self-reporting; the local board directly observes non-compliance with its own reporting orders and acts on it, so no missing third-party surfacing mechanism is at issue.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: This is a narrow procedural sequencing change to induction ordering with no identifiable precedential reach into unrelated domains or downstream effects beyond its stated purpose.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the Selective Service System and its local boards are implicated; the order redirects or countermands no other agency's statutory mandate, and the armed forces physical exam continues under existing arrangements.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The amendment creates no exemption structure; it narrows an avenue by which a registrant could effectively remove himself from induction eligibility, tightening rather than loosening coverage.

note: Partial amendment of a single regulatory provision (Sec. 1631.7) within an ongoing, routinely-superseded administrative framework; assessed on its own baseline per the diff-aware convention. Clean architecture across all flags.

title: Establishing the War Relocation Authority in the Executive Office of the President and Defining Its Functions and Duties

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A single Director, appointed by and responsible only to the President, is authorized and directed to formulate and effectuate the entire program of removal, relocation, maintenance, and supervision of designated persons, with sweeping authority to make delegations, employ personnel, expend funds, purchase real property, and make loans and grants (Secs. 2-3, 5) — authority over the lives and liberty of an excluded population pooled in one official with no external check, defeating any accountable design.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Director reports only to the President ('keep the President informed... and perform such related duties as the President may from time to time assign,' Sec. 9); no independent body has obligation or capacity to correct failure in the forced relocation and supervision of the affected persons, and the Liaison Committee (Sec. 8) merely 'assists' at the Director's call with no enforcement authority.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order addresses a single problem frame — administering the removal and relocation of persons designated under EO 9066 — and does not package distinct remedies or conflate multiple problem frames requiring separate accountability.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Director may 'prescribe regulations necessary or desirable,' provide for needs 'in such manner as may be appropriate,' and enlist persons into a War Relocation Work Corps under terms he sets (Secs. 3(d), 3(b), 7); the standards governing supervision, employment terms, and compulsory work are left entirely to the Director's discretion, so two officials could apply the program in substantially different ways.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order authorizes the Director to provide for 'employment of such persons at useful work' and to enlist them in a War Relocation Work Corps 'for the duration of the present war' with compensation he prescribes (Secs. 3(b), 7), creating an incentive for the implementing authority to extract labor from a captive population on terms it controls; the underlying wartime authority (EO 9066) was active and unexpired, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is an emergency/exceptional-powers grant of exactly the kind that should have a natural end tied to the emergency's resolution — mirroring the EO 9066 anchor which scores PRESENT — yet the Authority and Director's program carry no sunset or reauthorization trigger, persisting until revoked (the Work Corps is tied to 'the duration of the present war' but the Authority itself is not).

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order directs that 'Departments and agencies of the United States are directed to cooperate with and assist the Director' and subordinates other agencies' involvement to his findings (Sec. 6); combined with reporting solely to the President and no provision for judicial or congressional review of the relocation program, it channels authority into a body outside ordinary external checks over the affected persons.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No external actor with standing and incentive is given access to surface abuses in the relocation, maintenance, or supervision of the interned population; oversight of the Director's conduct depends entirely on the President and the Director's own reporting, with the affected persons themselves stripped of ordinary recourse.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order operationalizes and institutionalizes the mass removal, relocation, and supervision of an entire class of persons designated by race/national origin under EO 9066, establishing a durable federal apparatus and precedent for detaining and controlling a civilian population without individualized process — a second-order institutional entrenchment that vastly deepens and normalizes the underlying deprivation of liberty.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order carves relocation functions out of and layered onto the War Department's and Attorney General's/Alien Property Custodian's existing roles (Secs. 5, 6, 10), directing the War and Justice Departments to provide 'protective, police, and investigational services' the Director finds necessary and requiring coordination to 'avoid duplication of evacuation activities,' creating overlapping mandates and jurisdictional friction with agencies already holding statutory roles.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order contains no waiver or carve-out structure; coverage of the designated persons is defined by EO 9066's designations, and this order provides no discretionary exemption mechanism that could swallow its own program.

note: EO 9102 operationalizes the EO 9066 mass-exclusion regime (predecessor text reviewed in referenced-predecessors), creating a single-Director authority over the relocation, maintenance, supervision, and compelled labor of an interned civilian population; scored on its own architecture, which collapses core accountability and concentrates unchecked power (CRITICAL on Flags 1, 2, 9), paralleling the EO 9066 anchor pattern.

title: Liquidation of War Agencies

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A Director of Liquidation coordinates winding-up efforts but must act 'in consultation with a Liquidation Advisory Committee' of seven agencies (Sec. 1), and Sec. 3 expressly bars divesting any agency of its own liquidation functions — authority is coordinative and advisory-checked, not pooled.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Director has a defined duty to 'further proper preparations... for the orderly and timely winding up' (Sec. 2), works with a named multi-agency committee, and each war agency retains responsibility for its own liquidation; responsibility is traceable.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order addresses a single coherent function — coordinating the orderly liquidation of temporary war agencies — and bundles no distinct policy questions or problem frames.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Director's role is advisory and coordinative (develop plans, receive reports, establish uniform practices, suggest, advise and assist per Sec. 2(a)-(f)); there is no coercive enforcement standard to be vague about, and the coordination duties are specified.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order's aim is to accelerate and improve liquidation, and the Director gains no expanding authority from failure — his function is inherently self-terminating as war agencies wind down; the First War Powers Act, 1941 is the cited authority and is not acknowledged as lapsed, so no Zombie Emergency Trap.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order establishes a Director of Liquidation and coordinating apparatus with no termination trigger, review, or reauthorization requirement — an office tied to a transitional, inherently temporary function (winding up war agencies) that should track a natural end-state yet is left to persist until revoked.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order removes no existing oversight; Sec. 3 explicitly preserves each agency's functions and reserves final winding-up transfers to the Treasury or other departments, and named oversight bodies (GAO, Bureau of the Budget, Civil Service Commission) sit on the Advisory Committee.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Compliance is surfaced through 'periodic reports which shall be submitted by each war agency' to the Director and a multi-agency advisory committee (including GAO and the Bureau of the Budget) with access and standing to review liquidation status (Secs. 1, 2(c)).

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: This is an internal executive housekeeping order to coordinate the dismantling of temporary agencies, with no identifiable precedential reach or downstream effect beyond its stated purpose.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Multiple agencies are implicated, but Sec. 3 expressly provides that 'nothing in this order shall be deemed to divest any agency... of any of its functions,' preventing mandate conflict; coordination among the named bodies is clean, so this scores ABSENT rather than firing.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order creates no exemption or waiver structure over any regulated population; Sec. 3's carve-out simply preserves existing agency functions rather than exempting anyone from coverage.

note: A coordinative, advisory-checked internal liquidation mechanism with an explicit non-divestiture clause (Sec. 3); the only flag firing is absent-sunset (Flag 6), since a transitional wind-down office should have a natural end-state but lacks a termination trigger.

title: President's Commission on Strategic Forces

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Commission is a purely advisory body that 'provide[s] appropriate advice to the President, the National Security Council, and the Department of Defense' (Sec. 2); it holds no decision authority over eligibility, enforcement, or implementation to concentrate.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Commission has a defined deliverable — 'report to the President by February 18, 1983' (Sec. 2(b)) — and administration is assigned to the Secretary of Defense (Sec. 3(c)); responsibility is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order establishes a single advisory commission with one review task (strategic modernization / ICBM basing) and bundles no distinct remedies or problem frames.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Commission is advisory with no compliance rule, standard of conduct, or enforcement mechanism imposed on any party, so there is no ongoing regulatory relationship for Vague Enforcement to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order creates no behavioral rule or regulated-party relationship — a time-limited advisory commission generates no compliance incentive for any actor class to game; underlying authority (FACA, Constitution) is active, so no Zombie Trap.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order includes an explicit sunset: 'The Commission shall terminate 30 days after its report, unless sooner extended' (Sec. 4(b)), and a fixed reporting deadline — a positive finding of a built-in expiration.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order removes no pre-existing oversight; it operates within FACA and, while shielding Commission information from public inspection 'to the extent permitted by law' (Sec. 3(a)), it reserves FACA's congressional reporting function and adds no removal of existing external checks.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no ongoing enforcement or compliance regime with violations for a third party to surface — the Commission produces a one-time advisory report — so the third-party-standing question does not map onto this instrument.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A time-limited advisory commission reporting on strategic-force modernization has no identifiable precedential reach into unrelated domains or durable institutional change; while its information is exempted from public inspection, that clause is bounded 'to the extent permitted by law' and does not establish transferable precedent.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Secretary of Defense supplies administrative support and performs FACA functions (Secs. 3-4); the Commission is advisory and redirects or countermands no agency's statutory operational mandate, so coordination is clean.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order imposes no coverage on a regulated population from which exemptions could be granted; it merely constitutes an advisory body, so there is no exemptions architecture to assess.

note: A clean, FACA-compliant, time-limited advisory commission with an explicit termination clause and fixed reporting deadline; the four compliance-regime flags (4, 5, 8, 11) are NOT_APPLICABLE because the order creates no ongoing behavioral or compliance relationship.

title: Providing for Stabilization of Prices, Rents, Wages, and Salaries

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Sec. 3(a) delegates to a single Cost of Living Council 'all of the powers conferred on the President by the Economic Stabilization Act of 1970,' and Sec. 4 lets the Council alone define terms, grant exemptions, issue binding regulations and orders, and redelegate to any agency or official — a nationwide price/wage/rent control power pooled in one body it chairs, with no external check built into the design.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Council both administers the freeze and controls its own definitions, exemptions, and record requirements (Secs. 4, 5), and it 'in its discretion' decides whether to request enforcement via the Justice Department (Sec. 7(b)); the body that determines compliance also determines whether and when to enforce, leaving no independent actor obligated to correct implementation failure.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order pursues a single coherent frame — economy-wide stabilization of prices, rents, wages, and salaries — and although it covers several categories, they are all instances of the same stabilization remedy rather than distinct unrelated policy questions forced into one package.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The substantive ceilings turn on undefined judgment calls — 'a substantial volume of actual transactions' and 'like or similar commodities or services' (Sec. 1(a)) — and the Council may 'make exceptions or grant exemptions' and take 'such other actions as it determines to be necessary and appropriate' (Sec. 4(a)); two officials could in good faith reach different determinations of what price a given seller may charge.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A price/wage freeze against pre-freeze 'highest' levels rewards actors who had inflated prices during the base period and creates incentives to game definitions, reclassify goods as 'like or similar,' and seek discretionary exemptions from a Council empowered to grant them (Secs. 1, 4-5); the Economic Stabilization Act of 1970 is invoked as active and unexpired, so no Zombie Emergency Trap fires.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The 90-day freeze in Sec. 1 has a fixed term, but the Cost of Living Council itself is established with no termination or reauthorization trigger and is expressly directed to 'develop and recommend... additional policies... after the expiration of the 90-day period' (Sec. 3(b)) — an emergency-stabilization authority whose institutional apparatus is designed to persist past the crisis window without a natural end-state or reauthorization pressure.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order removes no pre-existing external check; enforcement runs through statutory penalties and Justice Department injunction actions under Sec. 205 of the Act (Sec. 7), and it neither excludes judicial review nor disables existing oversight of the stabilization authority.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Sellers must 'maintain available for public inspection a record of the highest prices or rents charged' (Sec. 1(b)), giving consumers and competitors visible information and standing to surface overcharges, so enforcement does not rest solely on regulated-party self-reporting.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Delegating 'all of the powers' of the Economic Stabilization Act to a presidentially-chaired council with authority to define terms, grant exemptions, and redelegate establishes a durable precedent and institutional template for peacetime executive control over the entire economy's prices and wages — an authority claim extensible well beyond the immediate 90-day freeze.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Council may 'redelegate to any agency, instrumentality or official of the United States any authority under this Order' and 'utilize the services of any other agencies, Federal or State' (Sec. 4(b)), directing existing departments and agencies to furnish assistance and information (Secs. 4(c)-(d)) in service of a cross-cutting control regime that overlays their statutory mandates without corresponding legislative reallocation.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The Council has broad, largely unbounded discretion to 'make exceptions or grant exemptions' (Sec. 4(a)(ii)) and to 'make such exemptions from any requirement otherwise imposed as are consistent with the purposes of this Order' (Sec. 5), with only a self-referential 'consistent with the purposes' standard — exemption power concentrated in the single administering body with no external check on grants.

note: Economy-wide wage/price/rent freeze delegating 'all' Economic Stabilization Act powers to a single presidentially-chaired Cost of Living Council with self-controlled definitions and exemptions; Flag 1 rises to CRITICAL as the concentration of nationwide control authority in one body defines the order's architecture, with multiple related weaknesses PRESENT.

title: Science and Technology Policy Functions

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order distributes transferred statutory functions across the Directors of OSTP, OMB, and NSF along defined subject lines (Secs. 2-5) under Reorganization Plan No. 1 of 1977; authority is dispersed among multiple named officials rather than pooled in one, each performing functions 'in coordination with' others.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each delegated function is assigned to a specifically named Director with defined coordination obligations, and OMB's Director is charged to 'make such determinations, issue such orders, and take all actions necessary or appropriate to effectuate the transfers' (Sec. 7); responsibility is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order executes a single coherent reorganization — effecting the transfers provided by Reorganization Plan No. 1 of 1977 and re-delegating the affected science-and-technology functions — without packaging unrelated policy questions or conflating problem frames.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: This is an internal delegation-and-reorganization order that reassigns functions among executive officials; it imposes no compliance standard or enforcement mechanism on any regulated party, so there is no ongoing regulatory relationship for Vague Enforcement to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order creates no behavioral rule for any regulated actor class — it allocates policy functions internally — so there is no incentive structure to assess; the cited authorities (Reorganization Plan No. 1 of 1977, the Act) are active, so no Zombie Trap.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a standing administrative reorganization implementing a statutory reorganization plan and maintained through ordinary supersession; its subject matter (permanent allocation of science-and-technology policy functions) is evergreen administrative structure with no natural end-state that reauthorization pressure would track, so absent-sunset does not fire.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order removes no external oversight; it abolishes certain advisory panels/committees per the Reorganization Plan and re-establishes a Federal Coordinating Council and an Intergovernmental Advisory Panel under FACA (Secs. 1(b), 2(d), 5) — reshuffling advisory machinery, not disabling pre-existing checks.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no enforcement or compliance regime with violations for any third party to surface — the order reallocates internal executive functions — so the third-party-standing question does not map onto this instrument.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order effects a bounded, statutorily-authorized reallocation of specific science-and-technology functions among named offices, with records, property, and personnel transferred accordingly (Sec. 6); no precedential reach into unrelated domains or systemic downstream effect is identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
