flags.justification: Although multiple offices (OSTP, OMB, NSF) are implicated, the reallocation is expressly authorized by Reorganization Plan No. 1 of 1977 and the underlying Act, and functions are assigned along clean subject lines with coordination clauses (Secs. 2-3), so it reflects authorized reorganization rather than mandate conflict — ABSENT, not firing.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order imposes no coverage on a regulated population from which exemptions could be granted; it is an internal function-transfer order, so there is no exemptions architecture to assess.

note: A statutorily-grounded internal reorganization implementing Reorganization Plan No. 1 of 1977, distributing science-and-technology functions across named offices with clean coordination clauses; compliance-regime flags (4, 5, 8, 11) are NOT_APPLICABLE as no ongoing behavioral relationship is created. No numbered EO predecessor is amended, so no diff-aware comparison was required.

title: Authorizing the Secretary of War to Take Possession of and Operate the Plants and Facilities of the Springfield Plywood Corporation, Located At Springfield, Oregon

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of War alone is authorized and directed to seize and operate the plant, 'to the extent that he may deem necessary' take any property, exercise the corporation's contractual rights, employ persons, and 'take any other steps that he deems necessary' (Sec. 1) — broad seizure-and-operation authority pooled in a single official with the exercise gated on his own judgment.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of War is the single named, accountable actor responsible for the seizure and operation and is bound to specified conditions — operate under existing employment terms (Sec. 2), preserve existing management to the maximum degree (Sec. 3), and terminate possession within 60 days of restoring productive efficiency (Sec. 5); responsibility is clearly located and its failure chain traceable.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order addresses a single, discrete action — seizure and operation of one named plant to end a war-effort-impeding labor interruption — and bundles no distinct policy questions or problem frames.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The operative standard is defined: the Secretary operates under 'the terms and conditions of employment which are in effect at the time possession thereof is taken, subject to... Section 5 of the War Labor Disputes Act' (Sec. 2), and the termination trigger (restoration of prior productive efficiency, then within 60 days per Sec. 5) is specified rather than left to open-ended discretion.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The seizure is bound to a specific factual condition (restoration of pre-interruption productive efficiency) that triggers mandatory termination within 60 days (Sec. 5), giving the implementing authority no incentive to prolong control for its own benefit; authority rests on the active, unexpired Selective Training and Service Act of 1940 as amended by the War Labor Disputes Act, so no Zombie Emergency Trap fires.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order contains an express termination trigger — possession 'shall be terminated by the Secretary of War within 60 days after he determines that the productive efficiency... has been restored' (Sec. 5) — a built-in end condition, so the absence-of-sunset flag does not fire.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order removes no pre-existing oversight; it expressly operates 'subject to the provisions of Section 5 of the War Labor Disputes Act' (Sec. 2) and directs other federal agencies to cooperate, without excluding or disabling any existing review mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The seizure operates under existing terms of employment and the War Labor Disputes Act framework, with continuing plant management retained (Sec. 3) and the affected workforce present; compliance is not left to unverifiable self-reporting by a single regulated party, and the statutory labor framework provides parties with standing over the operation's terms.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Exercising the plant-seizure power to resolve a single labor dispute reinforces a broadly-usable executive precedent — federal takeover of private facilities based on the President's 'find[ing]' that a labor interruption impedes the war effort — an authority claim applicable to other plants and disputes beyond this one facility (the same category of power later contested in the steel-seizure context).

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Other federal bodies (War Manpower Commission, Selective Service, Department of Justice) are 'directed to cooperate... to the fullest extent possible' (Sec. 4) in support of the Secretary of War's operation; this is cooperative assistance, not redirection or countermanding of any agency's own statutory mandate, so coordination is clean.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: This is a one-time seizure-and-operation of a single named facility; it imposes no ongoing regulatory coverage on a population from which exemptions could be granted, so there is no exemptions architecture to assess.

note: A single-facility wartime plant seizure with a clean design — express termination trigger (Sec. 5), preserved employment terms and management, and statutory labor-framework subjection; Flag 11 is NOT_APPLICABLE (no ongoing coverage). The two firings reflect single-official seizure power (Flag 1) and the transferable seizure precedent (Flag 9).

title: Waiver Under the Trade Act of 1974 With Respect to Tajikistan and Turkmenistan

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order is a single, self-executing exercise of a statutory waiver power expressly conditioned on the President 'having made the report to the Congress required by section 402(c)(2)'; it concentrates no ongoing discretionary authority in any official beyond this one-time act.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The action is a defined statutory waiver by the President with a required congressional report already made; the responsible actor and the statutory basis are clearly identified, leaving no diffuse or circular accountability.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order performs a single discrete act — waiving sections 402(a) and 402(b) of the Trade Act with respect to two named countries under one statutory provision — and bundles no distinct policy questions.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: This is a one-time waiver of statutory trade restrictions; it establishes no ongoing compliance rule or enforcement standard for any party, so there is no enforcement mechanism to assess for vagueness.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A one-time waiver creates no behavioral rule or regulated-party relationship generating incentives to assess; the Trade Act of 1974 authority is active and expressly 'continues to apply... pursuant to section 402(d),' so no Zombie Emergency Trap arises.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Section 402(c) waivers under the Trade Act are of a kind that carry a natural end-state — they are conventionally subject to periodic renewal/review — yet this order's text states the waiver with no expiration or reauthorization trigger of its own, leaving continuation to default rather than affirmative reauthorization within the order.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Far from removing oversight, the waiver is expressly predicated on the statutorily-required report to Congress 'having [been] made'; it disables no pre-existing external check.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The order imposes no compliance obligation on any regulated party, so there is no violation for an external third party to surface — the third-party-standing question does not map onto a one-time waiver.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: This is a narrow, statutorily-bounded waiver of specified trade restrictions for two named countries with no identifiable precedential reach beyond the routine exercise the Trade Act contemplates.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order implicates no second agency's operational mandate — it is a direct presidential waiver under the Trade Act — so there is no inter-agency mandate conflict to assess.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The waiver itself is a statutorily-defined act, not an exemption structure layered over an ongoing coverage population; there is no discretionary carve-out regime to assess against a covered population.

note: A one-sentence, self-executing statutory Trade Act waiver for two named countries, predicated on a completed congressional report; compliance-regime flags (4, 5, 8, 11) and Flag 10 are NOT_APPLICABLE as no ongoing regulatory relationship or second agency is implicated. Only absent-sunset (Flag 6) fires, since such waivers carry a natural renewal cadence not reflected in the order's own text.

title: Creating an Emergency Board to Investigate Disputes Between the Carriers Represented by the National Railway Labor Conference and Certain of Their Employees

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order creates a three-member investigatory board 'to investigate these disputes' and report findings under section 10 of the Railway Labor Act; it vests no decision or enforcement authority in any single official — the board's function is fact-finding and reporting, per the statute.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The board has a defined obligation — 'report its findings to the President... within thirty days' — and the statutory status-quo obligation on carriers and employees is specified; responsibility is clearly located within the Railway Labor Act framework.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order performs a single statutory act — creating an emergency board to investigate a defined set of rail-labor disputes — and bundles no distinct policy questions or problem frames.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The operative rule is precisely defined by statute: 'no change, except by agreement, shall be made by the carriers... or by their employees, in the conditions out of which the disputes arose' for thirty days after the board reports — a clear standard with a defined trigger and duration.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The board is a neutral fact-finder ('No member... shall be pecuniarily or otherwise interested in any organization of railroad employees or any carrier'), and the status-quo freeze is designed to preserve rather than distort the parties' positions; the Railway Labor Act is active and unexpired, so no Zombie Emergency Trap fires.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order is inherently time-limited — the board reports within thirty days and the status-quo obligation runs for thirty days after that report — a built-in expiration cadence rather than open-ended authority.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order removes no pre-existing oversight; it invokes the Railway Labor Act's standard emergency-board procedure and the National Mediation Board's judgment, adding an investigatory step rather than disabling any existing check.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The statutory status-quo obligation is mutual and public, and the board itself is the independent third party charged with investigating and reporting on the disputes; both carriers and unions have standing and incentive to surface violations of the freeze, so no third-party gap exists.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: This is a routine, statutorily-prescribed emergency-board creation for a specified rail-labor dispute; it establishes no precedent usable beyond the Railway Labor Act's own contemplated mechanism and produces no identifiable downstream effect beyond its stated purpose.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The National Mediation Board's role (judging that the disputes threaten interstate commerce) is invoked consistent with its statutory function, and the newly created board operates within the Railway Labor Act; no agency's operational mandate is redirected or countermanded, so coordination is clean.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order imposes a mutual status-quo obligation on defined parties for a fixed period but creates no exemption or waiver structure over a coverage population, so there is no exemptions architecture to assess.

note: A routine, statutorily-prescribed Railway Labor Act emergency board with a neutral three-member composition, fixed reporting deadline, and time-limited status-quo obligation; clean across all applicable flags. Flag 11 is NOT_APPLICABLE as no exemption structure exists. The extensive List A of railroads is a coverage schedule, not a bundling of distinct policy questions.

title: Delegating Certain Functions of the President Under the Defense Production Act of 1950

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: While functions are distributed across many named officers by subject (Secs. 101, 201, 301-303, 401), the order also creates a new Economic Stabilization Administrator to whom 'the functions conferred upon the President by Title IV' are delegated with power to 'establish price ceilings and stabilize wages and salaries' (Sec. 401), and the NSRB Chairman is empowered to 'resolve interagency issues,' 'prescribe policy and program directives,' and coordinate on the President's behalf (Sec. 802) — pooling broad economic-control and coordination authority in single officials with the President as the only check.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Authority is spread across numerous delegates, each empowered to redelegate and successively redelegate functions and to 'create an agency or agencies... to administer functions' (Sec. 902(b)), diffusing responsibility across a self-multiplying web of sub-delegates; when a given priorities/allocations or stabilization function fails, no single actor is clearly obligated to correct it.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The single order packages substantively distinct remedies — priorities and allocations, requisitioning, expansion of productive capacity and loan guarantees, economy-wide price/wage stabilization, real-estate credit controls, labor supply, and voluntary antitrust-immunized agreements (Parts I-VII) — under one Defense Production Act delegation, so distinct policy questions (e.g., wage control versus RFC loan guarantees) cannot be accepted or corrected independently within this instrument.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Delegates exercise their priorities and allocations powers 'in such manner as will in his judgment promote adequate supplies' (Sec. 102) and the Economic Stabilization Administrator establishes price ceilings and stabilizes wages 'where necessary' (Sec. 401(b)(4)) — open-textured standards under which two officials could in good faith reach substantially different allocation, requisition, or price-control decisions.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The design lets each delegate act as a 'claimant before the other such delegates' for the materials and facilities it controls (Sec. 103) and grants broad, redelegable, self-expanding authority (Sec. 902(b) power to create administering agencies), incentivizing each authority to maximize its own claims and build out its bureaucracy; the Defense Production Act of 1950 is active and unexpired, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order stands up a wartime-mobilization economic-control apparatus — including a new Economic Stabilization Agency and price/wage machinery — that is exactly the kind of emergency/crisis-response authority that should track a natural end-state, yet it contains no termination, review, or reauthorization trigger and persists until revoked.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order removes no pre-existing external check; it preserves statutory guardrails — Senate advice-and-consent for the Administrator and Price Stabilization Director (Secs. 401, 402), Attorney General consultation and approval before voluntary agreements (Sec. 701(c)), CSC classification review for senior positions (Sec. 902(d)), and a bounded subpoena power (Sec. 902(b) proviso) — rather than disabling any existing oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The priorities/allocations, requisitioning, and stabilization regimes depend on delegate-administered compliance and information-gathering (Secs. 903, 902(b) subpoena) with no external actor given standing and incentive to surface non-compliance; enforcement rests on the implementing agencies' own inquiry rather than any independent third party with access to identify violations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order builds a durable institutional template — a new Economic Stabilization Agency, Wage Stabilization Board, and cascading redelegation/agency-creation authority (Secs. 401-403, 902(b)) — for peacetime-persisting executive control over prices, wages, credit, and industrial allocation, an institutional precedent extensible well beyond the immediate Korean-War mobilization.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order overlays Defense Production Act functions onto numerous existing agencies and creates parallel/claimant structures among them (Sec. 103's inter-delegate claimant system; RFC directed to make loans, Federal Reserve Banks designated as fiscal agents, GSA directed to purchase materials — Secs. 302-303), directing appropriated resources and personnel toward mobilization functions and generating jurisdictional friction among bodies with pre-existing statutory mandates.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order allocates and delegates control functions but contains no waiver or exemption structure by which covered parties are carved out of the priorities, allocation, requisition, or stabilization coverage; exclusions in the text (e.g., Sec. 902(c)) limit which functions delegates hold, not which regulated actors escape coverage.

note: A sweeping Defense Production Act delegation building an economy-wide mobilization apparatus (priorities/allocations, requisitioning, capacity expansion, price/wage stabilization, credit controls, labor supply, voluntary agreements) with cascading redelegation and agency-creation power; many flags fire PRESENT, but none rises to CRITICAL because statutory guardrails (Senate confirmation, AG approval, CSC review, bounded subpoena) preserve meaningful checks. Reviewed fully despite length; no numbered EO predecessor is amended.

title: Creating an Emergency Board to Investigate a Dispute Between the United Air Lines, Inc., and Certain of its Employees

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Authority is exercised by the President under Section 10 of the Railway Labor Act to appoint a three-member investigative board; the board has fact-finding, not adjudicative or enforcement, authority, and a conflict-of-interest bar ('No member of the board shall be pecuniarily or otherwise interested') is built in, so no unchecked concentration of decision authority is created.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The board has a single, clear task (report findings within thirty days) and reports directly to the President; responsibility for the narrow function assigned is unambiguous.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order contains a single provision (creation of one fact-finding board for one dispute) with nothing else to bundle against.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order's only operative rule -- a 30-day cooling-off period during which 'no change, except by agreement, shall be made' -- is a clear, statutorily defined standard drawn directly from Section 10 of the Railway Labor Act.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The board's neutrality requirement and the statutory cooling-off period create no incentive for either party to game the process; the order invokes an active statute (Railway Labor Act), not an expired one, so the Zombie Trap does not apply.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The board's mandate is inherently self-terminating: it must report within thirty days and the associated status-quo period runs only thirty days after that report, giving the order a natural, built-in end-state.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order creates a standard statutory emergency board mechanism under the Railway Labor Act and removes no existing judicial, legislative, or administrative check.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no ongoing compliance regime here for a regulated population; this is a one-time fact-finding appointment tied to a specific labor dispute, not an enforcement structure with third parties to police it.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: This is a narrow, single-dispute administrative action under an established statutory procedure with no systemic or precedential implications beyond the named parties.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the National Mediation Board is referenced for its prior factual determination; no second agency's mandate is redirected, countermanded, or duplicated by this order.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: There is no coverage population or compliance obligation from which anyone could be exempted; the order simply convenes a board.

note: A narrow, statute-driven emergency board appointment under the Railway Labor Act; several compliance-regime and systemic-effect flags are genuinely not applicable because the order creates no ongoing regulatory relationship.

title: Possession, Control, and Operation of the Transportation System, Plants, and Facilities of the Great Lakes Towing Company, Cleveland, Ohio

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Director of the Office of Defense Transportation is given sole authority to seize, operate, and later release the company's entire transportation system and property, with termination of that possession left entirely to his own determination ('shall be terminated by the Director when he determines'), and no judicial or legislative check is specified.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Director both exercises the seizure authority and is the sole judge of when 'possession, control, and operation are no longer necessary' -- the same actor determines both the exercise and the end of the power, with no external reviewer named.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order addresses a single coherent action -- seizure and operation of one company's facilities -- and does not bundle unrelated remedies or frames.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Director may operate the seized facilities 'in such a manner as he may deem necessary to carry out the provisions, and accomplish the purposes of this order,' a standardless discretionary formulation that gives no fixed criteria for what compliance requires.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order invokes the still-active First War Powers Act and Selective Training and Service Act as amended by the War Labor Disputes Act; there is no acknowledgment that any underlying statute has lapsed or expired, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire, and no other independent perverse incentive is evident in the text.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Although termination is nominally tied to the Director's own necessity determination, this is discretionary rather than a fixed reauthorization requirement; wartime emergency seizure authority is exactly the kind of authority that should have a natural end tied to resolution of the emergency, and leaving termination solely to the seizing official's discretion is a real design gap under the permanence carve-out's own terms (paralleling EO 9066's PRESENT finding).

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order does not remove any pre-existing oversight mechanism; it operates 'subject to applicable provisions of existing law, including the orders of the Office of Defense Transportation issued pursuant to Executive Order 8989, as amended,' which per that predecessor's own text left ODT operating under presidential direction and coordinating with, rather than displacing, other agencies' existing functions.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Compliance with terms of operation rests entirely with the Director and the possessing government instrumentalities; the order bars 'attachment by mesne process, garnishment, execution, or otherwise' without the Director's consent, foreclosing an ordinary external check (creditors/courts) that would otherwise exist against the seized entity.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order establishes a precedent for using wartime seizure authority against a private company for a labor dispute rather than direct war production failure, and the blanket bar on legal process against seized property is a template usable in future seizures beyond this one company.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Functions, powers, and duties vested by statute in the Secretary of War (under the 1916 Act) are transferred to the Director of the Office of Defense Transportation for purposes of this order, redirecting statutory authority from the agency that originally held it to a different official.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order is a targeted seizure of one specific company's assets rather than a rule of general coverage, so there is no broader regulated population from which exemptions could be carved.

note: A wartime seizure order structurally similar in kind to other Office of Defense Transportation/War Powers Act seizures; EO 8989 was reviewed per the diff-aware convention and shows the seizure occurs 'subject to' its existing framework rather than displacing it, so Flag 7 is scored ABSENT rather than PRESENT.

title: Possession and Operation of Coal Mines

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of the Interior is authorized to seize and operate 'any and all mines producing coal in which a strike or stoppage has occurred or is threatened' and to 'do all things necessary or incidental' to production and distribution, a broad grant to a single official, though it is bounded by required consultation with the National War Labor Board on contract terms.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of the Interior determines both when seizure is necessary and, per the termination clause, when 'possession and operation hereunder are no longer required,' concentrating the assessment of the order's own success or failure in the same office that administers it.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order addresses a single coherent subject -- seizure and operation of coal mines experiencing strikes -- without bundling unrelated remedies.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Secretary may operate the mines 'in such manner as he deems necessary for the successful prosecution of the war,' an open-ended standard without defined metrics for compliance or trigger conditions beyond the Secretary's own judgment.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Authority rests on the active War Labor Disputes Act with no acknowledgment of any lapsed or expired statute, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire; the order's built-in worker protections do not create an evident incentive to undermine the order's purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order contains an explicit, self-executing termination trigger: possession ends 'as soon as he determines that possession and operation hereunder are no longer required... but in no event more than sixty days after the restoration of the productive efficiency thereof prevailing prior to the taking possession,' a defined outer bound tied to a measurable condition rather than indefinite continuation.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order explicitly preserves prior oversight machinery, stating 'Nothing in this order shall be construed to repeal or amend Executive Order No. 9370... authorizing the Economic Stabilization Director to take certain action in connection with the enforcement of directives of the National War Labor Board,' and requires Secretary of Interior contracts to go through National War Labor Board approval under Section 5 of the War Labor Disputes Act.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Beyond the National War Labor Board's contract-approval role, there is no independent third party with standing and incentive to surface mismanagement or noncompliance by the Secretary during the operation period; enforcement of proper operation depends on the same executive chain running the mines.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order establishes a template for government seizure of an entire industry sector in response to labor disputes rather than direct production sabotage, a precedent with implications for labor relations and executive seizure power well beyond the immediate coal strike.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order carefully coordinates rather than conflicts with other agencies: the Secretary of War is asked (not directed) to assist with protection, and the National War Labor Board and Economic Stabilization Director's existing statutory roles are expressly preserved.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: As a targeted seizure order applying to a defined set of mines where strikes occur or are threatened, there is no broader regulated population structure with carve-outs to assess.

note: EO 9370 was checked per the diff-aware convention; the order does not amend it but explicitly disclaims any repeal or amendment, which supports an ABSENT finding on Flag 7 given the express preservation of existing War Labor Board machinery.

title: Environmental Effects Abroad of Major Federal Actions

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Each agency's own 'officers having ultimate responsibility for authorizing and approving actions' determine what counts as a 'significant effect,' what documents to prepare, and whether exemptions apply (Sec. 2-5(a)(i): 'as determined by the agency'), concentrating both the substantive and the exemption-determination authority in the same actor without external check.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The order explicitly forecloses judicial accountability: 'nothing in this Order shall be construed to create a cause of action' (Sec. 3-1), meaning that if an agency's self-determination of 'significant effect' or exemption is wrong, no external party has a mechanism to compel correction.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order addresses a single coherent subject -- procedures for considering foreign environmental effects of federal actions -- and its component sections are all directly related to and justified by that single stated purpose.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Central operative terms are agency-self-defined with no external standard: whether an action 'significantly affects' the environment is 'as determined by the agency' (Sec. 2-5(a)(i)), and agencies may create 'categorical exclusions' and additional exemptions for 'emergency circumstances... and other such special circumstances' (Sec. 2-5(c)) -- two different agencies could reach opposite good-faith conclusions on identical facts with no way to reconcile them, directly undermining the order's stated purpose of ensuring environmental considerations are taken into account.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Because the same agency that decides whether to prepare an environmental document also decides whether its own action 'significantly affects' the environment, agencies are incentivized to find no significant effect to avoid the paperwork and delay of Sec. 2-4 procedures; this does not involve any lapsed statute so the Zombie Trap does not apply.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order creates a standing, permanent procedural framework for all future federal actions abroad with no review, renewal, or reauthorization mechanism, and unlike the standing-procedural-framework carve-out, its text shows no evidence of revision through supersession of a numbered predecessor or periodic amendment pattern -- it is freestanding, continuing authority subject only to future revocation.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order does not remove any pre-existing oversight mechanism; it states it 'represents the United States government's exclusive and complete determination' of NEPA procedures abroad but this establishes new internal procedure rather than stripping an oversight mechanism that previously existed, and Sec. 2-4(c) preserves existing agency regulations adopted under court order or judicial settlement.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The bar on any 'cause of action' (Sec. 3-1) combined with agency self-determination of significance eliminates any external actor's standing to challenge an agency's environmental assessment or its decision to invoke an exemption, leaving enforcement entirely dependent on the agency's own voluntary self-assessment with no third party able to surface noncompliance through any legal channel.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: By declaring itself 'the United States government's exclusive and complete determination' of NEPA's application abroad while foreclosing any cause of action, the order forecloses courts from independently interpreting NEPA's extraterritorial reach in future disputes, an institutional precedent limiting judicial review options well beyond the specific actions covered here.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order requires agencies to consult with the Department of State and Council on Environmental Quality and assigns lead-agency coordination for multi-agency actions (Sec. 3-3), creating cooperative rather than conflicting inter-agency structures.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Section 2-5 exempts broad categories including all actions taken by or at the direction of the President or a Cabinet officer 'when the national security or interest is involved,' all intelligence activities and arms transfers, and further allows agencies to create additional 'categorical exclusions' for 'emergency circumstances' and 'exceptional foreign policy and national security sensitivities' -- exemption criteria broad and self-determined enough that a sophisticated agency could exempt nearly any sensitive action, undermining the rule's coverage.

note: A substantive procedural order whose combination of agency self-determination of coverage, broad self-expandable exemptions, and an explicit bar on any cause of action compounds several flags into CRITICAL findings; each flag was independently tested against its own operational test before being scored CRITICAL.

title: Amending Executive Order No. 11030 of June 19, 1962, with Respect to the Preparation of Presidential Proclamations

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order makes narrow, formal amendments to proclamation-drafting and routing procedures without concentrating any new decision authority in any official; EO 11030's existing routing chain is left intact.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The amendments are stylistic and procedural (a witness recitation, a routing correction, seal-affixing scope) and do not touch or obscure the existing chain of responsibility for proclamation preparation established in EO 11030.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order bundles four distinct amendments to three different predecessor orders (EO 11030 Sec. 1 and Sec. 3(a), EO 10347 Item 1, and EO 3577's applicability) under a single title framed only as amending EO 11030, so a reviewer cannot accept or reject any one piece without addressing the whole instrument.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: This order concerns internal document-preparation formatting and routing with no regulated public conduct or compliance obligation to enforce against any external party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: There is no ongoing behavioral or compliance relationship created by this purely internal administrative-formatting order, so no actor class has an incentive structure to assess.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a standing procedural framework for internal document preparation; EO 11030 itself explicitly superseded EO 10006, and this order in turn amends EO 11030's own text, demonstrating the pattern of revision through ordinary supersession/amendment rather than a frozen indefinite rule, which supports the standing-procedural-framework carve-out.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order does not remove any oversight mechanism; EO 11030's routing and approval chain through the Bureau of the Budget and Attorney General (Sec. 2) is left untouched by these narrow amendments.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no regulated party or public compliance obligation created by this internal drafting-procedure order for any third party to have standing to monitor.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: This is a narrow administrative order governing internal document formatting and routing with no systemic or precedential implications beyond proclamation drafting mechanics.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order touches the Secretary of State's seal-affixing role and the Director of the Federal Register's publication role without redirecting or conflicting with either office's existing statutory functions; it only narrows Item 1 of EO 10347 to proclamations of treaties and international agreements, a compatible refinement.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Section 4 narrows EO 3577's dating-recitation requirement so it 'shall not apply to proclamations, except proclamations of treaties, conventions, protocols, or other international agreements' -- a narrow, clearly bounded carve-out for a specific document type, not a broad or discretionary exemption structure.

note: EO 11030, EO 10347, and EO 3577 were all retrieved and reviewed per the diff-aware convention; each amendment is a narrow, compatible partial amendment to internal drafting/routing procedure that leaves the existing accountability chain intact, but the bundling of four separate amendments to three different predecessor orders under one instrument fires Flag 3.

title: Amendment of Executive Order No. 8389 of April 10, 1940, as Amended

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: This order extends EO 8389's sanctions framework -- under which the Secretary of the Treasury holds broad, largely unreviewed licensing and enforcement discretion over blocked-property transactions -- to Romania; per the full-incorporation rule, the order inherits EO 8389's own concentration of licensing and enforcement authority in the Secretary of the Treasury with no external check specified.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: As a full incorporation of EO 8389's framework (which vests the Secretary of the Treasury with authority to define terms, grant licenses, and modify regulations under Section 12 of that order), this order inherits the same accountability gap: the Secretary both administers and interprets the scope of the freeze with no independent reviewer named.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order makes a single, unified extension of an existing sanctions framework to one new country with nothing else in the order to bundle against.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Under full incorporation, this order extends EO 8389's regime, which itself relies on broad delegated definitions ('any interest of any nature whatsoever, direct or indirect') and Treasury discretion to determine what transactions are covered, an enforcement standard that could yield materially different good-faith determinations by different officials.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order operates under the active Trading with the Enemy Act framework (Section 5(b) of the Act of October 6, 1917, as amended) with no acknowledged statutory lapse, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire, and no other independent perverse incentive is apparent in this extension text.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The freeze authority being extended has no built-in expiration or reauthorization trigger and continues indefinitely until affirmatively revoked; this is exactly the kind of exceptional wartime-emergency financial-control authority that should have a natural end tied to resolution of the conflict, and none is provided.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order does not remove any pre-existing oversight mechanism; it extends an existing regulatory freeze framework to a new country's nationals without touching any review process.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Inherited from EO 8389's framework, compliance is verified largely through self-reporting by banking institutions under oath (Section 10) with the Secretary of the Treasury as the sole reviewing authority, and no independent third party with standing is given a role in surfacing violations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: This order establishes the precedent and mechanism for the President to unilaterally extend an entire financial-freeze apparatus to additional countries one at a time by simple executive order as European nations fell to occupation, a template repeatedly reusable for other countries without new congressional action.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency's existing statutory mandate is implicated or conflicted with; the order operates solely through the Secretary of the Treasury as EO 8389 itself did.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Inherited from EO 8389, transactions are prohibited 'except as specifically authorized in regulations or licenses issued by the Secretary of the Treasury,' placing unlimited, criteria-free licensing discretion in a single official who can effectively exempt any transaction from the freeze.

note: EO 8389 was retrieved and reviewed; this order is a full incorporation extending EO 8389's entire sanctions framework to Romania rather than a partial amendment, so per the incorporation-by-reference rule it inherits EO 8389's own flag findings for the extended subject rather than being scored as structurally clean merely because its own text is four sentences long.

title: Prescribing Regulations Governing the Enforcement of the Neutrality of the United States

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Enforcement authority is deliberately distributed across the War Department, Navy Department, Treasury and Commerce Departments, the Governor of the Panama Canal, and the Department of Justice by subject-matter jurisdiction, rather than pooled in a single official or body.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each department's responsibility is specifically delineated by domain (land forces to War, vessels to Navy, general commerce to Treasury/Commerce, the Canal Zone to its Governor, prosecutions to Justice), so responsibility for any given enforcement failure traces to an identifiable department.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Every provision in the order serves the single stated purpose of assigning neutrality-enforcement duties among departments consistent with the September 5 neutrality proclamation; nothing unrelated is bundled in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Paragraph 5 assigns the Department of Justice enforcement of neutrality 'not especially delegated to other departments,' a residual catch-all category whose boundaries depend on how the other enumerated jurisdictional assignments are read, creating room for good-faith disagreement about which department is responsible for a given act.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order implements an active, just-issued neutrality proclamation under a live treaty and law-of-nations obligation, not any lapsed statute, so the Zombie Trap does not apply; the assignment of clear jurisdictional lanes does not create an incentive for any department to under-enforce.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order's duties are tied to 'during said war' but provides no defined mechanism for determining when the war has ended or for reviewing and reauthorizing the assigned duties; emergency/wartime duty assignments are exactly the kind of authority that should have a clear resolution-tied end mechanism, and none beyond the informal 'during said war' phrase is specified.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds new enforcement duties 'in addition to the duties now prescribed, or hereafter prescribed, by law' and explicitly operates in a manner 'not in conflict' with existing law or prior orders, so it does not remove or reduce any pre-existing oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement of neutrality violations depends on the enumerated departments themselves identifying and prosecuting violations, with paragraph 6 relying on departments to furnish information to each other; no independent third party outside the executive branch is given standing or a defined role in surfacing violations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: By assigning military departments enforcement authority over neutrality matters domestically and in U.S. territories, the order establishes an institutional division of enforcement labor between military and civilian agencies over what is fundamentally a matter of international and domestic law, a precedent for military involvement in domestic-facing neutrality/law-enforcement functions that persists beyond this specific proclamation.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Rather than redirecting or conflicting with any agency's existing statutory mandate, the order assigns each department duties within its natural domain and calls for mutual cooperation and information-sharing.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order assigns enforcement duties among federal departments and does not create a regulated population with coverage and exemption criteria to map.

note: A wartime inter-agency duty-assignment order with generally clean, well-distributed jurisdictional design; the residual Justice Department catch-all clause and reliance on the departments themselves for enforcement create the identified PRESENT findings.

title: Prescribing Arrangements for Developing and Coordinating a National Program For Minority Business Enterprise

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of Commerce is given broad coordinating, information-demanding, and convening authority over other departments' minority-business programs (Sec. 1(b)) with no external check on how that coordinating authority is exercised, though Section 4 limits it from overriding other agencies' own statutory functions.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The order creates a coordinating role with no defined metric for success or failure of the overall national program, and the Advisory Council is purely advisory with no enforcement authority (Sec. 2(e)), leaving no actor with both the obligation and the capacity to correct implementation failure if the program does not achieve its purpose.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: All provisions -- the Secretary's coordinating functions, the Advisory Council's creation, and other agencies' cooperation duties -- serve the single stated purpose of developing and coordinating a minority business enterprise program.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Other departments' cooperation obligations are qualified by 'to the extent permitted by law and funds available' (Sec. 3(a)) with no defined standard for what satisfies this requirement, allowing wide latitude for good-faith disagreement about the scope of required cooperation.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order creates a coordinating and advisory apparatus among federal actors rather than an ongoing compliance regime governing external regulated-party or beneficiary conduct, so there is no incentive structure of the kind this flag addresses.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The Secretary's coordinating authority and the Advisory Council are established with no review, renewal, or expiration mechanism and no natural end-state tied to program completion; this is a discretionary programmatic authority, not an evergreen rights mandate or standing procedural framework, so the permanence carve-out does not apply.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Section 4 explicitly preserves existing agency authority: 'Nothing in this order shall be construed as subjecting any function vested by law in... any Federal department or agency... to the authority of any other agency,' so no pre-existing oversight or statutory function is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Compliance by other departments with information-sharing and cooperation requests depends entirely on their own voluntary response to the Secretary's requests, with no external party having standing or incentive to compel a non-cooperating department to participate.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: By establishing a Secretary-led coordinating structure with a supporting advisory council, the order creates a durable institutional model for cross-agency minority-enterprise policy coordination that persists as a bureaucratic structure and precedent independent of any specific program funding decision.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Section 4 explicitly disclaims any redirection or restriction of other agencies' statutory functions, and cooperation is requested rather than mandated over objection, avoiding conflict with any agency's existing mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order does not create a regulated population subject to compliance obligations from which exemptions could be granted; it is a coordinating and advisory structure among federal agencies.

note: A coordinating/advisory program-establishment order; the deeming clause referencing EO 11007 is a technical formation mechanism for the advisory council, not an amendment, extension, or revocation, so no predecessor lookup was required for it.

title: Extension of Trust Periods on Indian Lands Expiring During Calendar Year 1941

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order is self-executing: it extends all qualifying trust periods by a flat 25 years automatically, exercising no ongoing discretionary decision authority that is pooled in any official or body.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Because the extension applies automatically and uniformly to all lands meeting the stated criteria, with no case-by-case administrative determination required, there is no diffuse-responsibility or failure-chain question to trace.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order contains one uniform action (a flat extension of trust periods) with a single carve-out for congressionally reserved cases; there is nothing bundled in that is unrelated to this single stated purpose.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order self-executes a blanket extension with no compliance regime, discretion, or enforcement mechanism for any party's ongoing conduct.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: There is no ongoing behavioral or compliance relationship created by a blanket, automatic extension of trust periods, so no actor class has an incentive structure to evaluate.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order itself is a one-time, self-executing action (a 25-year extension applied at a single point in time) rather than an open-ended ongoing grant of authority; it does not newly create continuing discretionary authority requiring reauthorization pressure -- it fixes a new, definite expiration date for each affected trust.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order explicitly preserves Congress's role, stating it 'is not intended to apply to any case in which the Congress has specifically reserved to itself authority to extend the period of trust,' removing no existing oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no ongoing compliance regime or regulated conduct here for a third party to police; the order is a one-time automatic extension of a legal status.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: This is a narrow, self-executing administrative action extending an existing legal status by a fixed term, with no systemic or precedential implications beyond the affected trust lands.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency is implicated at all by this self-executing extension of trust status; only the pre-existing statutory trust framework and Congress's reserved authority are referenced.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order carves out 'any case in which the Congress has specifically reserved to itself authority to extend the period of trust,' a bounded and specific exemption tied to a defined legal condition; it is still an exemption from the blanket extension's coverage and is scored on the merits rather than waved off as inapplicable.

note: A narrow, self-executing, one-time land-status extension; the compliance-regime flags (4, 5, 8) and systemic-effect flags (9, 10) are genuinely not applicable because no ongoing regulatory relationship or systemic mechanism is created, per the mechanical NA test.

title: Providing for the Screening of the Ready Reserve of the Armed Forces

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order delegates to the Secretary of Defense (and the Secretary of the Treasury for the Coast Guard) the President's own statutory authority under 10 U.S.C. 271 to prescribe regulations screening Ready Reserve units and members, concentrating rulemaking authority in the Secretary with no external check specified in the text.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility for prescribing screening regulations is clearly and specifically assigned to the Secretary of Defense (and Secretary of the Treasury for the Coast Guard), leaving no ambiguity about which official is accountable for this function.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order contains two closely related provisions -- a delegation of screening authority and a revocation of the prior order on the same subject -- both directly serving the single stated purpose, so nothing unrelated is bundled in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order itself is a one-time delegation of rulemaking authority; it creates no enforcement mechanism of its own, leaving the actual content and enforcement of screening regulations to whatever the Secretary of Defense subsequently prescribes.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order is a bare delegation of rulemaking authority with no compliance regime or incentive structure of its own for any actor class to game.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The delegation of screening rulemaking authority to the Secretary of Defense is open-ended and permanent with no review or reauthorization mechanism; this is a discretionary administrative rulemaking delegation rather than an evergreen rights mandate, so the permanence carve-out does not apply, and the absence of any reauthorization trigger is a genuine gap.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Executive Order 10651, the order being revoked, was not recoverable in the referenced-predecessors folder, so it cannot be determined from the available text whether any oversight mechanism existed under that prior order that this revocation removes; the comparison called for by the diff-aware convention could not be made.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: This is a bare delegation of rulemaking authority with no compliance regime of its own yet in place for any third party to monitor.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: By delegating the President's own statutory screening authority to the Secretary of Defense, the order establishes a durable institutional shift of authority that will govern all future Ready Reserve screening determinations, a structural change with effects well beyond any single screening cycle.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The delegation to the Secretary of Defense and, separately, to the Secretary of the Treasury for the Coast Guard cleanly divides authority along existing departmental lines without redirecting either agency's mandate or creating conflicting parallel authority.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order is a bare delegation of rulemaking authority and does not itself define a regulated population or exemption structure; any exemptions would arise only in regulations the Secretary of Defense subsequently prescribes, which are not before this order's text.

note: Executive Order 10651, which this order revokes, was cited by number but was not found in the referenced-predecessors folder, so the diff-aware comparison could not be made; Flag 7 was scored NOT_APPLICABLE rather than guessed at, per the instructions, and this limitation is noted explicitly here.

title: Administration of Arms Export Controls

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Authority is distributed across four named cabinet officials (State, Defense, Treasury, Commerce) by specific statutory section, each with a defined slice; Sec. 2 requires State and Defense to consult each other and other agency heads, providing an external check rather than pooling authority in one office.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each delegated function is tied to a specific named official responsible for that function, and Sec. 2(b) makes the Secretary of State explicitly responsible for 'continuous supervision and general direction' of sales, so failure has a locatable owner.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order bundles many delegations, but each is a discrete, independently justifiable assignment of a specific Arms Export Control Act section to the official statutorily suited to it; there is a single coherent purpose and no unrelated matter is folded in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order delegates specific statutory functions with cross-references to the underlying Act's own standards; it does not itself create new open-ended enforcement standards beyond what the Act already defines.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order relies on the active, ordinary Arms Export Control Act, not a lapsed emergency statute, and creates consultation requirements among agencies rather than incentives to game outcomes; no zombie trap applies since no statute is described as expired.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a standing procedural delegation of a permanent statutory administrative function (arms export licensing); the order's own text shows it is maintained through ordinary supersession (it explicitly revokes and replaces EO 11501, which itself replaced an earlier delegation), consistent with the standing-procedural-framework carve-out.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Comparing to predecessor EO 11501, this order preserves the same consultation and coordination structure among Secretaries of State, Defense, Treasury, and the Arms Control and Disarmament Agency, and adds a new requirement to consult AID and OMB in specific subsections; no existing review mechanism is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement of arms transfer eligibility findings rests within the executive branch with congressional reporting under Sec. 25, but there is no independent third party outside government with standing to surface violations of the delegated determinations themselves.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Delegating discretionary 'strengthen the security of the United States and promote world peace' eligibility findings (Sec. 1(a) proviso) to the Secretary of State establishes a precedent for executive-level foreign policy determinations on arms transfers with effects reaching beyond individual transactions into broader foreign policy posture.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Multiple agencies are implicated, but the order carefully assigns non-overlapping statutory sections to each and mandates consultation (Sec. 2(a)), avoiding conflicting mandates.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order is a delegation of administrative authority rather than a coverage rule with carve-outs; no exemption class or waiver mechanism is created for regulated parties.

note: Compared against referenced predecessor EO 11501 per the diff-aware convention; the delegation scheme is substantially preserved and slightly expanded with additional consultation requirements, so no oversight preemption was found relative to baseline.

title: Establishing an Advisory Board on Just Compensation and Prescribing Its Functions

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The three-member Board sets standards for the War Shipping Administration's compensation decisions but does not itself hold enforcement or eligibility authority; its role is advisory/standard-setting, a multi-member body rather than a single unaccountable official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The War Shipping Administration remains responsible for determining and paying compensation (paragraph 5), guided by Board standards; responsibility for implementation is clearly located in the Administration.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order addresses a single subject -- establishing a board to set just-compensation standards for requisitioned vessels -- with no unrelated provisions bundled in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: This order creates no compliance obligation on regulated third parties; it sets up an internal advisory standard-setting body for a government administration's own payment determinations, so there is no ongoing enforcement relationship to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: There is no ongoing behavioral compliance regime created for any regulated party; the order establishes a temporary internal valuation board, so the perverse-incentive question does not map onto this instrument.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The Board's members 'shall serve for a period of three months or for such lesser period of time as may be required for the completion of the Functions' (paragraph 2), an explicit, self-limiting termination tied to task completion, so the absence-of-sunset flag does not fire.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order creates a new advisory body and does not reference or remove any existing review mechanism for vessel compensation determinations.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing regulatory relationship with an external regulated party is created; the order is an internal government valuation mechanism, so third-party enforcement standing does not apply.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order is narrowly confined to establishing valuation standards for requisitioned vessels during wartime and does not establish precedent or authority usable in unrelated contexts.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency's statutory mandate is implicated or redirected; the Board serves only the War Shipping Administration.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: There is no coverage population with exemption or waiver provisions; the order does not create carve-outs from any regulatory requirement.

note: A narrow wartime administrative order creating a time-limited internal valuation board; four flags (4, 5, 8, 11) and Flag 10 are genuinely not applicable since no ongoing regulatory or inter-agency relationship exists.

title: Coordination of Federal Activities Affecting the Fishery Industry

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of the Interior is designated sole Fishery Coordinator with authority to further delegate to a Deputy (paragraph 1) and to direct an Office of Fishery Coordination, concentrating wartime coordination authority over an entire industry in one official with no external check specified.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of the Interior is clearly and singularly named as responsible for the Fishery Coordinator function and its Office, so responsibility is traceable even though the authority itself is concentrated.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order addresses a single coherent purpose -- coordinating wartime fishery production and information-sharing -- with liaison and advisory functions both serving that one stated goal.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Office's functions are described as maintaining 'close liaison' and making 'specific recommendations' (paragraph 2), with no defined consequence or enforcement trigger if agencies or industry fail to cooperate, leaving compliance essentially voluntary and undefined.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order derives authority from the active First War Powers Act, 1941, not a lapsed statute, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not apply; no other perverse incentive structure is identifiable in the coordination/advisory design.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Paragraph 4 explicitly ties the order's duration to the underlying statute: it 'shall continue in force and effect so long as Title I of the First War Powers Act, 1941, remains in force,' an emergency-powers grant with its natural end-state tied to the wartime authority's own expiration, satisfying the permanence carve-out.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order creates new liaison and advisory machinery and does not reference or displace any pre-existing review process for fishery-related federal activities.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The order coordinates federal agency information-sharing and industry liaison rather than creating a compliance regime on regulated parties, so there is no ongoing violation-surfacing relationship for third parties to fill.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Designating a single Fishery Coordinator with sweeping cross-agency liaison authority over 'the fishery and allied industries' creates an institutional coordination precedent for wartime executive-branch industry oversight that could extend beyond the stated food-supply purpose.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order requires the head of every other federal agency to designate a liaison officer answerable to the Office of Fishery Coordination (paragraph 2(a)) and directs the Coordinator to advise the War Production Board on supply matters, inserting Interior into other agencies' operational lanes without a clear hierarchy for resolving conflicts.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order creates no regulatory coverage population subject to compliance from which carve-outs could be granted; it is a coordination and information-sharing mechanism.

note: A wartime coordination order; Flags 8 and 11 are not applicable since no regulated-party compliance regime exists, while Flags 1, 4, 9, and 10 fire at the PRESENT level due to concentrated coordinator authority and cross-agency liaison mandates.

title: Partial Revocation of Executive Order of March 21, 1914 (Public Water Restoration No. 63, Wyoming)

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: This is a one-sentence, self-executing revocation of a prior land-status order as to a single described parcel; no ongoing decision authority is exercised or created by any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The order self-executes with no ongoing implementation responsibility assigned to anyone, so there is no failure chain to trace and thus no gap.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order contains a single provision (revocation of a water reserve as to one described parcel) with nothing else to bundle against.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order creates no ongoing behavioral rule or compliance obligation for any actor; it is a one-time change in land status, so there is no enforcement regime to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing regulatory relationship or incentive structure is created by this one-time land-status revocation.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order is a one-time, self-executing revocation with no ongoing grant of authority, so there is nothing to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order revokes a prior withdrawal as to one parcel but does not reference or remove any judicial, legislative, or administrative review mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing compliance regime exists for any regulated party, so there is no third-party enforcement question to evaluate.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: This is a narrow, one-time administrative land action affecting a single small parcel with no systemic or precedential implications identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency is implicated in any way by this single-parcel land status revocation.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: There is no regulatory coverage population and no exemption or waiver mechanism at issue in this land-status action.

note: A one-sentence, self-executing partial land-status revocation affecting a single described parcel; only the always-answerable decision-structure flags (1, 2, 7) apply and all are clean, with the remaining eight flags genuinely not applicable given the absence of any ongoing authority, compliance regime, or inter-agency relationship.

title: Withdrawal of Public Land for Use of Department of Agriculture as Addition to Agricultural Field Station

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order is a self-executing land withdrawal for use by the Department of Agriculture; no ongoing discretionary decision authority over people or eligibility is vested in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The withdrawal is self-executing and the land is simply reserved for Department of Agriculture use, with no implementation function assigned that could fail.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order contains a single land-withdrawal provision with nothing else present to bundle against.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: No ongoing compliance obligation is created for any actor; this is a one-time reservation of land for agency use, so there is no enforcement regime to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing regulatory or behavioral relationship is created by this land withdrawal, so the perverse-incentive question does not map onto the instrument.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order states it 'shall continue in force until revoked by the President or by act of Congress,' an explicit grant of indefinite duration for a discretionary land-use reservation with no natural end-state or periodic reauthorization trigger built in, which is the kind of open-ended discretionary grant the flag is meant to catch.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order withdraws land from settlement/entry but does not reference or eliminate any existing judicial, legislative, or administrative oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing compliance regime affecting third parties is created; the order simply reserves land for agency use.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: This is a narrow, one-time land withdrawal for a specific agricultural field station addition with no systemic or precedential implications apparent from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency's mandate is implicated; the land is withdrawn from general public-land status for exclusive Department of Agriculture use with no conflicting agency claim identified.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No regulatory coverage population or exemption/waiver mechanism exists in this land-withdrawal order.

note: A narrow land-withdrawal order for an agricultural field station; because it grants an ongoing indefinite-duration reservation of land, Flag 6 is answerable and fires PRESENT, while the compliance-regime and precedential flags remain genuinely not applicable.

title: Delegating to the Secretary of Agriculture Certain Authority Relating to Emergency Livestock Feed

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of Agriculture is empowered to declare an 'acute distress area' and thereby trigger federal feed assistance 'without the approval, ratification, or other action of the President' (Sec. 1(a)), concentrating the distress-declaration authority in a single official with only a non-binding consultation obligation to the Secretary of the Interior (Sec. 3) as a check.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of Agriculture is the clearly named actor responsible both for declaring distress areas and for terminating assistance 'upon notice by the Secretary of Agriculture' (Sec. 2), so responsibility is traceable even though the authority is concentrated.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order addresses a single coherent subject -- emergency livestock feed assistance for Indian tribes affected by catastrophe -- and each section serves that one stated purpose.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The triggering standard -- 'chronic economic distress ... materially increased by severe drought, flood, hurricane, blizzard, or other uncontrollable catastrophe' (Sec. 1(b)) -- gives the Secretary broad, undefined discretion to determine what counts as sufficiently severe, without objective criteria two officials would necessarily apply the same way.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order relies on the active Agricultural Act of 1949, as amended, not a lapsed statute, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire; no other perverse-incentive structure for regulated parties or beneficiaries is apparent in the feed-assistance design.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: While Sec. 4 sets a specific end date for the particular declared distress area, the underlying delegation of authority to the Secretary in Sections 1-3 to declare future distress areas has no expiration or reauthorization requirement and persists indefinitely as standing emergency authority.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order delegates authority the President previously held directly to the Secretary but does not eliminate any existing external review; it adds a liaison/consultation requirement with the Secretary of the Interior (Sec. 3) rather than removing oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Distress-area declarations and their termination rest entirely with the Secretary of Agriculture's own determination (Sec. 1(b), Sec. 2), with no external party -- such as the affected tribes or an independent reviewer -- given standing to contest a declaration or its termination.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order is narrowly confined to emergency livestock feed relief for designated reservation lands and does not establish a broader precedent or definitional framework extending into unrelated authority.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Secretary of Agriculture is required to 'maintain liaison' and 'consult' with the Secretary of the Interior (Sec. 3), which manages Indian trust lands, but this is a coordination requirement rather than a conflicting or countermanding assignment of Interior's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order creates a discretionary relief-granting mechanism rather than a compliance regime with a covered population subject to exemptions or waivers.

note: An emergency relief delegation order; Flag 11 is not applicable since there is no coverage/exemption structure, and the vague distress-declaration standard combined with unilateral Secretarial discretion drives PRESENT findings on Flags 1, 4, 6, and 8.

title: Exemption of Arthur H. Chase From Compulsory Retirement for Age

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: This is a one-time, self-executing personal retirement exemption exercised directly by the President under a specific statute (5 U.S.C. sec. 715a); no ongoing discretionary authority is delegated to or concentrated in any other official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The order self-executes a named, specific personnel action with no ongoing implementation function assigned to any official that could fail.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order contains a single provision -- extending one individual's retirement exemption -- with nothing else present to bundle against.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
