flags.justification: No ongoing compliance obligation or enforcement regime is created; this is a one-time personnel exemption for a named individual.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing regulatory relationship or behavioral incentive structure is created by this individual retirement exemption.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order itself is explicitly time-bound, extending the exemption 'for a period of one year ending November 30, 1940,' a defined expiration date consistent with the prior EO 8011, which had granted the first one-year exemption -- comparison to the predecessor confirms this is a renewable, fixed-term grant, not an open-ended one.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order extends a personal statutory exemption and does not reference or remove any existing review mechanism; comparison to predecessor EO 8011 shows the same fixed-term structure repeated, not any weakening of process.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing regulatory compliance regime affecting third parties is created by this individual personnel exemption.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: This is a narrow, one-time personnel action concerning a single named individual with no systemic or precedential implications.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency's mandate is implicated by this single-employee retirement exemption within the Navy Department.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Although the order is titled an 'exemption,' it is a one-time individual personnel action, not a regulatory exemption structure carving a class out of a coverage population; there is no broader compliance regime for this exemption to undermine.

note: Compared against referenced predecessor EO 8011, which granted the first one-year exemption to the same named individual; this order simply extends it a second year on the same fixed-term basis, confirming a clean, bounded renewal rather than an open-ended grant.

title: Directing the Federal Works Administrator to Take Possession of and Operate a Certain Project of the Grand River Dam Authority

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Federal Works Administrator is authorized to 'enter upon, and take possession of, manage, and operate' the Grand River Dam Authority's licensed project (paragraph 1) and to retain control until the President alone determines retention is no longer necessary (paragraph 2), with no external check beyond the President's own later say-so.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Federal Works Administrator is clearly and specifically named as responsible for possession, management, operation, and eventual restoration of the project, so responsibility for the seizure and its wind-down is traceable to one named office.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order addresses a single coherent action -- wartime seizure and operation of one specific hydroelectric project for defense power production -- with compensation and personnel provisions that directly serve that one stated purpose.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Compensation is to be 'fixed by the Federal Power Commission upon the basis of a reasonable profit in time of peace' (paragraph 3), an underspecified valuation standard without a defined methodology, timeline, or dispute-resolution process for the licensee.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order invokes the active Federal Power Act and wartime safety authority, not an expired statute, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not apply; no other perverse incentive is evident from the seizure-and-compensation design.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order's authority is explicitly tied to a resolution condition: the Administrator 'shall retain possession ... until such time as it shall appear to the President that further retention ... is unnecessary for the accomplishment of the above-stated purposes' (paragraph 2), an emergency/wartime grant with its own natural end-state tied to the defense necessity, satisfying the permanence carve-out's logic even without a fixed calendar date.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order directs the Administrator to act 'without compliance with the requirements of the Civil Service Rules' (paragraph 4) in hiring personnel, an explicit removal of a pre-existing civil-service oversight mechanism for the duration of the seizure.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Compensation is fixed unilaterally by the Federal Power Commission with no role specified for the Grand River Dam Authority to contest the valuation or for any other external party to challenge the terms of the taking.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Directing government seizure of a licensed public authority's operating project under wartime safety powers, with a Civil Service exemption for staffing, establishes a precedent for executive seizure of state-chartered infrastructure that extends beyond this single dam project's stated defense-power purpose.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order redirects operational control of a project licensed and operated by the Grand River Dam Authority (a state instrumentality regulated under the Federal Power Act by the Federal Power Commission) to the Federal Works Administrator, displacing the licensee's statutory operating authority under its Federal Power Commission license.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order is a directed seizure of a single named project rather than a compliance regime with a coverage population subject to exemptions or waivers.

note: A wartime seizure order under the Federal Power Act; the explicit Civil Service Rules exemption for staffing (paragraph 4) fires Flag 7 as a genuine removal of pre-existing oversight, and the unilateral, unreviewable compensation-setting and control-retention design drive several other PRESENT findings.

title: Presidential Advisory Committee on Federalism

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Committee is purely advisory ('shall advise the President with respect to the objectives and conduct of the overall federalism policy,' Sec. 2), holds no enforcement, eligibility, or implementation authority, and members serve at the President's pleasure, so no independent decision authority is concentrated anywhere by this order.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Because the Committee has no implementation function beyond advice, there is no failure chain of the kind Flag 2 addresses; the President remains the decision-maker for federalism policy and the Committee members serve at the President's pleasure (Sec. 1(a)).

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order addresses a single coherent purpose -- establishing an advisory committee on federalism policy -- and its administrative provisions all directly serve that one stated purpose.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Committee is purely advisory with no enforcement function or compliance obligation imposed on any regulated party, so there is no enforcement regime for this flag to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral compliance relationship is created by this purely advisory committee, so the perverse-incentive question does not map onto the instrument.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Sec. 4(b) explicitly states 'The Committee shall terminate on December 31, 1982, unless sooner extended,' a defined termination date consistent with the two-year default under the Federal Advisory Committee Act, so the absence-of-sunset flag does not fire.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order creates a new advisory committee under the Federal Advisory Committee Act and does not remove or limit any pre-existing oversight mechanism; indeed Sec. 4(a) preserves FACA administrative-service arrangements via GSA.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no regulated party or compliance obligation created by this advisory committee, so there is no third-party enforcement standing question to evaluate.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order is confined to establishing a two-year advisory body with no enforcement or definitional authority, and no systemic or precedential effect beyond convening advice is identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order requires executive agencies to provide information to the Committee 'to the extent permitted by law' (Sec. 3(a)), a limited cooperation request that does not redirect or conflict with any agency's existing statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order creates an advisory committee with no regulatory coverage population, so there is no exemption or waiver structure to assess.

note: A clean, narrowly scoped FACA advisory committee with an explicit sunset date (Sec. 4(b)); Flags 4, 5, 8, and 11 are not applicable given the absence of any compliance regime, and all six applicable flags score ABSENT.

title: Federal Consumer Programs

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Chairperson of the Consumer Affairs Council is empowered to 'establish such policies, definitions, procedures, and standards to govern the implementation, interpretation, and application of this Order' (Sec. 1-303) and to approve or reject every agency's consumer program before final publication (Sec. 1-502), concentrating broad interpretive and approval authority in a single designated official with no external check specified.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Chairperson is clearly designated as responsible for approving agency programs and reporting to the President 'on government-wide progress' (Sec. 1-505), and each agency must designate its own senior official responsible for consumer-program compliance (Sec. 1-402), so responsibility is traceable at both the central and agency level.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order addresses a single coherent purpose -- improving management and coordination of federal consumer programs -- and each element serves that one stated purpose without unrelated matter folded in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Agencies must 'adequately' consider consumer concerns, provide 'meaningful' participation, and make 'affirmative efforts' to inform consumers (Sec. 1-401(b)), standards that are not defined with objective criteria and could be read very differently by different agencies in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order does not rely on any lapsed or expiring statute, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not apply; while agencies self-report their own compliance programs, no clear incentive to undermine the consumer-protection purpose is independently identifiable beyond what is already captured under Flag 8.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order creates a standing Council and ongoing Chairperson authority over agency consumer programs with no expiration, review-and-reauthorization requirement, or termination date anywhere in the text; its ongoing discretionary coordination authority has no natural end-state or built-in reauthorization trigger.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds new oversight machinery (public comment periods under Sec. 1-501, OMB budget review under Sec. 1-601) rather than removing any pre-existing review process; per the direction-matters rule, new imperfect checks do not fire this flag.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Compliance with the order rests on each agency's own self-assessment and self-published 'draft consumer program' with Chairperson sign-off (Sec. 1-502), and while public comment is invited, no external party has enforceable standing to compel a stronger program if an agency's self-assessment is inadequate.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Defining 'consumer' broadly (Sec. 1-901) and creating a government-wide Council with authority to set 'policies, definitions, procedures, and standards' establishes an institutional and definitional framework whose interpretive precedent could extend well beyond the specific consumer-protection reforms the order enumerates.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Twelve departments are required to participate in the Council and report to the Chairperson, but the order's role is coordinative -- 'promote efficiency and interagency cooperation' (Sec. 1-201) -- and does not redirect any agency's substantive statutory mandate or countermand another agency's authority.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Sec. 1-902 excludes independent regulatory agencies from coverage except as separately invited, incorporates further exceptions from EO 12044's categories, and additionally allows any agency to self-exempt within 30 days simply by demonstrating to the Chairperson's satisfaction that its activities have 'no substantial impact upon consumers' (Sec. 1-902(c)), a broad, agency-initiated, discretionarily-approved carve-out.

note: This order incorporates specific exemption categories by cross-reference from EO 12044 (Sec. 1-902(b)), which was checked in referenced-predecessors; that incorporation is a partial, specific cross-reference to named exemption categories rather than full incorporation of EO 12044's entire regulatory framework, so only the referenced exemption categories were assessed under Flag 11 rather than inheriting all of EO 12044's own flag findings.

title: Continuing the Regulation of Exports

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Authority to administer and enforce export controls rests in the Secretary of Commerce (via the incorporated EO 11533 framework), with the only check being an internal Export Administration Review Board the Secretary himself controls and refers matters to at his own discretion ('as he shall select'); there is no external judicial or legislative check named in the text.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: If export controls are administered poorly, responsibility sits with the Secretary of Commerce, who also chairs the review board that oversees his own referrals (EO 11533 Sec. 2-3, incorporated by reference), producing a self-review structure rather than independent accountability.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order addresses a single subject (continuation of export control administration) and does not bundle unrelated remedies or problem frames; each section (continuation, CFR carryover, non-effect on other delegations) is directly tied to that one purpose.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Enforcement is inherited wholesale from the CFR parts 368-399 and prior administrative action 'until amended or revoked by the Secretary of Commerce' (Sec. 2), with no textual standard given here for when violations trigger the $10,000 fine versus the excluded civil penalty, leaving application to the Secretary's own regulations.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order explicitly states it operates 'Notwithstanding the expiration of the Export Administration Act of 1969, as amended,' while continuing the Secretary of Commerce's full export-control authority under EO 11533/11683 as if the underlying statute were still in force -- the precise Zombie Emergency Trap pattern of acknowledged statutory lapse plus continued exercise of authority the agency would lose if the statute were properly reauthorized on its own terms.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Export control authority exercised notwithstanding the lapse of its authorizing statute is exactly the kind of exceptional, emergency-linked authority that should track reauthorization of that statute, yet the order contains no expiration or review mechanism and continues 'until amended or revoked' by the same Secretary who administers it.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order continues pre-existing administrative machinery (EO 11533's Board and CFR rules) without removing any judicial, legislative, or administrative review mechanism that previously applied; Section 3 explicitly preserves other existing delegations of authority.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement of export licensing depends on the Secretary of Commerce's own administrative action under the CFR provisions carried forward by Section 2, with no named external party (competitor, watchdog, or agency) given standing or incentive to surface violations independent of the administering agency.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: By continuing export-control authority past the expiration of its authorizing statute via successive prior EOs, this order entrenches a precedent that executive continuation can substitute indefinitely for congressional reauthorization of a lapsed regulatory statute, a template usable beyond this specific export-control context.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order operates entirely within the Secretary of Commerce's existing export-control apparatus and explicitly preserves other agencies' existing delegations of Presidential authority (Sec. 3), without redirecting another agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Section 2 caps and narrows penalties (capping the maximum fine at $10,000 and excluding the civil penalty under part 387.1(b)(3) entirely) relative to what existed before, softening the compliance consequences for regulated exporters without defined criteria tying the change to any risk-based rationale.

note: This order is a full incorporation of the EO 11533/11683 export-control framework (checked at referenced-predecessors/EO-11533.md and EO-11683.md); the Zombie Emergency Trap fired because the text explicitly acknowledges the Export Administration Act's expiration while continuing authority under it.

title: Implementing the Generalized System of Preferences

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The President alone designates beneficiary developing countries and eligible articles (Secs. 1, 8, 9), and while advice from agencies, the public, and the International Trade Commission is required as input, the final designation decision and its criteria rest solely with the President with no external body able to compel or veto a designation.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The order operates under a defined statutory chain (Trade Act of 1974 Title V and Section 604) requiring congressional notification before designations (as stated in the preamble), giving Congress a defined channel of visibility and making the President clearly the responsible actor for designation decisions.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: All provisions (country designations, tariff schedule amendments, GSP eligibility criteria, effective dates) serve the single stated purpose of implementing the Generalized System of Preferences under the Trade Act of 1974; nothing here is an unrelated remedy or a second problem frame.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Eligibility for duty-free treatment is governed by precise, quantified rules-of-origin thresholds in the new General Headnote 3(c) (e.g., 35 percent of appraised value from a single beneficiary country, or 50 percent from an association of countries), leaving little room for divergent good-faith readings of the compliance standard.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The value-content thresholds and country/article designation process create clear, verifiable eligibility conditions for duty-free treatment with no incentive structure identifiable in the text that would reward evasion or gaming by importers, exporters, or the administering agencies.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order implements a standing tariff-schedule framework maintained through ordinary supersession -- it explicitly supersedes its own numbered predecessor (EO 11844, Sec. 11) and states the President 'is authorized to modify at any time the list' with a 'continuing review' process, showing the category is revised through routine amendment rather than needing formal reauthorization, consistent with the standing-procedural-framework carve-out.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order preserves and relies on the pre-existing statutory oversight chain -- congressional notification before designation and International Trade Commission advice on articles (as recited in the preamble) -- and does not remove or bypass any of these mechanisms.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Compliance with the rules-of-origin percentage thresholds is verified through Treasury regulations and importer self-certification (Sec. 9(c)(ii)), with no named competitor, domestic industry group, or other third party given standing in the text to challenge a claimed origin determination.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Designating specific countries and articles as GSP-eligible under a discretionary, continuously revisable Presidential list (Sec. 8, with authority to modify the list 'at any time') establishes a durable precedent for using tariff preference designation as a foreign-policy and trade lever beyond the immediate goods covered here.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order coordinates the President, the Secretary of the Treasury (regulations), and the International Trade Commission (advice) in complementary, non-conflicting statutory roles under Title V of the Trade Act, without redirecting any agency's mandate or creating parallel competing authority.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Subdivision (c)(iii) creates an 'A*' designation carving out certain named beneficiary countries from duty-free treatment on specific items at the President's sole discretion, concentrating exemption/exclusion determinations in one actor without a defined external check on how broadly the A* exclusions can be applied.

note: Compared against referenced-predecessors/EO-11844.md, which this order supersedes (Sec. 11); EO 11844 was itself a narrower country-designation order under the same statute, and this order continues and elaborates the same statutory framework without weakening any mechanism found in its predecessor.

title: Regulating Transactions in Foreign Exchange and Foreign-Owned Property, Providing For the Reporting of All Foreign-Owned Property, and Related Matters

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of the Treasury alone authorizes, licenses, investigates, and adjudicates every prohibited transaction, and Section 7 states 'the decision of the Secretary with respect to the granting, denial, or other disposition of an application or license shall be final,' explicitly foreclosing any external review of the Secretary's determinations.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of the Treasury both writes the licensing rules and issues final, unreviewable decisions under them (Sec. 7), so if licensing determinations are wrong or abusive there is no separate actor positioned to correct the failure.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: All provisions -- prohibited transactions, securities handling, reporting duties, definitions, and penalties -- serve the single stated purpose of foreign-exchange and foreign-property control tied to the designated countries and the declared national emergency; nothing here is an unrelated remedy bundled in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Section 1(F) prohibits 'Any transaction for the purpose or which has the effect of evading or avoiding the foregoing prohibitions' -- an open-ended catch-all with no defined trigger -- while the definition of 'national' in Section 5(E) extends to any person 'there is reasonable cause to believe' acted on behalf of a designated country, giving enforcing officials essentially unbounded discretion to determine who and what conduct is covered.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order derives from an active, unexpired national emergency and the still-operative Trading with the Enemy Act provisions; there is no acknowledged statutory lapse or expiration in the text, so the Zombie Emergency Trap sub-flag does not apply, and no other independent perverse-incentive structure for regulated parties, officials, or beneficiaries is identifiable in the text.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is an emergency wartime asset-freeze power exactly of the kind that should have a natural end tied to the emergency's resolution, yet Section 9 states the order 'may be amended, modified, or revoked at any time' with no review or expiration mechanism, leaving continuation entirely to unilateral future executive action.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order amends and consolidates a prior control regime (EO 8389/EO 6560, per referenced-predecessors) but does not describe removing any judicial, legislative, or administrative review mechanism that existed before it; the pre-existing regime had no such external check to remove.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement of the prohibitions relies on Treasury/Attorney General investigation and mandatory recordkeeping by the regulated party itself (Sec. 4), with no external third party given standing or access to independently surface violations of the transaction prohibitions.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The sweeping, reasonable-cause-based definition of 'national' and 'foreign country' (Sec. 5) creates a template for expansive extraterritorial property control that could be extended to additional countries by simple schedule amendment (Sec. 3), a precedent-setting mechanism reaching well beyond the ten countries listed at the time of signing.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Section 4 explicitly gives both the Secretary of the Treasury and the Attorney General complementary investigative authority, and Section 6 clarifies the relationship to the prior EO 6560 regime, without stripping any other agency's existing statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Every prohibition in Sections 1 and 2 applies 'except as specifically authorized by the Secretary of the Treasury by means of regulations, rulings, instructions, licenses, or otherwise' -- an unbounded, criteria-free licensing-exemption power held by a single official that can swallow the entire prohibition structure for any transaction the Secretary chooses to license.

note: Diff-aware review against referenced-predecessors/EO-8389.md (which itself amended EO-6560, also reviewed) shows this order restates and expands the same Treasury-controlled, finality-clause licensing architecture rather than introducing new checks; both predecessors shared the same single-official finality and open licensing-exemption design.

title: Revoking Executive Order 9301 of February 9, 1943, Establishing a Minimum Wartime Workweek of Forty-Eight Hours

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order is a one-sentence, self-executing revocation exercised directly by the President with no ongoing authority delegated to any official or body; no decision-making power is concentrated anywhere by this text.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: There is no ongoing implementation for which responsibility could be diffused; the revocation is immediate and complete, with no actor left administering anything under this order.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order contains a single provision (revoking EO 9301) with nothing else to bundle against, so the bundling question does not map onto this instrument.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Revoking the minimum-workweek order ends the underlying compliance regime rather than creating or continuing one, so there is no ongoing enforcement relationship for this flag to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order terminates a wartime compliance obligation rather than creating an ongoing behavioral relationship for any actor, so there is no incentive structure for this flag to evaluate; the Zombie Emergency Trap does not apply since no lapsed statute is being relied upon here.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: A one-time revocation is a self-executing termination action with no ongoing grant of authority created by this order for anything to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The revocation eliminates a wartime workweek mandate rather than any oversight, review, or check mechanism; no judicial, legislative, or administrative oversight process is removed by this text.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: With the underlying 48-hour workweek requirement revoked, there is no ongoing compliance regime left for any third party to have standing to enforce.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Ending a wartime labor mandate as the war concludes is a narrow administrative wind-down with no identifiable downstream precedent, definitional expansion, or institutional effect beyond restoring the pre-war baseline.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency's mandate is implicated, redirected, or countermanded by this simple revocation; the War Manpower Commission's role under EO 9301 simply ends rather than being reassigned elsewhere.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: There is no coverage population left to exempt anyone from once the underlying 48-hour workweek requirement is revoked in full.

note: Checked referenced-predecessors/EO-9301.md; this order is a clean, complete revocation of that wartime workweek mandate with no surviving mechanism, so most compliance-regime and narrow-order flags are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE per the mechanical test.

title: Providing for the Restoration of Law and Order in the State of Illinois

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of Defense alone is authorized and directed to determine what force to use, which National Guard units to federalize, and for how long ('an indefinite period and until relieved by appropriate orders,' Sec. 3), with no external judicial, legislative, or administrative check named in the text over how that domestic military authority is exercised.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of Defense is given broad discretion to determine what is 'necessary' and 'appropriate' (Secs. 1-3) and may further delegate that authority to military department secretaries (Sec. 4), diffusing responsibility for any given operational decision across multiple discretionary layers with no named reviewing actor.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: All four sections address the single stated purpose of dispersing the described violence and restoring order via military force, with nothing bundled in that serves an unrelated remedy or problem frame.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order directs the Secretary of Defense to take 'all appropriate steps' and use 'such of the Armed Forces... as he may deem necessary' (Secs. 1-2) with no definition of the triggering conditions for escalation, de-escalation, or the standard governing use of force, leaving these core determinations entirely to unguided official discretion during an active domestic deployment.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order relies on the active Insurrection Act framework (Title 10 Chapter 15) and a contemporaneous proclamation, not a lapsed statute, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not apply; no other independent incentive structure rewarding prolonged deployment or non-resolution is identifiable in the text.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Domestic military deployment to quell civil disorder is exactly the kind of exceptional authority that should have a natural end tied to the disorder's resolution, yet the order sets no time limit or review point, authorizing Guard federalization 'for an indefinite period and until relieved by appropriate orders' (Sec. 3) with no defined trigger for when that relief must occur.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order invokes existing statutory authority (Title 10 Chapter 15) without describing removal of any judicial, legislative, or administrative review mechanism that previously applied to domestic deployment decisions.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Compliance with and correctness of the Secretary's use-of-force and deployment decisions is checked by no named external party in the text; the chain runs entirely within the Department of Defense with no independent standing given to any outside actor to contest specific operational decisions.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Federalizing state National Guard units 'for an indefinite period' under broad Secretary of Defense discretion (Sec. 3) sets a precedent for open-ended domestic military deployment authority that could be invoked in future civil-disorder contexts well beyond the specific 1968 Illinois disturbances.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Federalizing the Illinois Army and Air National Guard displaces the Governor's ordinary command authority over those units, redirecting personnel that otherwise operate under state statutory control to federal military purposes.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: This is a use-of-force deployment order rather than a compliance or licensing regime, so there is no coverage population from which anyone could be exempted.

note: No prior EO was cited by number for comparison (only a same-day proclamation and statutory citations), so no predecessor lookup applied; the order's indefinite deployment authority and undefined use-of-force standard were assessed directly from its own text.

title: Transferring Jurisdiction Over Blocked Assets to the Attorney General

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Attorney General is authorized to 'take such action as he may deem necessary' over remaining blocked foreign property and may exercise all powers under Sections 3(a) and 5(b) of the Trading with the Enemy Act (Sec. 1), with paragraph 3 further barring any affected person from challenging administrative action on jurisdictional grounds -- a foreclosure of one avenue of challenge concentrated in the Attorney General's discretion.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Attorney General administers the remaining blocked-asset program and determines the scope of his own necessary action (Sec. 1) with no separate reviewing body named, and paragraph 3 explicitly forecloses affected parties from challenging the Attorney General/Treasury jurisdictional split as a basis for relief.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order addresses a single subject -- transferring jurisdiction over remaining blocked assets from Treasury to the Attorney General -- and every section serves that one purpose without folding in unrelated remedies.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Attorney General is authorized to 'take such action as he may deem necessary' (Sec. 1) with no defined standard distinguishing necessary action from any other administrative choice, leaving the scope of enforcement to unguided discretion.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order continues authority under the still-active Trading With the Enemy Act rather than a lapsed statute, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not apply; no other independent perverse incentive is identifiable, as the order is a narrow jurisdictional handoff of already-blocked property rather than an ongoing behavioral rule for third parties.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Wartime asset-blocking authority carried over past the end of hostilities (the preamble notes 'the successful termination of hostilities') is exactly the kind of authority that should track resolution of the underlying emergency, yet the order sets no expiration or review point for when the Attorney General's blocked-asset jurisdiction itself must end.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Paragraph 3 states that no affected person 'may challenge the validity thereof or otherwise excuse his actions... on the ground that... such order... was within the jurisdiction of the Attorney General rather than the Secretary of the Treasury or vice versa,' explicitly foreclosing a jurisdictional challenge avenue that existed for affected parties before this order consolidated jurisdiction.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement of continuing blocked-asset restrictions depends entirely on the Attorney General's own administration (Sec. 1-2), with no external party given standing in the text to independently surface improper unblocking or continued wrongful blocking.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Consolidating Treasury's wartime blocking authority into the Attorney General's ongoing alien-property program (established under EO 9095, per referenced-predecessors) extends a wartime emergency-powers apparatus into peacetime administrative machinery beyond the war-driven blocking it was originally built for.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order transfers jurisdiction that the Secretary of the Treasury previously held over blocked assets to the Attorney General (Sec. 1-2), redirecting an existing agency's operational authority to a different agency, even though paragraph 3 tries to preserve underlying statutory powers of both.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order does not create new exemptions; it simply transfers jurisdiction over property not already unblocked, with unblocking criteria continuing to be governed by the pre-existing Treasury regulations and general licenses referenced in Section 1.

note: Checked referenced-predecessors/EO-8389.md and EO-9095.md, both cited by number; this order extends the EO 9095 Alien Property Custodian program's authority (as inherited by the Attorney General) into the remaining Treasury-blocked assets without altering either predecessor's underlying licensing criteria.

title: Transfer of the Federal Fire Council to the Department of Commerce

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Council remains a multi-member advisory body drawing representatives from numerous departments (Sec. 3) and the Secretary of Commerce chairs it, but authority is distributed across the governing body's members rather than concentrated in one unchecked official, and the Council's role is advisory (Sec. 4-5) with no independent enforcement power.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Council reports its investigations and recommendations directly to 'the head of the agency concerned' (Sec. 5), and the Secretary of Commerce is clearly named as the responsible official to whom the Council reports (Sec. 1), giving a clear chain of responsibility.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order's provisions all serve the single stated purpose of transferring and reconstituting the Federal Fire Council in the Department of Commerce, with no unrelated remedy or problem frame folded in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Council has only advisory and investigatory functions with no compliance obligation imposed on any regulated party, so there is no enforcement relationship for this flag to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: As a purely advisory fire-safety body with no compliance mandate on any outside actor, there is no ongoing behavioral relationship creating incentives for this flag to evaluate, and no lapsed statute is invoked so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not apply.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Federal fire-safety advisory functions are a standing administrative function with no natural end-state, and the order's own text shows this category is maintained through ordinary supersession -- it explicitly supersedes its two numbered predecessors (EO 7397 and EO 10257, Sec. 8), evidencing a pattern of periodic amendment rather than indefinite freezing, consistent with the standing-procedural-framework carve-out.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds a new consultation requirement with the Federal Advisory Council on Occupational Safety and Health (Sec. 7, referencing EO 11612) rather than removing any pre-existing oversight mechanism; no review process from the predecessor orders is eliminated.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The Council has no enforcement function over any regulated party, so there is no compliance violation for a third party to have standing to surface.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: This is a narrow administrative reorganization of an advisory fire-safety council with no systemic or precedential implications reaching beyond federal fire-protection coordination.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Multiple agencies (Interior, HEW, HUD, Army, Navy, Air Force, GSA) are named as Council participants in a coordinating, non-conflicting advisory role (Sec. 3), and the transfer from GSA to Commerce is accomplished cleanly without redirecting any agency's independent statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: There is no coverage population or compliance obligation established by this advisory-council order from which any actor could be exempted.

note: Checked referenced-predecessors/EO-7397.md, EO-10257.md, and EO-11612.md, all cited by number; the order cleanly transfers and continues the same advisory-only Council structure found in its predecessors without any weakening of mechanism.

title: Revocation of Executive Order 9103 of March 18, 1942, Providing Uniform Control Over the Publication and Use of Federal Statistical Information Which Would Give Aid and Comfort to the Enemy

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order is a one-sentence, self-executing revocation with no ongoing authority created or delegated to any official or body by this text.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: There is no continuing implementation for which responsibility could be diffused; the revocation is immediate and complete, ending the Bureau of the Budget's surveillance role under the prior order rather than leaving it in ambiguous hands.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order contains a single provision (revoking EO 9103) with nothing else present to bundle against.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Revoking the wartime statistical-control order ends the underlying compliance regime rather than creating or continuing an enforcement relationship for this flag to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order terminates a wartime information-control mandate rather than creating an ongoing behavioral relationship for any actor; no lapsed statute is being relied upon, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not apply.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: A one-time revocation is a self-executing termination with no ongoing grant of authority created by this order for anything to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The revocation eliminates a wartime information-suppression mandate rather than any oversight or review mechanism; no judicial, legislative, or administrative check is removed by this text.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: With the Bureau of the Budget's surveillance authority over statistical publication revoked, there is no ongoing compliance regime left for any third party to have standing to enforce.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Ending wartime restrictions on publishing federal statistical information as the war concludes is a narrow administrative wind-down restoring the pre-war publication baseline, with no identifiable downstream precedent or institutional effect beyond that.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency's mandate is implicated or redirected; the Bureau of the Budget's surveillance role under EO 9103 simply ends rather than being reassigned to another agency.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: There is no coverage population left to exempt anyone from once the underlying statistical-publication control is revoked in full.

note: Checked referenced-predecessors/EO-9103.md; this order is a clean, complete revocation of that wartime statistical-control mandate with no surviving mechanism, so most compliance-regime and narrow-order flags are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE per the mechanical test.

title: Inspection by Renegotiation Board of Income Tax Returns Made Under the Internal Revenue Code of 1954

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order simply authorizes Renegotiation Board access to tax returns 'in accordance and upon compliance with the rules and regulations prescribed by the Acting Secretary of the Treasury,' so decision authority over how inspection occurs rests in defined Treasury regulations rather than being pooled in a single unchecked official by this text.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Acting Secretary of the Treasury is clearly named as the official who prescribes the governing rules and regulations for the inspection, giving a clear, specific locus of responsibility for how the access is administered.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order contains a single provision -- authorizing Renegotiation Board inspection of tax returns under a named Treasury decision -- with no other distinct matter present to bundle against.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Inspection must occur 'in accordance and upon compliance with the rules and regulations prescribed' in a specific, named Treasury decision approved the same day, tying the standard to a defined external document rather than leaving it to undefined discretion.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: This creates an ongoing inter-agency information-access relationship governed by defined Treasury rules, and no incentive structure rewarding non-compliance or gaming by any actor is identifiable in the text; no lapsed statute is invoked, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not apply.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Tax-return information sharing between federal bodies for renegotiation purposes is a standing administrative function under the permanent Internal Revenue Code Section 6103(a) authority, with no natural end-state that would make continued access inappropriate, consistent with the evergreen procedural-framework carve-out.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order grants new inspection access to the Renegotiation Board without removing or limiting any existing judicial, legislative, or administrative oversight mechanism over tax-return confidentiality; it operates entirely under Section 6103(a)'s existing framework.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Access to tax returns is bounded by specific rules and regulations approved by the Acting Secretary of the Treasury the same day, and the order's narrow subject (inter-agency inspection rights) does not create a compliance regime for regulated private parties requiring third-party enforcement standing.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: This is a narrow administrative order granting one board inspection access to tax returns under defined rules, with no systemic or precedential implication reaching beyond that specific inter-agency information-sharing arrangement.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order coordinates the Renegotiation Board's access with Treasury's rulemaking role in a complementary, non-conflicting way rather than redirecting or countermanding either agency's existing statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: There is no coverage population being regulated with exemptions to map; this is a narrow inter-agency information-access grant, not a compliance regime with carve-outs.

note: No prior EO was cited by number as amended, extended, revoked, or superseded, so no predecessor lookup applied; this is a clean, narrowly defined inter-agency information-access order with no structural flags firing on the merits.

title: Withdrawal of Public Lands for the Use of the War Department

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order is a self-executing land-status withdrawal effective immediately by its own terms; it does not delegate any ongoing decision-making authority to any official or body, so no authority is concentrated anywhere by this text.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: There is no ongoing implementation program created by this order for which responsibility could be diffused; it is a one-time reservation of described land tracts for War Department use, with the boundary conditions clearly stated relative to prior withdrawals.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order contains a single land-withdrawal action with carefully specified boundary carve-outs tied to that one action, leaving nothing distinct present to bundle against.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time withdrawal of public lands from appropriation creates no ongoing behavioral compliance rule for any actor, so there is no enforcement relationship for this flag to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: This is a one-time land-status action with no ongoing regulatory relationship created for any actor's conduct, so there is no incentive structure for this flag to evaluate, and no lapsed statute is invoked.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order withdraws land for a defined military training purpose (a bombing and gunnery range) with no expiration or review mechanism tied to that purpose's continuation, in contrast to the reclamation-related carve-out described in the same paragraph, which does terminate 'upon notice to the War Department... that such lands are needed for reclamation purposes' -- showing the drafters could have built in a comparable trigger for the broader withdrawal but did not.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order explicitly preserves 'valid existing rights' and does not rescind or revoke the prior Public Water Reserve No. 61 withdrawal except as to one specific overlapping parcel, showing it narrows rather than removes any pre-existing review or competing-use mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: A land-status withdrawal from appropriation under public-land laws creates no ongoing compliance obligation for private parties that would require third-party enforcement standing.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: This is a narrow, geographically bounded land withdrawal for a specific military training purpose with defined acreage and explicit carve-outs for pre-existing reservations, with no identifiable precedent or systemic effect reaching beyond this specific parcel of Oregon land.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order takes precedence over the Interior Department's prior Public Water Reserve No. 61 withdrawal as to one overlapping parcel and displaces the Secretary of the Interior's prior reclamation-purpose reservation on other parcels (subject to a notice-based reversion), redirecting land from one agency's existing administrative purpose to the War Department's use.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: There is no regulatory coverage population being established by this land withdrawal from which anyone could be exempted; the boundary carve-outs described are jurisdictional limits on the withdrawal itself, not exemptions from a compliance rule.

note: No prior EO was cited by number for amendment/revocation (only an unnumbered 1919 Executive order and a Secretary of the Interior order, neither retrievable as numbered predecessors), so no referenced-predecessors lookup applied; the order's own text was sufficient to assess the applicable flags.

title: Adjusting Rates of Pay for Certain Statutory Pay Systems

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order mechanically adjusts statutory pay tables under 5 U.S.C. ch. 53 and 38 U.S.C. 4107; no discretionary authority is pooled in any official -- the rates are simply set by the order's own text and administered per existing statutory ceilings (Sec. 4, referencing 5 U.S.C. 5308).

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: There is no implementation failure mode to trace: the order sets fixed salary figures effective a specific date (Sec. 5), a self-executing administrative action with no discretionary judgment left to any actor.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order bundles several pay schedules under one stated purpose of statutory pay adjustment, but each schedule is independently justified by the same annual pay-adjustment authority and each could be accepted or rejected on its own without altering the others' substance.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A pay-rate schedule adjustment creates no ongoing behavioral compliance regime for any regulated party; there is nothing to enforce.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing regulatory relationship or emergency authority is created; this is a fixed-schedule pay adjustment with no incentive structure to evaluate.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order makes a one-time schedule adjustment effective on a set date (Sec. 5) rather than granting an ongoing discretionary authority that could require reauthorization.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order does not touch any existing review mechanism; salary ceilings under section 5308 remain expressly preserved (Sec. 4).

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no compliance regime and thus no question of third-party standing to surface violations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A routine statutory pay-table update carries no systemic or precedential implications beyond its immediate administrative effect.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No agency's statutory mandate is redirected, conflicted, or duplicated; each affected department merely applies its own updated pay table.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: There is no coverage population from which anyone could be exempted; the order sets pay rates rather than creating a compliance obligation.

note: A narrow, self-executing pay-schedule adjustment; seven flags were NOT_APPLICABLE because no ongoing regulatory or compliance relationship, discretionary authority, or agency conflict is created by the text.

title: Designating the Intergovernmental Maritime Consultative Organization as a Public International Organization Entitled To Enjoy Certain Privileges, Exemptions, and Immunities

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order is a one-time designation under the International Organizations Immunities Act; no discretionary authority over eligibility, enforcement, or interpretation is pooled in any official going forward.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The designation is self-executing and creates no ongoing implementation function whose failure would need tracing to a responsible actor.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order contains a single provision (designation of one organization) with nothing else to bundle against.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order confers immunities rather than creating any compliance obligation to enforce against a regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral relationship or agency incentive structure is created by a one-time immunities designation.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The designation is a one-time status conferral under the statute, not an ongoing discretionary grant of authority that would need reauthorization.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order expressly preserves any existing or future privileges the organization may acquire by treaty or congressional action, and removes no existing oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no compliance regime for which third-party enforcement standing would be relevant.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow immunities designation for one international organization has no identifiable systemic or precedential effect beyond its stated purpose.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second domestic agency's mandate is implicated by this designation.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: There is no coverage population or compliance obligation from which exemptions could be carved.

note: A narrow, one-time international-organization immunities designation; eight flags were NOT_APPLICABLE because no ongoing regulatory relationship, discretionary authority, or multi-provision structure exists to assess.

title: Authorizing the Secretary of War to Take Possession of and Operate the Plants and Facilities of the Farrell Cheek Steel Company, Located at Sandusky, Ohio

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of War is authorized to take possession of the plant, exercise its contracts, hire or continue employees, and 'do any other thing that he may deem necessary' (Sec. 1) with no external check on that discretion other than the President himself; authority over the seizure's scope and management is pooled entirely in one official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of War is the clearly named, singularly responsible actor for operation of the plant and must report to the President if net operating income is insufficient to cover wage increases (Sec. 2), giving a clear locus of responsibility.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order addresses a single remedy (seizure and operation of one named company's plant to resolve one labor disturbance) with no unrelated provisions bundled in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Termination is tied to when the Secretary 'determines' that 'productive efficiency... prevailing prior to the existing interruptions' has been restored (Sec. 4) -- a standard with no defined metric, leaving substantial discretion to the same official who benefits from continued operation to decide when the trigger is met.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The Secretary of War, who controls the plant during the seizure, is also the one who determines when productive efficiency has been 'restored' and thus when his own authority ends (Sec. 4), creating an incentive misalignment between the seizing authority and a neutral termination trigger. This does not invoke a lapsed emergency statute, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order contains an explicit termination mechanism: possession must end within 60 days after the Secretary determines pre-interruption productive efficiency has been restored (Sec. 4), tying the emergency wartime seizure authority to resolution of the triggering condition as required by the permanence carve-out's own logic (contrast EO 9066, which lacked any such tie).

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order operates in the shadow of existing War Labor Board directive orders but authorizes the Secretary to pay wage increases 'only out of the net operating income' (Sec. 2), effectively subordinating the Board's wage directives to the Secretary's own operational judgment and available income rather than the Board's independent determination.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement of proper operation depends entirely on the Secretary of War's own reporting to the President if income is insufficient (Sec. 2); no external party -- employees, the company's ousted management, or an independent board -- has standing to contest the Secretary's management decisions during the seizure.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order establishes a precedent for using wartime seizure authority to resolve a single-company labor dispute at the Secretary's discretion, a template usable in other labor disputes framed as impeding 'the war effort' without further legislative involvement.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order directs the Secretary of War to operate the plant consistent with existing War Labor Board directive orders (Sec. 2) rather than displacing the Board's statutory role or another agency's mandate; the two functions are coordinated rather than in conflict.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order applies to a single named company's plant with no broader covered population from which exemptions could be carved.

note: A wartime plant-seizure order targeting one named company; most flags fire at PRESENT due to concentrated, largely unchecked discretion in the Secretary of War over both operation and termination, but the explicit 60-day termination trigger keeps Flag 6 clean and no flag rises to CRITICAL given the narrow, time-limited scope.

title: Alaska Railroad Rates

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Rate-setting authority is held by the Secretary of Transportation but is explicitly made 'subject to the authority of the Interstate Commerce Commission' (Sec. 1) which may act on filed rates (Sec. 3); this is the same two-body check structure as predecessor EO 11107, so no new concentration is introduced.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of Transportation is clearly responsible for rate-setting and must file rates with the ICC (Sec. 2(a)), and the ICC has clear independent review authority (Sec. 3), preserving the same accountability structure as EO 11107.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: All provisions relate to the single stated purpose of establishing Alaska Railroad rate procedures; nothing unrelated is bundled in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order specifies concrete filing and review mechanics (rates 'filed with the Interstate Commerce Commission,' contracts available to other shippers under defined timing conditions in Sec. 2(b)), providing clearer procedural detail than the shorter predecessor EO 11107.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The rate-setting structure gives no actor an incentive to undermine the order's purpose of orderly rate administration; the ICC continues to provide an independent check per Sec. 3, as it did under EO 11107.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a standing procedural framework for setting freight rates with no natural end-state; the order's own text shows it is maintained through ordinary supersession (it explicitly supersedes EO 11107, Sec. 4(a)), which is the evidence the permanence carve-out requires for the standing-procedural-framework category rather than an open-ended discretionary grant.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Comparing against predecessor EO 11107, ICC review of Alaska Railroad rates is preserved and if anything elaborated (new shipper-access and connecting-carrier provisions in Sec. 2(b)-(c)); no existing oversight is removed by this order.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Section 2(b) gives other shippers standing to access any filed contract rate on comparable terms, and the ICC independently reviews filed rates (Sec. 3), providing external actors with both access and incentive to monitor rate-setting.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order extends an existing, narrow rate-administration framework to reflect the transfer of Alaska Railroad functions to the Secretary of Transportation, with no identifiable precedential effect beyond this specific administrative continuity.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The division of labor between the Secretary of Transportation and the Interstate Commerce Commission is coordinated by explicit cross-reference (Sec. 1, Sec. 3) rather than conflicting, mirroring the predecessor's clean division of authority.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order establishes rate-setting procedure rather than a compliance regime with a covered population from which exemptions could be carved.

note: This order explicitly supersedes EO 11107 (Sec. 4(a)); comparison against that predecessor's full text (available in referenced-predecessors/EO-11107.md) shows the ICC-check structure and shipper protections are preserved and elaborated rather than weakened, so it scores cleanly across all applicable flags.

title: Establishing the President's Advisory Commission on Narcotic and Drug Abuse

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Commission is purely advisory, tasked with developing recommendations to transmit to the President (Sec. 2(a)); it holds no enforcement, eligibility, or implementation authority that could be characterized as concentrated decision-making power.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Commission must transmit an interim report by April 1, 1963 and a final report by November 1, 1963 (Sec. 2(a), Sec. 6), with the Chairman and Executive Director as named responsible officials for its work product.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: All provisions serve the single stated purpose of studying narcotic and drug abuse policy; nothing unrelated is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: An advisory commission with no regulatory or compliance authority over any outside party creates no enforcement relationship to evaluate.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The Commission's function is limited to study and recommendation with fixed per diem compensation (Sec. 4); there is no ongoing regulatory relationship in which an actor's incentives could be misaligned with a compliance purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The Commission has an explicit termination date: final report due by November 1, 1963, and termination no later than December 31, 1963 (Sec. 6), a clean example of a defined sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order creates a new advisory and coordinating body without removing any existing oversight mechanism from the agencies it reviews (Sec. 2(b), Sec. 3).

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no regulated party or compliance obligation created by an advisory study commission, so third-party enforcement standing is not a relevant question.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: As a time-limited advisory commission producing recommendations for legislative consideration (Sec. 2(a)), the order creates no independent authority or precedent that would persist or be usable beyond its stated purpose.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Departments are asked only to designate liaisons and furnish information and legislative recommendations (Sec. 3), a coordinating role that does not redirect or conflict with any department's existing statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order creates a study commission rather than a compliance regime with a covered population, so there is no exemption structure to assess.

note: A time-limited advisory commission with a clean, well-bounded structure and explicit termination date; four flags were NOT_APPLICABLE because the order creates no ongoing compliance or enforcement relationship with any outside party.

title: Establishing the Federal Committee On Highway Safety

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Committee is a multi-agency coordinating body composed of representatives from numerous named agencies (Sec. 1), with no single official holding unilateral decision authority; agency heads are only 'requested,' not directed, to take action (Sec. 3).

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Committee's mandate is to 'promote' and 'encourage' cooperation and 'coordinate... to the extent permitted by law' (Sec. 2), and agency heads are merely 'requested' to cooperate (Sec. 3) with no named actor holding both the obligation and the capacity to correct implementation failure if highway safety coordination does not improve.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order addresses a single coherent purpose -- coordinating federal highway safety activities -- through a single committee mechanism; nothing unrelated is bundled in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order creates a purely advisory/coordinating committee with no compliance obligation imposed on any outside party, so there is no enforcement mechanism to assess for clarity.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing regulatory or behavioral compliance relationship is created; the Committee only encourages and coordinates voluntary agency cooperation.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The Committee is granted standing, ongoing coordinating authority with no termination date, review requirement, or reauthorization mechanism specified anywhere in the order, and its subject matter (an ongoing interagency coordination function) is not the kind of evergreen rights mandate the permanence carve-out protects.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order creates a new coordinating body without removing any pre-existing review mechanism from any of the named agencies.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no compliance regime or regulated party; the order concerns voluntary interagency coordination on highway safety, not enforcement against any actor.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The Committee's function is limited to promoting cooperation among agencies already possessing their own statutory highway-related authorities; no new precedent or authority claim usable in unrelated contexts is created.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Thirteen named agencies participate as equal representatives with the Committee only encouraging cooperation 'to the extent permitted by law' (Sec. 2); no agency's existing statutory mandate is redirected or overridden.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: There is no covered population or compliance obligation from which exemptions could be carved; the order only establishes a coordinating committee.

note: A standing interagency coordinating committee with no termination date and only hortatory ('requested'/'encourage') obligations; this combination produces a genuine, if mild, accountability gap and an open-ended sunset finding, while other flags are not applicable given the absence of any compliance regime.

title: Advisory Committee on Small and Minority Business Ownership

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Committee is purely advisory, tasked with monitoring and proposing incentives (Sec. 1-201) and reporting to the President through the SBA Administrator (Sec. 1-202); it holds no independent decision authority over eligibility or enforcement.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Committee must make periodic reports and an annual report to the President and Congress (Sec. 1-202, 1-203), and the SBA Administrator is named as the responsible official for administrative support and FACA compliance (Sec. 1-303, 1-401).

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: All provisions serve the single stated purpose implementing Section 7(j)(3)(A) of the Small Business Act; nothing unrelated to small/minority business subcontracting is bundled in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Committee has an advisory/monitoring role with no enforcement authority over any regulated party, so there is no compliance mechanism to assess for clarity.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order creates an advisory committee with fixed, statutorily-authorized travel reimbursement (Sec. 1-302) and no ongoing regulatory relationship whose incentives could be misaligned with the order's purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order sets an explicit termination date: 'The Committee shall terminate on December 31, 1980' (Sec. 1-402), a clean, defined sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order actually preserves FACA obligations, delegating the President's FACA functions to the SBA Administrator 'except that of reporting annually to the Congress' (Sec. 1-401), which itself remains with the President/Congress rather than being eliminated; no pre-existing oversight is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The Committee is an advisory body monitoring private-sector subcontracting practices rather than enforcing compliance obligations, so there is no third-party enforcement question to evaluate.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: As a one-year statutory advisory committee implementing a specific Small Business Act provision (Sec. 7(j)(3)(A)), the order creates no independent precedent or authority claim extending beyond its narrow, time-limited purpose.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Committee operates through and reports to the Administrator of the Small Business Administration (Sec. 1-202, 1-303), consistent with the SBA's own statutory mandate, without redirecting authority from any other agency.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order defines eligible small businesses for Committee monitoring purposes (Sec. 1-201(b)) rather than creating a compliance regime with exemptions to map.

note: A clean, statutorily-directed advisory committee with an explicit termination date and preserved FACA reporting obligations; five flags were NOT_APPLICABLE because the order creates no ongoing compliance or enforcement relationship.

title: Implementing the United States-Canada Free-Trade Implementation Act

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Rulemaking notice obligations run to 'each agency subject to' the APA generally (Sec. 1(a)), and panel-decision acceptance is a fixed presidential commitment under statute (Sec. 3) rather than open-ended discretionary authority pooled in one official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each covered agency remains independently responsible for its own notice-and-comment compliance (Sec. 1(a)), and the Secretariat's facilitation role for binational panels is clearly assigned within Commerce's International Trade Administration (Sec. 2).

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: All provisions directly implement the single stated purpose of the FTA Implementation Act; each is independently traceable to that statute.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Agencies may depart from the 75-day notice requirement 'where, in urgent circumstances, delay would frustrate the achievement of a legitimate domestic objective' (Sec. 1(a)) -- a standard broad enough that different agencies could reach substantially different judgments about when urgency excuses compliance.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order's structure (advance notice requirement, narrow urgent-circumstances exception, no self-dealing implementation body) does not create an incentive for any actor to act contrary to the order's trade-transparency purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order implements a standing procedural requirement tied to the life of the underlying Free-Trade Agreement itself (Sec. 5, effective upon entry into force), which is a standing administrative framework tethered to an external, ongoing treaty relationship rather than an open-ended discretionary grant needing independent reauthorization.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Section 4 states the order 'does not create any right or benefit, substantive or procedural, enforceable at law by a party against the United States,' foreclosing judicial review as an avenue to enforce the notice and procedural obligations the order itself creates.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Because Section 4 bars judicial enforceability, affected parties have no legal standing to compel agency compliance with the 75-day notice requirement, leaving enforcement entirely to the agencies' own self-assessment of 'urgent circumstances.'

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: By establishing a standing U.S. Secretariat to facilitate binational panels and committing in advance to accept 'as a whole, all decisions' of those panels and extraordinary challenge committees once the relevant statutory provision takes effect (Sec. 3), the order creates institutional and precedential machinery for a novel form of international dispute resolution that displaces conventional judicial review of trade remedy determinations.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Secretariat is established within Commerce's existing International Trade Administration to facilitate, not override, the operation of the FTA's dispute chapters (Sec. 2), and other agencies retain their normal APA rulemaking role subject only to the timing adjustment in Sec. 1.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Section 1(c) exempts all proposed rules related to agricultural, food, beverage, and related goods (as defined in Chapter Seven of the Free-Trade Agreement) from the entire notice requirement, and the 'urgent circumstances'/'legitimate domestic objective' carve-out in Sec. 1(a) gives implementing agencies unilateral, loosely bounded discretion to exempt individual rules from advance notice.
