note: A trade-agreement implementation order whose bar on judicial enforceability (Sec. 4) combined with a vague urgency exception and a categorical agricultural carve-out produces several PRESENT findings, though none rises to CRITICAL since the core notice-and-panel-acceptance architecture still substantially functions as intended.

title: Authorizing Certain Employees of the General Accounting Office To Acquire a Classified Civil-Service Status

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order permits continuance and civil-service conversion of a defined class of GAO employees 'upon recommendation by the Comptroller General,' but conversion itself is bounded by fixed, objective eligibility criteria (continuous service since July 1, 1935; passing prescribed noncompetitive tests; citizenship; no disqualification), leaving little discretionary authority concentrated anywhere.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Comptroller General's recommendation role and the Civil Service Commission's testing role (Proviso (2)) are both clearly named, with defined eligibility criteria against which any implementation failure could be checked.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order addresses a single coherent action -- continuance and status conversion of a defined class of employees whose earlier continuance was authorized by EO 8137 -- with no unrelated matter bundled in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order sets four specific, objective conditions (continuous satisfactory service since July 1, 1935; passing prescribed tests; citizenship; no rule-based disqualification) that leave little room for two officials to reach substantially different eligibility determinations.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order extends employment continuance and conversion eligibility under objective, previously-established criteria; no actor is incentivized to act against the order's purpose of regularizing employment status. This order does not itself acknowledge any statutory lapse, so no Zombie Emergency Trap analysis applies.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order converts a temporary emergency-hire status into permanent classified civil-service status for qualifying individuals -- a one-time status conversion rather than an ongoing discretionary grant of authority, so there is no ongoing authority to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Comparing to predecessor EO 8137 (a bare one-sentence continuance extension with no conversion process at all), this order does not remove any oversight -- it affirmatively adds Civil Service Commission testing and eligibility review (Proviso (2)) as a new check before status conversion, which does not fire this flag per the direction-of-change rule.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The Civil Service Commission independently administers the noncompetitive fitness tests (Proviso (2)) and enforces the civil service rules disqualification criteria (Proviso (4)), giving an external body with standing and incentive to check eligibility beyond the GAO's own self-certification.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: This is a narrow administrative order converting a small, defined class of existing GAO employees to permanent civil-service status, with no systemic or precedential implications beyond that specific personnel action.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Civil Service Commission's testing role and the Comptroller General's recommendation role are complementary rather than conflicting, and neither agency's existing statutory mandate is redirected or overridden.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order sets four specific, narrow eligibility conditions (Provisos 1-4) rather than a broad or discretionary exemption structure; conversion is a defined status change for a fixed cohort, not an exemption from an otherwise-applicable rule.

note: This order references EO 8137 (a one-sentence prior continuance extension, available in referenced-predecessors/EO-8137.md); comparison confirms this order adds a Civil Service Commission testing/eligibility layer rather than removing any check, so Flag 7 scores ABSENT rather than PRESENT.

title: Classified National Security Information

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Original classification authority is exercisable only by the President, agency heads, or officials they designate (Sec. 1.4(a)), and an agency head's decision to deny the Information Security Oversight Office access to a special access program can only be overridden through an internal executive appeal to the OMB Director in consultation with the National Security Advisor (Sec. 4.4(b)(3), Sec. 5.3(b)(4)) -- an executive-branch-internal check rather than an external one, though this concentration is inherent to any classification system and is narrower than predecessor EO 12356's equivalent structure.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement of classification standards depends on the Director of the Information Security Oversight Office referring suspected violations back to the same agency head who oversees the classifying official (Sec. 5.7(a)), with sanctions ultimately administered by that agency's own leadership (Sec. 5.7(c)-(d)) -- the agency responsible for proper classification also polices its own compliance.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order's six parts are all component pieces of a single unified classification system: each is independently justifiable as part of the stated purpose of prescribing 'a uniform system for classifying, safeguarding, and declassifying national security information.'

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The standard for original classification requires the classifying official to determine disclosure 'reasonably could be expected to result in damage to the national security' (Sec. 1.2(a)(4)) -- a judgment-laden standard under which two officials could reach different classification decisions on the same information in good faith, though the order narrows this compared to predecessor language by requiring the authority to be 'able to identify or describe the damage.'

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order includes explicit anti-gaming provisions -- information may not be classified to 'conceal violations of law, inefficiency, or administrative error' or to 'prevent embarrassment' (Sec. 1.8(a)) -- and a presumption against classification when there is 'significant doubt' (Sec. 1.2(b)), which affirmatively counters rather than creates an incentive to over-classify. This order does not invoke or continue authority past any lapsed statute, so the Zombie Emergency Trap does not fire.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Unlike a discretionary grant of unreviewed authority, this order builds in an affirmative, self-executing reauthorization pressure: all classified information is automatically declassified after a maximum 10-year period absent a specific extension decision (Sec. 1.6(b)-(c)) and historically valuable records are automatically declassified after 25 years with narrow, enumerated exemptions (Sec. 3.4(a)-(b)) -- a designed periodic-review mechanism, not permanence by default.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Relative to predecessor EO 12356, this order adds substantial new oversight machinery rather than removing any: a new Classification Challenges provision (Sec. 1.9) and a broadened Interagency Security Classification Appeals Panel (Sec. 5.4) -- all additions, not removals, under the flag's own direction-of-change rule.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Outside of the newly created internal Classification Challenges process (Sec. 1.9) and FOIA/Privacy Act/mandatory-review requests, there is no external party with independent standing to audit whether information was properly classified in the first instance; classification determinations remain within the originating agency's own discretion, and the compliance information needed to challenge an improper classification is itself often classified.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: By formalizing a governmentwide declassification database (Sec. 3.8) and mandatory automatic declassification timelines (Sec. 3.4), the order creates an institutional and technological infrastructure and set of expectations for public access to historical government records that persists and expands well beyond this order's immediate classification-system purpose.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Special access program authority is explicitly restricted to the Secretaries of State, Defense, Energy, and the Director of Central Intelligence or their principal deputies (Sec. 4.4(a)), a narrowing relative to broader agency-head authority under EO 12356 that reduces rather than creates jurisdictional conflict, and existing agency roles are clearly delineated with no statutory mandate redirected.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: An agency head may exempt entire categories of information from automatic declassification under nine broad, judgment-based categories (Sec. 3.4(b)), and may further exempt whole 'file series' of records determined to 'almost invariably' qualify (Sec. 3.4(c)) -- a structure that lets the same officials who classify information also determine, largely at their own discretion, what escapes the order's central declassification-transparency purpose.

note: This order explicitly revokes and replaces EO 12356 (Sec. 6.1(d)); diff-aware comparison against that predecessor's full text (available in referenced-predecessors/EO-12356.md) shows this order adds substantial new oversight machinery, so Flag 7 and Flag 10 score ABSENT rather than PRESENT despite the order's overall length and complexity.

title: Providing for the Performance by the Director of Defense Mobilization of Certain Functions Relating to Telecommunications

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: All telecommunications coordination, frequency assignment, and policy authority is vested in a single official, the Director of Defense Mobilization, with only self-initiated interagency consultation (Sec. 2) as a check; this mirrors the identical single-advisor concentration in predecessor EO 10297, so the structure is continued rather than newly created.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Director reports to the President and Sec. 4 preserves FCC jurisdiction as an external check on radio frequency matters, the same accountability structure carried over cleanly from EO 10297.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order addresses a single coherent subject (transfer of telecommunications advisory functions) with no unrelated provisions bundled in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: This is an internal reassignment of advisory/coordinating functions between executive offices with no compliance obligation imposed on any regulated party, so there is no ongoing enforcement relationship to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing regulatory or compliance relationship is created for any actor class; this is a pure functional transfer between executive bodies.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a standing procedural/administrative function (telecommunications coordination) transferred via explicit supersession of a numbered predecessor (Sec. 6 revokes EO 10297), the routine amendment/supersession evergreen category, so the permanence carve-out applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Sec. 4 explicitly preserves 'any existing authority or jurisdiction of the Federal Communications Commission,' the same oversight-preserving clause present in EO 10297 Sec. 5, so no existing check is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no regulated party or compliance scheme here for a third party to monitor; this is an intra-executive functional assignment.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order merely relocates an existing advisory function from one executive office to another without expanding its scope or creating new precedent beyond what EO 10297 already established.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Sec. 4 explicitly protects FCC jurisdiction and Sec. 3 preserves the Interdepartmental Radio Advisory Committee's advisory role, avoiding conflict with existing agency mandates.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: There is no coverage population or exemption scheme in this functional-transfer order.

note: This order revokes and replaces EO 10297; comparison with the predecessor's text shows the functions, FCC savings clause, and advisory-committee structure are carried over essentially unchanged, only the host office changes.

title: Establishing a National Commission on the Observance of International Women's Year, 1975

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Authority is distributed across a 35-member Commission appointed by the President, with the Attorney General/State Department roles limited to designation of a presiding officer for the task force; no single official holds unilateral decision authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Commission's only accountability mechanism is a final report to the President (Sec. 2(h)) with no requirement that anyone act on it, and the Commission self-terminates at year end, so there is no actor responsible for correcting implementation failures during its brief life.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order addresses a single coherent purpose, observance of International Women's Year, with no unrelated provisions bundled in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order creates an advisory/observance commission with no compliance obligation on any regulated party, so there is no enforcement relationship to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing regulatory or behavioral compliance relationship is created; the Commission is purely promotional and advisory.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Sec. 2(h) explicitly provides the Commission 'shall conclude its work by the end of the year 1975... The Commission shall then be terminated,' a clean, self-executing sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No existing oversight mechanism is removed or limited; the order only creates a new advisory body operating alongside existing agency programs.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no regulated conduct or compliance scheme for a third party to police; the Commission's role is advisory and promotional only.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: This is a narrow, time-limited observance commission with no systemic or precedential implications beyond its one-year mandate.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Sec. 4 creates a coordinating interdepartmental task force that works alongside existing agencies' own programs without redirecting or countermanding any agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: There is no regulatory coverage population or exemption scheme in this advisory commission order.

note: A narrow, time-limited advisory commission; most compliance-regime and systemic-effect flags are genuinely not applicable since no ongoing regulatory relationship or lasting institutional change is created.

title: Committee on Selection of Federal Judicial Officers

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Committee's inquiries and criteria are entirely dependent on the President and Attorney General, who set the procedures and qualification criteria (Sec. 3(b)) and can act with no external check, though the Committee's advisory-only role tempers this somewhat.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Committee reports directly to the President and Attorney General with named responsibility (Sec. 3(c)) and Sec. 4 bars members from being nominated to positions they vetted, providing clear internal accountability.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order addresses a single coherent purpose, an advisory judicial-selection committee, with no unrelated matters bundled in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: This is an advisory nomination-assistance body with no compliance obligation imposed on any regulated party, so there is no enforcement relationship to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing regulatory or compliance relationship exists; the Committee only investigates and reports candidate qualifications when asked.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Sec. 8 provides the Committee 'shall terminate on December 31, 1978, unless sooner extended,' a defined sunset date.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Sec. 7 states that FACA's annual reporting-to-Congress requirement does not apply to this Committee, shifting the remaining compliance oversight to the Attorney General under OMB guidelines rather than the standard FACA reporting channel, reducing a pre-existing statutory transparency mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: There is no regulated party whose compliance a third party would need to monitor; the Committee's work is internal candidate vetting.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Creating a formalized, criteria-driven pre-vetting body for non-Article-III judicial appointments establishes an institutional precedent for centralized executive screening of appointments that could be extended to other appointment categories.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Committee works through and with the Attorney General's existing role in judicial nominations rather than displacing any other agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: There is no regulated population with exemptions at issue; this is an advisory vetting committee, not a compliance regime with coverage and carve-outs.

note: Sec. 7's carve-out of FACA's congressional reporting requirement is a removal of a pre-existing statutory check, distinguishing this from a purely clean advisory-committee order.

title: Setting Aside for the Use of the United States Certain Public Lands and Other Public Property Located at the Kapalama Military Reservation, Hawaii

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: This is a self-executing land-status action; no ongoing discretionary authority is vested in any official or body.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: There is no ongoing implementation function that could fail; the order is a one-time property designation with nothing further for anyone to administer.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order sets aside a single described parcel for a single purpose, with no unrelated provisions bundled together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time land-status transfer creates no ongoing behavioral rule for anyone to comply with, so there is no enforcement question to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing regulatory or compliance relationship is created by a one-time property set-aside.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a one-time, self-executing land designation with no ongoing grant of authority to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order sets aside land 'subject to valid existing rights' and does not remove any judicial, legislative, or administrative check that previously applied.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: A one-time land-status transfer creates no ongoing compliance regime for a third party to monitor.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: This is a narrow administrative land action with no systemic or precedential implications beyond the specific parcel described.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency's existing statutory mandate is implicated by this one-time set-aside of land for general United States use.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: There is no regulatory coverage population or exemption scheme; this is a one-time property designation.

note: A narrow, self-executing land set-aside order; nearly all flags are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE because no ongoing authority, compliance regime, or discretionary power is created.

title: Amendment of Subdivision IX, Schedule B, Civil Service Rules

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The amendment adds a promotion pathway subject to Civil Service Commission approval, and does not concentrate new discretionary authority in any single official; the Commission's approval role is an existing check, not new pooled power.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Civil Service Commission remains the named body responsible for approving promotions under the amended rule, preserving clear accountability for administration of this narrow personnel rule.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order makes a single narrow amendment to one civil-service rule provision with nothing else bundled in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The amended rule creates an ongoing eligibility relationship (promotion of unclassified laborers to classified positions) and the standard is clear: promotion requires 'non-competitive examination' and 'the approval of the Civil Service Commission,' a defined trigger and approving actor.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The rule ties promotion eligibility to Commission-approved non-competitive examination and explicitly withholds full classified status/transfer rights, closing an obvious loophole rather than creating an incentive to game the classification system.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a standing procedural civil-service rule amendment maintained through ordinary rule revision (it is itself an amendment to an existing numbered rule schedule), the standing-procedural-framework evergreen category, so the permanence carve-out applies.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The amendment continues to require Civil Service Commission approval for these promotions and does not remove any existing review mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement of the promotion criteria depends on internal Civil Service Commission approval with no external party (such as affected competing applicants) given standing or a defined mechanism to challenge an improper promotion.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: This is a narrow, single-agency personnel rule amendment with no systemic or precedential implications beyond the specific postal custodial and equipment positions named.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order notes the change is made 'in view of the agreement by the Fourth Assistant Postmaster General,' indicating coordinated agreement between the Civil Service Commission and the Post Office rather than a conflict with any agency's mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The rule narrowly and specifically covers only 'Classified positions in the Custodial Service, and in the Division of Equipment and Supplies under the Fourth Assistant Postmaster General,' with no broad or discretionary carve-out language.

note: A narrow but ongoing civil-service eligibility rule; most flags are answerable and clean, with only a minor Third-Party Incentive Gap given the lack of any external check on Commission approval decisions.

title: Withdrawal of Public Land for Classification

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: This is a self-executing land withdrawal; no ongoing discretionary authority is vested in any official or body.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: There is no ongoing implementation function that could fail; the withdrawal is self-executing pending future legislation.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order addresses a single coherent action, temporary withdrawal of described Alaska lands for classification, with a clean carve-out of a specific excluded tract, not unrelated bundled matters.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A land withdrawal from settlement/entry creates no ongoing behavioral compliance rule for a regulated party to be enforced against, so there is no enforcement relationship to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing regulatory or compliance relationship is created by this land withdrawal; there is no statutory lapse referenced, so the Zombie Emergency Trap sub-flag is not implicated.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The withdrawal is expressly temporary and 'in aid of legislation,' a category that should naturally resolve once Congress acts, yet the order provides no review mechanism or expiration date and 'shall continue in force until revoked by the President or by act of Congress,' leaving indefinite continuation as the default.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The withdrawal is explicitly made 'subject to all valid existing rights and to all existing withdrawals and reservations,' preserving rather than displacing prior legal interests and oversight.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: A land withdrawal from entry creates no ongoing compliance regime for a third party to monitor.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: This is a narrow administrative land-classification withdrawal with no systemic or precedential implications beyond the specific Alaska islands described.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order carefully excludes from its withdrawal the area of the prior Naval Reserve established by EO 8278, explicitly preserving that separate agency's existing withdrawal rather than conflicting with it.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: There is no regulatory coverage population or exemption scheme; the excluded acreage is a specific surveyed carve-out, not a discretionary exemption mechanism.

note: The order references EO 8278 only to exclude a previously-withdrawn naval tract from its own boundary, not to amend, revoke, or supersede it, so the diff-aware amendment convention does not apply; the predecessor text confirms this is a boundary-drawing cross-reference only.

title: Directing the Director of the Office of Defense Transportation To Take Control of the Properties of the American Railroad Company of Porto Rico

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Director of the Office of Defense Transportation is given sole discretion to seize, operate, manage employment terms for, and make financial advances against the railroad's assets (Secs. 1, 2, 4, 5), with no external check besides a Presidential determination of when to end possession (Sec. 6).

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Director's broad operational discretion (e.g., 'in such manner as he deems necessary,' 'in his discretion') has no defined standard against which his performance could be judged a failure, and the only correction mechanism is the President's own unreviewable determination to end possession under Sec. 6.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: All provisions relate to the single coherent purpose of seizing and operating the railroad during the labor dispute; nothing unrelated is bundled in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Sec. 4 bars attachment or garnishment against the seized property 'except with the prior written consent' of the Director, and Sec. 5 lets him return property he 'deems unnecessary,' both standards left to his unstructured discretion with no defined criteria.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Sec. 4 allows the Director to make advances from the railroad's own earnings that 'shall not be repayable before the time of final determination' of the railroad's compensation claim, giving the government-appointed operator financial latitude with delayed accountability; this is not a Zombie Emergency Trap since no statutory lapse is invoked, only an active wartime justification, but the parent flag fires on its own merits.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Sec. 6 ties termination of possession to the President's determination 'that such possession and operation are no longer required for the successful prosecution of the war,' a defined resolution condition tracking the wartime emergency's end rather than open-ended permanence.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Sec. 4 explicitly bars ordinary judicial remedies against the seized property ('no attachment by mesne or garnishee process or on execution or otherwise shall be levied'), removing a pre-existing legal recourse available to creditors and claimants absent the Director's consent.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Oversight of the Director's operation of the railroad rests entirely with the Director himself and the President, with the railroad's compensation claim deferred until final adjudication and no independent auditor positioned to surface mismanagement during the seizure.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order establishes a precedent for using wartime transportation authority to seize a private company's assets in response to a labor dispute rather than a physical wartime emergency.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Sec. 3 assigns the National War Labor Board to determine the underlying labor dispute while Sec. 8 has the Director consult the Secretary of the Interior and Puerto Rico's government, creating parallel authority streams without a clear hierarchy resolving conflicts between them.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: This is a one-time seizure of a specific named company's property, not a compliance regime with a covered population and exemptions.

note: A wartime seizure order concentrating broad, largely unreviewable operational and financial discretion in a single official with limited judicial recourse for affected creditors; no predecessor EO is cited by number so no diff-aware comparison applies.

title: Amendment of Executive Order No. 10590, Establishing the President's Committee on Government Employment Policy

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The amendment only expands Committee membership from five to seven (adding a Department of Defense representative and a third public member) while preserving the same multi-member, quorum-based structure as EO 10590; no authority is newly concentrated by this change.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The amended Sec. 1 retains the same Presidential appointment and at-pleasure service structure as the predecessor, with accountability unchanged from EO 10590's baseline.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order amends a single provision (Committee composition) with only one thing to change, so there is nothing to bundle against.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Per the diff-aware partial-amendment convention, this amendment touches only membership composition; the enforcement mechanics of the underlying nondiscrimination policy (Secs. 2-7 of EO 10590) are untouched and remain as clearly defined as under the predecessor.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Adding a Department of Defense representative to the Committee does not alter the underlying incentive structure of the nondiscrimination complaint process established by EO 10590.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The Committee's subject matter, ensuring nondiscriminatory Federal employment policy, is an evergreen rights-mandate with no natural end-state, and this amendment does not change that underlying evergreen character.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Comparing to EO 10590's baseline, the amendment adds a member rather than removing any oversight mechanism; the advisory-opinion and complaint-referral processes of the predecessor (Secs. 2(d), 3(a)) are untouched by this text.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: This is a partial amendment only to Committee membership; EO 10590's complainant-initiated referral and appeal process (Sec. 3(a)), which gives affected employees standing to trigger review, continues to operate unchanged under its own terms.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: This is a narrow, single-provision membership amendment with no systemic or precedential implications beyond adjusting the Committee's composition.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Adding a Department of Defense representative alongside the existing Civil Service Commission, Labor, and Office of Defense Mobilization representatives integrates another agency into the same advisory body without redirecting any agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: This membership-only amendment does not touch EO 10590's coverage or exemption structure, which remains defined by the unamended sections of the predecessor.

note: A partial amendment touching only Committee membership in EO 10590; per the partial-amendment convention, only this specific provision was assessed against its baseline, and the change is a clean expansion of representation with no weakening of the underlying framework.

title: Providing for the Most Effective Mobilization and Utilization of the National Manpower and Transferring the Selective Service System to the War Manpower Commission

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: This order transfers the Selective Service System and Director's functions to the Chairman of the War Manpower Commission (Sec. 2(a)-(b)) and gives the Chairman sweeping powers to designate essential workers, control hiring channels, and issue binding rules (Secs. 5, 7), a substantial escalation over predecessor EO 9139's more distributed, consultative structure where authority was exercised 'after consultation with the members of the Commission.'

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Chairman both designs the essential-worker labor-control system and adjudicates appeals about its application (Sec. 7(c)), with only a discretionary Presidential appeal as an external check (Sec. 9).

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order bundles transferring Selective Service to WMC, imposing new draft age/induction limits (Sec. 4), and creating a compulsory labor-channeling/hiring-freeze regime (Sec. 5) under a single 'manpower mobilization' framing, preventing separate consideration of these distinct policies.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Sec. 5's hiring-freeze provision applies to any 'establishment, plant, facility, occupation, or area designated by the Chairman' with no defined criteria for that designation, and the 'gross inequities or undue hardships' standard in Sec. 7(b) is left to the Chairman's unguided judgment.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The Chairman's authority over designating 'essential' establishments and workers (Sec. 5) creates an incentive for employers and workers to seek favorable designations rather than compete on actual wartime need; this is not a Zombie Emergency Trap since it relies on active wartime statutes, not an acknowledged lapse, but the parent flag fires independently.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Sec. 13 ties the order's duration to 'the termination of Title I of the First War Powers Act, 1941,' but the order itself provides no interim review point, depending entirely on a separate statute's own termination rather than any built-in reauthorization mechanism.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Sec. 2(b) transfers the Director of Selective Service's functions to the Chairman, and Sec. 9 subjects departments to Presidential appeal 'or to such agent or agency as the President may designate' rather than independent review, narrowing the individualized review channels that existed under EO 9139's more consultative Commission structure.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Compliance with hiring-channel and essential-worker rules is enforced through the Chairman's own procedures (Sec. 7(c)) with no external party given standing to challenge designations.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Sec. 5's authority to freeze workers in place and funnel all hiring through government-approved channels establishes a labor-market command precedent extending well beyond military manpower into civilian employment relations generally, far beyond what EO 9139 authorized.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Sec. 2(a)-(c) subordinates the Selective Service System and its Director to WMC Chairman supervision, and Sec. 3 requires the Secretaries of War and Navy to consult the Chairman on induction numbers, redirecting authority they previously held more independently.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Sec. 4 exempts anyone under 18 or over 38 or with a prior bona fide voluntary enlistment application from the induction rule, and the Chairman has broad discretion under Sec. 7(b) to grant relief from 'gross inequities or undue hardships,' with only loosely defined criteria.

note: This order substantially escalates concentrated, less consultative authority relative to its named predecessor EO 9139; the diff-aware comparison shows real strengthening of centralized control rather than a cosmetic change.

title: Regulations Relating to the Safeguarding of Vessels, Harbors, Ports, and Waterfront Facilities of the United States

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Commandant of the Coast Guard is given essentially unilateral, unreviewable authority to deny employment documents, revoke security cards, and designate exempt vessel categories (Sec. 6.10-1, 6.10-7) based solely on his own subjective satisfaction as to an individual's 'character and habits of life,' with no external judicial check on the underlying denial standard.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: While an appeals board exists (Sec. 6.10-9), it only 'recommends' final action back to the Commandant, who retains ultimate decision authority over the very denials being appealed.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: All subparts relate coherently to the single stated purpose of waterfront/vessel security, with no unrelated matters bundled together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The core denial standard, that a person's 'character and habits of life... would be inimical to the security of the United States' (Sec. 6.10-1, 6.10-7), provides no defined criteria, allowing two officials to reach substantially different conclusions about the same individual in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Because the 'inimical to security' standard is undefined, the Commandant faces no incentive against over-broad denial since erring toward exclusion carries no cost while an erroneous approval could be blamed on him; this is not a Zombie Emergency Trap since it derives from an active statute (Public Law 679) rather than an acknowledged lapse.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The regulations rest on a finding 'that the security of the United States is endangered by reason of subversive activity,' an emergency-type finding that should carry natural reauthorization pressure tied to resolution of that threat, yet no review or expiration mechanism is provided.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Sec. 6.10-1's proviso lets the Commandant unilaterally 'designate categories of merchant vessels' exempted from screening with no outside check, and the appeals process in Sec. 6.10-9 returns final authority to the same Commandant who made the original denial rather than an independent adjudicator.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Sec. 6.16-1 requires that evidence of sabotage or subversive activity be reported to the FBI as well as the captain of the port, giving an independent federal law enforcement body standing and access outside the Coast Guard's own chain.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The 'character and habits of life' security-screening framework for waterfront employment establishes a precedent for excluding individuals from an entire industry based on an undefined loyalty/character standard rather than specific conduct, with implications for due process and employment rights extending beyond immediate sabotage-prevention purposes.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Sec. 6.10-7 requires the Commandant to consult the Secretary of Labor on port security card procedures, and Sec. 6.10-9 requires labor and management nominees for appeal boards, coordinating rather than conflicting with Department of Labor interests.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The Commandant holds unbounded, undefined discretion under Sec. 6.10-1's proviso to 'designate categories of merchant vessels' exempt from the entire screening regime, with no criteria specified, meaning coverage of the underlying security rule is entirely subject to one official's unreviewable discretion.

note: No predecessor EO is cited by number in this order (it implements a statute, Public Law 679, not a prior executive order), so no diff-aware predecessor comparison applies; the core structural weakness is the undefined 'character and habits of life' standard controlling both denial and exemption determinations.

title: Abolishing Customs Collection District Number 44 (Iowa); Extending Limits of Customs Collection District Number 39 (Chicago) To Include the State of Iowa; and Revoking the Designations of Des Moines, Iowa, Oklahoma City, Oklahoma, and Tulsa, Oklahoma, as Customs Ports of Entry

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order is a self-executing reorganization of customs field boundaries under a specific statute; it vests no discretionary authority in any official, so no authority is concentrated.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: A one-time boundary consolidation with no ongoing implementation to fail; responsibility for customs administration remains with the existing statutory structure.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: All four provisions concern a single coherent customs-district reorganization; nothing distinct is packaged to force an accept/reject on unrelated matter.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order is a one-time district/port redesignation creating no ongoing compliance rule for anyone, so there is no enforcement mechanism to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing regulatory relationship or behavioral rule is created, so there is no incentive structure to evaluate.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a self-executing structural change with no ongoing grant of authority that could be subject to reauthorization pressure.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is excluded, limited, or bypassed; the order simply redraws administrative boundaries.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The order creates no enforcement chain or compliance obligation, so there is no violation for any third party to surface.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow administrative boundary change with no systemic or precedential implications beyond customs geography.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only the customs organization within one department is implicated; no second agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order contains no coverage population or exemption structure to assess.

note: Narrow self-executing customs reorganization; compliance-regime and systemic flags are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE per the Application Notes test, while decision-structure flags 1/2/7 are answerable and score ABSENT.

title: Further Providing for the Administration of the Defense Production Act of 1950, as Amended

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order redirects all references from the Defense Production Administrator to the Director of Defense Mobilization and continues subordinate bodies 'under the jurisdiction of the Director... unless and until other disposition thereof is made by him,' pooling continuing discretionary control in a single official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Authority is clearly assigned to a named officer (Director of Defense Mobilization) accountable to the President; no diffuse or circular responsibility is created.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The provisions all effect a single reorganization consolidating defense-mobilization administration; no unrelated matters are packaged together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order re-designates administrative authority rather than setting a conduct standard; the underlying DPA enforcement mechanics are unchanged and defined elsewhere.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A reorganization of who exercises delegated authority; no actor-class incentive to undermine the stated purpose is created by the text.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order continues mobilization authority (rooted in DPA emergency powers) indefinitely with no reauthorization or expiration mechanism, and the subject matter is emergency/mobilization authority that has a natural end-state warranting periodic reauthorization pressure.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order revokes and re-routes internal delegations but does not remove any external oversight mechanism (judicial review, reporting, IG access) applicable to the subject matter.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: This is an intra-executive reallocation of delegated authority, not a compliance regime over regulated parties for which third-party enforcement standing is relevant.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The consolidation of mobilization authority under one Director is a foreseeable, contained administrative effect with no identifiable precedent usable in unrelated contexts.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Records, property, personnel, and functions of the Defense Production Administration are transferred to the Office of Defense Mobilization, redirecting one agency's operations into another and merging the Economic Stabilization Agency headship ex officio into the Director.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order defines no coverage population subject to exemptions; it reorganizes administrative authority only.

note: Diff-aware: this partially amends EO 10200 (available in predecessors) by revoking its Section 1 while continuing subordinate bodies under the Director's open-ended discretion, and re-heads the Economic Stabilization Agency; scored on the reorganization it actually effects.

title: Designating Public International Organizations Entitled to Enjoy Certain Privileges, Exemptions, and Immunities

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A one-time presidential designation of three named organizations under a specific statutory finding; no ongoing discretionary authority is pooled in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The designation is self-executing under the International Organizations Immunities Act; there is no implementation chain that could fail without a responsible actor.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order does a single thing—designate named organizations for statutory immunities—without packaging distinct policy questions.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order confers status under a statute rather than establishing an ongoing conduct rule to enforce against any party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A fixed status designation with no ongoing behavioral rule that any actor could game.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: A one-time designation with no ongoing grant of discretionary authority that could be subject to reauthorization pressure.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed; the order explicitly preserves immunities acquired by treaty or Congressional action.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance regime or violations exist that a third party would have standing to surface.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow status designation of three organizations with no systemic or precedential implications beyond the statute's own framework.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No agency's statutory mandate is redirected; the order simply supplements prior immunity-designation orders.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The immunities conferred are the statute's own; the order creates no discretionary carve-out structure that could swallow a rule.

note: Supplements EOs 9698/9751/9823 (adds three organizations); a full-incorporation extension but the parent framework is a clean statutory immunity designation, so extended findings are also clean. EO 9698 is in predecessors and confirms this pattern.

title: Assigning Emergency Preparedness Functions to Federal Departments and Agencies

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Director of the Office of Emergency Preparedness is given continuing guidance, evaluation, coordination, and function-transfer authority (Secs. 103, 3013) over all agencies' preparedness planning, concentrating cross-government planning direction in one office, though Sec. 105/3011 withhold implementation power.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Responsibility is diffused across 28 departments/agencies each 'severally charged,' with the OEP Director evaluating readiness he also coordinates; if planning fails, no single actor bears clear correction responsibility.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order consolidates 21 Executive orders and 2 Defense Mobilization orders into one instrument covering dozens of distinct agency domains, forcing a single package that forecloses targeted revision of any one assignment.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Agencies are told to 'develop such plans and take such actions... as may be necessary' and attain 'an appropriate state of readiness' with no defined standard or metric, so two officials could reach substantially different readiness judgments in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Because Secs. 105 and 3011 explicitly confer no implementation authority and require separate statutory/presidential authorization to act, the planning mandate creates no strong incentive for any actor to game the framework.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This emergency-preparedness authority is exactly the crisis-response subject matter with a natural end-state, yet it continues indefinitely with no reauthorization or expiration mechanism, persisting until revoked.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Sec. 3014 retains all existing statutory and Executive-order assignments and Sec. 105 preserves Congress's role in authorizing any actual implementation; no pre-existing external check is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The order governs internal government planning coordination, and readiness is subject to OEP evaluation across agencies; no reliance on regulated-party self-reporting arises, so the gap does not fire.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order institutionalizes a permanent, government-wide 'continuity of government'/mobilization planning apparatus (succession, predelegation, alternate headquarters) whose framework can be activated far beyond any specific emergency, establishing durable standby authority claims.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order assigns overlapping resource, transportation, and manpower planning across many agencies and lets the OEP Director transfer functions between departments by mutual consent (Sec. 3013), creating parallel authority structures with friction potential, though Sec. 3014 tries to preserve existing mandates.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The planning duties apply uniformly to all covered agencies with no discretionary carve-out structure exempting actors from coverage.

note: Very large consolidation of 21 EOs + 2 DM orders (revoked list in Sec. 3015; several revoked EOs are in predecessors). Its explicit no-implementation-authority carve-out (Secs. 105, 3011) materially clears Flags 5 and 7 that a superficial reading might have fired.

title: Protection and Enhancement of Environmental Quality

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Responsibilities are distributed across all federal agency heads and the Council on Environmental Quality in an advisory/coordinating role; no single official holds unchecked authority over eligibility or enforcement.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Agency heads must 'monitor, evaluate, and control on a continuing basis' their own activities and assess their own progress, so the implementing agencies also judge whether they have failed, creating a self-assessment accountability gap.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order coherently implements NEPA Title I across agencies; its provisions all serve the single environmental-quality purpose without packaging unrelated policy questions.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Directives to take 'measures needed,' ensure 'the fullest practicable' public information, and act 'where appropriate' set no defined enforcement trigger or standard, so agencies could apply them very differently in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Because each agency both develops and self-assesses its environmental programs, an agency preferring its program objectives has a built-in incentive to grade its own compliance leniently, undermining the stated protection purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Environmental protection is an evergreen policy mandate with no natural end-state, and it implements a standing statute (NEPA); permanence here is reasonable, so the flag does not fire per the carve-out.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds oversight—CEQ evaluation, public hearings, guidelines—rather than removing any pre-existing external check; per the direction rule, new imperfect machinery does not fire this flag.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement of agencies' environmental self-monitoring rests largely on the agencies and CEQ; while public-information procedures are required, no external actor is given standing plus access with incentive to surface an agency's non-compliance.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: By making CEQ the issuer of NEPA Section 102(2)(C) impact-statement guidelines government-wide, the order establishes durable institutional machinery whose guideline authority shapes agency action far beyond any single program.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: CEQ coordinates and advises rather than countermanding agencies' statutory mandates; Sec. 4's amendments to EO 11472 reassign an advisory committee's naming/structure, not operational statutory authority.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order applies to all federal agencies with no discretionary exemption structure that would carve major actors out of coverage.

note: Sec. 4 partially amends EO 11472 (in predecessors) by renaming its council/committee and adjusting membership; that is a partial amendment assessed on its own terms and does not itself add structural weight. Core scoring reflects the self-monitoring architecture.

title: Amending Executive Order 11157 as It Relates to a Basic Allowance for Quarters While on Sea Duty

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order amends a definition ('sea duty') in a pay-allowance regulation; it vests no discretionary authority in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: A self-executing definitional amendment to an existing allowance framework; responsibility for pay administration remains with the existing structure.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order makes a single narrow amendment (defining a term and striking a subsection); nothing distinct is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The amendment actually sharpens the eligibility rule with a bright-line ('Duty for less than three months is not considered to be sea duty'), reducing rather than creating ambiguity in the ongoing allowance determination.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The tightened three-month threshold narrows gaming of the allowance definition; no incentive to undermine the stated purpose is created.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a standing procedural pay framework maintained by ordinary supersession/amendment (it amends EO 11157 as amended), an evergreen administrative function, so absence of sunset is not a design gap.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed by refining an allowance definition.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The ongoing allowance eligibility determination is administered by the military pay system; the narrow definitional change does not create a self-reporting enforcement gap.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow pay-definition amendment with no systemic or precedential implications.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only military pay administration within one department is implicated; no second agency's mandate is affected.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The amendment refines who counts as on 'sea duty' with specific criteria rather than creating a discretionary exemption that swallows the rule.

note: Partial amendment of EO 11157 (in predecessors) touching only the 'sea duty' definition and striking subsection (f); assessed on that provision, the change tightens rather than loosens the ongoing eligibility rule, scoring clean.

title: Establishing the Office of Defense Health and Welfare Services in the Executive Office of the President and Defining its Functions and Duties

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A single Director (the Federal Security Administrator) heads the Office, may organize it and appoint advisory committees, and acts 'subject to such policies... as the President may... prescribe,' concentrating coordinating authority in one official checked only by the President.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Director is a named officer reporting directly to and supervised by the President, so responsibility for the coordinating function is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order establishes one coordinating office for defense health/welfare services; its provisions are coherent to that single purpose.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Office coordinates, studies, and advises rather than enforcing conduct rules; there is no enforcement standard whose ambiguity could produce inconsistent application.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A coordinating/advisory body reliant on cooperation with existing agencies; the text creates no incentive for any actor to undermine its purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The Office is tied to 'the national emergency as declared by the President on May 27, 1941'—crisis-response subject matter with a natural end-state—yet no expiration or reauthorization mechanism is provided, so it persists until revoked.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order creates a new coordinating office and explicitly directs collaboration with existing agencies; it removes no pre-existing external check.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The Office coordinates government and public/private services rather than running a compliance regime over regulated parties, so third-party enforcement standing is not the relevant question.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order sets up a wartime coordinating office collaborating through existing agencies; no precedent-setting authority claim usable in unrelated contexts is identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Office is expressly directed to collaborate with and utilize existing departments' facilities rather than redirect or countermand their statutory mandates.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: No coverage population subject to exemptions is defined; this is an internal coordinating-office establishment.

note: Wartime coordinating-office establishment; the emergency framing without any sunset drives Flag 6, and single-Director design drives a modest Flag 1, but the office's advisory/collaborative posture keeps most flags clean.

title: Exemption of Robert W. Shumate from Compulsory Retirement for Age

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The President exercises a specific statutory exemption for one named individual; the authority is bounded by the statute and exhausted by the single act, concentrating nothing ongoing.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: A one-time individual exemption with a fixed end date (January 1, 1938); there is no ongoing implementation that could fail without a responsible actor.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order does a single thing—exempt one named employee from mandatory retirement—with nothing else packaged in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time personnel exemption creates no ongoing conduct rule to enforce against anyone.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral rule or incentive structure is created by a single individual retirement exemption.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The exemption itself carries a built-in expiration date ('until January 1, 1938'), so it is time-limited rather than open-ended.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order applies a statutory exemption the President is authorized to grant; no pre-existing oversight mechanism is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance regime exists over which a third party could have enforcement standing.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A single individualized personnel action with no systemic or precedential implications.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Only one employee's status within one agency is affected; no second agency's mandate is implicated.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order is itself a discretionary exemption granted on the sole standard that 'the public interest so requires,' an essentially unbounded criterion concentrated in one actor—though here applied to a single named person, the exemption mechanism it exercises is definitionally open-ended.

note: Narrow individualized retirement exemption; compliance-regime and systemic flags are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE, and Flag 11 fires only modestly to reflect the unbounded 'public interest' standard, though its application is a single person.

title: Amending Executive Order No. 10422, as Amended, Prescribing Procedures for Making Available to the Secretary General of the United Nations Certain Information Concerning United States Citizens Employed or Being Considered for Employment on the Secretariat of the United Nations

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Investigative authority is distributed between the Secretary of State and the Civil Service Commission with defined roles; no single official gains unchecked discretionary control.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The amended procedures assign specific investigative steps to named actors (State, CSC), so responsibility for each stage is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The amendments coherently revise UN-employee investigation procedures and the derogatory-information standard; no unrelated matters are packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The amendment specifies concrete investigative triggers (National Agency Check contents, break-in-service thresholds) and tightens the membership standard to require 'specific intent,' increasing definitional precision in the ongoing screening process.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The revised screening procedures create no incentive for any actor to undermine the security-investigation purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a standing procedural framework for UN-employment security investigations, maintained through ordinary amendment (it amends EO 10422 as amended), so permanence is appropriate and the flag does not fire.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The amendment adds Privacy Act consistency requirements ('consistent with the Privacy Act of 1974... and other applicable law'), enhancing rather than removing external legal checks.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The screening relies on agency file checks (FBI, CSC, military intelligence) with defined investigative actors rather than on regulated-party self-reporting, so no third-party gap fires.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The amendments refine an existing investigative procedure; no new precedential authority claim usable in unrelated contexts is identifiable.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: State and the Civil Service Commission operate under defined, non-conflicting roles; no agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The break-in-service provision waiving a new National Agency Check is narrowly bounded by specific conditions (prior favorable check, defined employment continuity), not a discretionary carve-out that swallows the coverage.

note: Partial amendment of EO 10422 (in predecessors) revising investigation procedures and the derogatory-information standard; the changes add Privacy Act consistency and tighten the membership standard, so the amended provisions score cleanly.

title: Delegation of Certain Functions of the President to the Secretary of Defense

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A broad set of statutory presidential functions over naval/Marine personnel matters is delegated to the Secretary of Defense (and his designees) to perform 'without the approval, ratification, or other action of the President,' pooling numerous discretionary authorities in one official with the President's check affirmatively removed.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The functions are delegated to a named officer (Secretary of Defense) who remains accountable to the President; responsibility for each delegated action is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Although many statutory authorities are listed, they form a coherent single category (delegation of the President's naval/military personnel functions), not distinct policy questions forced together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Each delegated authority is tied to a specific enumerated statute defining its scope; the delegation itself creates no ambiguous conduct standard.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A routine reallocation of statutory decision authority within Defense; no incentive to undermine any stated purpose is created.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a standing administrative delegation framework tied to permanent statutory functions and revised by ordinary supersession; the underlying functions are evergreen, so absence of sunset is not a design gap.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The delegation expressly removes the step of presidential 'approval, ratification, or other action,' eliminating a pre-existing check that formerly required the President himself to act on these matters.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: This is an internal reallocation of presidential functions, not a compliance regime over regulated parties for which third-party enforcement standing is relevant.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The delegation of enumerated statutory functions to Defense is contained; no precedent usable in unrelated contexts beyond routine delegation practice is identifiable.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Authority flows from the President to the Secretary of Defense over Defense's own matters; no other agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order defines no coverage population with an exemption structure; it delegates enumerated functions.

note: Routine Section 301 delegation; Flags 1 and 7 fire modestly on the express removal of the presidential-action step, but the delegation is bounded by enumerated statutes and remains accountable within Defense.

title: Establishing the Office of Production Management in the Executive Office of the President and Defining Its Functions and Duties

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order creates an Office of Production Management with sweeping authority to 'formulate and execute... all measures needful' to direct national defense production, including binding priority determinations, concentrated in a Director General/Associate Director General acting under the President.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Named officers (Director General, Associate Director General, plus War/Navy Secretaries) head the Office under direct presidential supervision, so responsibility for the production program is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order coherently establishes one production-management office; its divisions and functions all serve the single defense-production purpose.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Office is empowered to accord priorities and issue mandatory orders and 'take all lawful action necessary,' but the standards triggering such binding directives are broad and undefined, so two officials could apply them very differently.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The production-mobilization design creates no evident incentive for any actor class to undermine the stated defense-production purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The Office is tied to 'the national emergency as declared by the President to exist on September 8, 1939'—crisis-response authority with a natural end-state—yet no expiration or reauthorization mechanism is provided.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order establishes a new coordinating office and directs maximum use of existing War/Navy departments; it removes no pre-existing external oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The Office directly administers priorities and allocations across industry through mandatory orders rather than relying on regulated-party self-reporting, so the third-party gap does not fire.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Establishing a central body with binding authority over industrial priorities and production allocation creates durable institutional precedent for peacetime-into-wartime economic direction extending well beyond any single procurement need.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Office assumes coordination and priority authority over defense purchasing that overlaps the War/Navy Departments and the Advisory Commission to the Council of National Defense, and it serves as the channel between them, creating parallel authority with friction potential.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Its priority/allocation authority applies broadly to defense production with no discretionary exemption structure carving actors out of coverage.

note: Revokes EO 8572 as amended by EO 8612 (both in predecessors), which established the predecessor advisory arrangement; this order replaces advisory coordination with an executing office holding binding priority authority, which the scoring reflects.

title: Prescribing Additional Regulations Governing Persons and Vessels in Defensive Sea Areas

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order prescribes fixed statutory regulations (a camera/photography ban and custody duties) under Criminal Code section 44; it vests no discretionary authority in any single official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement runs through ordinary criminal prosecution under section 44, a defined and externally checked mechanism, so responsibility is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The regulations coherently address a single subject—photographic devices in defensive sea areas—without packaging unrelated matter.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The rule is specific: possession of cameras/film in a defensive sea area is prohibited, masters must take custody, and violators 'shall be liable to prosecution as provided in section 44,' with a clear trigger and consequence.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The prohibition creates no incentive structure that would reward undermining its national-defense purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: These wartime restrictions on persons in defensive sea areas are emergency/exceptional-powers regulation with a natural end tied to the war, yet the order provides no expiration or reauthorization mechanism.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Enforcement is by ordinary criminal prosecution; no pre-existing external oversight (including judicial review of prosecutions) is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Vessel masters are affirmatively obligated to take custody of prohibited devices and violations are criminally prosecutable, so enforcement does not rest solely on regulated-party self-reporting.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow wartime photography restriction with no identifiable precedent usable in unrelated contexts beyond defensive-sea-area regulation.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order relies on existing criminal-enforcement channels and vessel masters; no other agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Exemptions are narrow and specific (official duty in national defense, or authorization under Public Law 627 and its regulations), not broad or discretionary enough to swallow the prohibition.

note: Clean wartime criminal-regulation order; only Flag 6 fires because the emergency-linked restriction lacks any end-tied-to-the-war mechanism. Enforcement is defined and externally checked via ordinary prosecution.

title: Designation of the Secretary of Transportation to Approve and Certificate Containers and Vehicles For Use in International Transport

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The Secretary of Transportation is designated to take 'all actions, including the issuance of regulations, which he considers appropriate' to approve and certificate containers/vehicles under two conventions, with authority to set fees and further delegate—broad discretionary authority pooled in one official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The designated actor is a named officer (Secretary of Transportation) accountable to the President; responsibility for certification is clearly located even though the standard is broad.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order coherently designates one official to implement two closely related international transport conventions; no unrelated matters are bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The certification standard is anchored to 'the procedures and technical conditions set forth in the Annexes' to the named Conventions, supplying an external, defined technical standard for approval decisions.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The container-certification scheme creates no evident incentive for any actor to undermine its purpose of enabling international transport under customs seal.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a standing administrative certification framework implementing ongoing treaty obligations—an evergreen administrative function—so the absence of a sunset is not a design gap.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds a certification function; it removes no pre-existing external oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Certification is an affirmative government-issued approval tied to Convention technical conditions, not a self-reporting compliance regime lacking any external checker.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Implementing container certification under existing treaties is contained; no precedent usable in unrelated contexts is identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The certification function is assigned to Transportation without redirecting or countermanding another agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
