flags.justification: The Secretary may delegate his authority and functions, and authorize further delegations, 'to nonprofit firms or associations which he shall deem competent'—a discretionary handoff of certification authority to private bodies bounded only by his own competence judgment.

note: Flag 1 fires on the broad 'as he considers appropriate' grant and Flag 11 on discretionary delegation of certification to private nonprofit firms; the Convention Annexes supply a defined technical standard that clears Flag 4.

title: Establishing the President's Committee on Manpower

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order establishes a multi-agency advisory committee chaired by the Secretary of Labor to assist and recommend; it vests no concentrated decision authority in any single official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Committee's role is advisory to the Secretary of Labor and other officials, whose underlying statutory responsibilities remain; there is no operational function whose failure would leave no responsible actor.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order coherently establishes one manpower-assessment committee to support the Manpower Development and Training Act; nothing distinct is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Committee only evaluates and recommends; it enforces no conduct rule against any party, so there is no enforcement mechanism to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A purely advisory body with no ongoing behavioral rule creates no incentive structure any actor could game.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The Committee is a standing advisory body with no expiration or reauthorization mechanism; as temporary/discretionary program machinery it should face periodic reauthorization pressure and does not, so it persists until revoked.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Sec. 4 states nothing shall abrogate, modify, or restrict any function vested by law in any agency; the order adds an advisory layer and removes no pre-existing external check.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance regime over regulated parties exists for which third-party enforcement standing would be relevant.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: An interagency advisory committee coordinating manpower projections has no identifiable precedential authority claim usable in unrelated contexts.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Committee coordinates and advises across agencies, and Sec. 4 expressly preserves each agency's statutory functions, so no mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order defines no coverage population subject to exemptions; it is an advisory-committee establishment.

note: Advisory-committee establishment; only Flag 6 fires (no sunset on a standing advisory body). The compliance-regime flags are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE because the committee only advises and recommends.

title: Abolishing Certain Committees on Government Organization and Management Improvement

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order abolishes two advisory committees and transfers only wind-up functions to the Director of the Bureau of the Budget; no ongoing discretionary authority is pooled in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The named Director is responsible for orderly termination of affairs; responsibility for the wind-up is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order coherently abolishes two related organization/management committees and revokes/amends the enabling orders; nothing unrelated is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Abolishing committees creates no ongoing conduct rule to enforce against any party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A dissolution action creates no ongoing behavioral rule or incentive structure to evaluate.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order terminates bodies rather than granting ongoing authority, so there is nothing to sunset (indeed it is itself a termination).

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order abolishes advisory committees but removes no external oversight mechanism over any subject matter; wind-up functions merely go to the Budget Director.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance regime exists for which third-party enforcement standing would be relevant.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow administrative dissolution of two advisory committees with no systemic or precedential implications.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Transferring only termination/wind-up functions to the Budget Director does not redirect or countermand any agency's operating statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order defines no coverage population subject to exemptions.

note: Diff-aware: revokes EO 10432 and amends EO 10072 (both in predecessors) by deleting the committee-creating provisions; the action is pure dissolution/wind-up, so it scores clean with compliance-regime flags NOT_APPLICABLE.

title: Establishing the Surplus War Property Administration

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A single Surplus War Property Administrator is given 'general supervision and direction of the handling and disposition of surplus war property,' may prescribe binding regulations no agency may contravene, and may exercise other agencies' disposal authority—broad discretionary control pooled in one official (subject only to the Director of War Mobilization).

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Administrator is a named officer under the general supervision of the Director of War Mobilization, so responsibility for surplus disposition is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order coherently establishes one administration for the single function of surplus war property disposition; no unrelated matters are packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Administrator may prescribe regulations and directions 'necessary to effectuate the purposes' and determine what is 'surplus' with which agencies dispose of it, with broad discretion and no defined standards bounding those determinations.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The surplus-disposition design creates no evident incentive for an actor class to undermine its purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This wartime surplus-property administration is crisis-response machinery with a natural end tied to the war, yet the order provides no expiration or reauthorization mechanism.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order creates a new administration and directs other agencies to cooperate; it removes no pre-existing external oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The Administration directly directs government agencies' disposal actions and requires reports, so enforcement does not rest on regulated-party self-reporting lacking any external checker.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The clause allowing the Administration to exercise 'all functions, powers, and duties relating to the transfer or disposition of surplus war property, heretofore conferred by law on any Government agency' establishes an authority claim to override statutory agency disposal powers usable broadly across government.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order lets the Administration exercise disposal functions statutorily conferred on other agencies and directs agencies to execute transfers as it determines, redirecting authority those agencies already hold by law.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The disposition scheme applies broadly to surplus war property with no discretionary exemption structure carving actors out of coverage.

note: Wartime administration with broad regulatory and cross-agency disposal authority; Flags 1, 4, 9, and 10 fire on the Administrator's discretion and the clause absorbing other agencies' statutory disposal powers, and Flag 6 on the missing wartime sunset.

title: Blocking Iraqi Government Property and Prohibiting Transactions with Iraq

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The President declares a national emergency and the Secretary of the Treasury is authorized to take 'such actions, including the promulgation of rules and regulations, as may be necessary,' with power to redelegate—broad discretionary control over an entire sanctions regime pooled in one official under IEEPA.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Authority is clearly assigned to the President and the Secretary of the Treasury (in consultation with State); responsibility for administering the blocking is located in named officers.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The prohibitions (imports, exports, transport, credits, travel-related transactions) all coherently serve the single stated purpose of pressuring Iraq; they are not distinct unrelated policy questions.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Core prohibitions apply 'except to the extent provided in regulations which may hereafter be issued,' leaving the actual scope of permitted/prohibited conduct to future, undefined Treasury regulations, so the operative standard is indeterminate at the order's issuance.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The anti-evasion clause (subsection h) directly targets circumvention; the sanctions design does not reward behavior undermining its purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order rests on a newly declared national emergency—exactly the crisis-response authority that should have a natural end tied to the emergency's resolution—yet the order itself contains no sunset or reauthorization trigger (relying on NEA's separate annual renewal is not stated in the text).

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order is transmitted to Congress and published in the Federal Register as IEEPA/NEA require; it removes no pre-existing external oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Compliance with the transaction prohibitions rests on U.S. persons' self-adherence and Treasury enforcement; the text creates no external actor with standing, access, and incentive to surface concealed prohibited transactions.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order exemplifies and entrenches the IEEPA blocking-and-embargo template—broad presidential authority to freeze a foreign government's assets and bar nearly all transactions—as precedent readily deployable against other countries in unrelated future disputes.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Treasury (with State) administers the sanctions and all agencies are directed to cooperate within their authority; no agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Prohibitions operate 'except to the extent provided in regulations which may hereafter be issued,' concentrating in Treasury an open-ended discretionary power to create exceptions to the sanctions with no criteria bounding future carve-outs.

note: IEEPA/NEA emergency sanctions order; broad Treasury discretion and the 'except as provided in future regulations' structure drive Flags 1, 4, and 11, with the newly declared emergency lacking a textual sunset driving Flag 6.

title: Consultations on the Admission of Refugees

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order distributes functions among the Secretary of State, Attorney General, and the U.S. Coordinator for Refugee Affairs, and expressly reserves the key determinations (refugee numbers, special circumstances) to the President, so authority is dispersed and top-checked.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each delegated function is assigned to a named officer with defined consultation partners, so responsibility for each step of the refugee-consultation process is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling

title: Authorizing the Secretary of War to Take Possession of and Operate the Plants and Facilities of the United States Rubber Company, Located in Or Around Detroit, Michigan

flags.name: Power Concentration

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Secretary of War is the single named responsible officer for the seizure and operation, so responsibility is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order coherently addresses a single plant seizure to end a labor-disturbance production interruption; nothing unrelated is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order's operative standard—operate the plant to resume war production and terminate possession once efficiency is restored—is defined by concrete conditions rather than an ambiguous conduct rule; the seizure is under the War Labor Disputes Act's terms.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The seizure to restore production creates no incentive structure that rewards undermining the war-production purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order contains a built-in termination trigger: possession 'shall be terminated by the Secretary of War within 60 days after he determines that the productive efficiency... has been restored,' so it is not open-ended.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order operates under the War Labor Disputes Act and preserves existing employment terms and Section 5 protections; it removes no pre-existing external oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: This is a direct government takeover and operation of a single plant, not a compliance regime over regulated parties for which third-party enforcement standing is relevant.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Even directed at one plant, the order exercises and reinforces the wartime plant-seizure power under Section 9 of the Selective Training and Service Act as amended, a precedent for executive seizure of private industry usable in future labor/production disputes.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Other agencies (War Manpower Commission, Selective Service, Justice/FBI) are directed to cooperate in support roles, not to act against their statutory mandates.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order targets one facility's seizure and defines no coverage population with an exemption structure.

note: Single-plant wartime seizure; Flag 6 is cleared by the explicit 60-day termination trigger, but Flag 1 fires on the broad seizure discretion and Flag 9 on the seizure-power precedent.

title: Providing for the Stabilizing of the National Economy

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order creates an Economic Stabilization Director with power to 'formulate and develop a comprehensive national economic policy' over prices, rents, wages, salaries, profits, rationing, and subsidies and to 'issue directives on policy' binding all federal departments, with delegation decisions declared 'final'—sweeping economy-wide authority concentrated in one official, a core concentration that defines the order's design.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Director is a single named officer acting with presidential approval and advised by a defined Stabilization Board, so responsibility for the stabilization program is clearly located even though authority is broad.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order packages multiple distinct policy domains—wage/salary stabilization, agricultural price controls, profit limits, subsidies, and a $25,000 net-salary cap—under one instrument, forcing them together under the single inflation-control frame and foreclosing targeted acceptance or rejection.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Standards such as approving wage changes only to 'correct maladjustments or inequalities,' 'eliminate substandards of living,' or 'correct gross inequities,' and preventing profits the Administrator deems 'unreasonable or exorbitant,' are open-ended criteria that two officials could apply very differently in good faith.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The wage/price/profit controls are designed to suppress inflationary behavior and the order disregards non-complying payments for tax and contract purposes; the text does not reward behavior undermining stabilization.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This wartime economic-stabilization authority is emergency/crisis-response power with a natural end tied to the war and postwar transition, yet the order provides no expiration or reauthorization mechanism.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order suspends specified provisions of the Emergency Price Control Act inconsistent with it (Title II §9, Title IV §4) and declares the Director's delegation decisions 'final,' reducing statutory constraints and insulating certain choices from review.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Enforcement runs through the National War Labor Board approval process and denial of tax/contract deductions for violating payments, giving external administrative actors a role rather than relying solely on regulated-party self-reporting.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Vesting one office with binding directive authority over the entire wage-price-profit-subsidy structure of the economy establishes durable precedent for centralized executive economic control extending well beyond the immediate anti-inflation purpose.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Director may issue binding directives to and direct the use of authority of numerous agencies (Agriculture, Commerce, RFC, Price Administrator, War Labor Board), redirecting their statutory functions to conform to his policy, creating parallel authority over their mandates.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order authorizes the War Labor Board to 'make such exemptions from the provisions of this title... as it deems necessary' for small wage changes and gives the Director discretion over salary and other exceptions, a discretionary carve-out power bounded only by the administrators' own judgment.

note: Sweeping wartime economic-stabilization order; concentrates economy-wide directive authority in one Director (Flag 1 CRITICAL) with multiple bundled domains, vague criteria, statutory suspensions, discretionary exemptions, and cross-agency direction, driving a high score.

flags.justification: Enforcement is assigned to the Secretary of the Navy with local law-enforcement cooperation, so responsibility for the reservation is clearly located.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The entry prohibition creates no incentive structure rewarding undermining of the defense purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The reservation is national-defense/emergency-linked authority with a natural end-state, yet it is established with no expiration or reauthorization mechanism, persisting until revoked.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Enforcement is by defined statutory penalties (with judicial process); no pre-existing external oversight mechanism is removed.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Violations are criminally punishable and enforced by the Navy with local law enforcement, so enforcement does not rest solely on regulated-party self-reporting.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Establishing defensive sea areas/airspace around three islands under specific statutes is contained; no precedent usable in unrelated contexts is identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order relies on Navy enforcement with cooperating local law enforcement; no other agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The Secretary of the Navy holds unbounded discretion to authorize entry ('unless authorized by the Secretary of the Navy') with no criteria governing when access exceptions are granted, a discretionary carve-out concentrated in one actor.

note: Defensive-sea-area/airspace reservation; enforcement is clean and penalty-defined, but the Secretary's unbounded entry-authorization discretion fires Flags 1 and 11, and the missing emergency sunset fires Flag 6.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Designating management and scientific authorities to implement CITES is contained within the treaty/statutory framework; no precedent usable in unrelated contexts is identifiable.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order builds a permit and scientific-review architecture for wildlife trade without creating a discretionary exemption structure that would swallow coverage.

note: Clean implementation of CITES/Endangered Species Act; authority is dispersed across a Management Authority and a multi-agency Scientific Authority with defined statutory anchors and broad consultation, so all flags score ABSENT.

flags.justification: The Division is a records/reporting clearinghouse headed by an Executive Officer acting 'subject to such policies and directions as the President may... prescribe'; it exercises no substantive discretionary authority over eligibility or enforcement.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: A named Executive Officer heads the Division under direct presidential direction, so responsibility for its clearinghouse function is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order coherently establishes one division to clear and record Lend-Lease-type aid transactions; nothing unrelated is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A records/reporting clearinghouse enforces no conduct rule against any regulated party, so there is no enforcement mechanism to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A reporting/coordination body creates no ongoing behavioral rule that any actor could game.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The Division is tied to 'the national emergency as declared by the President on September 8, 1939' and administers a wartime aid statute—crisis-response machinery with a natural end—yet no expiration or reauthorization mechanism is provided.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The Division records under Bureau of the Budget-approved systems and reports to the President; it adds recordkeeping rather than removing any pre-existing external check.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The Division coordinates and records government aid transactions rather than running a compliance regime over regulated parties, so third-party enforcement standing is not the relevant question.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A reporting clearinghouse for defense-aid transactions has no identifiable precedential authority claim usable in unrelated contexts.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Division serves as a clearinghouse for participating agencies and is to use existing OEM business services; it does not redirect or countermand any agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order defines no coverage population subject to exemptions; it establishes a reporting division.

note: Wartime reporting/clearinghouse division; only Flag 6 fires because the emergency-linked body has no sunset, while the compliance-regime flags are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE for a records-coordination function.

title: Conforming the Central Intelligence Agency and Civil Service Retirement and Disability Systems With Respect to Cost of Living Adjustments

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order prescribes a mechanical COLA computation the Director of Central Intelligence must perform on fixed dates using a defined price index; the Director exercises no discretion, so no authority is concentrated.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Director of Central Intelligence is the named actor bound to compute and apply the adjustments by formula, so responsibility is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order does one thing—conform the CIA retirement COLA mechanism to the civil-service system—without packaging distinct matters.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The COLA calculation is fully specified (price-index comparisons on set dates, adjustment to the nearest 1/10 of 1 percent, defined effective dates), leaving no room for divergent good-faith application.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A formulaic annuity-adjustment mechanism creates no incentive for any actor to undermine its purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a standing administrative formula for recurring annuity adjustments—an evergreen administrative function—so the absence of a sunset is not a design gap.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order conforms a benefit computation to statute and removes no pre-existing external oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The adjustment is a mechanical entitlement computation for annuitants, not a self-reporting compliance regime lacking any external checker.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A narrow, formulaic retirement-COLA conforming action with no systemic or precedential implications.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order conforms the CIA system to the civil-service COLA standard without redirecting or countermanding any agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The COLA applies uniformly to covered annuities by formula with no discretionary exemption structure.

note: Mechanical retirement-COLA conforming order; the ongoing annuity computation makes Flags 4/8/11 applicable but they score ABSENT because the formula is fully specified. Systemic flags are NOT_APPLICABLE on this narrow action.

title: Removing Wage and Salary Controls Adopted Pursuant to the Stabilization Act of 1942

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order terminates all wage/salary stabilization controls; it grants no ongoing discretionary authority to any official, and is a self-executing removal.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The termination is self-executing with a saving clause preserving prior rights/liabilities for pending actions; there is no ongoing implementation that could fail without a responsible actor.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order does a single thing—terminate wage/salary controls under the Stabilization Act—with an integral saving clause; nothing unrelated is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Removing controls ends rather than creates an ongoing conduct rule, so there is no enforcement mechanism to assess going forward (the saving clause only preserves past matters).

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Terminating a control regime creates no ongoing behavioral rule or incentive structure to evaluate.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order removes authority rather than granting ongoing authority, so there is nothing to sunset.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order dismantles a control program but removes no external oversight mechanism over any continuing subject matter; it preserves prior rights for pending enforcement.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No ongoing compliance regime remains for which third-party enforcement standing would be relevant.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Deregulating wage/salary controls as part of the postwar transition is a stated, contained effect; no precedent usable in unrelated contexts is identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Terminating controls redirects no other agency's ongoing statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order eliminates a regime rather than defining a coverage population with exemptions.

note: Self-executing termination of a wartime control regime with a saving clause; decision-structure flags answer ABSENT and the compliance-regime/sunset/lateral flags are NOT_APPLICABLE because no ongoing rule remains.

title: Amending Executive Order No. 10450, as Amended, Relating to Security Requirements for Government Employment, and for Other Purposes

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order removes the Attorney General's and Subversive Activities Control Board's authority to designate organizations and abolishes the designated list; it withdraws a discretionary power rather than concentrating any new authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The amendments are self-executing revisions to EO 10450's text (revoking Section 12's list mechanism and refining the membership standard); administration of employee security remains with the existing structure, so responsibility is located.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The provisions coherently dismantle the organization-designation apparatus and conform the related membership standard; they are not distinct unrelated policy questions forced together.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The revised membership standard is tightened to require 'knowing membership with the specific intent of furthering the aims of' proscribed organizations, narrowing rather than broadening the operative standard in the ongoing employee-screening regime.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Removing the designation list and requiring specific intent creates no incentive that rewards undermining the security-screening purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This amends a standing employee-security procedural framework (EO 10450 as amended) through ordinary supersession; the underlying function is evergreen, so absence of a sunset is not a design gap.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order removes an executive designation/list-publication mechanism, not an external check on the executive; per the direction rule, dismantling a government-run listing power does not fire this removal-of-oversight flag.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The employee-security screening continues to be administered by agencies against defined standards; abolishing the organization list does not create a self-reporting enforcement gap.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Abolishing the Attorney General's list and revoking EO 11605 narrows a designation apparatus; the change forecloses rather than enables a broader authority claim, and no adverse precedent usable in unrelated contexts is identifiable.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order withdraws designation authority from the Attorney General and SACB but does so by amendment rather than redirecting those agencies' remaining statutory mandates into conflict.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order narrows a coverage/standard rather than creating a discretionary exemption structure that would swallow the security-screening rule.

note: Diff-aware: amends EO 10450 (revoking its Section 12 list mechanism and tightening the Section 8 membership standard) and revokes EO 9835 and EO 11605 (in predecessors); the net effect narrows and dismantles a designation apparatus, so all flags score ABSENT.

title: Federal Compliance With Pollution Control Standards

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Compliance responsibility is placed on each executive agency head, with EPA providing oversight and OMB resolving conflicts; authority is distributed across multiple actors rather than pooled in one official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Each agency head is 'responsible for compliance' with pollution standards for its own facilities and prepares its own pollution-control plan, so the entity that must comply also largely judges and plans its own compliance, creating a self-assessment gap softened but not closed by EPA review.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order coherently addresses one subject—federal-facility compliance with environmental pollution standards—across several statutes; nothing distinct is bundled.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The operative standard is defined as 'the same substantive, procedural, and other requirements that would apply to a private person,' importing existing statutory standards, and the conflict-resolution and notice-and-plan mechanics are specified.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: Because agencies self-plan and can seek presidential exemptions, and conflicts route through OMB (a budget body), an agency facing compliance costs has some incentive to pursue exemption or delay rather than abate, in tension with the compliance purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: Federal compliance with pollution-control law is an evergreen mandate tied to standing statutes, implemented through a permanent administrative structure and revised by supersession (it revokes EO 11752), so absence of a sunset is not a design gap.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds EPA review/inspection authority over federal facilities and preserves other enforcement procedures and sanctions (Sec. 1-604); it removes no pre-existing external oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: EPA and State/interstate/local agencies are given notice, review, and inspection roles to surface federal-facility violations, so enforcement does not rest solely on agency self-reporting.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order operationalizes existing statutory federal-facility compliance duties; no new precedential authority claim usable in unrelated contexts is identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: EPA's oversight and OMB's conflict-resolution roles are coordinating functions layered on agencies' own compliance duties; no agency's operational statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Exemptions from pollution standards may be granted, but the order channels them through required presidential statutory determinations (national security or paramount U.S. interest) with EPA advice and OMB timing—so while bounded, exemption authority is concentrated in the President with broad 'paramount interest' criteria that can carve facilities out of coverage.

note: Federal-facility pollution-compliance order; largely clean with EPA oversight added, but agency self-planning fires Flags 2 and 5 and the presidential 'paramount interest' exemption power fires Flag 11. Revokes EO 11752 (in predecessors).

title: Adopting and Ratifying the Capture of the German Steamship "Milwaukee"

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The President performs a single statutory act—adopting and ratifying a specific prize capture; the authority is exhausted by the one-time ratification and concentrates nothing ongoing.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: A one-time ratification of a named vessel's capture under specific statutes; there is no ongoing implementation that could fail without a responsible actor.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order does a single thing—ratify one prize capture—with nothing else packaged in.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time prize ratification creates no ongoing conduct rule to enforce against any party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral rule or compliance relationship is created by ratifying a single capture.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing ratification grants no ongoing authority that could be subject to reauthorization pressure.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Ratifying a prize capture under the cited statutes removes no pre-existing external oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance regime exists for which a third party would have enforcement standing.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A single-vessel prize ratification with no systemic or precedential implications.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No second agency's statutory mandate is redirected by ratifying a capture.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order defines no coverage population subject to exemptions.

note: One-time prize-capture ratification; decision-structure flags answer ABSENT and all compliance-regime, sunset, and systemic flags are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE for a single fixed act.

title: Executive Office of the President (Office of Administration)

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order establishes an Office of Administration headed by a Director who provides common administrative support and reports to the President, and expressly disclaims program/management authority over EOP units; it concentrates no substantive policy authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Director is responsible for administrative support while 'the head of each unit shall remain responsible' for program and management functions (Sec. 4(b)), so responsibility lines are explicitly drawn.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order coherently effectuates one reorganization—centralizing common administrative services in the EOP—without packaging distinct policy questions.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Office provides internal administrative services and enforces no conduct rule against any regulated party, so there is no enforcement mechanism to assess.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: An internal shared-services reorganization creates no ongoing behavioral rule over external actors that could be gamed.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a permanent administrative structure implementing a Reorganization Plan, an evergreen administrative function, so the absence of a sunset is not a design gap.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order transfers common administrative support functions to the new Office but expressly preserves functions vested by law in unit heads (Sec. 5); it removes no external oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The Office serves internal EOP units rather than running a compliance regime over regulated parties, so third-party enforcement standing is not relevant.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Centralizing shared administrative services within the EOP is a contained organizational change with no identifiable precedent usable in unrelated contexts.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order transfers and reassigns the administrative support functions, records, property, personnel, and appropriations of multiple EOP units to the new Office of Administration (Secs. 5-6), redirecting functions and resources those units previously performed, creating some reassignment friction.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The transfer carves out functions vested by law in unit heads and those the White House Office performs in direct support of the President—defined, narrow limits rather than a discretionary exemption swallowing the reorganization.

note: EOP shared-services reorganization; largely clean with explicit responsibility carve-outs, but the transfer of administrative functions/resources from multiple units to the new Office fires Flag 10 modestly. Compliance-regime flags are NOT_APPLICABLE for an internal-services body.

title: Organization for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A one-time presidential designation of one named organization for statutory immunities under a specific finding; no ongoing discretionary authority is pooled in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The designation is self-executing under the International Organizations Immunities Act; there is no implementation chain that could fail without a responsible actor.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order does one thing—designate the OPCW for statutory immunities—without packaging distinct matters.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order confers status under a statute rather than establishing an ongoing conduct rule to enforce against any party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A fixed status designation creates no ongoing behavioral rule that any actor could game.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: A one-time designation grants no ongoing discretionary authority that could be subject to reauthorization pressure.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The designation removes no pre-existing oversight mechanism and expressly preserves immunities acquired by international agreement or congressional action.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance regime exists for which a third party would have enforcement standing.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A single organization's immunity designation with no systemic or precedential implications beyond the statute's framework.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: No agency's statutory mandate is redirected by an immunity designation.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The immunities conferred are the statute's own; the order creates no discretionary carve-out structure.

note: One-time international-organization immunity designation (parallel to order-253/289 pattern); decision-structure flags answer ABSENT and the compliance-regime, sunset, and systemic flags are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE.

title: Amending Regulations Relating to the Safeguarding of Vessels, Harbors, Ports, and Waterfront Facilities of the United States

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The amendments vest the Commandant (and captain of the port/District Commander) with broad discretion to deny port-security cards when unsatisfied that a person's 'character and habits of life' are non-inimical to security, to revoke cards, and to 'prescribe such conditions and restrictions... as he finds to be necessary,' concentrating open-ended screening and restriction authority in one officer.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Commandant and captain of the port are named responsible officers within a defined Coast Guard chain of supervision, so responsibility for the security determinations is located.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The amendments coherently strengthen a single subject—waterfront/vessel security—by updating enforcement, credentialing, and facility-safety provisions; nothing unrelated is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Credentialing turns on whether the Commandant is 'satisfied that the character and habits of life' would not be 'inimical to the security of the United States'—a standard with no defined criteria under which two officials could reach very different good-faith denials.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The port-security screening scheme creates no incentive that rewards undermining its stated security purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: These security controls rest on the Magnuson Act's national-emergency footing (Public Law 679 amending the 1917 Act), crisis-linked authority with a natural end-state, yet the amended regulations carry no expiration or reauthorization mechanism.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The amendments update an existing regulatory framework and add facility-safety provisions; they remove no pre-existing external oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The scheme places affirmative security responsibility on masters, owners, and operators (Subpart 6.19) and is enforced by the captain of the port, so it does not rest solely on unchecked self-reporting.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Amending waterfront-security regulations is contained within the existing port-security framework; no precedent usable in unrelated contexts is identifiable from the text.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The Commandant consults the Secretary of Labor on credential forms but the authority stays within Coast Guard port-security functions; no other agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The Commandant 'may designate categories of merchant vessels to which the foregoing shall not apply' and may recognize other credentials in lieu of the port-security card—discretionary carve-out power over coverage concentrated in one officer with no bounding criteria.

note: Amends EO 10173's port-security regulations; the Commandant's open-ended 'character and habits'/'inimical to security' screening discretion drives Flags 1, 4, and 11, and the emergency-linked authority without sunset drives Flag 6. EO 10173 is not in the predecessors folder, so the amendment was assessed on this order's own detailed text.

title: Blocking Libyan Government Property in the United States or Held by U.S. Persons

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order blocks all Libyan government property and authorizes the Secretary of the Treasury to 'employ all powers granted to me by the International Emergency Economic Powers Act' to carry it out, concentrating broad discretionary administration of the blocking in one official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Authority is clearly assigned to the President and the Secretary of the Treasury (in consultation with State), so responsibility for administering the blocking is located in named officers.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order does one thing—block Libyan government property as an additional step to an existing emergency—without packaging distinct policy questions.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order grants Treasury the full sweep of IEEPA powers 'to carry out the provisions of this Order' without specifying trigger conditions or limits, so the operative scope of implementing regulations is left indeterminate.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The asset-blocking design creates no incentive that rewards undermining its stated national-security/foreign-policy purpose.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order builds on a declared national emergency—crisis-response authority with a natural end tied to the emergency—yet contains no sunset or reauthorization trigger in its own text.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order is transmitted to Congress and published in the Federal Register as IEEPA/NEA require; it removes no pre-existing external oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Compliance with the blocking rests on U.S. persons holding Libyan property and on Treasury enforcement; the text creates no external actor with standing, access, and incentive to surface concealed blocked assets.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: The order reinforces the IEEPA asset-blocking template—broad presidential freezing of a foreign government's property administered by Treasury—as precedent readily deployable against other states in unrelated future disputes.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Treasury (with State) administers the blocking under its own delegated authority; no agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: By authorizing Treasury to employ 'all powers' under IEEPA to carry out the Order, it delegates open-ended discretion (including licensing/exception authority) to create exceptions to the blocking with no criteria bounding future carve-outs.

note: IEEPA blocking order building on EO 12543 (in predecessors) and its declared emergency; broad Treasury 'all powers' delegation drives Flags 1, 4, and 11, with the emergency lacking a textual sunset driving Flag 6, paralleling order-267.

title: Termination of the Office of Strategic Services and Disposition of Its Functions

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order transfers OSS functions and gives the Secretary of State and Secretary of War broad discretion to transfer, curtail, or discontinue the inherited intelligence activities 'whenever he deems it compatible with the national interest,' concentrating disposition discretion over these functions in single officers.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The transferred functions are assigned to named officers (Secretaries of State and War) with the Budget Director effectuating transfers, so responsibility for the disposition is clearly located.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order coherently terminates the OSS and distributes its research/intelligence functions between State and War; nothing unrelated is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: This is an internal reorganization/termination that enforces no conduct rule against any regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A reorganization of intelligence functions creates no ongoing behavioral rule over external actors that could be gamed.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order contains a built-in termination: the Interim Research and Intelligence Service 'shall be abolished as of the close of business December 31, 1945,' and the OSS is terminated, so the transitional structure is time-limited rather than open-ended.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order redistributes functions among departments but removes no pre-existing external oversight mechanism over intelligence activities.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: This is an internal reorganization, not a compliance regime over regulated parties for which third-party enforcement standing is relevant.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Transferring OSS research/intelligence functions into the Department of State and War rather than disbanding them preserves and relocates a standing peacetime intelligence capability, an institutional precedent that shaped the later national intelligence structure well beyond the immediate wind-down.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order carves OSS functions, personnel, and records between State and War (with the Budget Director redistributing as needed), reassigning operational intelligence capability across departments and creating potential jurisdictional friction over the transferred functions.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order defines no coverage population subject to exemptions; it disposes of an agency's functions.

note: OSS termination/redistribution; Flag 6 is cleared by the explicit December 31, 1945 abolition date, but the broad disposition discretion, preserved intelligence capability, and cross-department reassignment fire Flags 1, 9, and 10.

title: Administration of Scientific Research by Agencies of the Federal Government

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The National Science Foundation is given a recommending/coordinating role over federal basic-research policy while each agency retains its own research programs; authority is distributed rather than pooled in one body.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Foundation and each agency head have defined responsibilities (consult NSF, review programs, ensure effective practices), so responsibility for the research-coordination scheme is located.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order coherently addresses one subject—coordinating federal scientific research administration—without packaging distinct policy questions.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order sets internal coordination and consultation duties among agencies and enforces no conduct rule against any regulated party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: An internal research-coordination framework creates no ongoing behavioral rule over external actors that could be gamed.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: This is a standing administrative-coordination framework for a permanent government function (federal scientific research), an evergreen administrative matter, so the absence of a sunset is not a design gap.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds NSF consultation and interagency-sharing requirements and removes no pre-existing external oversight mechanism; it expressly preserves other agencies' basic-research roles.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The order concerns intra-government research coordination, not a compliance regime over regulated parties for which third-party enforcement standing is relevant.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Assigning NSF a coordinating role in federal research administration is contained; no precedent usable in unrelated contexts is identifiable from the text, and it safeguards institutional independence of universities.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: NSF acts 'in concert with each Federal agency concerned' and recommends rather than countermanding; agencies retain their mission research, so no statutory mandate is redirected.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order defines no coverage population subject to exemptions; it coordinates research administration.

note: Interagency research-coordination framework centered on NSF's recommending role; decision-structure and sunset flags answer ABSENT, and the compliance-regime flags are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE for an internal coordination order.

title: Establishing the Tybee Migratory Bird Refuge

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A self-executing land reservation setting apart described lands as a bird refuge under specific statutes; no discretionary authority is concentrated in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The refuge is placed under the Department of Agriculture with defined coordination for existing Commerce/War Department uses; there is no ongoing implementation that could fail without a responsible actor.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order does a single thing—reserve described lands as a named refuge subject to existing uses—without packaging distinct matters.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time land reservation creates no ongoing conduct rule to enforce against any party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral rule or compliance relationship is created by a refuge reservation.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing land reservation grants no ongoing discretionary authority that could be subject to reauthorization pressure.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The reservation is 'subject to existing valid rights' and expressly subordinate to Commerce/War Department uses, removing no pre-existing external oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance regime exists for which a third party would have enforcement standing.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A single refuge establishment with no systemic or precedential implications.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order expressly subordinates the Agriculture refuge use to existing Commerce and War Department jurisdiction 'without interference,' so it coordinates rather than redirects any agency's mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order defines no coverage population subject to exemptions.

note: Self-executing wildlife-refuge land reservation that expressly defers to existing agency uses; decision-structure flags answer ABSENT and compliance-regime/sunset/systemic flags are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE.

title: Continuing the Regulation of Exports

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order continues export-control administration under the Secretary of Commerce, keeping in force all export regulations and licensing authority under section 5(b) of the 1917 Act, concentrating broad control over exports in one department without a defined external check in the text.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Administration is clearly assigned to the Secretary of Commerce continuing the EO 11533 framework, so responsibility for export controls is located in a named officer.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order coherently continues one subject—export regulation—citing multiple statutory bases for the single continuation purpose; nothing distinct is packaged.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The order keeps in force the detailed existing Commerce export regulations (15 CFR pts. 368-399) and even specifies penalty limits ($10,000 maximum fine), so the operative enforcement standard is defined by that existing regulatory body.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: The order expressly continues export-control authority 'Notwithstanding the expiration of the Export Administration Act of 1969, as amended,' exercising the framework's authority under section 5(b) after the authorizing statute lapsed—the Zombie Emergency Trap, since the implementing agency continues exercising authority without the lapsed statute, reducing pressure to let the framework be reauthorized on its own terms.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order continues emergency-linked export controls indefinitely under standing national-emergency proclamations with no expiration or reauthorization mechanism—precisely the crisis-response authority that should face periodic reauthorization pressure and does not.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: By continuing the regulatory scheme under section 5(b) of the 1917 Act 'notwithstanding the expiration' of the Export Administration Act, the order routes export controls around the lapsed statute's own reauthorization process, bypassing the legislative check that expiration was meant to impose.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: Export controls are administered and enforced by Commerce with license and penalty mechanisms in the retained regulations, so enforcement does not rest solely on unchecked self-reporting.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: Using section 5(b) of the Trading with the Enemy Act to sustain an entire regulatory program after its authorizing statute expired sets a precedent for continuing lapsed statutory frameworks via emergency powers, an authority claim usable to keep other expired programs running.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The order preserves other officials' delegations under section 5(b) and keeps administration within Commerce; no agency's statutory mandate is redirected or countermanded.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order continues the existing licensing/regulatory scheme without creating a new discretionary exemption structure that would swallow export coverage.

note: The order continues export controls 'notwithstanding the expiration of the Export Administration Act of 1969,' triggering the Zombie Emergency Trap sub-flag (Flag 5 CRITICAL) per its narrowed v1.2.2 test, and firing Flags 6, 7, and 9 on the lapsed-statute continuation. Continues EO 11533/11683 (in predecessors).

title: Critical Infrastructure Protection

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: The order creates a Commission, Principals Committee, Steering Committee, Advisory Committee, and an interagency Task Force with distributed membership and layered review; no single official is given unchecked substantive authority.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The Commission reports through a Principals Committee overseen by a Steering Committee, with the IPTF chaired by the FBI and administered by DOD, a layered structure in which the study/coordination function's failure would not clearly fall on any single responsible actor.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order coherently addresses one subject—assessing and coordinating protection of critical infrastructures—without packaging distinct unrelated policy questions.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Commission studies, consults, and recommends and the IPTF coordinates existing efforts; the order enforces no conduct rule against any regulated party (Sec. 8(b) disclaims creating enforceable rights).

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: A study commission and coordination task force create no ongoing behavioral rule over external actors that could be gamed.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: The order contains explicit sunsets: the Commission and committees 'shall terminate 1 year from the date of this order' and the IPTF 'no later than 180 days after the termination of the Commission,' unless extended, so review/expiration pressure is built in.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The order adds a commission and task force, complies with the Federal Advisory Committee Act, and states (Sec. 8(a)) it is 'not intended to change any existing statutes or Executive orders'; it removes no pre-existing external oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The bodies study and coordinate rather than running a compliance regime over regulated parties, so third-party enforcement standing is not the relevant question.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: By defining 'critical infrastructures' and 'cyber threats' and building standing federal machinery (Commission plus an FBI-chaired IPTF sharing threat information across agencies), the order establishes a durable definitional and institutional framework for expanding federal cyber/infrastructure authority well beyond the one-year study.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The IPTF is placed in Justice under FBI chairmanship with DOD/NSA members to 'identify and coordinate existing expertise' across agencies, creating a new interagency coordination structure that overlays and could compete with existing agency infrastructure-protection programs (though Sec. 7(b) says it will not supplant them).

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order defines no coverage population subject to exemptions; it establishes study and coordination bodies.

note: Study-commission-plus-interagency-task-force order; Flag 6 is cleared by explicit termination dates, but the layered reporting structure (Flag 2), the durable definitional/institutional framework (Flag 9), and the new FBI-chaired interagency task force (Flag 10) fire. Compliance-regime flags are NOT_APPLICABLE.

title: Withdrawal of Public Land for Use of the War Department

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A self-executing withdrawal of a described 14.24-acre parcel from settlement/entry and reservation for War Department military use under specific statutes; no discretionary authority is concentrated in any official.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The land is reserved for the War Department by the order's terms; there is no ongoing implementation that could fail without a responsible actor.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: The order does a single thing—withdraw and reserve one described parcel for military use—without packaging distinct matters.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: A one-time land withdrawal creates no ongoing conduct rule to enforce against any party.

flags.name: Perverse Incentives
flags.justification: No ongoing behavioral rule or compliance relationship is created by a land withdrawal.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions
flags.justification: A one-time, self-executing land withdrawal grants no ongoing discretionary authority that could be subject to reauthorization pressure; its 'until revoked' clause reflects a permanent land-status change, not an ongoing authority grant.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: The withdrawal is 'subject to... valid existing rights' and removes no pre-existing external oversight mechanism.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: No compliance regime exists for which a third party would have enforcement standing.

flags.name: Second/Third-Order Effects
flags.justification: A single small-parcel land withdrawal with no systemic or precedential implications.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: The withdrawal reserves public land for the War Department without redirecting or countermanding another agency's statutory mandate.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: The order defines no coverage population subject to exemptions.

note: Self-executing public-land withdrawal/reservation; decision-structure flags answer ABSENT and the compliance-regime, sunset, and systemic flags are genuinely NOT_APPLICABLE per the Application Notes land-order test.
